title: Data, information, and records
chapter: Chapter 1. What a Database System Is
text: From raw data to structured record — context and structure turn meaningless values into queryable information. Every database system exists to answer one question: how do we keep track of things that matter? Before you write a single line of SQL, it helps to be precise about what "keeping track" actually means — and that starts with distinguishing three terms that people often blur together: data , information , and records . Data vs. Information Data is raw, uninterpreted values. The number 32 , the string "Paris" , the timestamp 2024-03-15 09:00:00 — each is just a value sitting in isolation. By themselves they mean nothing. You don't know whether 32 is a temperature, an age, a score, or a row count. Information is data that has been given context and meaning. Once you know that 32 is the current outside temperature in degrees Celsius in a city called "Paris" recorded at 2024-03-15 09:00:00 , those three values become a single piece of information: it was 32 °C in Paris on the morning of March 15, 2024. Note: This distinction isn't just philosophy — it drives database design. A column named val REAL stores data. A column named temperature_celsius REAL stores data with enough context to become information the moment someone reads it. The job of a database is to store data in a structured way so that the context is always recoverable and queries can reconstruct information reliably. Records: Data Organized for Retrieval A record is a named, structured collection of related data values that describe a single real-world entity or event. In a relational database a record maps directly to a row in a table, and each column in that row holds one attribute of the entity. Column Value What it represents observation_id 4821 A unique identifier for this measurement city Paris Where the reading was taken temperature_celsius 32.0 The measured value recorded_at 2024-03-15 09:00:00 When it was taken Every row in the table has the same columns (the same schema ), so the database can retrieve, compare, and aggregate records consistently. That uniform structure is what separates a database from a pile of text files. What makes a good record? Three properties matter most: Atomicity of fields — each column should hold one indivisible value. Storing "Paris, France" in a single column is tempting, but splitting it into city and country lets you query by country without string-parsing. A unique identifier — every record needs something that distinguishes it from all others. This is the primary key (more on keys in a later chapter). Consistent types — a column defined as REAL should never sneak in a string like "N/A" . Type discipline lets the database enforce meaning automatically. Seeing It Live The widget below creates a tiny weather_observations table and seeds it with a few records. Run the default query to see the raw rows, then try changing the WHERE clause to filter by city or temperature — you are turning data into information by adding the context of a specific question. Interactive SQL · Data vs. Records Notice that the same raw number 32.0 carries full meaning only because the surrounding columns supply the who , where , and when . Strip those away and you are back to plain data. The Takeaway Term Definition Database analog Data Raw, context-free values Individual cell values Information Data + context + interpretation A query result with meaningful column names Record A structured set of data describing one entity A row in a table These three concepts underpin everything else in this guide. When you design a table you are deciding which data to capture , how to give it context through column names and types , and how to group it into records that represent the real-world things you care about. Previous — Next → Database vs file storage

title: Database vs file storage
chapter: Chapter 1. What a Database System Is
text: Files store bytes. Databases store structured records and let you query, update, and protect them safely. If you have ever stored data in a CSV file, a JSON file, or even a plain text file, you have already solved the same problem a database solves — just with different tools. Understanding why developers reach for a database instead of a file is one of the most useful things you can know before writing your first CREATE TABLE . What file storage looks like Imagine a small app that tracks employee salaries. The simplest approach is a CSV: id,name,department,salary 1,Ada,Engineering,95000 2,Bola,Marketing,72000 3,Chidi,Engineering,88000 This works fine for a few hundred rows and one user reading the file at a time. To find all Engineering salaries you write a script that opens the file, reads every line, and filters in memory. To update Ada's salary you read the whole file, rebuild it with the new value, and write it all back out. Where files fall short Files are a blunt instrument once you need any of the following: Need File approach Problem Query a subset of data Read entire file, filter in code Slow; all filtering logic lives in your app Update one record Read, modify, rewrite entire file Risk of corruption if the process crashes mid-write Concurrent access Multiple writers race to overwrite Data loss or file corruption Relationships Store IDs, join files in code No guarantee referential integrity is maintained Search on a non-ID field Scan every row No way to index; gets slower as the file grows These aren't hypothetical problems — they are precisely the bugs that motivated the creation of relational database systems in the 1970s. What a database adds A database management system (DBMS) layers several capabilities on top of raw storage: Structured querying. Instead of writing file-parsing code, you describe what you want in SQL and let the DBMS figure out how to get it efficiently. Atomicity. When you update Ada's salary, the DBMS guarantees the write either completes fully or not at all — a crash halfway through leaves the data intact, not half-written. Concurrent access. Multiple clients can read and write simultaneously. The DBMS serialises conflicting writes so neither overwrites the other. Indexes. The DBMS can maintain a sorted index on any column so lookups by department or salary are fast without scanning every row. Referential integrity. Foreign keys let the DBMS enforce that an employee_id in a salaries table actually refers to a real employee — something a CSV can never guarantee. Note: A DBMS is not magic — it still writes to files on disk. The difference is that those files are managed by a purpose-built engine that understands structure, concurrency, and recovery. See it in SQL The widget below recreates the employees example as a real database table running in your browser. Try changing the WHERE clause, or add ORDER BY salary DESC — no file parsing needed. Interactive SQL · employees Notice what you did not have to write: no file open, no loop, no manual filter, no sort algorithm. The database handles all of that — and it would handle it just as efficiently with a million rows as with five. When a file is actually fine Databases are not always the right answer. A flat file is perfectly appropriate when: Data is written once and read sequentially (logs, exports, backups). There is only one writer and one reader at a time. The dataset is small enough that a full scan is instant and structure overhead is unnecessary. You need a portable snapshot that any tool can open without a running server. The decision is not "files are bad, databases are good." It is: does your access pattern require querying, concurrent writes, integrity guarantees, or scale? If yes, reach for a database. If no, a file may be simpler and perfectly correct. ← Previous Data, information, and records Next → Database, table, row, column

title: Database, table, row, column
chapter: Chapter 1. What a Database System Is
text: A database is a container of tables; each table has columns (the schema) and rows (the records). Columns define the shape; rows hold the data. Every database system organizes data around four concepts that show up everywhere: the database , the table , the row , and the column . Master these four and you have the mental model that underlies almost every query you will ever write. The Building Blocks Think of a database as a named container — a single file or a managed set of files that holds related data together. A business might have one database for its product catalog and a separate one for its HR records. Keeping them apart prevents unrelated data from mixing and makes access control simpler. Inside a database you have tables . A table is a two-dimensional structure: rows going down, columns going across — exactly like a spreadsheet tab, but with stricter rules. Every table has a fixed set of columns declared upfront, and every row must conform to that structure. Concept Analogy Holds Database A filing cabinet One or more tables Table A drawer with labeled slots Rows that share the same columns Row A single filled-in form One record's worth of data Column A field on the form One attribute, same type in every row A column (also called a field or attribute ) describes one property of the thing you are tracking. Each column has a data type — INTEGER , TEXT , REAL , DATE , etc. — that tells the database what values are allowed. Declaring types upfront catches mistakes early: you cannot accidentally store the word "hello" in a column meant for prices. A row (also called a record or tuple ) is one complete entry in the table. If the table is employees , each row is one employee. Rows do not have a guaranteed order unless you ask for one with ORDER BY . A Concrete Example Imagine you are building a small bookshop app. You might start with a single table called books : id title author price 1 The Pragmatic Programmer Hunt & Thomas 49.95 2 Designing Data-Intensive Applications Kleppmann 54.99 3 Database Internals Petrov 44.99 The database might be named bookshop . The table is books . Each horizontal line of data is a row — one book. id , title , author , and price are the four columns . Notice that id is an integer, title and author are text, and price is a decimal number. Those type distinctions matter: the database will refuse to store a price like "free" if the column is declared as REAL . Note: The id column here acts as a primary key — a value that uniquely identifies each row. Primary keys are introduced in a later chapter, but you will see them in almost every real table. Try It Live The widget below creates the books table, inserts the three rows above, and runs a simple SELECT . Edit the query and hit Run to explore. Interactive SQL · Books table basics Try changing ORDER BY price ASC to ORDER BY title ASC , or add a WHERE price < 50 clause to filter rows. Each experiment reinforces the relationship between columns (the structure) and rows (the data). Why the Separation Matters Splitting data into tables, rows, and columns is not bureaucracy for its own sake. It enables three things that scale: Consistency — the column definition enforces the same shape for every row. You never get a customer record with a missing phone-number field and another with two. Efficient retrieval — the database can skip straight to the columns you need instead of scanning unstructured blobs of text. Relationships — columns in one table can reference rows in another (via foreign keys), letting you link customers to orders, orders to products, and so on — without duplicating data. These four concepts — database, table, row, column — are the grammar of every SQL statement you will write. Every SELECT , INSERT , UPDATE , and DELETE is just a way of reading or changing rows in tables. ← Previous Database vs file storage Next → Queries, transactions, and schemas

title: Queries, transactions, and schemas
chapter: Chapter 1. What a Database System Is
text: Three pillars: the schema defines the shape, queries retrieve and manipulate, transactions keep multi-step changes safe. Every time you ask a database "who are my top customers?" or "how much stock is left?", three distinct concepts are at work under the hood: a query that fetches the answer, a transaction that keeps changes safe, and a schema that defines the shape of the data. Understanding each — and how they relate — is the foundation for everything else in this guide. Schemas: the blueprint A schema is a formal description of how data is organized: which tables exist, what columns each table has, and what type of value each column holds. It is the contract between the application and the database. CREATE TABLE orders ( id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, customer TEXT NOT NULL, amount REAL NOT NULL, placed TEXT NOT NULL -- stored as ISO date string in SQLite ); The schema says: every order must have an id , a customer name, a numeric amount , and a placed date. The database enforces this — try inserting a row with a missing amount and it will refuse. Think of a schema as the spreadsheet's column headers, but with rules attached: required vs. optional, integers vs. text, references to other tables. Those rules are called constraints , and they are what let you trust the data you get back. Note: "Schema" sometimes refers to a single table's structure, and sometimes to the full set of tables in a database. The meaning is usually clear from context. Queries: asking questions A query is a request sent to the database — typically written in SQL (Structured Query Language). The database engine interprets the query, finds the relevant rows, and returns a result set. SQL queries are declarative : you say what you want, not how to find it. The database figures out the efficient path on its own. The core of most queries is SELECT … FROM … WHERE : Clause Purpose SELECT Which columns to include in the result FROM Which table(s) to read WHERE Filter rows to only those matching a condition ORDER BY Sort the result LIMIT Cap the number of rows returned Try the widget below. The setup creates an orders table with five rows. The default query finds orders above a threshold — edit the amount or add an ORDER BY clause to explore. Interactive SQL · queries Transactions: keeping changes safe A transaction is a group of database operations that are treated as a single, indivisible unit. Either every operation in the group succeeds and is committed to disk, or — if anything goes wrong — none of them are, and the database is rolled back to where it started. The classic example is a bank transfer. Moving $200 from account A to account B involves two writes: Deduct $200 from A. Add $200 to B. If a crash happens between step 1 and step 2, the money vanishes. A transaction prevents this by wrapping both steps in a single commit. The database guarantees they happen together or not at all. Transactions are governed by four properties, collectively known as ACID : Property What it means Atomicity All operations in the transaction succeed, or none do Consistency The transaction can only leave the database in a valid state (all constraints still satisfied) Isolation Concurrent transactions don't see each other's partial work Durability Once committed, the change survives crashes and power loss The widget below simulates the two-step transfer. Watch what happens when you run it — then try commenting out the COMMIT line (prepend -- ) and re-running: the change is visible inside the transaction but is never finalized. Interactive SQL · transactions How the three fit together Schemas, queries, and transactions operate at different layers but depend on each other: The schema defines what can exist in the database and enforces correctness at write time. Queries read (and write) data within the structure the schema defines. Transactions wrap queries into safe, atomic units so that concurrent users and unexpected failures can't corrupt the data. Remove any one of the three and the system breaks down: a schemaless store has no guarantees about shape; a store without queries can't be asked meaningful questions; a store without transactions can't safely handle more than one writer at a time. ← Previous Database, table, row, column Next → The role of the database engine

title: The role of the database engine
chapter: Chapter 1. What a Database System Is
text: The database engine is the software that sits between your app and disk, handling query processing, transactions, storage, and recovery. When you store data in a spreadsheet, you are the engine — you decide where data lives, how to find it, and what happens when two people edit the same cell at once. A database engine (also called a storage engine or database management system kernel) is the software layer that takes those responsibilities away from you. It sits between your application and the raw bytes on disk, turning high-level requests like "give me all orders placed today" into precise read/write operations — and making sure nothing goes wrong in the process. What the Engine Actually Does The database engine has four core jobs, each one solving a real problem: Job The problem it solves Example Storage management Files and disk blocks are a poor fit for structured data Arranges rows and indexes on disk pages for fast access Query processing Applications shouldn't need to know how data is stored Parses SQL, builds an execution plan, returns results Concurrency control Multiple clients reading/writing simultaneously can corrupt data Uses locks or MVCC so transactions don't interfere Recovery Crashes happen; committed data must survive them Replays a write-ahead log to restore a consistent state None of these are glamorous, but every application that touches a database depends on all four working correctly at the same time. The Engine and the Query Lifecycle When your application sends a SQL statement, the engine processes it through several internal stages before a single byte of data moves: SQL text │ ▼ Parser ← checks syntax, builds a parse tree │ ▼ Optimizer ← rewrites the query into the cheapest execution plan │ ▼ Executor ← runs the plan: scans tables, applies filters, sorts │ ▼ Storage layer ← reads/writes pages from the buffer pool or disk The optimizer stage is where the engine earns its keep. Given SELECT * FROM orders WHERE customer_id = 42 , it decides whether to scan every row in the table or use an index — a choice that can mean the difference between milliseconds and minutes on a large dataset. Note: The optimizer works with statistics about your data (row counts, value distributions) rather than reading every row. Keeping those statistics fresh — via ANALYZE in PostgreSQL or UPDATE STATISTICS in SQL Server — is one of the most impactful routine maintenance tasks you can do. Trying It: the Engine Working Behind the Scenes The widget below creates a small orders table and seeds it with a few rows. The default query uses a WHERE clause — behind the scenes, SQLite's engine parses it, checks whether an index exists on customer_id , and chooses an execution plan. Try running EXPLAIN SELECT ... to see the engine's decision exposed. Interactive SQL · Query lifecycle Now create an index and re-run the plan to watch the engine switch strategies: Interactive SQL · Index effect The Engine Is Not the Database It is worth drawing one clear boundary: the engine is the processing and storage machinery; the database is the organised collection of data it manages. A single database server (like PostgreSQL or MySQL) runs one engine but can host many databases. Some systems — such as MySQL with InnoDB and MyISAM — even let you choose a different engine per table, though in practice most teams pick one and stick with it. Understanding the engine's role matters because every design decision you make — schema shape, index strategy, transaction scope — is really an instruction to the engine about how to do its job. The better you understand what the engine is optimising for, the better your schema choices will be. ← Previous Queries, transactions, and schemas Next → Clients, servers, and drivers

title: Clients, servers, and drivers
chapter: Chapter 1. What a Database System Is
text: Three roles: the client calls, the driver translates, and the server owns the data. The wire protocol bridges them all. When you run a query, you are rarely talking to the database directly. Almost every database interaction involves three distinct roles: a client that asks questions, a server that stores and processes data, and a driver that makes them speak the same language. Understanding these three pieces explains why databases work the same way across a dozen programming languages — and what actually happens in the milliseconds between you calling a function and getting a result back. The client-server model A database server is a long-running process dedicated to managing data. It listens on a network port (PostgreSQL defaults to 5432 , MySQL to 3306 , SQL Server to 1433 ), accepts connections, runs queries, and returns results. The server owns the data files on disk and enforces rules — access control, transactions, constraints — on everything that touches them. A client is anything that connects to that server to send queries and receive results. The client could be: Client type Example Interactive shell psql , mysql , sqlite3 GUI tool DBeaver, TablePlus, DataGrip Application code A Node.js API, a Django app Another server A reporting service, an ETL job The key insight: the server does the heavy lifting. Sorting a million rows, joining three tables, enforcing a foreign-key constraint — all of that happens inside the server process. The client just sends SQL text and gets rows back. Note: SQLite breaks this pattern intentionally. It is an embedded database — the "server" is a library linked directly into your application, and the data file is local. There is no network port or separate process. This makes SQLite ideal for local tools and tests but unsuitable for multiple simultaneous writers. What a driver does The client and server need a common wire protocol — a byte-level agreement on how to frame a query, authenticate, stream rows, and signal errors. Writing that protocol by hand for every application would be painful. That is what a driver (sometimes called a connector or adapter ) is for. A driver is a language-specific library that: Opens and manages a TCP connection to the server. Handles authentication (username/password, SSL certificates, Kerberos, etc.). Serialises your query string into the protocol format the server expects. Deserialises the binary response back into native language types (strings, integers, dates). Exposes a clean API your application code can call. Each database has official or community drivers for every popular language: Database Python driver Node.js driver Java driver PostgreSQL psycopg2 / asyncpg pg pgjdbc MySQL mysql-connector-python mysql2 MySQL Connector/J SQLite built-in sqlite3 module better-sqlite3 xerial/sqlite-jdbc A higher-level library (an ORM like SQLAlchemy or Prisma) sits on top of a driver — it generates SQL for you, but it still needs the driver to actually talk to the server. Putting it together: a query's round trip Here is what happens when an application runs SELECT * FROM orders WHERE status = 'pending' : Application code │ calls driver API (e.g., cursor.execute(...)) ▼ Driver │ serialises query → TCP bytes → sends to port 5432 ▼ Database server (network) │ parses SQL, plans query, reads data pages │ serialises result rows → TCP bytes → sends back ▼ Driver │ deserialises bytes → Python list / JS array / Java ResultSet ▼ Application code │ iterates rows The round-trip time on a local network is typically under 1 ms. Over the public internet it can be 50–200 ms — which is why database servers are always deployed close to the application servers that query them, and why connection pooling (reusing an already-open TCP connection rather than opening a new one for every query) matters at scale. Try it live The widget below runs SQLite entirely in your browser — sql.js plays the role of both the driver and the embedded server. The data-setup attribute seeds the database (like migrations run at startup), and the textarea is the query your "application" sends. Try changing 'pending' to 'shipped' and re-running. Interactive SQL · orders round-trip ← Previous The role of the database engine Next → Why database systems matter

title: Why database systems matter
chapter: Chapter 1. What a Database System Is
text: Without a database, every app reinvents the data wheel, duplicating logic and introducing bugs. With one, apps share a battle-tested single source of truth. Before the relational database existed, every application that needed to store data had to invent its own storage format, its own query logic, and its own approach to handling two users writing at the same time. This led to a landscape of incompatible, fragile, and expensive-to-maintain systems. Database systems emerged to solve that problem once — correctly — so every application built on top of them could inherit the solution for free. The core problems databases solve At its heart, a database system exists to answer five recurring challenges that arise whenever software needs to persist and retrieve structured data at any meaningful scale. Problem Without a database With a database Finding records Scan every record in the file Query with an index; fetch only what you need Concurrent writers Two writers overwrite each other Locking and transaction isolation prevent conflicts Partial failure A crash mid-write corrupts data The engine rolls back incomplete changes automatically Enforcing rules Application code checks every write Constraints and foreign keys enforced at the storage layer Sharing data Each app maintains its own copy Multiple apps query one authoritative source Each of these problems sounds manageable when you are building something small. They compound quickly once you have thousands of records, dozens of concurrent users, or a codebase maintained by multiple teams. A motivating scenario Consider a ride-sharing app. At any given second: A driver updates their location. A passenger books a new ride. A billing service reads trip totals for invoicing. An analytics job counts completed rides per city. Without a database, each of those four actions would need its own file or in-memory store, carefully synchronised by hand. A crash between writing a booking and debiting a wallet would leave the system in an inconsistent state. Recovering from that inconsistency would require custom code — code that is notoriously hard to get right. A database system handles all four workloads simultaneously, protects against the mid-write crash, and lets engineers focus on ride-matching logic rather than storage plumbing. Note: "Database system" (or DBMS — Database Management System) refers to the engine itself: the software that manages storage, concurrency, and recovery. The database is the collection of data it manages. The distinction matters; you will hear both terms throughout this guide. What you get out of the box When you reach for a database system instead of rolling your own storage, you inherit decades of engineering work without writing a single line of infrastructure code: Durability. Committed data survives a server crash. The engine uses a write-ahead log (WAL) to replay any writes that didn't fully flush to disk before the failure. Isolation. Concurrent queries don't see each other's half-finished work unless you explicitly ask them to. Declarative querying. You describe what you want; the query planner figures out the most efficient how . A shared interface. SQL is understood by thousands of tools, languages, and frameworks — one skill transfers everywhere. The widget below lets you see declarative querying in action. The database holds a simplified snapshot of rides. Try changing 'completed' to 'active' , or swap SUM for COUNT — notice you never tell the database how to find the rows, only what to return. Interactive SQL · ride-sharing snapshot Why this matters beyond the technical details The existence of reliable database systems changed what software could do. Before them, building a payroll system, an airline reservation system, or a hospital record system meant months of custom storage work before you could write a single line of business logic. Database systems moved that burden out of individual projects and into a shared, well-tested layer. Today, virtually every piece of software you interact with — your bank's website, the app on your phone, the service that streams your music — rests on a database system of some kind. Learning how they work is not an academic exercise: it is the foundation for understanding how data-driven software is built, scaled, and kept correct under pressure. ← Previous Clients, servers, and drivers Next → Common kinds of database workloads

title: Common kinds of database workloads
chapter: Chapter 1. What a Database System Is
text: The workload spectrum: OLTP handles many small operations fast; OLAP crunches large datasets for insight. Most real systems sit somewhere in between. Every database stores data, but not every database is asked to do the same kind of work. A system that handles thousands of customer checkouts per second has completely different needs from one that crunches a year's worth of sales data overnight. Recognizing these workload patterns early is one of the most useful instincts you can build — it shapes every design decision that follows. OLTP: Online Transaction Processing OLTP is what most people picture when they imagine a running application: a web store, a banking app, a ride-share platform. The defining characteristics are: Short, targeted operations — read or write a small number of rows at a time. High concurrency — many users hitting the database simultaneously. Strong correctness guarantees — a payment must either complete fully or not at all. A typical OLTP query looks like this: -- Fetch a single customer's recent orders SELECT order_id, total, placed_at FROM orders WHERE customer_id = 42 ORDER BY placed_at DESC LIMIT 10; Indexes are critical here. Without one on customer_id , the database would scan every row in the table just to find this one customer's orders. Try it yourself — the widget below has a small orders table with and without an index so you can explore the shape of OLTP queries: Interactive SQL · OLTP queries Note: OLTP databases are usually normalized — data is split into many related tables (customers, orders, products) to avoid duplication and make writes fast and consistent. OLAP: Online Analytical Processing OLAP is the opposite end of the spectrum. Instead of serving one user's request quickly, it answers big analytical questions: "What were total sales by region last quarter?" or "Which product category has the highest return rate?" Key characteristics: OLTP OLAP Rows touched per query Few (1 – thousands) Many (millions – billions) Query frequency Very high Lower Query type Lookup / insert / update Aggregation / scan Who runs it Application code Analysts, BI tools Schema style Normalized Often denormalized or columnar A typical OLAP query: -- Total revenue per product category for Q1 2024 SELECT category, SUM(revenue) AS total_revenue FROM sales_fact WHERE sale_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-03-31' GROUP BY category ORDER BY total_revenue DESC; This query might touch millions of rows. OLAP systems are optimized for this with techniques like columnar storage (reading only the columns the query needs) and pre-aggregated summaries. HTAP and Hybrid Workloads A growing category sits in the middle: HTAP (Hybrid Transactional/Analytical Processing) . Businesses increasingly want real-time analytics on the same data that their OLTP system is writing — without the delay of copying data to a separate warehouse. Examples of HTAP-leaning databases include TiDB and SingleStore. Even PostgreSQL can handle moderate analytical queries with the right indexes and query planner hints. Reveal: Why can't you just run analytics on your OLTP database? You can , but it causes problems. Long-running analytical scans hold locks or consume I/O, slowing down concurrent short transactions that real users are waiting on. OLAP workloads also benefit from different physical layouts (columnar vs. row storage) that are fundamentally incompatible with how OLTP databases store rows. Separating the two systems — or using a purpose-built HTAP engine — keeps each workload from degrading the other. Other Workload Patterns Beyond OLTP and OLAP, a few other patterns appear often enough to name: Search workloads — full-text search, fuzzy matching, ranked results. Standard SQL databases handle this poorly; systems like Elasticsearch or PostgreSQL's tsvector columns are better suited. Time-series workloads — sensor readings, metrics, financial ticks. Data arrives in strict time order and is almost never updated. Databases like TimescaleDB or InfluxDB compress and query this kind of data far more efficiently than a general-purpose system. Graph workloads — social networks, recommendation engines, fraud detection where relationships between entities matter more than individual records. SQL joins can model graphs, but graph databases (Neo4j, Amazon Neptune) store and traverse edges natively. Knowing which workload category your system falls into is the first step toward choosing the right database engine — and the right schema, indexes, and hardware to go with it. ← Previous Why database systems matter Next → A first mental model

title: A first mental model
chapter: Chapter 1. What a Database System Is
text: A layered mental model: your app talks SQL to the engine, which handles parsing, optimization, transactions, and physical storage — all transparently. Before you write a single query, it helps to have a picture in your head — a simple mental model you can reach for whenever something confusing comes up. This page builds that picture from scratch. The Core Idea: Organized, Queryable Storage A database system is, at its heart, a program that stores data and answers questions about that data — reliably and efficiently, even when the dataset is large, multiple people are reading and writing at the same time, and the power goes out mid-write. That last part is what separates a database from a folder of files. A spreadsheet can store data. A text file can store data. But neither can guarantee that a half-written update won't corrupt everything else, or let a thousand users query the same records simultaneously without stepping on each other. Hold this image: a database system is a trusted intermediary that sits between your application and your data. Your app never touches the raw storage directly — it sends requests, and the database handles the rest. Your Application │ │ "Give me all orders over $100" ▼ ┌─────────────────────┐ │ Database Engine │ ← parses, plans, and executes your request └─────────────────────┘ │ ▼ Stored Data (disk) Three Layers to Picture When you interact with a database system, three distinct layers are always in play. Keeping them separate in your mind prevents a lot of confusion. Layer What it is Example Logical How you think about the data — tables, rows, columns, relationships A customers table with an email column Query The language used to ask for or change data SELECT email FROM customers WHERE country = 'US' Physical How data is actually stored on disk — files, pages, indexes B-tree index on email ; heap file for rows The great insight of relational databases (and the reason SQL became so dominant) is that the logical layer is decoupled from the physical layer . You describe what you want; the engine figures out how to find it. You never need to manually seek through a file to retrieve row 4,721. Note: This separation is called data independence . It means you can add an index, reorganize storage, or upgrade hardware without rewriting a single query. A Concrete Analogy Imagine a large library. You do not wander through the stacks yourself — you ask a librarian. The librarian knows where every book is, enforces rules (no borrowing the same book twice simultaneously), keeps a log of every loan, and can restore order if something goes wrong. The library's catalog describes what exists (logical layer); the physical shelves hold the actual books; and you interact only via the librarian (the engine). A database system works the same way: The catalog (sometimes called the schema ) describes tables, column types, and relationships. The engine receives your query, consults the catalog, finds the data, and returns a result. The storage layer holds the actual bytes — you never touch it directly. Try It: The Engine at Work The widget below sets up a tiny orders table. Run the default query, then try modifying it — notice that you describe what rows you want, not how to find them. The engine handles the search. Interactive SQL · Orders table Try changing the WHERE conditions — filter by country = 'UK' , or remove the amount threshold entirely. You are always describing the result you want , not the steps to retrieve it. That is the logical layer in action. What This Model Gets You With this mental model in place — trusted intermediary, three distinct layers, describe-what-not-how — several things that might otherwise seem magical start to make sense: Indexes are a physical-layer optimization. They speed up queries without changing what results you get. Transactions protect the logical layer: either all your changes land, or none do — no half-written state. Query optimizers translate your logical request into the most efficient physical access path. Each of those topics gets its own chapter. For now, carry this picture forward: a database is not just storage — it is a disciplined, queryable, crash-safe system with clear layers, and you interact with the logical layer through a declarative language called SQL. ← Previous Common kinds of database workloads Next → Chapter 1 Summary: What We Learned

title: Chapter 1 Summary: What We Learned
chapter: Chapter 1. What a Database System Is
text: Page Topic Takeaway in One Sentence 001 Data, information, and records Data becomes information when structured into records — the fundamental unit databases operate on. 002 Database vs file storage Databases add indexing, concurrency, crash safety, and declarative querying on top of raw storage — files alone cannot do this. 003 Database, table, row, column Tables organize data into rows (records) and columns (fields), giving every piece of data a predictable place. 004 Queries, transactions, and schemas Queries read/write, transactions bundle operations atomically, and schemas define the shape of the data. 005 The role of the database engine The engine is the brain: it parses SQL, optimizes access paths, and manages storage, concurrency, and recovery. 006 Clients, servers, and drivers Applications talk to databases via drivers/libraries; the database server handles multiple concurrent client connections. 007 Why database systems matter Databases are the source of truth for every business-critical application — they guarantee durability and correctness at scale. 008 Common kinds of database workloads OLTP (many small writes/reads), OLAP (few big analytical queries), and hybrid patterns define how databases are used. 009 A first mental model A database has three layers (logical, query, physical); you say what you want, the engine decides how to get it. Reveal: How do these ideas connect to the next chapter? Chapter 1 gave you the big picture: what a database engine does, how clients connect, and why databases matter. You now know the three-layer model — logical, query, physical. Chapter 2 zooms into that logical layer and answers the question you are probably already asking: how is data actually organized inside a database? You will learn about tables, keys, constraints, data types, and the fundamental tradeoff between normalization and denormalization. Everything you build in later chapters rests on this organizational foundation. ← Previous A first mental model Next → Logical structure vs physical structure

title: Logical structure vs physical structure
text: Logical structure is what you design (tables, keys, relations). Physical structure is how the engine stores bytes. They're deliberately decoupled — that's data independence. When you write a query, you think in terms of tables, rows, and columns. The database engine, meanwhile, is thinking about bytes, pages, and disk seeks. These two worlds — what the data means and how it is stored — are deliberately kept separate. Understanding that separation is one of the most clarifying ideas in all of database engineering. The Logical Structure: What You See The logical structure is the data model as the user perceives it. It answers the question: what does the data look like and how does it relate? In a relational database, the logical structure is defined by your schema — the tables, columns, data types, primary keys, and foreign-key relationships you declare. When you write SELECT name, email FROM users WHERE active = 1 , you are reasoning entirely at the logical level. You do not care whether users lives in one file or ten, or whether the rows are stored in alphabetical order or at random. Layer Vocabulary Who cares? Logical Tables, rows, columns, keys, views, joins Application developers, data analysts Physical Pages, blocks, B-trees, heap files, indexes, WAL Database engine, DBAs tuning performance This split is sometimes called data independence : changing how data is stored should not break the SQL that reads it. The Physical Structure: What Actually Happens on Disk The physical structure is how the database engine materializes your logical tables into bytes. Every major relational engine uses a similar set of primitives: Pages (or blocks): Storage is divided into fixed-size chunks — typically 4 KB to 16 KB. Reading or writing always happens at the page boundary, even if you only need one row. Heap files: The simplest physical layout — rows are written to pages in insertion order, with no guarantee about their sequence. B-tree indexes: A sorted tree structure built alongside a heap. Lookups by an indexed column skip most pages and jump straight to the right leaf. This is physical machinery; it is invisible to a SELECT statement. Write-ahead log (WAL): Before changing a page, the engine records the change in a sequential log file. This is how crash recovery works — again, entirely hidden from SQL. Note: SQLite, which powers the interactive examples in this guide, stores an entire database in a single file divided into 4 KB pages. Every table and index lives somewhere in that file. You never see pages when you write SQL, but they explain why an INSERT can be slow on a nearly full page. Seeing the Separation in Action The clearest demonstration is an index . An index is a purely physical artifact — it does not change what rows the database contains, only how fast it can find them. From a logical standpoint, the table before and after CREATE INDEX is identical. From a physical standpoint, a new B-tree structure now exists on disk. Try the widget below. The setup creates an orders table and seeds it with data. The query uses a WHERE clause that would benefit from an index. Interactive SQL · Logical query, physical index Try dropping the index ( DROP INDEX idx_customer ) and re-running the SELECT . The result is identical — the logical answer does not change. Only the physical path the engine takes is different. You can also run EXPLAIN SELECT id, amount, status FROM orders WHERE customer_id = 42; to see SQLite describe the physical access strategy it chose. Why the Distinction Matters Keeping logical and physical concerns separate pays off in three concrete ways: Portability. The same SQL works on SQLite, PostgreSQL, and MySQL even though each engine uses a very different physical layout under the hood. Your application code does not change when you switch engines. Tuning without breakage. A DBA can add an index, change a storage engine, or move a table to faster disks without touching a single line of application SQL. The logical interface stays stable. Reasoning clearly about bugs. When a query returns the wrong rows, the problem is almost always logical (wrong join, wrong filter). When a query returns correct rows but runs slowly, the problem is almost always physical (missing index, table scan on millions of rows). Knowing which layer to inspect saves hours of debugging. The rest of Part I focuses on the logical level — schemas, relationships, and queries. Later chapters on indexing and storage engines will pull back the curtain on the physical side. For now, the key takeaway is simple: your SQL describes what you want, and the database engine decides how to get it. ← Previous A first mental model Next → Tables and relations

title: Tables and relations
text: A table is a relation: a set of tuples sharing the same heading. No duplicate rows, no implicit order — pure set semantics. At the heart of a relational database is a deceptively simple idea: store everything in tables . Each table represents one kind of thing — customers, orders, products — and the power comes from linking those tables together. This chapter unpacks that structure and explains why it matters for every query you will ever write. Anatomy of a Table A table is a grid of rows and columns — you have already seen the terms. The important details are the rules that govern it: Term What it means Column (field) One attribute of the entity — its name, data type, and constraints are fixed for every row Row (record / tuple) One instance of the entity — the actual data Cell The intersection of a column and a row; holds exactly one value Primary key A column (or combination) whose value is unique per row and never NULL — it is the row's identity A column's data type is a hard contract. If a column is declared INTEGER , the database rejects text values. This strictness is a feature: it catches bad data at write time rather than at 3 a.m. when a report breaks. CREATE TABLE products ( product_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, price_cents INTEGER NOT NULL CHECK (price_cents >= 0), in_stock INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 1 -- 1 = true, 0 = false ); Notice NOT NULL , CHECK , and DEFAULT — these are constraints , rules the engine enforces on every insert and update so you never have to police them in application code. Relations: Linking Tables Together "Relational" does not mean rows are related to each other inside a table. It means tables are related to each other through shared values — specifically through foreign keys . A foreign key in one table points to the primary key of another table. The database engine enforces that the value you write actually exists in the referenced table (this is called referential integrity ). CREATE TABLE orders ( order_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, customer TEXT NOT NULL, product_id INTEGER NOT NULL REFERENCES products(product_id), quantity INTEGER NOT NULL CHECK (quantity > 0) ); Here orders.product_id must match a value in products.product_id . You cannot create an order for a product that does not exist; you cannot delete a product that has orders. The engine says no. The three common relationship types One-to-many — one product appears in many order rows. This is the most common relationship and is implemented with a foreign key on the "many" side. Many-to-many — a student enrolls in many courses, a course has many students. Needs a junction table ( enrollments ) with two foreign keys. One-to-one — rarer; used to split a wide table or store optional detail (e.g., a user_profiles table that extends users ). Querying Across Tables with JOIN Once data lives in separate tables you retrieve it together using a JOIN . A JOIN matches rows from two tables on a shared column — typically a foreign key to primary key pairing. Try this widget. It creates a small products and orders table, seeds them, then runs a JOIN. Edit the query to filter by product or calculate a total — the database is all yours. Interactive SQL · Tables & Relations Note: A JOIN without a WHERE or ON condition would pair every row in one table with every row in the other — a Cartesian product — producing nonsense results. Always specify the matching column. Why This Design Matters Splitting data into related tables rather than one giant flat file is the core of normalization — eliminating redundancy. In the example above, a product's name and price live in exactly one row. If you need to update the price, you change one cell; every order that references it automatically reflects the new value. In a flat file you would hunt down every duplicate and hope you caught them all. Tables and relations are the vocabulary of relational databases. Everything else — indexes, transactions, query plans — is built on top of this foundation. ← Previous Logical structure vs physical structure Next → Keys and identifiers

title: Keys and identifiers
text: Keys uniquely identify rows. Natural keys come from the domain; surrogate keys are synthetic and immutable. Choose surrogates for stability. Every table needs a way to tell rows apart. Without that, you cannot reliably update a single record, link two tables together, or enforce that the same thing is not stored twice. Keys solve this — they are the identifiers that give every row a unique, stable address. Primary Keys: One Row, One Identity A primary key is a column (or combination of columns) whose value uniquely identifies each row in a table. The database enforces two rules automatically: Uniqueness — no two rows may share the same primary key value. Not null — a primary key must always have a value; it cannot be left blank. CREATE TABLE customers ( customer_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, email TEXT NOT NULL ); Here customer_id is the primary key. If you try to insert two rows with customer_id = 7 , the database rejects the second one immediately — no application-level check needed. Natural vs. Surrogate Keys There are two schools of thought on what to use as a primary key: Type What it is Example Trade-off Natural key A real-world value that is already unique Email address, ISBN, passport number Meaningful, but can change or be longer than needed Surrogate key A system-generated value with no business meaning Auto-incrementing integer, UUID Stable and compact, but opaque Most production tables use surrogate keys — typically an auto-incrementing integer ( SERIAL PRIMARY KEY in SQLite auto-increments) or a UUID — because natural identifiers change more than people expect. An email address seems permanent until a customer asks to change it. Note: In SQLite, declaring a column as SERIAL PRIMARY KEY automatically makes it an alias for the internal rowid , giving you auto-increment behavior with no extra syntax. Foreign Keys: Linking Tables Together A foreign key is a column whose value must match an existing primary key in another table. It is how relationships are expressed in a relational database. CREATE TABLE orders ( order_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, customer_id INTEGER NOT NULL, placed_at TEXT NOT NULL, FOREIGN KEY (customer_id) REFERENCES customers(customer_id) ); The FOREIGN KEY constraint tells the database: "every customer_id in orders must exist in customers ." Trying to insert an order for a customer that does not exist fails. Trying to delete a customer who still has orders also fails — unless you have configured cascading rules. This constraint is what prevents orphan records — orders pointing at customers who no longer exist. Composite Keys and Unique Constraints Sometimes no single column uniquely identifies a row, but a combination of columns does. A composite primary key uses two or more columns together. CREATE TABLE course_enrollments ( student_id INTEGER NOT NULL, course_id INTEGER NOT NULL, enrolled_at TEXT NOT NULL, PRIMARY KEY (student_id, course_id) ); A student can enroll in many courses, and a course can have many students — but the pair (student_id, course_id) must be unique. Attempting to enroll the same student in the same course twice is rejected. A UNIQUE constraint is a softer version: it enforces uniqueness without making the column the primary key. This is useful for things like email addresses that should not repeat but are not the main identifier. CREATE TABLE users ( user_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, email TEXT NOT NULL UNIQUE ); Try It Live The widget below sets up a small customers and orders schema and seeds a few rows. Run the query to see how a join uses the primary/foreign key relationship to connect the two tables. Then try inserting an order with a customer_id that does not exist and observe what happens. Interactive SQL · Keys in action To test the foreign key constraint, try adding this statement after the SELECT : INSERT INTO orders VALUES (999, 42, 100.00); Customer 42 does not exist, so SQLite (with PRAGMA foreign_keys = ON ) will reject the insert. Why Keys Matter Beyond Correctness Keys do more than prevent bad data. They are the foundation of two other critical database features: Indexes — the database automatically creates an index on the primary key, making lookups by that column fast even across millions of rows. Foreign key columns are good candidates for manual indexes too. Joins — every JOIN in SQL works by matching values across tables. Without a reliable primary/foreign key relationship, joins produce ambiguous or incorrect results. A well-chosen key strategy is one of the earliest and most consequential design decisions you make when building a schema. Get it right early — changing primary keys in a live system is painful. ← Previous Tables and relations Next → Constraints and rules

title: Constraints and rules
text: Four constraint types: NOT NULL (required), CHECK (custom rules), FOREIGN KEY (referential integrity), and DEFAULT (fallback values). All enforced by the engine. A database without rules is just a pile of data waiting to become a mess. Constraints are the rules you bake into the schema itself — enforced automatically by the database engine every time data is inserted, updated, or deleted. Instead of writing defensive code in every application that touches your data, you define the rules once and let the engine guard them forever. The Core Constraints Every major relational database supports a standard set of constraints. Here are the ones you will encounter constantly. Constraint What it enforces NOT NULL The column must always have a value — NULL is rejected UNIQUE No two rows may share the same value in this column (or column group) PRIMARY KEY NOT NULL + UNIQUE combined; identifies each row unambiguously FOREIGN KEY A value in this column must exist in a referenced column of another table CHECK A boolean expression that every row must satisfy DEFAULT Supplies a value automatically when none is provided Primary and foreign keys working together A primary key is the anchor of a table — it uniquely names every row. A foreign key in another table points back to that anchor, creating a referential link between the two. The database will refuse to insert a foreign key value that has no matching primary key, and (depending on configuration) will refuse to delete a primary key row while child rows still reference it. CREATE TABLE departments ( id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL UNIQUE ); CREATE TABLE employees ( id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, department_id INTEGER NOT NULL REFERENCES departments(id) -- foreign key ); If you try INSERT INTO employees VALUES (1, 'Alice', 99) when no department with id = 99 exists, the database rejects it immediately — the constraint fires before the row is written. CHECK constraints CHECK lets you express any rule that can be written as a SQL expression. Common uses: enforcing a range, restricting a column to a known set of values, or requiring that one column's value relates sensibly to another's. CREATE TABLE products ( id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, price REAL NOT NULL CHECK (price >= 0), status TEXT NOT NULL CHECK (status IN ('active', 'discontinued', 'draft')) ); Note: PostgreSQL enforces CHECK constraints on INSERT and UPDATE , and enforces foreign keys by default — no pragma needed. Seeing Constraints Fail in Real Time The best way to understand constraints is to violate them and watch the engine push back. The widget below creates a small orders table with several constraints in place. Try running each of the commented statements to see exactly which constraint fires. Interactive SQL · Constraints in action Uncomment one statement at a time, re-run, and read the error message the engine returns. Notice that the engine tells you which constraint was violated, not just that something went wrong. Why Define Rules in the Schema? You might wonder: why not just validate data in the application layer? The short answer is that applications come and go — the database often outlives several generations of software. Rules in the schema are enforced by every client unconditionally: the web app, the admin script, the data-import tool, and the junior developer poking around in a SQL shell. Constraints also let the engine make smarter decisions. A PRIMARY KEY tells the engine it can build an efficient index for lookups. A NOT NULL column can be stored more compactly. Rules are not just about correctness — they are information the engine uses to optimize. Key idea: Constraints shift data-quality guarantees from "every application must remember to check" to "the database makes it impossible to forget." The next topic explores how the schema itself is defined and changed over time — a process called schema migration. ← Previous Keys and identifiers Next → NULL and missing values

title: NULL and missing values
text: NULL means "unknown" — not zero, not empty string. Most operations with NULL return NULL. Use IS NULL/IS NOT NULL for comparisons, COALESCE for defaults. Every table you design will eventually face a question no data type can answer on its own: what do you store when the value is unknown or does not apply? The answer in SQL is NULL — and it behaves in ways that surprise almost every newcomer. Understanding NULL now will save you from subtle bugs later. What NULL Is (and Is Not) NULL is not zero, not an empty string, and not false . It is the explicit absence of a value — a marker that says "this cell has no known value." Value Means 0 The number zero — a real, known value '' An empty string — still a real, known value NULL Unknown, missing, or not applicable Consider a users table where phone is optional. A user who has not provided a phone number should have NULL there, not an empty string. That distinction matters when you later query "give me all users who have a phone number" — an empty string would silently slip through. CREATE TABLE users ( user_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, email TEXT NOT NULL, phone TEXT -- NULL = not provided ); The column phone has no NOT NULL constraint, so the database will accept rows that omit it. The Three-Valued Logic of NULL Here is the trap. In ordinary logic, a statement is either true or false. In SQL, comparisons involving NULL produce a third result: UNKNOWN . A row only makes it into your result set if its WHERE condition evaluates to TRUE — not UNKNOWN. This has one critical consequence: you cannot compare NULL with = . -- This returns NO rows, even if phone IS NULL in the table SELECT * FROM users WHERE phone = NULL; -- This is correct — always use IS NULL or IS NOT NULL SELECT * FROM users WHERE phone IS NULL; The same rule applies to inequality: phone != NULL also returns UNKNOWN for every row. SQL gives you dedicated operators exactly for this: Operator Use it to IS NULL Find rows where the value is absent IS NOT NULL Find rows where a value is present NULL in aggregate functions Most aggregate functions silently skip NULLs: SELECT AVG(score) FROM results; -- AVG ignores NULL rows; it averages only the non-NULL scores COUNT(*) counts all rows. COUNT(column) counts only non-NULL values in that column. Knowing which one you want matters. Experimenting with NULL The widget below sets up a small orders table where shipped_at is optional — orders that have not shipped yet have NULL there. Try the starter query, then edit it to find only shipped orders, or calculate how many are still pending. Interactive SQL · NULL and missing values Note: Try replacing COUNT(shipped_at) with COUNT(*) and notice the difference. Then add a WHERE shipped_at IS NULL clause to list only the pending orders by name. Designing Around NULL NULL is useful, but overusing it creates headaches. A few guidelines: Use NOT NULL as the default. Add it to every column unless you have a specific reason for the value to be absent. Fewer NULLs means simpler queries. Distinguish "unknown" from "not applicable." If a column simply does not apply to certain rows, consider whether those rows belong in a separate table instead. Be careful with COALESCE . The COALESCE(a, b) function returns the first non-NULL argument. It is handy for providing fallback values in queries, but using it to paper over a design problem can hide bugs. -- Return a display label, falling back to 'No phone' if NULL SELECT name, COALESCE(phone, 'No phone') AS phone_display FROM users; NULL is one of the most frequently misunderstood corners of SQL. The key insight to carry forward: NULL is not a value — it is the absence of one, and the database treats it that way at every step from comparison to aggregation. ← Previous Constraints and rules Next → Data types and domains

title: Data types and domains
text: Types define what values are valid in a column. A domain is a type plus constraints — a reusable, named type definition with built-in validation. Every column in a table carries a data type — a contract with the engine about what values it will hold. Get that contract right and the engine becomes a tireless validator, rejecting bad data at write time before it can corrupt your records. This page covers the major type families, how domains narrow them further, and why choosing well matters more than it might seem. The Major Families of Data Types All major database systems share a handful of fundamental type families, even if the exact keyword varies by vendor: Family Common SQL names What it stores Integer INTEGER , BIGINT , SMALLINT Whole numbers (no decimal point) Decimal / Numeric NUMERIC(p,s) , DECIMAL , REAL , FLOAT Numbers with fractional parts Text TEXT , VARCHAR(n) , CHAR(n) Strings of characters Boolean BOOLEAN True / false (SQLite stores as 1 / 0) Date & Time DATE , TIME , TIMESTAMP Calendar dates, clock times, or both Binary BLOB , BYTEA Raw bytes — images, files, encrypted data Choosing the right type is not pedantry. Storing a price in TEXT means you cannot do arithmetic on it; the database cannot stop someone from writing "free" into the column. Storing a phone number in INTEGER silently strips leading zeroes. Types are guardrails. Integer vs. Decimal — a common trap REAL and FLOAT are binary floating-point numbers. They are fast but approximate — the decimal 0.1 cannot be represented exactly in binary, so arithmetic can produce surprising rounding errors. For money, always use NUMERIC (or store cents as an INTEGER ). -- Floating-point rounding in action (SQLite REAL) SELECT 0.1 + 0.2; -- returns 0.300000000000000 ... sometimes SELECT 0.1 + 0.2 = 0.3; -- may return 0 (false!) due to rounding -- Safe money arithmetic: store cents as INTEGER SELECT 10 + 20; -- 30, exact SELECT 10 + 20 = 30; -- 1 (true), always Note: SQLite's type system is more flexible ("type affinity") than strict SQL, but the principle holds in PostgreSQL, MySQL, and every other engine. Use NUMERIC or integer-cents for financial data. Domains: Narrowing the Type Further A domain is a named, reusable type with extra constraints on top of a base type — "an integer, but only 1–100" or "text, but only from this list". SQL defines domains with CREATE DOMAIN (PostgreSQL and the standard). SQLite omits the keyword, but you get the same effect with CHECK constraints: -- PostgreSQL / standard SQL syntax (for reference) -- CREATE DOMAIN positive_price AS NUMERIC(10,2) CHECK (VALUE > 0); -- SQLite equivalent: inline CHECK on the column CREATE TABLE products ( product_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, price_cents INTEGER NOT NULL CHECK (price_cents > 0), rating INTEGER CHECK (rating BETWEEN 1 AND 5) ); The column rating is still an INTEGER at the storage level, but the database will reject any value outside 1–5. That is a domain in practice, even without the formal keyword. Note: Domains are most valuable when the same constraint would appear on dozens of columns across many tables. Centralizing it in a domain means you fix a rule in one place rather than hunting through every table definition. Seeing Types in Action The widget below creates an employees table with mixed types. Run the default query, then try changing a value — for example, swap '2022-03-15' for 'not-a-date' — to see how permissive or strict SQLite is compared to engines like PostgreSQL. Interactive SQL · Data Types Now try adding a row with a violated constraint — change the CHECK and see what the engine says: Interactive SQL · Constraint Rejection Why Getting Types Right Matters Early Changing a column's data type after a table has millions of rows is painful — most engines must rewrite the entire table. A few minutes of thought at design time pays back quickly. The rule of thumb: Store numbers you compute with as numeric types, not text. Store money as integer cents or NUMERIC , never FLOAT . Store dates as DATE / TIMESTAMP , not VARCHAR . Use CHECK constraints to encode domain rules in the database, not just in application code. Types and domains are the first line of data quality — the safety net that catches what application code misses. ← Previous NULL and missing values Next → Normalized and denormalized data

title: Normalized and denormalized data
text: Normalized design splits facts apart to eliminate redundancy. Denormalization deliberately duplicates for read performance — a deliberate tradeoff, not a mistake. When you store the same fact in multiple places, you invite trouble: update it in one row and forget the others, and your database starts disagreeing with itself. Normalization is the practice of organizing data to eliminate that redundancy. Denormalization is the deliberate decision to reintroduce some redundancy for the sake of read performance. Both are tools — understanding when to use each is one of the most practical skills in database design. The Problem Normalization Solves Imagine a single table that tracks every order a customer has ever placed: order_id customer_name customer_email product price 1 Alice Wong alice@example.com Headphones 79.99 2 Alice Wong alice@example.com USB Cable 9.99 3 Bob Patel bob@example.com Keyboard 49.99 Alice's email appears twice. If she changes it, you must update every row — miss one, and your data is inconsistent. This is called an update anomaly . There are also insertion anomalies (you can't record a customer until they place an order) and deletion anomalies (deleting the last order erases the customer record entirely). Normalization fixes these by splitting the table so each fact lives in exactly one place. Normal Forms: The Key Ideas Normalization is defined in levels called normal forms (1NF, 2NF, 3NF, and beyond). In practice, reaching Third Normal Form (3NF) is the standard target for transactional databases. Normal Form Rule (simplified) 1NF Every cell holds one atomic value; no repeating groups 2NF 1NF + every non-key column depends on the whole primary key 3NF 2NF + no non-key column depends on another non-key column For the orders example, a 3NF design separates customers from orders: -- Customers: each email stored once CREATE TABLE customers ( id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, email TEXT NOT NULL UNIQUE ); -- Orders: reference the customer by id CREATE TABLE orders ( id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, customer_id INTEGER REFERENCES customers(id), product TEXT NOT NULL, price REAL NOT NULL ); Now Alice's email exists in exactly one row. Change it once, and every order automatically reflects the update. Try the widget below. Run the default query, then modify Alice's email and see that both her orders pick up the change through the join. Interactive SQL · Normalized design When Denormalization Makes Sense Normalization is great for writes, but reads can suffer. Every time you need the customer's name alongside an order, you must perform a JOIN — and joins across large tables are expensive. Denormalization trades storage and write complexity for faster reads. A reporting table might intentionally store customer_name directly on the order row so a dashboard query never needs a join: -- Denormalized orders table (for read-heavy analytics) CREATE TABLE orders_flat ( order_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, customer_name TEXT, customer_email TEXT, product TEXT, price REAL ); This pattern is common in data warehouses and analytical (OLAP) workloads , where data is loaded in bulk and queried heavily but updated rarely. The widget below lets you compare query complexity. Notice how the flat table needs no join: Interactive SQL · Denormalized flat table Note: In a denormalized table, updating Alice's email requires touching every order row she has. That's acceptable in a warehouse loaded nightly from a source-of-truth system — but would be a maintenance nightmare in a live transactional database. Choosing the Right Approach Normalized Denormalized Best for Transactional apps (OLTP) Analytics, reporting (OLAP) Update cost Low — change once High — change many rows Read cost Higher — requires joins Low — single table scan Data integrity Strong Relies on ETL discipline Most real systems use both: a normalized operational database feeding a denormalized data warehouse or read replica. The key insight is that normalization is not an absolute law — it is a default that you deviate from consciously, with a clear reason. ← Previous Data types and domains Next → Documents, graphs, and key-value shapes

title: Documents, graphs, and key-value shapes
text: Four data model families: relational (tables + joins), key-value (simple lookups), document (self-contained JSON), graph (nodes + edges). Each optimized for different access patterns. Not every problem fits neatly into rows and columns. Over the decades, engineers hit cases where the relational table model felt like forcing a square peg into a round hole — deeply nested objects, rapidly changing fields, or data that is fundamentally a network of connections. Three alternative shapes emerged to handle these cases: key-value , document , and graph . Understanding their structure helps you recognise when each is the right tool. Key-Value: The Simplest Shape A key-value store is exactly what the name suggests — a giant dictionary. Every piece of data has a unique key (a string or binary blob) and an opaque value attached to it. The database does not care what the value contains; it stores and retrieves it without inspecting its structure. Concept Analogy Example key Example value Key Dictionary word session:user:4291 {"token":"abc","expires":1719000000} Value Dictionary definition product:sku:NB-001 {"name":"Notebook","price":299} Namespace / prefix Book chapter cache:homepage <rendered HTML blob> When it shines: session storage, caching, rate-limit counters, feature flags — anywhere you look things up by a single exact key and speed matters most. Redis is the canonical example. What it cannot do easily: queries that search inside values (e.g., "find all products under $5") require scanning every key, because the database treats values as black boxes. Document: Structure Without a Fixed Schema A document store lets each record — called a document — carry its own nested structure, typically as JSON (or BSON, a binary form). Unlike a relational table, two documents in the same collection do not have to share the same fields. // Document 1 — a simple product { "id": "NB-001", "name": "Notebook", "price": 2.99, "tags": ["stationery", "paper"] } // Document 2 — a product with richer specs { "id": "LP-042", "name": "Laptop", "price": 999.00, "specs": { "ram_gb": 16, "storage_gb": 512, "os": "Linux" }, "tags": ["electronics", "computers"] } Both documents live in the same products collection yet have different fields. This schema flexibility is the primary selling point — teams can evolve data shapes without running ALTER TABLE migrations. MongoDB and CouchDB are well-known examples. Note: Schema flexibility is a trade-off, not a free lunch. Without discipline, documents in the same collection drift into inconsistent shapes, making queries and application code harder to reason about. Many teams add application-level validation or optional schema definitions to stay sane. When documents beat tables Documents are a natural fit when: A record has deeply nested, variable-length sub-structures (e.g., an order with line items, each with options). Different record types share a collection but have mostly different fields. You always retrieve the full object at once and rarely need to join across entities. They are a poor fit when you need ad-hoc queries across many fields, strong relationships between entities, or multi-document transactions with strong consistency. Graph: Relationships as First-Class Citizens In a relational database, relationships live in foreign keys — they are implicit. A graph database makes relationships explicit and stores them as first-class objects alongside the data. The model has two primitives: Node — an entity (a person, a city, a product) Edge — a directed, labelled relationship between two nodes ( :FOLLOWS , :PURCHASED , :LOCATED_IN ) Both nodes and edges can carry properties (key-value pairs). (Alice)-[:FOLLOWS]->(Bob) (Alice)-[:PURCHASED {on: '2024-03-01'}]->(Notebook) (Bob)-[:LIVES_IN]->(Helsinki) This makes traversals — "find all people Alice follows who have also purchased a Notebook" — efficient and natural. In a relational database the same query requires multiple joins that grow expensive as relationship depth increases. Neo4j is the leading graph database; Amazon Neptune is a managed option. Classic use cases: social networks, recommendation engines, fraud detection, knowledge graphs, and any domain where the path between entities matters as much as the entities themselves. Choosing the Right Shape No model is universally superior. The question is always: what does your data look like, and what queries will you run? Model Core strength Reach for it when… Key-value Blazing-fast single-key lookup Caching, sessions, counters Document Flexible nested structures Variable-schema records, document-centric apps Graph Deep relationship traversal Networks, recommendations, lineage queries Relational Rich queries across structured data Most business data with clear entities and joins Many production systems combine models — a relational database for transactional records, a key-value store for caching, and a graph database for recommendations — each doing what it does best. Reveal: Why can't a relational database just use JSON columns to get document-store benefits? Modern relational databases (PostgreSQL, SQLite, MySQL) do support JSON column types, and for many use cases that is enough — you get flexible nesting while keeping the rest of your data relational. The difference is indexing and query ergonomics. A dedicated document store indexes into nested fields natively and exposes a query language built around document shapes. A relational JSON column can be indexed and queried, but it often feels bolted-on. If JSON flexibility is a minority concern in your schema, a relational JSON column is a pragmatic choice. If most of your data is deeply nested and schema-free, a document store is worth considering. Below, explore how PostgreSQL handles a simple JSON-like scenario using its built-in -> / ->> operators — a small taste of how relational databases reach toward document-store territory. Interactive SQL · JSON in SQLite ← Previous Normalized and denormalized data Next → Choosing the right model

title: Choosing the right model
text: The four major data models each trade off query power, flexibility, and speed. Start with relational for most applications; reach for the others only when a specific access pattern demands it. By now you know what tables, rows, columns, keys, constraints, and NULL are. But the relational model — tables with fixed schemas and foreign-key relationships — is not the only way to organize data. Several other models exist, each making different trade-offs. Picking the wrong one early can cost weeks of painful migration later. This page gives you a clear map for making that call. The Major Data Models at a Glance Every database organizes data around a central metaphor. Here are the four you will encounter most often: Model Central metaphor Typical system Relational Tables of typed rows connected by keys PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQLite Document Self-contained JSON/BSON objects in collections MongoDB, Firestore Key-value A giant dictionary: look up a value by its key Redis, DynamoDB (simple mode) Graph Nodes (things) and edges (relationships between things) Neo4j, Amazon Neptune A fifth model — wide-column (Cassandra, HBase) — is used in very high-throughput analytics, but it is specialized enough that you will know when you need it. When Each Model Wins Relational — your default choice If you are not sure which model to use, start here. The relational model is 50 years old and battle-tested. It excels when: Your data has a predictable, consistent structure (every user has an email, every order has a total). You need cross-table queries — finding all unpaid invoices for customers in a specific region, for example. Data integrity matters — foreign keys, unique constraints, and transactions prevent your data from ending up in an inconsistent state. The cost is rigidity: adding a new column to a table with 100 million rows can be slow, and storing truly irregular data (where different records have different fields) feels awkward. Document — when your data is naturally nested A document store treats each record as a self-contained blob of JSON. This fits well when: Records vary significantly from one to the next (a product catalog where a TV has 20 specs and a book has 5 different ones). You almost always read or write a record as a whole unit — you load the full user profile, not half of it. Your team is iterating fast and the schema is still changing. The trade-off: joining across documents is painful and often done in application code instead of the database. Consistency guarantees are typically weaker than in relational systems. Key-value — for speed above all else A key-value store is deliberately simple: store a value, retrieve it by key, delete it. Nothing more. This model wins when: You need sub-millisecond reads (session tokens, user preferences, feature flags). Your access pattern is always "get by ID" — no filtering, no aggregation, no joins. The values can be opaque blobs the database does not need to interpret. If you ever find yourself embedding query logic into your keys (e.g., user:42:orders:recent ), that is a sign you have outgrown the model. Graph — when relationships are the data A graph database stores nodes and the edges between them as first-class objects. It shines when: You need to traverse chains of relationships — friends of friends, shortest path between two people, hierarchies of arbitrary depth.
