Relationship attributes (when did Alice follow Bob? with what weight?) matter as much as the node attributes. Fraud detection, social networks, and knowledge graphs are classic fits. For most business applications, a relational database with a self-join handles "graph-like" queries well enough. A Practical Decision Checklist Work through these questions in order: Is the structure predictable and consistent? → Start with relational. Are you joining across many entities, or enforcing referential integrity? → Relational stays the right call. Are records deeply nested and variable, read as whole units? → Consider document. Is the primary access pattern a single key lookup that must be fast? → Key-value. Is the query "how are these things connected, and by how many hops?" → Graph. Note: Many modern applications use more than one model. A typical stack might use PostgreSQL for core business data, Redis for caching session tokens, and a graph store for a recommendations feature. Each tool does what it is best at. Seeing the Trade-off in SQL The relational model's biggest strength is expressive querying across related tables. Try this example — it is the kind of question that is trivial in SQL and painful in a document or key-value store. Interactive SQL · Cross-table query (relational strength) Try changing the WHERE clause to filter by country ( AND c.country = 'US' ), or replace the join with an aggregation ( GROUP BY c.country ) to see total unpaid debt per country. A key-value or document store would require you to load records individually and do this logic in your application code. The right data model does not just affect query complexity — it affects data integrity, scalability, and how fast your team can move. Relational is the right default for most applications. Reach for a different model only when you can name the specific trade-off it solves for you. ← Previous Documents, graphs, and key-value shapes Next → Chapter 2 Summary: What We Learned

title: Chapter 2 Summary: What We Learned
text: Page Topic Takeaway in One Sentence 011 Logical structure vs physical structure Logical structure is what you see (tables); physical structure is how data is stored (pages, indexes) — they are decoupled. 012 Tables and relations A table is a relation — a set of tuples with named attributes. Thinking in sets, not loops, is the key shift. 013 Keys and identifiers Keys (primary, unique, foreign) identify rows and link tables together — they are the backbone of relational integrity. 014 Constraints and rules Constraints (NOT NULL, CHECK, UNIQUE, FOREIGN KEY) move data validation into the database where it cannot be bypassed. 015 NULL and missing values NULL is not zero or empty string — it means 'unknown' and requires special handling with three-valued logic (IS NULL, COALESCE). 016 Data types and domains Choosing the right type (INT, TEXT, TIMESTAMP, etc.) affects storage size, query performance, and data correctness. 017 Normalized and denormalized data Normalization minimizes redundancy; denormalization trades some redundancy for faster reads. Both have their place. 018 Documents, graphs, and key-value shapes Not all data fits tables: document (JSON), graph (nodes/edges), and key-value models each solve different problems. 019 Choosing the right model Start with relational as the default; reach for document/graph/key-value only when a specific access pattern demands it. Reveal: How do these ideas connect to the next chapter? Chapter 2 showed you how data is structured in tables with keys, constraints, and types. You also glimpsed that not all data fits neatly into rows and columns — document, graph, and key-value models each have their place. Chapter 3 goes deeper into the relational model specifically: the mathematical foundation of relations, tuples, and attributes that makes SQL so powerful. Understanding what a relation truly is — a set, not a spreadsheet — will forever change how you think about database design. ← Previous Choosing the right model Next → Relations, tuples, and attributes

title: Relations, tuples, and attributes
text: A relation has a heading (the set of attributes) and a body (the set of tuples). Cardinality = number of tuples; degree = number of attributes. The relational model gives databases a precise mathematical foundation. Instead of talking loosely about "tables" and "rows," it defines exactly what structure data must have and what operations are valid on it. Those definitions all start with three words: relation , tuple , and attribute . What Is a Relation? A relation is the formal name for what most people call a table. The word comes from set theory — a relation is a set of tuples , where every tuple shares the same structure. Two properties follow directly from that definition: No duplicate tuples. Because a relation is a set, every row must be unique. No ordering. Sets have no inherent order, so rows have no "first" or "last" position. Any ordering you see in a query result is imposed by the query itself, not stored in the relation. These aren't just theoretical niceties. They're the reason the relational model can guarantee consistent, predictable results: you're always reasoning about a set , not a list. Tuples and Attributes Each row in a relation is called a tuple . A tuple is a fixed-length, ordered collection of values — one value per attribute . An attribute is a named column with a declared domain (the set of allowed values). The domain can be as broad as "any text string" or as narrow as "an integer between 1 and 100." Every value in a tuple must belong to its attribute's domain. Put together: Term Everyday word What it means precisely Relation Table A set of tuples with a common structure Tuple Row One element of a relation; a fixed set of named values Attribute Column A named component of a tuple with a declared domain Domain Data type + constraints The complete set of valid values for an attribute A Concrete Example Consider a relation called employee : employee_id name department salary 1 Ada Engineering 95000 2 Brian Marketing 72000 3 Carmen Engineering 105000 This relation has 4 attributes ( employee_id , name , department , salary ) and 3 tuples . The domain of salary might be "positive integers"; the domain of department might be restricted to a known list of department names. Any attempt to insert a tuple with a value outside those domains is rejected. Note: In formal relational theory, an attribute's position inside a tuple does not matter — what matters is the attribute name . This means you should never rely on column order in production SQL; always name your columns explicitly. Exploring Relations with SQL SQL is the language that lets you work with relations. CREATE TABLE defines the relation's name, attributes, and domains; INSERT adds tuples; SELECT retrieves them. Try running the query below. It retrieves all tuples from a small employee relation. Then try modifying the WHERE clause to filter by department or salary. Interactive SQL · Relations & Tuples Notice that the result is itself a relation — a new set of tuples derived from the original. This is a core idea: relational operations always take relations as input and produce relations as output, so you can chain them together. Relation Schema vs. Relation Instance One last distinction worth locking in: the schema of a relation is its structure — the name, the list of attributes, and their domains. The instance (or extension ) is the actual set of tuples in the relation at a specific moment in time. Schema (stable): employee(employee_id: INT, name: TEXT, department: TEXT, salary: INT) Instance (changes with inserts/updates/deletes): the four rows above The schema rarely changes; the instance changes every time data is modified. When database engineers talk about "designing a schema," they're deciding which relations to define and what attributes each one should have — the structure that will hold the data, independent of any particular data values. Reveal: Can two tuples in a relation have the same values for every attribute? No. A relation is formally a set , and sets do not allow duplicate elements. If two proposed tuples are identical across every attribute, they are the same tuple and only one is stored. In practice, SQL databases enforce this through a primary key — a constraint that guarantees at least one attribute (or combination of attributes) is unique per tuple. Without a primary key, some SQL engines will allow duplicate rows, which means the table is technically not a true relation in the formal sense. ← Previous Choosing the right model Next → Primary keys and foreign keys

title: Primary keys and foreign keys
text: Primary keys uniquely identify each row. Foreign keys link one table to another, enforcing referential integrity — every FK value must exist as a PK in the referenced table. Every table in a relational database needs a way to tell its rows apart, and every relationship between tables needs a way to express "this row over here belongs with that row over there." Primary keys and foreign keys are the two mechanisms that make both of those things possible — and together they form the backbone of the relational model. Primary Keys: The Identity of a Row A primary key is a column (or combination of columns) whose value uniquely identifies each row in a table. Three rules apply: Unique — no two rows may share the same primary key value. Not null — every row must have one; a missing identity makes no sense. Stable — the value should not change after the row is created (though the database doesn't always enforce this automatically). Consider a customers table: customer_id name email 1 Alice Mwangi alice@example.com 2 Ben Okafor ben@example.com 3 Clara Souza clara@example.com customer_id is the primary key. Even if two customers share a name, their IDs keep them distinct. Surrogate vs. Natural Keys You have two broad choices for what to use as a primary key: Surrogate key — a value invented purely for identification, usually an auto-incrementing integer ( 1, 2, 3, … ) or a UUID. It carries no real-world meaning. Natural key — a value that already exists in the domain, such as a national ID number, an ISBN, or an email address. Surrogate keys are the more common default because they stay stable even when the real-world data changes (a customer can update their email without breaking anything). Natural keys can be useful when the identifier is truly permanent and unique, like an ISBN for a book. Foreign Keys: References Between Tables A foreign key is a column in one table whose values must match a primary key value in another table. It expresses a relationship — "this order belongs to that customer." CREATE TABLE orders ( order_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, customer_id INTEGER NOT NULL, placed_at TEXT NOT NULL, FOREIGN KEY (customer_id) REFERENCES customers(customer_id) ); The FOREIGN KEY … REFERENCES clause tells the database engine to enforce referential integrity : you cannot insert an order for customer_id = 99 unless a customer with that ID already exists, and you cannot delete a customer who still has orders (unless you tell the database what to do in that case via ON DELETE CASCADE or similar). Note: SQLite parses foreign key syntax but does not enforce it by default. You must run PRAGMA foreign_keys = ON; at the start of each connection to activate enforcement. Most other databases enforce foreign keys automatically. What Happens Without Foreign Keys? Without a foreign key constraint, the database has no way to catch orphan rows — orders that reference a customer that no longer exists, or never existed. Queries that join the two tables would silently produce wrong results, or no results at all. Foreign keys move this class of bug from application logic into the database itself, where it can be caught at every entry point. Seeing It in Action The widget below sets up a small customers / orders schema and seeds it with sample data. Try the default query to see how a join uses the foreign key relationship, then experiment — for example, try inserting an order with a customer_id that doesn't exist ( 99 ) and observe what happens with PRAGMA foreign_keys = ON . Interactive SQL · Primary & Foreign Keys Composite Primary Keys Sometimes no single column is unique on its own, but a combination of columns is. For example, a table tracking which students are enrolled in which courses might use both columns together as the primary key: CREATE TABLE enrollments ( student_id INTEGER NOT NULL, course_id INTEGER NOT NULL, enrolled_on TEXT NOT NULL, PRIMARY KEY (student_id, course_id) ); This is called a composite primary key . It guarantees that a student can enroll in a given course only once, while still allowing the same student in many courses and many students in the same course. Reveal: Can a foreign key point to a composite primary key? Yes. A foreign key can reference a composite primary key, but it must include all the same columns. For example, a grades table that references enrollments would need both student_id and course_id as its foreign key columns. In practice, many designers prefer to add a surrogate key to such tables to keep foreign key references simple. Primary keys and foreign keys are not just database bookkeeping — they encode the rules of your data model directly in the schema, making those rules enforceable and self-documenting for anyone who reads the table definitions. ← Previous Relations, tuples, and attributes Next → Set-based thinking

title: Set-based thinking
text: Procedural code says "how" to iterate row by row. SQL says "what" you want — the engine chooses the strategy. This is the essence of declarative set-based thinking. Most programmers learn to solve problems one item at a time: loop over a list, check each element, do something to it, move on. Relational databases flip that instinct on its head. SQL is built on set-based thinking — you describe what you want from a whole collection of rows, and the database engine figures out how to get it. Embracing this shift is the single biggest mental upgrade for writing fast, clear SQL. Rows Are Members of a Set In mathematics, a set is an unordered collection of distinct elements. A database table is close to that idea: it is a collection of rows, and you operate on all the matching rows at once rather than one by one. When you write: UPDATE accounts SET status = 'inactive' WHERE last_login < '2024-01-01'; You are not looping through rows in your head — you are declaring a condition that selects a subset, and the engine applies the change to every member of that subset in one logical operation. The database might touch thousands of rows; you wrote one statement. This is the essence of set-based thinking: express the desired state of a set, not a sequence of steps . The Contrast with Row-by-Row (Cursor) Logic To make the difference concrete, imagine you want to give every employee in the engineering department a 10 % raise. Row-by-row approach (procedural pseudocode): for each employee: if employee.department == 'engineering': employee.salary = employee.salary * 1.10 save(employee) Set-based SQL: UPDATE employees SET salary = salary * 1.10 WHERE department = 'engineering'; The SQL version sends one statement to the engine. The engine applies it to the entire matching set — atomically, and using whatever indexes or parallel strategies it has available. The pseudocode sends N round-trips (or cursor fetches) and gives the engine no opportunity to optimise across rows. Note: SQL does have cursors for row-by-row processing, but they are a last resort. Experienced database developers reach for them only when a problem genuinely cannot be expressed as a set operation — which is rare. Thinking in Filters, Projections, and Joins Set-based thinking maps naturally onto three operations you will use constantly: Operation What it does SQL keyword Filter Narrow the set to rows matching a condition WHERE Project Select only certain columns from the set SELECT col1, col2 Join Combine two sets by matching rows JOIN ... ON Each of these produces a new set — a result set — that you can then filter, project, or join further. The whole query is a pipeline of set transformations. Try the widget below. The default query filters, then projects. Experiment: remove the WHERE clause to see the full set, or add a second condition with AND . Interactive SQL · Filter and project Now try the set-based UPDATE from the earlier example — no loop needed: Interactive SQL · Set-based update Why It Matters Beyond Style Set-based queries are not just cleaner — they unlock the engine's optimizer. When you hand the database a full set operation, it can: Choose between a full table scan or an index lookup based on statistics Execute in parallel across partitions Avoid redundant disk reads by caching pages across rows in the same pass A row-by-row cursor forces the engine into a rigid execution path you dictated. A set-based query gives it freedom to find the fastest plan. This is why two queries that return identical results can differ in speed by orders of magnitude. The data is the same; what changes is whether you let the optimizer work as intended. Key insight: SQL's declarative nature — saying what , not how — is not a limitation. It is the feature. Set-based thinking is how you write SQL that stays fast as your data grows. ← Previous Primary keys and foreign keys Next → Projection, selection, and join

title: Projection, selection, and join
text: Three fundamental operations: selection (σ) filters rows, projection (π) picks columns, and join (⋈) combines tables. They compose cleanly — the heart of relational algebra. The relational model is not just a way to store data — it defines a set of operations for querying it. Three of those operations are the backbone of almost every query you will ever write: selection (filter rows), projection (pick columns), and join (combine tables). Understanding what each one does, and why, makes SQL feel inevitable rather than arbitrary. Selection: Filter Rows Selection takes a relation and returns only the tuples that satisfy a given condition. The result has the same columns as the input — you haven't changed the shape, just reduced the number of rows. In SQL, selection is expressed with a WHERE clause: SELECT * FROM employee WHERE department = 'Engineering'; That query returns every column ( * ) but only the rows where department equals 'Engineering' . The predicate can be as simple as a single equality check or as complex as a combination of AND , OR , NOT , range comparisons, and pattern matches. Note: Formal relational algebra uses the Greek letter sigma (σ) for selection: σ_condition(R) means "return all tuples in R that satisfy the condition." SQL's WHERE clause is that operation. Projection: Pick Columns Projection takes a relation and returns only the columns you specify. Every row survives, but each row is narrowed to the chosen attributes. SELECT name, salary FROM employee; This returns all rows, but strips every column except name and salary . In formal notation, projection uses pi (π): π_name,salary(employee). Projection is how you avoid selecting more data than you need — a habit that pays off in performance and clarity. It also matters when you're combining operations: projecting to a smaller set of columns before a join reduces the amount of data the engine has to shuffle around. Note: In pure relational algebra, projecting can reduce the number of rows if two tuples become identical after the unwanted columns are removed (since a relation is a set). SQL does not remove duplicates automatically — you must add DISTINCT if you want that behavior. Join: Combine Tables A join combines two relations by matching tuples from each based on a condition, producing a new relation. It is the operation that makes normalization practical: you can split data across multiple tables and reassemble it on demand. The most common form is the inner join , which returns only the tuples where the join condition is satisfied in both tables: SELECT e.name, d.location FROM employee e INNER JOIN department d ON e.dept_id = d.id; Here employee and department are matched wherever e.dept_id equals d.id . Rows with no match on either side are dropped. Other Join Types Join type What it returns INNER JOIN Only rows with a match in both tables LEFT JOIN All rows from the left table; NULLs where no match on the right RIGHT JOIN All rows from the right table; NULLs where no match on the left FULL OUTER JOIN All rows from both tables; NULLs where no match on either side CROSS JOIN Every combination of rows from both tables (the Cartesian product) The CROSS JOIN is rarely what you want in practice — two 1 000-row tables produce 1 000 000 result rows — but it is the mathematical foundation from which all other joins are derived: an inner join is a cross join with a filter applied. Putting It All Together The real power comes from composing these operations. Selection, projection, and join are each closed over relations: each one takes relations as input and produces a relation as output, so you can chain them freely. Try the widget below. The default query demonstrates all three operations at once: a join (two tables), a selection ( WHERE salary > 70000 ), and a projection (named columns rather than * ). Edit the query to explore — try removing the WHERE clause, changing the join condition, or adding more columns. Interactive SQL · Selection, Projection & Join Now try a LEFT JOIN instead of INNER JOIN — you will not see any difference here because every employee has a matching department. To see the distinction, insert an employee with a dept_id that does not exist in dept (say, dept_id = 99 ) and compare the results of INNER JOIN versus LEFT JOIN . Reveal: What happens if you omit the ON clause from a JOIN? Without an ON clause, most SQL dialects treat it as a CROSS JOIN — every row from the left table is paired with every row from the right table. With 5 employees and 3 departments that produces 15 result rows, most of which are meaningless combinations. This is almost never what you want. Always specify your join condition explicitly to avoid accidental Cartesian products. ← Previous Set-based thinking Next → Why relations are powerful

title: Why relations are powerful
text: Navigational databases needed pre-built pointers. The relational model's closure property — operations take and return relations — means you can answer any question by composing the right operations. The relational model has dominated databases for over fifty years — not by accident, but because it solves a genuinely hard problem: how do you store data in a way that lets you ask questions you didn't anticipate at design time? The answer is a single elegant idea — the relation — and a small set of mathematical operations that compose cleanly on top of it. Data lives in one place, questions can come from anywhere Before the relational model, databases were navigational: to get from one record to another, you followed pre-built pointers hard-coded at design time. If the pointer for your question didn't exist, you were out of luck — or you rebuilt the database. Relations break that dependency. Each table is self-describing: every row has the same columns, every column has a declared type, and rows carry no pointer to other tables. Relationships between tables are expressed at query time using shared values (keys), not at storage time using pointers. This means: You can add new queries without restructuring the data. You can combine tables in ways the original designer never imagined. The engine — not the application — figures out how to navigate to the answer. Note: This property is sometimes called data independence — the logical structure of your data is decoupled from how it is physically stored and navigated. Keys and joins unlock unlimited combinations The mechanism that makes this work is the join . When two tables share a column (a foreign key referencing a primary key), you can combine rows from both tables where those values match. From three simple tables you can produce dozens of different result sets — without duplicating a single byte of data. Try it below. The schema stores orders and customers separately. The query joins them at query time: Interactive SQL · Relations & Joins Notice that each table stores only its own facts. The join assembles a tailored view at query time. Try adding WHERE c.country = 'US' to filter by country, or swapping ORDER BY total_cost DESC for ORDER BY customer . Relations compose — that's the real superpower Individual joins are useful, but the deeper power is composability . Because every query result is itself a table (a relation), you can layer filters, aggregations, and joins on top of other queries. SQL's WITH clause (Common Table Expressions) makes this explicit: WITH us_orders AS ( SELECT o.id, o.qty, o.product_id, c.name AS customer FROM orders o JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id = c.id WHERE c.country = 'US' ) SELECT customer, SUM(qty) AS total_items FROM us_orders GROUP BY customer; us_orders is a temporary relation — the outer query treats it exactly like a real table. You can keep stacking these layers without touching the underlying data at all. This is what mathematicians call closure : relational operations (select, project, join, aggregate) take relations as input and return relations as output. The system never needs a special exit ramp to handle intermediate results — it's the same concept all the way down. One model, many workloads Because the relational model is built on math rather than a specific access pattern, the same schema can answer: Query style Example Lookup by key SELECT * FROM customers WHERE id = 3 Filter by value SELECT * FROM orders WHERE qty > 5 Aggregate SELECT country, COUNT(*) FROM customers GROUP BY country Multi-table join The example above Subquery / CTE Nested logic without new tables A navigational or document model may outperform a relational one for a specific pre-defined access path — but it loses the moment you ask a question outside that path. Relations trade a small amount of raw speed on the happy path for near-unlimited flexibility everywhere else. For most applications, that trade is an obvious win. Note: Modern relational engines (SQLite, PostgreSQL, MySQL) use sophisticated query planners that choose physical access strategies — indexes, hash joins, scans — automatically. You write the what ; the engine figures out the how . ← Previous Projection, selection, and join Next → Data independence

title: Data independence
text: Three levels of data independence: the physical level can be reorganized without touching apps; the view level can be reshaped without touching the base schema. One of the most practical gifts the relational model gave database engineering is a guarantee you probably already rely on without realizing it: you can reorganize how data is stored, or restructure how it is presented to one group of users, without rewriting the applications that use it. That guarantee has a name — data independence — and it is one of the primary reasons relational databases became the dominant model for business data. Two Kinds of Independence Edgar Codd, who formalized the relational model in 1970, identified two distinct levels of independence. Each one protects a different audience from a different kind of change. Kind What is shielded What can change underneath Physical data independence Applications and queries How data is stored on disk (files, indexes, page layout) Logical data independence External views and applications The logical schema (adding tables, splitting a table, renaming columns) Think of it as two insulating layers. Physical independence sits at the bottom, separating the storage engine from the relational schema. Logical independence sits above that, separating the schema from the views and queries that users write. Physical Data Independence Physical data independence means that the way data is physically stored — which files it lives in, what page size is used, which indexes exist — can change without affecting the SQL you write. You saw a glimpse of this in the Logical vs. Physical Structure page. Adding or dropping an index is the clearest example: the SELECT statement and its result stay exactly the same; only the internal access path changes. A DBA can add an index on a busy production table to fix a slow query, and not a single line of application code needs to touch. Other physical changes that should not break your SQL: Moving a database to faster SSD storage Changing the internal page size or buffer pool size Partitioning a large table across multiple physical files Switching the storage engine (e.g., from one B-tree format to another) The database engine's query optimizer is the mechanism that makes this work. When you submit a query, the optimizer consults the current physical layout and generates an efficient execution plan. As the layout changes, the optimizer generates new plans — automatically, transparently. Logical Data Independence Logical data independence is the harder of the two. It means that changes to the relational schema — the tables and their columns — should not break the views and queries that sit on top of it. The usual mechanism is a view : a named, stored query that looks like a table to any code that uses it. Imagine a users table that is later split into two tables for organizational reasons: -- Original schema CREATE TABLE users ( id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT, email TEXT, country TEXT ); -- After refactoring: split into two tables CREATE TABLE user_accounts (id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT, email TEXT); CREATE TABLE user_locations (user_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, country TEXT); -- A view that reconstructs the original interface CREATE VIEW users AS SELECT a.id, a.name, a.email, l.country FROM user_accounts a JOIN user_locations l ON l.user_id = a.id; Any application that was querying SELECT name, email FROM users WHERE country = 'TH' keeps working without modification. The view acts as an adapter between the new schema and the old interface. Note: Logical data independence is harder to achieve in full because views over complex schema changes (like splitting a column or merging tables) become difficult to keep writable. Read-only views are easy; updatable views that preserve the original behavior require more care. Seeing It in Action The widget below sets up the split-schema scenario from above. First, run the query against the users view — it looks just like a regular table. Then try querying the underlying tables directly to see what the view is abstracting away. Interactive SQL · View as logical adapter Why This Matters in Practice Data independence is not just a theoretical elegance — it solves real problems: Schema evolution without big-bang deployments. As a system grows, the schema needs to change. With views and a well-designed schema boundary, you can migrate tables incrementally and keep old application code running while the new code adopts the new structure. Performance tuning without coordination. A DBA can add indexes, reorganize storage, or enable table partitioning during off-peak hours without scheduling a coordinated release with every development team. Multiple audiences, one source of truth. Different teams (analytics, operations, external partners) can be given views tailored to their needs — different column names, filtered rows, aggregated data — while the underlying tables remain unchanged. The relational model earns this by strictly separating what data means (the schema and the algebra) from how it is stored (the engine's internals). That separation is not accidental; Codd put it at the center of the design. It is why a query you write today against a well-designed schema can still run correctly years later, even after the hardware, the storage engine, and several rounds of schema refactoring have come and gone. ← Previous Why relations are powerful Next → Integrity by design

title: Integrity by design
text: Three pillars of integrity: entity (unique PK), domain (valid types/ranges), and referential (FKs must exist). The engine enforces them on every write. Data quality is not something you bolt on after a system is built — it is something you architect from the start. The relational model gives you a set of tools to express the rules of your data directly in the schema, so the database engine enforces them on every write, from every client, forever. This is integrity by design. Three Kinds of Integrity Database integrity breaks into three distinct guarantees. Understanding each one helps you know which tool to reach for. Kind What it promises Enforced by Entity integrity Every row is uniquely identifiable PRIMARY KEY Referential integrity Every foreign key value points to a row that exists FOREIGN KEY Domain integrity Every column value is valid for that column's meaning NOT NULL , CHECK , data types Together, these three layers mean a database can actively reject bad data rather than silently store it and let problems emerge later. Entity Integrity: No Ambiguous Rows A table without a primary key is a table where rows can be identical — there is no reliable way to address, update, or delete a specific row. The PRIMARY KEY constraint eliminates that ambiguity. It combines NOT NULL and UNIQUE into a single declaration: every row must have an identifier, and no two rows may share one. CREATE TABLE products ( product_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, -- never null, always unique name TEXT NOT NULL, price REAL NOT NULL CHECK (price >= 0) ); Choosing what to use as a primary key is a design decision. Surrogate keys (auto-incrementing integers, UUIDs) are stable by default — a product's internal ID won't change if its name or price changes. Natural keys (e.g. ISBN, country code) work when the real-world value is truly permanent and unique. In practice, surrogate keys are the safer default. Referential Integrity: No Dangling References A foreign key is a promise: the value in one column must match a primary key value in another table. Without enforcement, you can end up with orphan rows — order records referencing a customer that was deleted, log entries pointing to a user that never existed. Queries over such data produce silently wrong results. CREATE TABLE orders ( order_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, customer_id INTEGER NOT NULL REFERENCES customers(customer_id), total REAL NOT NULL CHECK (total > 0) ); The REFERENCES clause tells the engine to check every INSERT and UPDATE on orders against the customers table. But what should happen when a referenced row is deleted ? You have options, declared with ON DELETE : Action Behavior RESTRICT (default) Block the delete if any child rows exist CASCADE Automatically delete the child rows too SET NULL Set the foreign key column to NULL in child rows SET DEFAULT Set the foreign key column to its default value The right choice depends on your domain. For orders, RESTRICT is usually correct — you rarely want to silently erase order history. For a table of session tokens, CASCADE makes sense: delete the user, delete their sessions. Note: PostgreSQL enforces foreign key constraints automatically — no pragma required, unlike SQLite which requires PRAGMA foreign_keys = ON; . Domain Integrity: Column Values That Make Sense Domain integrity ensures that column values fall within the set of values that are meaningful for that column. The relational model handles this at three levels: Data type — a REAL column cannot store the text "hello" . NOT NULL — absence of a value is not allowed when presence is required. CHECK constraint — any boolean expression the engine evaluates on every write. CREATE TABLE employees ( employee_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, hire_date TEXT NOT NULL, salary REAL NOT NULL CHECK (salary > 0), role TEXT NOT NULL CHECK (role IN ('engineer', 'manager', 'analyst')) ); The CHECK on role turns an open text column into an enforced enumeration. The engine rejects any value not in the list — no application code required. Seeing All Three Layers Together The widget below builds a small schema that exercises all three integrity types. The default query shows the clean data. Try the commented INSERT statements one at a time to see each constraint fire. Interactive SQL · Integrity in action Why Schema-Level Rules Beat Application-Level Checks Every application that touches a database can have its own validation logic — but there will always be another client: a migration script, an admin tool, a colleague running SQL directly. Application-level checks protect one entry point; schema constraints protect all of them. There is also a subtler benefit: the engine can use constraints as optimization hints. A NOT NULL column can be stored more compactly. A PRIMARY KEY implies an index. A UNIQUE constraint is itself an index. Rules are not just guards — they are information the engine uses to store and retrieve data more efficiently. Key idea: Integrity by design means the database is the authoritative enforcer of your data's rules — not any single application. Define the rules once, in the schema, and every client benefits automatically. ← Previous Data independence Next → Common misunderstandings

title: Common misunderstandings
text: Four common myths debunked. Tables aren't spreadsheets; NULL isn't zero; foreign keys aren't performance killers; and SQL is not a perfect implementation of the relational model. The relational model is deceptively simple to describe but surprisingly easy to misuse. A few stubborn myths trip up newcomers — and even experienced developers — leading to slow queries, fragile schemas, and hard-to-fix bugs. This page names them plainly so you can avoid them from the start. Misunderstanding 1: "A table is just a spreadsheet" At first glance, a table and a spreadsheet look identical: rows and columns, values in cells. But the relational model makes a guarantee that spreadsheets do not: rows in a table are an unordered set . There is no row 1, row 2, row 3 in any meaningful sense unless you ask for an explicit order. This trips people up in two common ways: Assuming SELECT * returns rows in insertion order. It might — but only by accident of the current execution plan. Add an index, vacuum the table, or scale the data and that order can change silently. Using a row's physical position to mean something. Spreadsheet users often rely on "this is the 5th row so it's the 5th entry." In a relation, position carries no meaning. The fix is simple: if order matters to your result, write ORDER BY . That is the only reliable contract. -- Wrong: relies on undefined ordering SELECT * FROM events; -- Right: declare the order you need SELECT * FROM events ORDER BY occurred_at; Note: The relational model inherits this from set theory. A mathematical set has no inherent sequence — and neither does a SQL table. ORDER BY is not a hint; it is the authoritative way to impose sequence. Misunderstanding 2: "NULL means zero (or empty string, or false)" NULL is one of the most misunderstood concepts in SQL. It does not mean zero, blank, or false. It means the value is unknown or inapplicable . That distinction has real consequences because NULL follows a three-valued logic: a comparison with NULL yields neither TRUE nor FALSE — it yields NULL. Expression Result NULL = NULL NULL (not TRUE) NULL = 0 NULL (not TRUE) NULL <> 'hello' NULL (not TRUE) 1 + NULL NULL This means WHERE column = NULL never matches any row , even rows where the column is NULL. The correct test is IS NULL or IS NOT NULL . Try it yourself — notice how many rows each version matches: Interactive SQL · NULL traps A related trap: COUNT(column) counts only non-NULL values, while COUNT(*) counts all rows. Both are useful — but only if you know which one you mean. Misunderstanding 3: "More tables means slower queries" New schema designers sometimes denormalize aggressively — stuffing everything into one giant table — because they fear joins are slow. This is backwards. Joins are not inherently expensive. A well-indexed join between two tables is often faster than a full scan of a single bloated table, because: The optimizer can use indexes on the join columns to skip most rows. Smaller, focused tables fit more rows per disk page. Redundant data in a denormalized table means more bytes to read. The real cost drivers are missing indexes and returning more data than needed — not the join itself. That said, normalization has real trade-offs. Heavy aggregation workloads (analytics, reporting) sometimes benefit from pre-joined, denormalized structures. The right choice depends on your workload — not a blanket fear of joins. Interactive SQL · Join vs. flat table Notice that orders_flat duplicates Alice's name and email on every order row. If Alice changes her email, you must update every row. The normalized schema stores it once — update the customers row and every query instantly reflects the change. Misunderstanding 4: "The primary key must be an auto-increment integer" Auto-increment integers ( id SERIAL , id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT ) are convenient defaults, but they are not a requirement of the relational model. A primary key must be unique and non-NULL — that is all. Natural keys — values that already exist in the domain — are perfectly valid: Table Natural key candidate countries ISO country code ( 'US' , 'DE' ) currency_pairs ('USD', 'EUR') composite employees Employee number assigned by HR Surrogate keys (auto-increment integers) win when no natural key is stable or guaranteed unique. Natural keys win when you want human-readable references and the value truly won't change. Neither is universally correct — the choice is about stability and legibility of the data, not a rule of the model itself. Reveal: Can a table have no primary key at all? Technically yes — SQL allows tables without a primary key (they are called heap tables in some engines). But the relational model assumes every relation has a key: without one, you can have duplicate rows, which breaks set semantics and makes updates and deletes ambiguous. In practice, always define a primary key. ← Previous Integrity by design Next → Where the relational model shines

title: Where the relational model shines
text: Four enduring strengths: ad-hoc query power, built-in integrity, data independence, and a mature ecosystem of tools and practitioners. The relational model is not the only way to organize data — earlier chapters introduced documents, graphs, and key-value stores. So why does the relational model remain the dominant choice decades after its invention? Because a handful of its properties work together to solve problems that appear in almost every real application. Flexible Querying Without Knowing Queries in Advance When Edgar Codd proposed the relational model in 1970, one of his central goals was data independence : the ability to ask new questions of existing data without redesigning how it is stored. A spreadsheet or a hand-coded file format forces you to decide up-front what you want to retrieve. A relational database does not. Because data is stored in normalized tables with declared relationships between them, SQL lets you combine, filter, and aggregate that data in ways that were never anticipated when the schema was designed. A table of orders created for billing can later be queried for customer retention analysis, geographic heat maps, or supply-chain forecasting — without touching the storage layout. Note: This is sometimes called the "closed-world" benefit: every fact is stored once, and any question answerable from those facts can be expressed as a query. Referential Integrity Keeps Data Consistent Relational databases enforce relationships between tables at the database level, not just in application code. A foreign key constraint guarantees that a value referencing another table's primary key must actually exist there. Consider an orders table that references a customers table. Without a constraint, nothing stops application code from inserting an order for customer ID 9999 if that customer was never created — or from deleting a customer while their orders remain. Foreign keys prevent both: Interactive SQL · Foreign Key Integrity Try editing the query: change SUM to AVG , add a HAVING clause to filter only customers who spent more than $30, or swap JOIN for LEFT JOIN to include customers with no orders. The same two tables answer all those questions. Transactions Protect Multi-Step Operations Real work rarely involves a single row. Transferring money between bank accounts, placing an order that decrements inventory, registering a user while creating their profile — all of these require multiple writes that must either all succeed or all be rolled back together. Relational databases have supported ACID transactions since their earliest implementations: Property What it guarantees Atomicity All writes in a transaction succeed, or none of them do Consistency The database moves from one valid state to another — constraints are never violated mid-transaction Isolation Concurrent transactions do not see each other's partial work Durability Once committed, writes survive crashes and power loss This is not glamorous, but it is the reason you can trust that your bank balance is correct after a network hiccup. Declarative SQL Hides Physical Complexity In most programming, you specify how to do something step by step. SQL lets you specify what you want, and the query optimizer decides how to retrieve it efficiently. Whether a table has 100 rows or 100 million, the same SQL statement expresses the same intent — the database engine chooses index access paths, join strategies, and parallelism on your behalf. This separation means: Application code does not need to know about indexes or storage layouts. Performance can often be improved just by adding an index or updating statistics, with zero code changes. A query that runs slowly today may run faster next month after an engine upgrade, again with no code changes. Reveal: When might the relational model be the wrong choice? The relational model is a poor fit when your data is highly variable in shape (e.g., each record has different attributes), when you need to traverse deeply nested relationships efficiently (graph databases handle this better), or when you need to scale writes horizontally across many machines with very low latency (some key-value and document stores make this easier). The relational model also assumes your schema is reasonably stable — frequent, sweeping structural changes are expensive. Knowing where it shines makes it easier to recognize when something else is a better tool. ← Previous Common misunderstandings Next → Chapter 3 Summary: What We Learned

title: Chapter 3 Summary: What We Learned
text: Page Topic Takeaway in One Sentence 021 Relations, tuples, and attributes A relation is a mathematical set — no duplicate rows, no ordering, just facts with named attributes. 022 Primary keys and foreign keys Primary keys uniquely identify rows; foreign keys reference them, enforcing referential integrity across tables. 023 Set-based thinking SQL operates on sets, not individual rows — filter, transform, and combine entire collections in one statement. 024 Projection, selection, and join Projection (SELECT columns), selection (WHERE rows), and join (linking tables) are the three fundamental relational operations. 025 Why relations are powerful The closed-world assumption means every fact is stored once, and any answer derivable from those facts is expressible as a query. 026 Data independence Change storage layout, add indexes, or upgrade hardware — your SQL queries stay the same. 027 Integrity by design Constraints and foreign keys guarantee data stays consistent at the database level, not in bug-prone application code. 028 Common misunderstandings NULL handling, three-valued logic, and the difference between a table and a relation trip up even experienced developers. 029 Where the relational model shines Ad-hoc queries, strong integrity, data independence, and a mature ecosystem make relational the safest default. Reveal: How do these ideas connect to the next chapter? Chapter 3 gave you the relational model's rules: sets, keys, foreign keys, constraints, and the closed-world assumption. Those rules are the theory. Chapter 4 is where theory meets practice. You will learn how to translate real-world entities and relationships into a concrete schema — which tables to create, how to name them, when to use a join table, and how to evolve a schema safely as requirements change. The relational model tells you what is possible; data modeling tells you what is wise. ← Previous Where the relational model shines Next → From real world to schema

title: From real world to schema
text: Three-stage journey: from the real-world domain to a conceptual model (entities + relationships), then to a physical schema (CREATE TABLE statements). Every database starts as a description of the real world — customers, products, appointments, sensor readings. The challenge is translating that messy reality into the precise, structured form a database engine can enforce and query efficiently. That translation process is called data modeling , and the result is a schema : the formal definition of tables, columns, types, and relationships that governs how data is stored. Step 1 — Identify Entities and Attributes The first step is to ask: what are the distinct "things" the system needs to track? Each distinct thing is an entity and becomes a table. The properties that describe it are attributes and become columns. Take a small online bookstore as a running example. Three obvious entities emerge: Entity What it represents Example attributes books A title in the catalog title, author, isbn, price customers A person who buys name, email, signup_date orders A purchase event placed_at, total Notice that orders is itself an entity — not just a property of a customer. Anything that has its own lifecycle, its own set of facts, or needs to be referenced independently usually deserves its own table. Note: Not everything that sounds like a thing needs its own table. A customer's billing address might be a handful of columns on the customers table rather than a separate addresses table — unless you need to store multiple addresses per customer, in which case it earns its own table. Step 2 — Define Relationships Entities rarely stand alone. After listing them, identify how they relate: A book can appear in many orders (and one order can contain many books) — a many-to-many relationship. A customer can place many orders , but each order belongs to exactly one customer — a one-to-many relationship. Many-to-many relationships cannot be expressed with a single foreign key. They require a junction table (also called a bridge or associative table) that holds one row per pairing: -- The junction table for books ↔ orders CREATE TABLE order_items ( order_id INTEGER NOT NULL REFERENCES orders(id), book_id INTEGER NOT NULL REFERENCES books(id), quantity INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 1, PRIMARY KEY (order_id, book_id) ); order_items turns the many-to-many into two one-to-many relationships, each expressed with a foreign key. This is the standard relational solution. Step 3 — Choose Data Types and Constraints Once you know the columns, assign a data type to each and decide which constraints to enforce. This is where the database becomes an active guardian of your rules, not just a passive store. CREATE TABLE books ( id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, title TEXT NOT NULL, isbn TEXT NOT NULL UNIQUE, price REAL NOT NULL CHECK (price >= 0), published TEXT -- nullable: unknown for some books ); CREATE TABLE customers ( id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, email TEXT NOT NULL UNIQUE, signup_date TEXT NOT NULL ); CREATE TABLE orders ( id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, customer_id INTEGER NOT NULL REFERENCES customers(id), placed_at TEXT NOT NULL ); PostgreSQL note: PostgreSQL offers richer type alternatives for the columns above. Use TEXT or VARCHAR(n) for strings (both work, TEXT has no length limit), DATE for calendar dates, TIMESTAMPTZ (timestamp with time zone) for moment-in-time values like placed_at , and NUMERIC(precision, scale) instead of REAL when exact decimal arithmetic matters (e.g. prices). Every constraint above has a concrete reason: NOT NULL — title, email, and price must always be present; there is no meaningful default. UNIQUE on isbn — two books cannot share the same ISBN. CHECK (price >= 0) — a negative price is a data error, caught at the boundary. REFERENCES — an order must belong to a real customer; orphan orders are prevented. The widget below creates this schema and seeds it with sample data. Run the default query to see a full order summary, then try modifying the WHERE clause or adding a new column to the SELECT list. Interactive SQL · Bookstore schema From Model to Living Schema A schema is not final on day one. Real systems evolve: new requirements appear, edge cases surface, performance needs shift. What a good initial schema buys you is a clean foundation — clear entity boundaries, enforced constraints, and relationships expressed explicitly rather than implied by application code. The modeling loop repeats whenever requirements change: Identify the new entity or attribute. Decide where it belongs (new table, new column, or a junction table). Enforce the relevant constraints. Migrate the existing data to the new shape. Getting comfortable with this loop — moving fluidly between the real-world description and the table-and-column representation — is the core skill of schema design. The earlier chapters gave you the vocabulary (tables, keys, constraints, types); this chapter is about applying that vocabulary deliberately, starting from a blank page and a set of requirements. Reveal: Why not just use one big table for everything? One giant table forces you to repeat data (customer name on every order row), leaves many cells empty (a book column on an order makes no sense), and makes updates dangerous (change a name in one place but miss another). Splitting into entities eliminates redundancy, keeps each table focused on one concept, and lets constraints protect each piece of data independently. This is exactly the problem normalization (covered in the previous chapter) was designed to solve. ← Previous Where the relational model shines Next → Entities and relationships

title: Entities and relationships
text: Entities are the nouns of your data model (Customer, Order). Relationships are the verbs (places, contains) connecting them. This drives your table design. Every real-world domain is full of things that matter and connections between them. Data modeling is the art of naming those things precisely and deciding how they relate — before you write a single CREATE TABLE . Get this step right and everything downstream (queries, indexes, application logic) becomes easier. Get it wrong and you spend months untangling it. Entities: the things you track An entity is any distinct object or concept worth storing information about. In an online bookstore you might identify: Book — title, ISBN, year published Author — name, nationality Customer — email, shipping address Order — date placed, total amount Each entity becomes a table. Each instance of that entity (a specific book, a specific customer) becomes a row. The properties you record about an entity are its attributes , which map to columns. Note: Not every noun deserves its own entity. A book's genre might just be a column if it is a simple string; it warrants its own table only when you need to store extra facts about genres or link them in complex ways. Relationships: how entities connect Entities rarely live in isolation. The interesting work is capturing how they relate. Every relationship has a cardinality — the maximum number of instances on each side. Cardinality Real-world example How you implement it One-to-many (1 : N) One author writes many books Foreign key on the "many" side Many-to-many (M : N) Many authors co-write many books Junction (bridge) table One-to-one (1 : 1) One customer has one loyalty profile Foreign key + UNIQUE constraint One-to-many is by far the most common. You saw foreign keys in Chapter 2.2 — they are the mechanical expression of a relationship in a relational database. Junction tables for many-to-many When the relationship itself carries data (for example, which role an author played on a book — writer, editor, translator), a junction table is the right tool. It holds foreign keys to both sides plus any attributes of the relationship itself. -- Entities CREATE TABLE authors (id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL); CREATE TABLE books (id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, title TEXT NOT NULL); -- Junction table: one row per author-book pairing CREATE TABLE book_authors ( author_id INTEGER REFERENCES authors(id), book_id INTEGER REFERENCES books(id), role TEXT DEFAULT 'writer', -- attribute of the relationship PRIMARY KEY (author_id, book_id) ); The composite primary key (author_id, book_id) ensures no duplicate pairing is recorded. From diagram to tables Designers often sketch an Entity-Relationship (ER) diagram before writing SQL — boxes for entities, lines (with crow's-foot or UML notation) for relationships. The translation rules are mechanical: Each entity box → one table. Each 1:N line → foreign key column on the N side. Each M:N line → new junction table with two foreign keys. Relationship attributes → columns on the junction table. Try the widget below to see a small bookstore schema in action. The JOIN stitches the relationship back together at query time. Interactive SQL · Entities & Relationships Try adding a second author to one of the books — insert a row into book_authors with the same book_id but a different author_id and role , then re-run the query to see how the junction table fans out. Why modeling matters before coding Skipping the modeling step and jumping straight to CREATE TABLE statements is tempting but costly. Structural mistakes — like storing multiple values in one column, or conflating two distinct entities — are hard to fix once data accumulates. Taking even thirty minutes to list your entities, name their attributes, and draw the relationships on a whiteboard pays dividends every time you write a query. ← Previous From real world to schema Next → One-to-one, one-to-many, many-to-many

title: One-to-one, one-to-many, many-to-many
text: Three relationship patterns: 1:1 (FK also a unique PK), 1:N (FK on the child), and M:N (junction table with two FKs). Every time two kinds of things in your system are connected — a user and a profile, an order and its line items, a student and their courses — you are describing a relationship . Relational databases express these connections with foreign keys, but before you write a single REFERENCES clause you need to know what kind of relationship you are modelling. There are three patterns, and choosing the wrong one leads to data you cannot query cleanly or constraints you cannot enforce. One-to-One A one-to-one relationship means each row in table A corresponds to at most one row in table B, and vice versa. This is the rarest pattern in practice. A classic example: a users table and a user_profiles table. Every user has exactly one profile, and every profile belongs to exactly one user. users user_profiles user_id (PK) ←──────── user_id (PK, FK) email bio created_at avatar_url The profile table holds user_id as both its primary key and a foreign key back to users . That double role enforces the one-to-one constraint: you cannot have two profile rows for the same user because primary keys are unique. Note: You could add profile columns directly to users . Splitting into a separate table makes sense when the extra columns are large, rarely needed, or belong to a different subsystem. It is a design choice, not a rule. One-to-Many One-to-many is by far the most common relationship. One row in table A links to many rows in table B, but each row in B points back to exactly one row in A. Examples abound: one customer places many orders; one blog post has many comments; one department employs many staff members. The pattern is straightforward: the many side holds a foreign key column that references the one side. CREATE TABLE customers ( customer_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL ); CREATE TABLE orders ( order_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, customer_id INTEGER NOT NULL, -- the foreign key lives on the "many" side placed_at TEXT NOT NULL, FOREIGN KEY (customer_id) REFERENCES customers(customer_id) ); Notice the foreign key always goes on the many side. Putting it the other way — storing a list of order IDs inside a customer row — is not possible in a plain relational column and is a sign the design needs to change. Try the widget below to explore a one-to-many schema. The default query lists each customer alongside a count of their orders. Interactive SQL · One-to-Many Many-to-Many A many-to-many relationship means rows on both sides can relate to multiple rows on the other side. A student enrols in many courses; a course contains many students. A product belongs to many tags; a tag applies to many products. You cannot model this directly with a single foreign key column — there is no single column that can hold multiple references. The standard solution is a junction table (also called a bridge table or associative table) that sits between the two main tables and holds one row per connection. CREATE TABLE students ( student_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL ); CREATE TABLE courses ( course_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, title TEXT NOT NULL ); -- The junction table: each row = one enrolment CREATE TABLE enrolments ( student_id INTEGER NOT NULL, course_id INTEGER NOT NULL, enrolled_on TEXT NOT NULL, PRIMARY KEY (student_id, course_id), FOREIGN KEY (student_id) REFERENCES students(student_id), FOREIGN KEY (course_id) REFERENCES courses(course_id) ); The composite primary key (student_id, course_id) prevents duplicate enrolments. The junction table also gives you a natural place to store attributes that belong to the relationship itself — like the date a student enrolled, or a grade. Use this widget to query a many-to-many schema. Try changing the WHERE clause to filter by a different student or course. Interactive SQL · Many-to-Many Choosing the Right Relationship Relationship Example Where the FK lives One-to-one user → profile FK on the dependent table (profile) One-to-many customer → orders FK on the many side (orders) Many-to-many students ↔ courses Junction table with two FKs When you are unsure which pattern fits, ask: "Can a row on side A link to more than one row on side B — and can a row on side B link to more than one row on side A?" If only one direction is many , it is one-to-many. If both directions are many , you need a junction table. Getting this right before you build the schema saves painful migrations later. ← Previous Entities and relationships Next → Lookup tables and enums

title: Lookup tables and enums
text: Lookup tables are preferred for dynamic lists — they let you add values via INSERT, not ALTER TABLE. ENUMs/CHECK constraints are fine for truly immutable small sets. When a column can only hold one of a fixed set of values — status, category, priority, colour — you have two main tools: a lookup table (also called a reference table or code table) and an enum type . Both constrain what goes in the column, but they trade flexibility against simplicity in different ways. Choosing the right one is a recurring design decision you'll face on almost every schema. The Problem They Both Solve Suppose you're storing orders. Each order has a status: pending , shipped , delivered , or cancelled . If you store that as a plain VARCHAR , nothing stops a developer from inserting 'Shippped' or 'in transit' . Queries that filter by status become fragile because you can't be sure which spellings exist in production. You want the database itself to reject invalid values — not your application code, which can be bypassed or can crash. Lookup Tables A lookup table is an ordinary table whose only job is to hold the valid values for a domain. Other tables reference it with a foreign key. CREATE TABLE order_status ( code TEXT PRIMARY KEY, label TEXT NOT NULL, sort_order INTEGER NOT NULL ); INSERT INTO order_status VALUES ('pending', 'Pending', 1), ('shipped', 'Shipped', 2), ('delivered', 'Delivered', 3), ('cancelled', 'Cancelled', 4); CREATE TABLE orders ( id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, customer TEXT NOT NULL, status_code TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES order_status(code) ); The foreign key constraint makes it impossible to insert an order with status_code = 'Shippped' — the database refuses it. Better still, the lookup table can carry extra columns: a display label, a sort order, a description, an "is terminal state" flag, or whatever your application needs. Adding a new status is a single INSERT ; no schema migration required. Note: Lookup tables work in every database engine — SQLite, PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQL Server — because they use nothing more exotic than a foreign key. That portability is a real advantage. Try it below. The INSERT into orders with a bad status will fail; fix the status code to see a successful insert, then query the join. Interactive SQL · Lookup tables Enums Some databases offer a native enum type that bakes the allowed values directly into the column definition: -- PostgreSQL syntax (not available in SQLite) CREATE TYPE order_status AS ENUM ('pending', 'shipped', 'delivered', 'cancelled'); CREATE TABLE orders ( id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, customer TEXT NOT NULL, status order_status NOT NULL ); SQLite has no native enum, but you can approximate it with a CHECK constraint: CREATE TABLE orders ( id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, customer TEXT NOT NULL, status TEXT NOT NULL CHECK (status IN ('pending','shipped','delivered','cancelled')) ); Enums are compact and self-documenting — the schema itself tells you every valid value. They're a good fit when the set of values is small and genuinely stable (think: compass directions, coin sides, boolean-like states). Lookup Table vs. Enum — A Quick Comparison Concern Lookup table Enum / CHECK Adding a new value INSERT — no migration ALTER TYPE or schema change Extra metadata per value Easy (add columns) Not possible Portability Any SQL database Varies by engine Schema self-documents values No (query the table) Yes Storage overhead Small extra table Minimal The rule of thumb most teams use: if the list will grow or needs metadata, use a lookup table. If the values are truly fixed and you want them visible in the schema, use an enum or CHECK constraint. Practical Tips Always enforce at the database layer. Application-level validation alone is not enough; migrations, bulk loads, and direct SQL access bypass it. Name lookup tables clearly. Suffixes like _type , _status , or _category signal their role: payment_method , ticket_priority . Seed lookup tables in your migrations. Treat the rows as schema, not data — they should be part of your version-controlled migration scripts. Avoid numeric codes without a label column. status = 3 is opaque; status_code = 'delivered' is readable in every query result and log line. ← Previous One-to-one, one-to-many, many-to-many Next → Derived data and computed columns

title: Derived data and computed columns
text: Derived values (like total = price × qty) shouldn't be stored as regular columns — they drift. Use generated columns, views, or compute at query time. Sometimes the data you need is not stored directly — it is calculated from data that is already there. A customer's age follows from their birth date. A line item's total follows from unit price times quantity. You could compute these values in your application every time, or you could let the database handle it. Derived data is any value produced by transforming or combining stored values. Computed columns (also called generated columns) are the database feature that bakes that derivation directly into the schema. Deriving Values in Queries The simplest form of derived data is a column expression in a SELECT statement. Nothing is stored; the database computes it on the fly each time the query runs. SELECT product, quantity, unit_price, quantity * unit_price AS line_total FROM order_items; line_total does not exist in the table. The database evaluates the expression row by row and hands back the result. You can filter, sort, and aggregate on it just like a real column: SELECT product, quantity * unit_price AS line_total FROM order_items WHERE quantity * unit_price > 50 ORDER BY line_total DESC; Note: Repeating the expression in WHERE and ORDER BY is required in standard SQL because those clauses are evaluated before SELECT . Some databases let you reuse the alias in ORDER BY , but SQLite does too — try it in the widget below. Try the widget to see derived columns in action. Edit the query to add a column that shows a 10 % discount amount. Interactive SQL · Derived columns in SELECT Generated (Computed) Columns Inline expressions work well, but they scatter the same formula across many queries. If the formula changes, you must hunt down every copy. Generated columns solve this by defining the expression once, in the table definition. The database then makes it available as a regular column. SQLite (version 3.31+), PostgreSQL, MySQL, and SQL Server all support generated columns, though the syntax differs slightly. In SQLite: CREATE TABLE order_items ( id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, product TEXT NOT NULL, quantity INTEGER NOT NULL, unit_price REAL NOT NULL, line_total REAL GENERATED ALWAYS AS (quantity * unit_price) VIRTUAL ); Now any query can just write SELECT line_total FROM order_items — no formula needed at the call site. Virtual vs. Stored Generated columns come in two flavors: Kind How it works Disk space Can be indexed? Virtual Recomputed on every read No extra space Limited support Stored Computed on insert/update, persisted to disk Uses space Yes, in most engines Use stored when you query the column frequently and want to index it. Use virtual when storage is tight and the expression is cheap to recompute. PostgreSQL note: PostgreSQL supports GENERATED ALWAYS AS ... STORED for computed columns since version 12. (Virtual generated columns are not yet supported in PostgreSQL as of 2024; use a view or an inline expression instead.) For auto-increment columns, PostgreSQL recommends GENERATED ALWAYS AS IDENTITY (introduced in PostgreSQL 10) over the older SERIAL pseudo-type. -- Stored generated column (persisted to disk) CREATE TABLE order_items ( id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, product TEXT NOT NULL, quantity INTEGER NOT NULL, unit_price REAL NOT NULL, line_total REAL GENERATED ALWAYS AS (quantity * unit_price) STORED ); The widget below creates a table with a generated column. Try inserting a new row — notice you do not supply line_total ; the database fills it in automatically. Interactive SQL · Generated column Views as Reusable Derived Data When derived data spans multiple tables or involves complex expressions, a view is often a better fit than a generated column. A view is a named, saved query that looks like a table to any query that uses it. CREATE VIEW order_summary AS SELECT oi.id, oi.product, oi.quantity * oi.unit_price AS line_total, c.name AS customer FROM order_items oi JOIN customers c ON c.id = oi.customer_id; Queries can now do SELECT * FROM order_summary WHERE line_total > 50 without knowing the underlying join. The derivation lives in one place, and callers stay simple. Note: Standard views in most databases are not stored on disk — they re-run the underlying query each time. Materialized views (available in PostgreSQL and others) do persist the result for faster reads, at the cost of needing periodic refresh. SQLite does not support materialized views natively. When to Use Each Approach Approach Best when… Inline expression in SELECT Ad-hoc queries; formula used once Generated column (virtual) Formula used often; storage is precious Generated column (stored) Formula queried heavily and needs an index View Formula spans tables or is reused across many queries The guiding principle is the same as normalization: define each fact in one place. If line_total is always quantity * unit_price , encode that relationship in the schema rather than scattering the formula through application code and queries. ← Previous Lookup tables and enums Next → Naming tables and columns

title: Naming tables and columns
text: Consistent naming: plural table names, snake_case, descriptive FK names (customer_id not just id), and timestamps following a pattern (created_at/updated_at). The names you give your tables and columns are part of your schema's public contract. Every query, every piece of application code, and every teammate who reads your SQL will use those names constantly. Good naming makes the structure self-explanatory; bad naming forces everyone to consult external documentation just to understand what a column holds. The Core Rules Most SQL engines are case-insensitive for identifiers, but follow these principles regardless of the database you use: Use lowercase with underscores (snake_case). Mixed case is technically allowed, but many engines fold identifiers to lowercase internally, and inconsistent casing causes subtle bugs. Snake_case is the dominant convention in SQL communities. -- Preferred CREATE TABLE order_items ( id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, order_id INTEGER NOT NULL, unit_price REAL NOT NULL ); -- Avoid — camelCase and PascalCase look odd in SQL CREATE TABLE OrderItems ( Id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, orderId INTEGER NOT NULL, unitPrice REAL NOT NULL ); Name tables as plural nouns. A table is a collection of things, so customers , orders , and products read naturally: "this table holds many customers." Some teams use singular names ( customer , order ) — consistency matters more than which convention you pick, but plural is more common in practice. Name columns as singular noun phrases. A column holds one value per row: email , created_at , unit_price . Reserve plural names for columns that genuinely store lists — which, in a normalized schema, is rare. Be specific, not generic. A column named name is ambiguous in any table other than the most obvious. Prefer customer_name , product_name , or category_name . Similarly, id alone is fine for a table's own primary key, but a foreign key pointing to customers.id should be named customer_id — the reference target is implied by the suffix. Naming Foreign Keys and Join Columns Foreign key columns follow a simple pattern: <referenced_table_singular>_id . This convention makes relationships visible in the schema itself without needing a diagram. Column name What it means customer_id Foreign key to customers.id product_id Foreign key to products.id created_by Foreign key to users.id (role-based alias — acceptable when the role matters) Note: When you have two foreign keys to the same table — for example, sender_id and recipient_id both referencing users.id — use role-based names instead of repeating user_id . Role clarity beats mechanical consistency here. Timestamps, Booleans, and Other Common Columns A few column types have community-standard naming patterns that signal their purpose instantly: Purpose Preferred name(s) Avoid Row creation time created_at date , timestamp , when Last update time updated_at modified , last_update Soft-delete flag deleted_at (NULL = active) is_deleted , active Boolean flag is_active , has_verified_email active (adjective, not a noun) Display order position , sort_order order (reserved word in SQL!) The is_ and has_ prefixes make boolean columns read like true/false questions — a reader can immediately infer the domain of values. Note: Avoid names that are SQL reserved words: order , group , select , table , value . If you use them as identifiers, you must quote them everywhere — a maintenance headache. Most engines will let you do it; you just should not. PostgreSQL note — identifier folding and length limit: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase automatically. CREATE TABLE MyTable creates a table named mytable , not MyTable — if you reference it as MyTable without quotes, it resolves to mytable . If you need mixed case, you must quote the name everywhere: "MyTable" . This is the main reason snake_case dominates the PostgreSQL ecosystem. Additionally, PostgreSQL enforces a 63‑byte identifier length limit ( NAMEDATALEN ). Long table names like inventory_warehouse_location_mapping_archive will be silently truncated, which can cause hard-to-debug name collisions. Keep names descriptive but concise. See It in Practice The widget below shows a small e-commerce schema with carefully named tables and columns. Run the default query, then try renaming a column alias in the SELECT list to see how name clarity affects readability. Interactive SQL · Naming conventions When to Break the Rules Conventions exist to serve communication — not the other way around. There are legitimate reasons to deviate: A legacy schema you are extending may already use a different convention. Match the existing style rather than mixing two conventions in the same database. Abbreviations are acceptable when the full word is genuinely unwieldy and the abbreviation is universally understood in your domain ( qty for quantity, amt for amount). Domain language should win over generic naming. If the business universally says "SKU" and not "product_code", name the column sku . The underlying goal is always the same: a reader unfamiliar with your codebase should be able to look at a table definition and understand what each column holds without asking anyone. ← Previous Derived data and computed columns Next → Avoiding duplication

title: Avoiding duplication
text: Normalization eliminates duplication by splitting facts into separate tables connected by foreign keys. Each fact lives in exactly one place. Duplication is the quiet enemy of a healthy database. When the same fact is stored in more than one place, you create two problems: extra storage (the minor one) and the risk that copies drift out of sync (the serious one). This page shows you how duplication enters a schema, what harm it causes, and the design moves that eliminate it. What Counts as Duplication? A single fact — say, a customer's city — should be recorded exactly once. If it appears in multiple rows or multiple tables, any update must touch every copy simultaneously. Miss even one, and your database holds contradictory information. That contradiction has a name: data anomaly . There are three classic anomaly types: Anomaly What goes wrong Update anomaly You change a value in one row but forget the copies in other rows Insertion anomaly You cannot record a new entity without also recording something else Deletion anomaly Removing one thing accidentally destroys an unrelated fact All three stem from storing multiple independent facts in a single table. The solution is to give each fact its own home. Spotting Duplication in Practice Consider an employees table that also tries to record department information: CREATE TABLE employees ( id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT, dept_name TEXT, dept_floor INTEGER -- which floor the department sits on ); A sample of the data: id name dept_name dept_floor 1 Ada Engineering 3 2 Bruno Engineering 3 3 Carmen Marketing 2 dept_floor is a fact about the department , not about the employee. It repeats for every person in Engineering. If Engineering moves to floor 4, you must update every Engineering row — and the database will not stop you from setting them to different values.
