The statistics that drive this are stored in the catalog and updated by commands like ANALYZE (PostgreSQL/SQLite) or UPDATE STATISTICS (SQL Server). Stale statistics are one of the most common causes of bad query plans in production. Note: SQLite's optimizer is a simplified CBO. It tracks row counts and some column statistics, and it can choose between index scans and full table scans based on those numbers. Running ANALYZE in SQLite updates these statistics in the sqlite_stat1 table. How Cost Estimates Drive Plan Choice Consider a join between orders (large) and customers (small). The optimizer considers options like: Option A: Hash-join(orders, customers) -- build hash table on customers (small side) Option B: Nested-loop(customers, orders) -- for each customer, scan orders Option C: Index-lookup on orders.customer_id -- follow index pointers per customer Without statistics, a rule might blindly pick the index. With statistics, the optimizer sees that the customer_id filter is highly selective and the customers table is small, making the hash join cheapest. That is cost-based reasoning. The widget below sets up two tables — a small customers table and a larger orders table. Try running EXPLAIN to see which access strategy SQLite's CBO chooses. Then drop the index and run ANALYZE again to see if the plan changes. Interactive SQL · CBO in action with EXPLAIN RBO and CBO Together In practice, modern engines layer both approaches. Rule-based transformations happen first — the optimizer applies algebraic rewrites (push predicates down, eliminate unnecessary joins) that are always beneficial regardless of data. Only after that structural cleanup does the cost model kick in to choose among the remaining physical options. This hybrid approach means: Deterministic rewrites (RBO) handle structural improvements cheaply and predictably. Cost estimation (CBO) handles data-sensitive choices like join order and access paths. Understanding which layer is responsible for which decision is the key to diagnosing slow queries: if a structural rewrite did not fire, check whether your query can be expressed more cleanly; if the wrong physical plan was chosen, check whether your statistics are fresh. Reveal: Why might a query suddenly run slower after a data load? The cost-based optimizer made its plan choice using statistics collected when the table was smaller. As data grows, old statistics describe a different distribution — a column that was once highly selective may now return a large fraction of rows, making an index scan more expensive than a full scan. Running ANALYZE (or the equivalent for your database) refreshes these statistics so the optimizer can make an accurate new decision. Routine statistics maintenance is a standard DBA task in production systems for exactly this reason. ← Previous Cost estimation Next → Why the same query can run differently

title: Why the same query can run differently
text: The same SQL query can produce vastly different execution plans. Index availability, stale statistics, and data skew all influence which physical plan the optimizer picks — and thus how fast the query runs. When you write a SQL query, you describe what you want — not how to get it. The database engine is free to choose any execution strategy it likes, as long as the result is correct. That freedom is powerful, but it also means the same query can behave very differently depending on the data, the indexes available, and even the current state of internal statistics. Understanding why helps you write queries that stay fast as your data grows. The Query Optimizer's Job Before a query runs a single row scan, it passes through the query optimizer . The optimizer's job is to translate your logical request into a concrete query plan — a sequence of physical operations like index lookups, table scans, hash joins, and sorts. The optimizer considers many possible plans and picks the one it estimates will be cheapest (usually measured in I/O operations or CPU cycles). Its estimates are based on statistics the database maintains: how many rows a table has, how many distinct values a column holds, how data is distributed across pages. -- The same logical query... SELECT name FROM orders WHERE customer_id = 42; -- ...might be executed as either: -- Plan A: full table scan, filter each row -- Plan B: index seek on customer_id, fetch matching rows Which plan wins depends on the optimizer's current statistics and what indexes exist. Three Reasons the Same Query Can Run Differently 1. Presence or absence of an index An index turns a table scan into a direct lookup. Without one, the engine reads every row. With one, it jumps straight to the matching rows. The same WHERE clause becomes dramatically faster — or slower for bulk operations where the index adds overhead. 2. Table statistics are stale or inaccurate Optimizers rely on statistics, not real data, to estimate row counts. If a table has grown significantly since statistics were last collected, the optimizer may choose a plan that was good for 1,000 rows but terrible for 10 million. Most databases update statistics automatically on a schedule, but heavy write workloads can outpace those updates. 3. Data skew Even accurate statistics can mislead when data is unevenly distributed. Imagine a column status where 99% of rows have status = 'completed' and only 1% have status = 'pending' . A query filtering for 'pending' benefits enormously from an index; the same query filtering for 'completed' might be faster as a full scan because the index would touch nearly every row anyway. Filter value Selectivity Better plan status = 'pending' High (1% of rows) Index seek status = 'completed' Low (99% of rows) Full table scan Note: Databases expose the chosen plan via EXPLAIN (or EXPLAIN in SQLite). Reading the plan is the most reliable way to understand why a query runs the way it does. Seeing It Yourself The widget below sets up a small orders table and lets you inspect the query plan for different filters. Try running EXPLAIN on queries that filter by an indexed column versus an unindexed one. Interactive SQL · Query plans and indexes Run the first plan and look for USING INDEX idx_customer in the output. Then uncomment the second block and run it — you should see SCAN orders instead, meaning SQLite reads every row. Both queries return correct results; only the path differs. What This Means in Practice The optimizer tries hard, but it cannot see the future. As a developer or analyst: Create indexes on columns you filter or join on frequently — they give the optimizer better options. Keep statistics fresh — run ANALYZE (SQLite/PostgreSQL) after large data loads. Use EXPLAIN before tuning — never assume; check what the engine actually plans to do. Test with realistic data volumes — a query that looks fast on 100 rows in development can behave completely differently on 10 million rows in production. The key insight is that SQL is declarative: you state the goal, and the engine picks the route. The engine's choice is only as good as the information it has — so giving it good indexes and fresh statistics is how you steer it toward fast plans. ← Previous Rule-based and cost-based planning Next → Reading an explain plan

title: Reading an explain plan
text: An EXPLAIN plan shows the optimizer's chosen execution strategy as a tree of operators annotated with cost estimates, row counts, and scan types. Read from innermost indent outward, and watch for seq scans where indexes should be used. When something runs slowly, the first thing a database engineer reaches for is the explain plan. You saw EXPLAIN in the previous section — this page goes one level deeper: how to read the numbers, what each term signals, and how to recognize the patterns that warn you a query is in trouble. What an Explain Plan Actually Shows An explain plan is the optimizer's annotated recipe for executing your query. It is a tree of operators , evaluated bottom-up: leaf nodes read raw data, inner nodes transform or combine it, and the root node produces the final result set. Every node in a PostgreSQL-style plan carries three key estimates: Field Meaning cost=N..M Estimated work units. N is startup cost (before first row); M is total cost. rows=K Estimated number of rows this node will emit. width=B Estimated average row size in bytes. These are estimates computed from statistics — not measured timings. When you run EXPLAIN ANALYZE (PostgreSQL) the engine also shows actual rows and time, letting you compare estimate vs. reality. A simplified PostgreSQL explain output looks like this: Hash Join (cost=12.50..38.80 rows=120 width=72) Hash Cond: (orders.customer_id = customers.id) -> Seq Scan on orders (cost=0.00..18.40 rows=840 width=36) -> Hash (cost=8.20..8.20 rows=370 width=36) -> Seq Scan on customers (cost=0.00..8.20 rows=370 width=36) Read it from the innermost indentation outward : the engine scans customers , builds a hash table from those rows, then streams orders through the hash to find matches. The root Hash Join emits 120 rows at a total estimated cost of 38.80 units. (This is PostgreSQL-style EXPLAIN output; the same tree-reading principle applies to SQLite's EXPLAIN and other engines.) The Signals That Matter Scan type The scan type tells you how the engine is reading a table: Scan label What it means When it is OK Seq Scan / SCAN Reads every row in the table Table is tiny, or you need most rows anyway Index Scan / SEARCH … USING INDEX Follows an index, then fetches matching rows Selective filter — few rows match Index Only Scan Reads the index alone, never touches the table All needed columns are in the index Bitmap Heap Scan Collects matching pages via index, then reads in order Multiple range conditions, moderate selectivity A Seq Scan on a million-row table with a WHERE id = 42 clause is almost always a bug — it means no index is being used where one should exist. Row estimate accuracy The ratio of estimated rows to actual rows is one of the most revealing numbers in the plan. When the optimizer guesses 10 rows but 100,000 arrive at a join, it may have chosen the wrong join algorithm and the wrong join order. Stale statistics are the most common cause; running ANALYZE (PostgreSQL) or ANALYZE tablename (SQLite) refreshes them. Note: A cost unit has no fixed real-world unit — it is relative within one plan. Do not compare cost numbers across different databases or even across different queries; use them only to compare nodes within the same plan. Nested loops with large inputs A nested-loop join is fine when the outer side is small. If you see a nested loop where the outer scan emits tens of thousands of rows and there is no index on the inner join key, every outer row triggers a full scan of the inner table. That is O(n × m) work and will be painfully slow on any real dataset. Putting It Together: A Worked Example The widget below sets up a small orders / customers schema. Run the default EXPLAIN and note the scan types. Then add the index in the comment and re-run — watch the plan change from a full scan to an index search. Interactive SQL · Reading EXPLAIN Without the index, SQLite scans the entire orders table for every customer lookup. With idx_orders_customer in place, it switches to SEARCH orders USING INDEX — reading only the relevant rows. A Quick Checklist When Reading a Plan Start at the leaves — identify what is being scanned and whether indexes are used. Check row estimates — a large gap between estimate and actual signals stale statistics or a missing histogram. Look at join types — nested loops on large un-indexed inputs are the most common source of runaway query times. Find the most expensive node — in EXPLAIN ANALYZE output, look for the node with the highest actual time. That is where to focus optimization effort. Verify after every change — re-run the explain after adding an index or rewriting a query. Plans can shift in unexpected ways. Explain plans are dense at first glance, but they follow a consistent grammar. Once you can parse the scan type, cost estimate, and join algorithm at each node, you have everything you need to diagnose and fix slow queries systematically. ← Previous Why the same query can run differently Next → Query planning mistakes

title: Query planning mistakes
text: Six common query planning mistakes, each with its symptom and fix. The most insidious is stale statistics — the optimizer picks the right plan for the wrong data size. EXPLAIN is your diagnostic tool for all of them. Even a perfectly correct SQL query can run slowly — or grind a database to a halt — when the query planner makes a poor decision about how to execute it. Understanding common planning mistakes helps you write queries that cooperate with the planner, and gives you the vocabulary to diagnose problems when something is inexplicably slow. What the Planner Does (in One Sentence) When you submit a SQL query, the database doesn't execute it literally. It hands the query to the query planner (also called the optimizer), which generates a set of candidate execution plans — different orderings of joins, index choices, and scan strategies — and picks the one it estimates will cost the least. The estimate is built from statistics : approximate counts of rows, the spread of values in a column, and index metadata. When those statistics are wrong or the planner misreads them, the chosen plan can be far worse than the optimal one. The Five Most Common Mistakes 1. Full table scan instead of index seek If a column has an index but the query wraps the column in a function or casts it, the planner can no longer use the index efficiently — it must evaluate the expression for every row. -- Forces a full scan; the index on `created_at` cannot be used SELECT * FROM orders WHERE TO_CHAR(created_at, 'YYYY') = '2024'; -- Index-friendly: compare against a range instead SELECT * FROM orders WHERE created_at >= '2024-01-01' AND created_at < '2025-01-01'; The fix is almost always to move the transformation off the indexed column and onto the literal value being compared. 2. Stale statistics causing bad cardinality estimates The planner estimates how many rows a filter will return (its cardinality ). If statistics haven't been updated after a large data load, the planner may think a table has 10 000 rows when it actually has 10 million. It then chooses a nested-loop join (cheap for small tables) instead of a hash join (better for large ones), and the query slows down by orders of magnitude. In most databases you fix this by running an explicit statistics update: -- PostgreSQL ANALYZE orders; -- SQLite (updates stats used by its planner) ANALYZE; Note: Many databases run ANALYZE automatically on a schedule, but after a bulk insert or delete it's worth triggering it manually before running expensive queries. 3. Join order producing a giant intermediate result When joining several tables, the order in which rows are combined matters. Joining two large tables first, before filtering with a small lookup table, creates a huge intermediate result that must be sorted or hashed — only to be discarded moments later. Join order Intermediate rows Cost orders JOIN products JOIN customers WHERE customers.country = 'DE' millions, then filtered high customers WHERE country = 'DE' → join orders → join products thousands from the start low Most planners sort this out automatically with correct statistics, but explicit WHERE clauses and proper indexes on join columns are the best way to give the planner the information it needs. 4. Missing index on a foreign key used in a join A foreign key constraint guarantees referential integrity, but — in PostgreSQL and SQLite — it does not automatically create an index on the referencing column. (MySQL's InnoDB engine is an exception: it does auto-create an index on FK columns.) Without that index, every join on that column requires a full scan of the child table. -- If orders.customer_id has no index, this join scans all of orders SELECT c.name, COUNT(o.id) FROM customers c JOIN orders o ON o.customer_id = c.id GROUP BY c.name; -- Fix: create the index once CREATE INDEX idx_orders_customer_id ON orders(customer_id); This is one of the most common performance oversights in schema design. 5. LIKE with a leading wildcard A LIKE '%term' pattern cannot use a B-tree index because the matching characters are at the end of the value — the index is sorted by the beginning. The planner falls back to a full scan. -- Cannot use index on email SELECT * FROM users WHERE email LIKE '%@example.com'; -- Can use index (leading characters are fixed) SELECT * FROM users WHERE email LIKE 'alice%'; If you need full-text or suffix matching at scale, the right tool is a full-text search index (e.g. SQLite's FTS5, Postgres's GIN with tsvector ), not a standard B-tree. Try It: Spot the Scan The widget below creates a small orders table with and without an index. Run EXPLAIN on both versions to see the difference in the plan SQLite chooses. Look for SCAN (full table scan) vs. SEARCH … USING INDEX . Interactive SQL · Index vs. Full Scan Now edit the query to remove the index hint by wrapping the column: WHERE customer_id + 0 = 42 — notice the plan switches to a SCAN . That one-character arithmetic trick breaks index usability, illustrating exactly how function wrapping kills plans in production. Key Takeaways The planner is only as good as its statistics — keep them fresh after large data changes. Never wrap an indexed column in a function or expression inside a WHERE clause if you can avoid it. Always index foreign key columns on the child table; the constraint alone is not enough. Use EXPLAIN (or EXPLAIN in SQLite) early and often — it's free information about what the database is actually going to do. ← Previous Reading an explain plan Next → Chapter 6 Summary: What We Learned

title: Chapter 6 Summary: What We Learned
text: Page Topic Takeaway in One Sentence 051 Parsing and validation The parser checks syntax and resolves table/column names against the schema catalog. 052 Logical plans A logical plan is an abstract tree of relational operations (scan, filter, join) that represents what the query means. 053 Physical plans A physical plan adds implementation details: which index to use, which join algorithm, sort vs hash. 054 Operators such as scan, filter, join Each plan node is an operator; the executor calls iterators (open, next, close) to pull rows through the tree. 055 Cost estimation The optimizer uses table statistics (row counts, value distributions) to estimate how expensive each plan will be. 056 Rule-based and cost-based planning Rule-based optimizers apply fixed heuristics; cost-based optimizers enumerate alternatives and pick the cheapest. 057 Why the same query can run differently Statistics change, schemas change, data grows — the optimizer can choose a new plan at any time. 058 Reading an explain plan EXPLAIN shows the plan tree; start from the innermost node, check estimated vs actual row counts, and look for full scans. 059 Query planning mistakes The optimizer is not omniscient — stale statistics, correlated columns, and UDFs can lead to bad plans. Reveal: How do these ideas connect to the next chapter? Chapter 6 showed you the query execution pipeline — parsing, planning, optimization, and execution. You learned that the optimizer chooses between scanning and seeking based on what is available. Chapter 7 dives into the single most important thing you can provide to the optimizer: indexes. You will learn B-trees, hash indexes, composite indexes, covering indexes, and the critical truth that indexes speed up reads but slow down writes. This is where performance tuning begins. ← Previous Query planning mistakes Next → Why scanning everything is slow

title: Why scanning everything is slow
text: A full table scan reads every page — O(n). An index lookup traverses a B-tree to the exact pages needed — O(log n + k). For a million-row table, that's the difference between reading 10,000 pages and 4. Every query you write ultimately needs to find rows that match your criteria. The naive way to do that is also the obvious way: start at the first row in the table and check every single one. This is called a full table scan (or sequential scan), and it works perfectly well — until your table has a million rows and you only need three of them. The Cost of Reading Every Row A database stores rows in pages — fixed-size blocks of data on disk (typically 8 KB or 16 KB each). When a full scan runs, the engine must read every page of the table into memory, even if the rows you want are clustered at the very start. Consider a table of 1 000 000 orders: Table size Pages to read (at 8 KB/page, ~100 rows/page) Rows actually needed Wasted reads 1 000 000 rows ~10 000 pages 3 ~9 999 pages 10 000 000 rows ~100 000 pages 3 ~99 999 pages Disk I/O is the slowest operation a database performs — orders of magnitude slower than CPU work or reading from RAM. Reading 100 000 pages you do not need is not just inefficient; on a busy system it floods the I/O bandwidth and slows down every other query running at the same time. Even when data is cached in memory, a scan through a million-row table still costs CPU time to evaluate the WHERE predicate against each row, one by one. Seeing the Problem in Practice The widget below creates an orders table with 10 000 rows using SQLite's generate_series . The first query scans every row to find the three orders belonging to a specific customer. Try running it and watch how many rows the engine processes to return just a handful of results. Interactive SQL · Full scan cost SQLite retrieves the right rows, but it examined all 10 000 rows to find them. Now run EXPLAIN SELECT id, product, amount FROM orders WHERE customer_id = 42; — you will see SCAN orders in the output, confirming a full table scan with no index involved. Why Linear Scaling Hurts A full scan is an O(n) operation: double the rows, double the work. That is fine when n is 500. It is catastrophic when n is 50 000 000. Rows in table Time for full scan (relative) ----------- ---------------------------- 10 000 1× 100 000 10× 1 000 000 100× 10 000 000 1 000× Real-world tables in production systems routinely hold tens or hundreds of millions of rows. A query that runs in 20 ms on a development database with 10 000 rows can silently balloon to 20 seconds in production — not because the query changed, but because the data grew. Note: This is why query performance problems so often appear suddenly, long after a feature ships. The code is fine; the table just grew past the point where a scan is tolerable. What We Need Instead The fix is to give the database a data structure it can search quickly — one that locates matching rows without reading the entire table. That data structure is an index . An index trades a small amount of write overhead and disk space for dramatically faster reads. Instead of O(n) row-by-row checking, a well-designed index turns the same lookup into an O(log n) tree traversal — finding 3 rows in a 10-million-row table by reading roughly 24 pages instead of 100 000. The rest of this chapter explains how indexes are built, how the query planner decides when to use them, and how to choose the right indexes for your queries. Reveal: If a full scan is so slow, why does the engine ever choose one? A full scan is sometimes the best option. If a query needs the majority of rows in a table — say, a monthly report that aggregates every order — reading the table sequentially in large, contiguous page chunks is faster than jumping around the file to follow index pointers. The query optimizer estimates how many rows will match the WHERE predicate; if the fraction is high enough (a rough rule of thumb: above ~5–10% of the table), it skips the index and scans instead. Indexes pay off most when you are finding a small needle in a large haystack. ← Previous Query planning mistakes Next → What an index really is

title: What an index really is
text: Without an index the database scans every row. With one, it jumps directly to matching rows using a sorted, searchable data structure — the index is a shortcut, not magic. Every time a query filters, sorts, or joins on a column, the database has to find the matching rows. Without any help, the only safe strategy is to read every single row in the table and check each one. That works fine for hundreds of rows. For millions, it becomes the kind of problem that makes engineers get paged at 2 a.m. An index is the structure that makes that problem go away — but only if you understand what it actually is. The Naive Approach: Full Table Scan Imagine a users table with one million rows. You run: SELECT * FROM users WHERE email = 'alice@example.com'; Without an index, the database engine executes a full table scan (also called a sequential scan): it reads every page of the table from disk, examines every row, and returns the ones where email matches. For one row out of a million, that is an enormous amount of wasted work. The cost scales linearly with table size — O(n). Double the rows, double the time. What an Index Actually Is An index is a separate data structure maintained by the database engine alongside your table. It stores a subset of the table's data — typically one or more column values — organized in a way that makes searching fast. The most common form is a B-tree index (balanced tree). Conceptually, it looks like this: [M] / \ [D–L] [N–Z] / \ / \ [D–G][H–L][N–R][S–Z] ... ... ... ... Each leaf node holds the indexed column value paired with a pointer (a row identifier) back to the actual row in the table. The tree stays balanced so that any lookup takes at most O(log n) steps — for a million rows, that is roughly 20 comparisons instead of 1,000,000. Note: The ~20 comparisons figure assumes a binary tree (log₂ 1M ≈ 20). Real database B-trees have much higher fanout — each node stores hundreds of keys. That means a B-tree for 1M rows is typically only 3–4 nodes deep, requiring even fewer I/O operations than the binary-tree model suggests. Note: An index is not magic — it is a trade-off. The engine must update every index on every INSERT , UPDATE , and DELETE . More indexes means slower writes and more disk space. The win is on reads. The table itself does not change when you create an index. The index is a parallel structure the optimizer can choose to use — or ignore — depending on the query. What the Engine Does With an Index When a query has a WHERE clause on an indexed column, the optimizer weighs two paths: Strategy How it works When it is chosen Full table scan Read all rows sequentially No usable index, or most rows match anyway Index scan Walk the B-tree to find matching row pointers, then fetch those rows Selective filter — few rows match Index-only scan Walk the B-tree; all needed columns are already in the index All SELECT ed columns are in the index "Selective" is the key word. If a query for status = 'active' matches 95% of the table, a full scan is actually faster — the overhead of bouncing back and forth between the index and the table pages costs more than just reading everything in order. Seeing It in Action The widget below creates a small orders table and lets you compare query plans with and without an index. Run the first EXPLAIN — notice the SCAN (full scan). Then uncomment the CREATE INDEX line, run it, and re-run the explain query to see it switch to SEARCH … USING INDEX . Interactive SQL · Index vs. Full Scan After adding the index you should see the plan change from SCAN orders to SEARCH orders USING INDEX idx_orders_email . The data returned is identical — the index only changes how the engine finds the rows, not which rows it returns. The Key Mental Model Think of a database index exactly like a book's index: the book's content (your table data) does not move, but there is a sorted list at the back that tells you exactly which pages contain the term you are looking for. You go straight to those pages instead of reading cover to cover. Three things to remember as you move forward: An index is a separate structure — it mirrors part of your table data in a search-friendly layout. The optimizer chooses whether to use it — writing a query that could use an index does not guarantee it will. Indexes cost something — write overhead and storage are real; you add them deliberately, not by default. The next pages cover the most important index types (B-tree, hash, partial, composite) and the rules that govern when the optimizer actually reaches for them. ← Previous Why scanning everything is slow Next → B-tree indexes

title: B-tree indexes
text: A B-tree: internal nodes hold keys that guide the search downward; leaf nodes hold actual key-pointer pairs in sorted order, linked for fast range scans. A database index is a separate data structure the engine keeps alongside a table so it can answer certain queries without reading every row. The most common index type — the one created when you write CREATE INDEX with no further qualification — is the B-tree (balanced tree). Understanding how it works lets you predict when an index will help, when it will be ignored, and why. The Core Idea: Trading Space for Lookup Speed Without an index, finding rows that match WHERE email = 'alice@example.com' means scanning the entire table. On a million-row table that is a million comparisons. A B-tree index solves this by keeping a sorted copy of the indexed column(s), organized as a shallow, wide tree where every lookup takes at most O(log n) steps. A B-tree is made of nodes , each holding an ordered list of keys and pointers: Leaf nodes hold the actual index keys plus a pointer (row ID / page address) back to the matching row in the table. Internal nodes hold separator keys and pointers to child nodes. All leaf nodes are linked left-to-right, making range scans efficient. A rough sketch for an index on last_name : [Morris] / \ [Davis | Kim] [Park | Zhang] / | \ ... rows rows rows To find 'Kim' , the engine reads three nodes (root → internal → leaf) instead of scanning every row. On a table with 1 million rows, a balanced B-tree is roughly 3–4 levels deep — the difference between 4 reads and 1,000,000. Note: SQLite, PostgreSQL, and MySQL all use B-tree variants as their default index structure. The details differ (PostgreSQL calls its flavor B+tree), but the lookup semantics are the same. What B-tree Indexes Are Good At Because the index is sorted, B-trees naturally support several operation types: Query pattern Example Works? Equality WHERE id = 42 Yes Range WHERE age BETWEEN 18 AND 35 Yes Prefix match WHERE name LIKE 'Ali%' Yes Suffix / contains WHERE name LIKE '%ali%' No — B-tree can't help ORDER BY (same column) ORDER BY created_at Yes — already sorted Arbitrary function WHERE LOWER(email) = 'a@b.com' No — unless indexed on the expression The rule of thumb: if the query can use the sort order of the index, the index can help. If it cannot, the engine falls back to a full table scan. Composite Indexes and Column Order You can index multiple columns together: CREATE INDEX idx ON orders(customer_id, status) . This is a composite index — the rows are sorted first by customer_id , then by status within each customer group. The leading-column rule governs when a composite index is usable: Filtering on customer_id alone — index used. Filtering on customer_id AND status — index used. Filtering on status alone — index not used (the leading column is skipped). Think of a phone book sorted by last name, then first name: you can look up "Smith, Alice" or all "Smiths", but you cannot efficiently find all "Alices" without knowing the last name. Seeing It in Action The widget below creates a small orders table, runs the same query with and without an index, and lets you inspect the query plan. Try running the EXPLAIN first to see SCAN orders (full table scan), then create the index and run it again — the plan should switch to SEARCH orders USING INDEX . Interactive SQL · B-tree index vs. full scan Once you create idx_orders_customer and re-run the explain, SQLite reports SEARCH orders USING INDEX idx_orders_customer — it walks the B-tree to find only the rows where customer_id = 103 instead of reading the whole table. The Cost of an Index Indexes are not free: Extra storage — the index is a separate on-disk structure, roughly proportional in size to the indexed column(s) and row count. Write overhead — every INSERT , UPDATE , or DELETE must also update each affected index. A table with five indexes pays five extra writes per inserted row. Optimizer choices — the query planner may choose a different (sometimes worse) plan when many indexes are present. Indexes should be added deliberately, not speculatively. A good rule: create an index when you have a real query that is slow and the column appears in a WHERE , JOIN ON , or ORDER BY clause with high selectivity (many distinct values). An index on a boolean column with two possible values is rarely worth the overhead. Reveal: Why does LIKE '%term%' skip the B-tree? A B-tree is sorted left-to-right by the full key value. A leading wildcard ( %term ) means the engine has no starting point in the sorted order — matching rows could be anywhere in the tree. It would have to visit every leaf node anyway, making the index useless. Only prefix patterns like term% benefit, because the engine can seek to the first key that starts with "term" and stop when the prefix no longer matches. ← Previous What an index really is Next → Hash indexes

title: Hash indexes
text: A hash index maps keys to buckets using a hash function. O(1) average for equality lookups, but collisions chain within a bucket. Cannot support range queries, ordering, or prefix matching. A B-tree index is great for range queries, but it is doing more work than necessary when all you ever ask is "give me the row where id = 42 ." A hash index is built for exactly that case — pure equality lookups — and it does so with O(1) average-case time instead of O(log n). How a Hash Index Works The idea is borrowed directly from hash tables in programming. When a row is inserted, the database applies a hash function to the indexed column's value. The hash function maps that value to a bucket — a slot in a fixed array. The bucket stores a pointer to the actual row on disk. hash("alice@example.com") → bucket 7 → [row pointer] hash("bob@example.com") → bucket 2 → [row pointer] hash("carol@example.com") → bucket 7 → [row pointer, row pointer] ← collision At query time the engine hashes the search value, jumps directly to that bucket, and returns the matching row pointers — no tree traversal required. Operation B-tree index Hash index Equality ( = 42 ) O(log n) O(1) average Range ( BETWEEN 10 AND 50 ) O(log n + k) Not supported ORDER BY / sorting Supported Not supported LIKE 'abc%' prefix Supported Not supported Space overhead Moderate Low to moderate Note: Hash indexes trade range capability for raw equality speed. If your workload is overwhelmingly point lookups (session tokens, UUIDs, email addresses), a hash index can be the right choice. If you need ordering or ranges even occasionally, a B-tree is safer. Collisions and Growth No hash function is perfect — two different values can land in the same bucket (a collision ). The common fix is chaining : each bucket holds a short linked list, and the engine scans the list for an exact match. With a healthy load factor (entries ÷ buckets) kept below ~0.75, chains stay short and lookups remain fast. When a table grows past that threshold, the index is rehashed — all entries redistributed into a larger bucket array. Database Support and Caveats Not every database offers hash indexes as a first-class citizen: PostgreSQL — explicit CREATE INDEX ... USING hash is supported and WAL-logged since PostgreSQL 10 (earlier versions had durability limitations). MySQL / InnoDB — InnoDB builds an adaptive hash index automatically in memory for frequently accessed B-tree pages; you cannot create one explicitly. SQLite — does not support hash indexes natively; all indexes are B-trees. The widget below uses a standard index but demonstrates the same equality-lookup pattern a hash index is optimized for. Note: SQLite's planner shows SEARCH ... USING INDEX for equality lookups regardless of index type. The O(log n) vs O(1) difference is internal to the index structure and not visible in plan output. Seeing Point Lookups in Action The widget below creates a sessions table — a classic hash-index use case, since applications almost always look up a session by its exact token. Run the default query, then try creating an index and comparing the query plan. Interactive SQL · Point lookups on session tokens With the index in place SQLite reports SEARCH sessions USING INDEX — it goes straight to the matching row. Drop the index and it falls back to SCAN sessions , reading every row. A true hash index skips even the log(n) tree descent and hashes directly to the answer. When to Choose a Hash Index Reach for a hash index when: You only query by exact equality — no ranges, no prefix matches, no ORDER BY on that column. The column has high cardinality — UUIDs, session tokens, email addresses. Low-cardinality columns (like a boolean) gain nothing from any index. Your workload is insert-heavy — hash indexes skip tree rebalancing, so maintenance cost can be lower than a B-tree (though rehashing has its own cost). When in doubt, a B-tree is the safer default — it handles equality nearly as fast and keeps all other query patterns open. ← Previous B-tree indexes Next → Composite indexes

title: Composite indexes
text: Composite indexes on (department, salary) are sorted first by department, then by salary within each department. The leftmost prefix rule determines which queries can use the index. A single-column index helps the database find rows by one attribute — but real queries rarely filter on just one column. A composite index (also called a multi-column or compound index) covers two or more columns in a defined order, letting the database narrow a search across several dimensions at once. How a Composite Index Is Built Imagine you have an orders table with columns customer_id , status , and created_at . A composite index on (customer_id, status) sorts rows first by customer_id , then by status within each customer. The result is a single B-tree whose keys look like: (42, 'pending') (42, 'shipped') (99, 'cancelled') (99, 'pending') (99, 'shipped') The database can leap directly to the right customer_id bucket and scan only the matching status entries — two filters served by one index seek. The Left-Prefix Rule This is the most important rule about composite indexes: the index is useful only when your query references a leading prefix of the indexed columns. If the index is on (a, b, c) , these filters can use it: Query filter Uses index? WHERE a = 1 Yes — leftmost column only WHERE a = 1 AND b = 2 Yes — first two columns WHERE a = 1 AND b = 2 AND c = 3 Yes — all three columns WHERE b = 2 No — skips a WHERE b = 2 AND c = 3 No — skips a WHERE a = 1 AND c = 3 Partial — uses a , cannot skip to c Note: The rule is about the prefix , not the order you write the conditions in SQL. WHERE b = 2 AND a = 1 is the same as WHERE a = 1 AND b = 2 — the optimizer reorders them. What matters is that the leftmost column(s) of the index are present in the filter. Range Conditions Stop the Prefix Equality predicates ( = ) let the index continue scanning into the next column. A range predicate ( > , < , BETWEEN , LIKE 'x%' ) stops the index's ability to prune further columns. For an index on (customer_id, created_at, status) : -- Uses all three columns WHERE customer_id = 42 AND created_at = '2024-01-15' AND status = 'shipped' -- Uses first two; 'status' cannot be filtered via the index WHERE customer_id = 42 AND created_at > '2024-01-01' AND status = 'shipped' This means column order in a composite index matters: put equality-filtered columns first, range-filtered columns last. Try It: Composite Index in Action The widget below creates an orders table and two indexes. Run the first query, observe the query plan, then try the queries in the comments to see which index gets used. Interactive SQL · Composite indexes Look for USING INDEX in the output. When you uncomment the second query (filter on status alone), you'll see a full table scan instead — proof that skipping the leftmost column defeats the index. Composite Indexes and Sorting A composite index can also eliminate a sort step. If your query orders by the same columns in the same direction as the index, the database reads them in order for free: -- idx_cust_date covers both the filter and the sort SELECT * FROM orders WHERE customer_id = 42 ORDER BY created_at; This is one reason composite indexes are so valuable in reporting queries: one index can satisfy WHERE , ORDER BY , and sometimes even SELECT (a covering index) in a single pass. Choosing Column Order A practical checklist for ordering columns in a composite index: Equality first — columns you filter with = should come before range columns. Selectivity matters — among equality columns, higher-cardinality columns first can reduce the scan size. Equality before range — if some query filters are equality ( = ) and others are range ( > , < , BETWEEN ), place equality columns first. This matters more than cardinality: an equality-first index allows the database to seek directly to the matching prefix and then scan the range portion, while a range-first index forces a partial scan across many more entries. Cover your ORDER BY — if a common query sorts by a column, include it at the end of the index. Don't over-index — each index costs write overhead. Prefer a well-ordered composite index over many narrow single-column indexes. ← Previous Hash indexes Next → Covering indexes

title: Covering indexes
text: A regular index still requires a table lookup for non-indexed columns. A covering index stores all needed columns directly in the index leaf — the query is satisfied without ever touching the table, eliminating random I/O per row. A covering index is an index that contains every column a query needs — so the database engine can answer the query entirely from the index, without ever touching the actual table rows. When this happens, the query is said to be covered by the index, and the engine skips what is often the most expensive step: the heap (or clustered) table lookup. Why Table Lookups Are Expensive Recall from the previous topic that a B-tree index stores key values in sorted order, with each leaf entry pointing back to the full row in the table. When you search by an indexed column, the engine: Walks the B-tree to find matching key entries. Follows each pointer to the table page that holds the full row. Reads whichever columns the query actually needs from that row. Step 2–3 is the row lookup (also called a heap fetch or key lookup ). Each lookup is a random I/O — potentially one page read per matching row. For a query returning thousands of rows, this adds up fast. A covering index eliminates steps 2–3 entirely. Because the index leaf already contains all the columns the query references (in SELECT , WHERE , ORDER BY , and JOIN conditions), the engine reads only the index and returns. Anatomy of a Covering Index Suppose you have an orders table and a common report query: SELECT customer_id, total, status FROM orders WHERE status = 'shipped'; A plain index on status helps narrow the rows, but the engine still has to fetch customer_id and total from the table. A covering index includes all three columns: CREATE INDEX idx_orders_covering ON orders (status, customer_id, total); Now the index leaf contains (status, customer_id, total) . The engine finds matching leaves where status = 'shipped' , reads customer_id and total directly from those leaves, and never touches the table. The query is fully covered. Note: Column order still matters for filtering and sorting. Put the equality-filter columns first, range-filter or ORDER BY columns next, and any remaining SELECT columns last — they're just "payload" carried along for the ride. Trying It Yourself The widget below creates an orders table, adds a covering index, and lets you compare two queries. Run the first query (no covering index), then swap the comment lines to use the covered version. In a real database you would use EXPLAIN to see whether an index-only scan is chosen — SQLite's query planner output is shown via EXPLAIN . Interactive SQL · Covering index demo Try editing the SELECT list to include a column not in the index (like created_at ) and re-run EXPLAIN . You should see the planner fall back to a full scan or row lookup, because the index no longer covers all needed columns. Trade-offs and When to Use Them Covering indexes are powerful but not free. Benefit Cost Eliminates expensive row lookups Larger index — more disk and memory Dramatically faster read queries Slower INSERT / UPDATE / DELETE (more indexes to maintain) Can satisfy ORDER BY without a sort Risk of index bloat if columns change often When they shine: High-read, low-write tables (reporting, analytics, dashboards). Queries with a known, stable shape that run thousands of times per second. Queries that touch only a handful of columns out of a wide table. When to be cautious: Tables with very frequent writes — every write now has to update a fat index. Ad-hoc queries whose shape changes constantly; you can't cover all of them. When the index would duplicate most of the table anyway (you may as well read the table). Note: Some databases (PostgreSQL, MySQL/InnoDB) have a concept of index-only scans that the planner enables automatically when an index covers the query. SQLite, SQL Server, and others handle this similarly. The terminology differs, but the principle is the same: if the index has everything the query needs, skip the table. A well-chosen covering index is one of the highest-leverage performance tools you have — but like any index, it should be driven by measured query patterns, not guesswork. ← Previous Composite indexes Next → Unique indexes

title: Unique indexes
text: A unique index enforces that no two rows share the same key — every INSERT or UPDATE must first probe the index and succeed only when the key is absent. An index normally exists purely for speed — it does not care whether two rows share the same value. A unique index adds a second job: it enforces that no two rows in the table can hold the same value (or combination of values) for the indexed column(s). You get fast lookups and a data-integrity guarantee from a single structure. What Makes a Unique Index Different Under the hood, a unique index is still a B-tree. The difference is that before the engine writes a new leaf entry, it checks whether that key already exists. If it does, the write is rejected with a constraint violation error. This check happens atomically — inside the same write operation — so there is no race condition even with concurrent inserts. You create one with the UNIQUE keyword: -- A standalone unique index CREATE UNIQUE INDEX idx_users_email ON users (email); -- Or a UNIQUE constraint on the column, which creates the same index implicitly CREATE TABLE users ( id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, email TEXT NOT NULL UNIQUE, name TEXT ); Both approaches produce identical behavior. The UNIQUE column constraint is just syntactic sugar; the engine creates a unique index behind the scenes. Note: A PRIMARY KEY is always backed by a unique index too. The difference is that primary keys also prohibit NULL , while a UNIQUE index typically allows it — and in most databases, multiple NULL values are permitted in a unique column because NULL is not considered equal to anything (including another NULL ). Unique Indexes on Multiple Columns Like regular indexes, unique indexes can span more than one column. The uniqueness constraint then applies to the combination of values, not each column individually. CREATE TABLE team_members ( team_id INTEGER, user_id INTEGER, joined_at TEXT, UNIQUE (team_id, user_id) -- a user can appear in many teams, but not twice in the same team ); team_id user_id Allowed? 1 42 Yes 1 99 Yes — different user 2 42 Yes — different team 1 42 No — duplicate (team 1, user 42) This pattern is common for junction tables (many-to-many relationships) where you want to prevent accidental duplicate rows. Try It: Enforcing Uniqueness The widget below creates a users table with a unique index on email . Try inserting a duplicate to see the constraint fire, then fix the data and re-run. Interactive SQL · Unique index in action Uncomment the second INSERT and run again. SQLite raises UNIQUE constraint failed: users.email and rolls back only that statement — the table is untouched. This is the constraint doing its job: bad data never lands. Unique Indexes vs. Application-Level Checks A common mistake is relying on the application to check for duplicates before inserting: -- Application does: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM users WHERE email = ? -- If 0 rows: INSERT INTO users (email, ...) VALUES (?, ...) This approach has a race condition : two requests can both pass the count check and both attempt the insert simultaneously. A unique index eliminates the race entirely because the check and the insert are one atomic operation inside the database engine. Note: The rule of thumb is to enforce uniqueness at the lowest possible layer. Application logic can be bypassed (direct DB access, scripts, migrations). An index-level constraint cannot. When to Add a Unique Index Add a unique index whenever a column or column combination is semantically unique in the real world: User email addresses or usernames Order or invoice numbers ISO codes (country codes, currency codes) Composite natural keys (e.g., (flight_id, seat_number) ) Avoid making a column unique speculatively. Unique constraints are schema commitments — removing one later requires a migration and may surprise callers who depended on the behavior. Reveal: Does a unique index speed up lookups the same way a regular index does? Yes — exactly the same. A unique index is still a B-tree, and the engine uses it for WHERE email = ? lookups just as it would any other index. The uniqueness guarantee is a bonus on top of the speed benefit, not a trade-off. In fact, because the engine knows a unique index can return at most one row for an equality predicate, it can sometimes stop searching even earlier than with a non-unique index. ← Previous Covering indexes Next → Secondary indexes

title: Secondary indexes
text: A secondary (non-clustered) index stores the indexed column plus a pointer to the primary key; retrieving the full row requires a second lookup into the clustered index or heap. Every table has at most one primary key — a built-in way to find a row by its unique identifier. But you rarely look up a user by their internal ID . More often you search by email address, filter products by category, or find orders placed after a certain date. A secondary index is any index built on a column (or columns) other than the primary key. It gives the database a fast path to rows based on the values of those columns. Primary vs. Secondary: What Changes With a B-tree primary-key index, the leaf nodes of the tree hold the actual row data — this is called a clustered index (the norm in InnoDB/MySQL; in SQLite every rowid table is effectively clustered). A secondary index is different: its leaf nodes store the indexed column values plus a pointer back to the full row (typically the primary key). Looking up a row via a secondary index therefore involves two steps: Traverse the secondary index B-tree to find the matching key(s) and their row pointers. Follow each pointer into the primary index (or heap) to fetch the complete row. That second lookup is called a row lookup or primary-key lookup . It's cheap for a handful of rows, but if a secondary index matches a large fraction of the table it can be slower than a plain full scan — the optimizer will skip the index in that case. Property Primary index Secondary index Uniqueness enforced Always (primary key) Optional ( UNIQUE or not) Leaf stores Full row (clustered) or row pointer Indexed columns + row pointer Number per table One Many (as many as you create) Write overhead Unavoidable One extra tree write per DML Creating and Dropping Secondary Indexes Secondary indexes are created with CREATE INDEX and dropped with DROP INDEX . They do not change the data — only the access structures the engine maintains alongside it. -- Non-unique index: one email can appear many times (unusual, but legal) CREATE INDEX idx_users_email ON users (email); -- Unique index: enforces uniqueness AND speeds up lookups CREATE UNIQUE INDEX idx_users_email_unique ON users (email); -- Index on a non-key column in orders CREATE INDEX idx_orders_status ON orders (status); -- Remove an index that is no longer useful DROP INDEX idx_orders_status; Note: In SQLite, a UNIQUE constraint on a column implicitly creates a unique index. In most other databases the same is true — you get the lookup speed for free when you declare the constraint. Selectivity: When a Secondary Index Pays Off Not all secondary indexes are equally useful. The engine estimates selectivity — roughly, how many rows match a given value. A highly selective index eliminates most rows early; a low-selectivity index does not. Think about a status column with only three possible values: 'pending' , 'shipped' , 'cancelled' . An index on status alone is nearly useless — every value matches 30–40% of the table. The engine will table-scan instead. By contrast, an index on email is extremely selective: each value matches at most one row. Column Distinct values Typical selectivity Index helpful? user_id (FK) Millions Very high Yes email Millions Very high Yes country_code ~250 Low Rarely alone; maybe composite status 3–5 Very low Rarely alone is_active 2 Minimal Almost never Note: Low selectivity is not always a dead end. A composite index that pairs a low-selectivity column with a high-selectivity one (e.g., (status, user_id) ) can be very useful when queries filter on both. Try It: Secondary Index Lookups The widget below creates a small products table, seeds it with data, and adds a secondary index on category . Run the EXPLAIN to see the index being used, then try the commented queries to observe when the engine skips it. Interactive SQL · Secondary indexes Look for USING INDEX idx_products_category in the plan output. When you uncomment the in_stock query you'll see a full SCAN — there's no index covering that column, so the engine reads every row. Covering Indexes: Skipping the Row Lookup A secondary index that contains all the columns a query needs is called a covering index . When the engine can satisfy the entire query from the index alone, it never touches the main table — the expensive row-pointer lookup disappears entirely. -- If we add price to the category index, this query is fully covered: CREATE INDEX idx_products_cat_price ON products (category, price); -- The engine reads (category, price) from the index — no row lookup needed SELECT name, price FROM products WHERE category = 'electronics' ORDER BY price; Covering indexes are one of the most powerful tuning techniques for read-heavy workloads. The trade-off is a wider index that costs more storage and slightly more overhead on every write. Reveal: Why does adding more columns to an index have a write cost? Every time you INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE a row, the database must also update all indexes that cover columns touched by that operation. A wider index means more data to write and more B-tree pages to potentially rebalance. In a write-heavy workload, too many secondary indexes can slow inserts significantly — each insert into a table with five indexes performs six tree writes instead of one. ← Previous Unique indexes Next → When indexes hurt

title: When indexes hurt
text: Indexes are not free — they cost extra writes, consume disk space, and may be ignored by the query optimizer when selectivity is too low. Indexes are one of the most powerful tuning tools in your kit — but they are not free. Adding an index in the wrong place can slow writes, bloat storage, and even confuse the query planner into making worse decisions. Knowing when not to index is just as important as knowing when to. The Cost Side of Every Index Every index is a secondary data structure that the database maintains in sync with the base table. That maintenance cost shows up in three places: Operation What the index adds INSERT Write new index entry for every indexed column UPDATE (indexed col) Delete old entry, insert new one DELETE Remove the index entry SELECT (wrong shape) Extra lookup step if the index is not selective enough On a table that is read far more than it is written, this overhead is usually worth it. On a table receiving thousands of writes per second — think event logs, audit trails, or sensor data — each extra index is a tax on every write. Low-Selectivity Columns An index on a low-selectivity column — one with very few distinct values — rarely helps. If 40 % of your rows have status = 'active' , the database often finds it cheaper to scan the whole table than to bounce between the index and the heap for 400,000 rows out of 1,000,000. Note: A common rule of thumb: if a column has fewer than ~10–20 distinct values and rows are spread roughly evenly across them, a full table scan usually beats an index scan. Always verify with EXPLAIN before assuming an index will help. Try it below. The events table has a type column with only two values. Notice that even with an index, SQLite's planner may choose a full scan for the high-frequency value but use the index for a rare one. Interactive SQL · Low-selectivity index Write-Heavy Tables Consider a page_views table that records every click on a website. If you add indexes on user_id , page_id , session_id , and created_at separately, each page view triggers four index writes in addition to the row insert. Under heavy load, those writes compete for I/O and locks — you can turn a fast insert path into a bottleneck. -- A table drowning in indexes CREATE TABLE page_views ( id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, user_id INTEGER, page_id INTEGER, session_id TEXT, created_at TEXT ); CREATE INDEX idx_user ON page_views(user_id); CREATE INDEX idx_page ON page_views(page_id); CREATE INDEX idx_session ON page_views(session_id); CREATE INDEX idx_ts ON page_views(created_at); -- Every INSERT now updates 4 additional B-trees The fix is usually to keep only the index(es) that real queries actually use, and to lean on a composite index that serves multiple query patterns at once (as covered in the previous topic). Index Maintenance During Bulk Loads A related trap: if you bulk-load millions of rows into a table that already has indexes, the database updates each index incrementally — row by row. This can make a load that would otherwise take seconds take minutes. The classic solution is to drop indexes before bulk loading and recreate them after : -- 1. Drop the index DROP INDEX IF EXISTS idx_user; -- 2. Load millions of rows (fast — no index to maintain) INSERT INTO page_views ...; -- 3. Recreate the index in one efficient pass CREATE INDEX idx_user ON page_views(user_id); Building the index in a single pass over sorted data is much faster than updating the B-tree one row at a time. Redundant and Duplicate Indexes It is easy to accumulate indexes that overlap. If you already have a composite index on (user_id, created_at) , a separate single-column index on user_id is usually redundant — the composite index already satisfies queries that filter only on user_id (the left-prefix rule from the previous topic). Keeping both wastes space and write overhead with no query benefit. Reveal: When would you keep the single-column index anyway? If the composite index is very wide (many bytes per key) and a high-volume query filters only on user_id , the narrow single-column index might produce smaller I/O — fewer bytes read per index entry. In practice this is rare; start by dropping the redundant index and measure. The Bottom Line Indexes are a deliberate trade: you pay in write overhead and storage to gain read speed. Before adding an index, ask: Is this query actually slow? Profile first — don't index speculatively. Is the column selective enough? Low-cardinality columns rarely benefit. How write-heavy is the table? The more writes, the higher the cost per index. Does a composite index already cover this? Avoid redundant single-column indexes. Will this be used for a bulk load? Consider dropping and recreating afterward. A lean, well-chosen set of indexes almost always outperforms a table buried under a dozen half-considered ones. ← Previous Secondary indexes Next → Chapter 7 Summary: What We Learned

title: Chapter 7 Summary: What We Learned
text: Page Topic Takeaway in One Sentence 061 Why scanning everything is slow Full table scans are O(n) — with millions of rows, that becomes seconds or minutes when applications need milliseconds. 062 What an index really is An index is a separate data structure that maps values to row locations, enabling O(log n) lookups. 063 B-tree indexes B-trees keep data sorted, support equality and range queries, and rebalance automatically — the workhorse of database indexing. 064 Hash indexes Hash indexes provide O(1) equality lookups but cannot do range scans — fast for exact-match queries only. 065 Composite indexes A multi-column index speeds up queries that filter on the leading columns in order — column order matters. 066 Covering indexes When an index contains all columns a query needs, the engine reads only the index (index-only scan), skipping the table entirely. 067 Unique indexes A unique index enforces uniqueness on the indexed columns; faster than a table-level unique constraint and serves double duty. 068 Secondary indexes Secondary indexes point back to the primary key (or row ID); they add write overhead but are essential for fast reads on non-key columns. 069 When indexes hurt Indexes slow down writes (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE), consume storage, and can confuse the optimizer — measure, don't guess. Reveal: How do these ideas connect to the next chapter? Chapter 7 covered indexes for single-table access — when your query filters on one table, an index can turn a scan into a seek. But real queries rarely touch just one table. Chapter 8 tackles the operations that make databases powerful and expensive: joins, aggregation, and sorting. You will learn nested-loop, hash, and merge joins; how GROUP BY works under the hood; and why sorting spills to disk when memory runs out. Indexes help here too, but the optimizer's choices multiply when multiple tables are involved. ← Previous When indexes hurt Next → Nested loop joins

title: Nested loop joins
text: Nested loop: for each row in the outer table, scan the entire inner table. O(M×N) — catastrophic without an index, efficient when the outer is small. When the database engine needs to combine rows from two tables, it must pick a strategy for finding matching pairs. The nested loop join is the most intuitive of those strategies — and understanding it gives you a solid mental model for reasoning about join performance in general. How It Works Picture two tables: an outer table and an inner table. The algorithm is exactly what the name says: for each row R in the outer table: for each row S in the inner table: if R and S satisfy the join condition: emit (R, S) That's a nested loop — one loop inside another. Every row in the outer table is compared against every row in the inner table. If the outer table has M rows and the inner table has N rows, the worst case is M × N comparisons. A Concrete Example Suppose you have these two tables: orders (outer) order_id customer_id 1 42 2 17 3 42 customers (inner) customer_id name 17 Priya 42 Leon To join them on orders.customer_id = customers.customer_id , the engine: Takes order row 1 (customer_id = 42) and scans all customer rows → finds Leon. Takes order row 2 (customer_id = 17) and scans all customer rows → finds Priya. Takes order row 3 (customer_id = 42) and scans all customer rows → finds Leon again. Three outer rows × two inner rows = six comparisons , three matches. When Nested Loop Joins Are Fast (and When They're Not) The nested loop join has a wide performance range depending on the data and available indexes. Best case — index on the inner table. If the inner table has an index on the join column, each outer-row lookup becomes an index seek rather than a full scan. The cost drops from O(M × N) to roughly O(M × log N). This is why databases almost always prefer nested loop joins when one side is small and the other side is indexed. Worst case — no index, large tables. Without an index, every outer row triggers a full scan of the inner table. For two tables with 100 000 rows each, that's 10 billion comparisons. In practice, the query planner switches to a hash join or merge join for large unindexed joins. Note: You can observe which join strategy the planner chose by running EXPLAIN (or EXPLAIN in SQLite) before your query. If you see "SCAN TABLE" for the inner side, it is doing an unindexed nested loop. Try It Live The widget below creates a small orders and customers table and runs a join. Try adding an index on customers(customer_id) and then re-running EXPLAIN to see the planner's note change. Interactive SQL · Nested loop join After running the join, try this to see the query plan: Interactive SQL · Query plan Note: SQLite's customer_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY implicitly creates an index, so you will already see an index seek on the inner table. Try adding a third table without a primary key and joining on a non-indexed column to observe a full scan instead. Key Takeaways A nested loop join iterates the outer table once and, for each row, probes the inner table. With an index on the inner join column, cost is O(M × log N) — efficient even for moderate-sized tables. Without an index, cost is O(M × N) — the planner will typically avoid nested loops for large tables and choose a hash join or sort-merge join instead. Choosing the smaller table as the outer input only helps when the inner table has an index — otherwise M×N equals N×M. The real benefit of swapping the outer/inner roles comes when the inner side can be probed via an index seek, turning a full scan into O(log N) per outer row. Most relational engines choose the join algorithm automatically, but understanding nested loops helps you interpret EXPLAIN output and write index-friendly queries. ← Previous When indexes hurt Next → Hash joins

title: Hash joins
text: Hash join: build a hash table from the smaller table, then probe with the larger. O(M+N) — a massive improvement over nested loops for equi-joins. The previous page showed that a nested loop join without indexes costs O(M × N) — a problem when both tables are large. The hash join is the standard remedy. It is the go-to algorithm for equi-joins (joins on = ) on large, unindexed data, and it appears in virtually every major database engine: PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQL Server, Oracle, DuckDB, and more. Note: SQLite does not implement hash joins — it relies on nested loop and sort-merge joins only. PostgreSQL, MySQL, and most other OLTP databases use hash joins for large, unordered equijoins. The interactive widgets on this page use SQLite for convenience, so you will see nested-loop plans; the concepts and SQL syntax are the same regardless of engine. The Two-Phase Algorithm A hash join works in two distinct phases. Phase 1 — Build Pick the smaller of the two tables (called the build input ). Scan it once and insert every row into an in-memory hash table , keyed on the join column. hash_table = {} for each row R in the build table: key = R[join_column] hash_table[key].append(R) This is O(N) work, where N is the number of rows in the build table. Phase 2 — Probe Scan the larger table (the probe input ) once. For each row, compute the same hash and look up the key in the hash table. Every hit produces an output row. for each row S in the probe table: key = S[join_column] for each matching R in hash_table[key]: emit (R, S) This is O(M) work for the scan, plus O(1) average per lookup. Total cost: O(M + N) — linear in the combined size of both tables, compared to O(M × N) for an unindexed nested loop. Comparing the Algorithms Property Nested Loop (no index) Hash Join Time complexity O(M × N) O(M + N) Memory required Very low Proportional to build table Requires sorted input? No No Works on non-equi joins? Yes No (equi-joins only) Best when… Inner table is small or indexed Both tables are large, unindexed Note: Hash joins only work for equality conditions ( a.id = b.id ). For range or inequality joins ( a.value < b.value ), the engine must fall back to a nested loop or use a sort-merge join. What Happens When Memory Runs Out? The build phase loads the smaller table into memory. If that table is too large to fit, the engine spills to disk using a technique called grace hashing : it partitions both tables into buckets using a first hash function, writes each bucket to a temporary file, then processes bucket pairs one at a time. The on-disk work adds cost, but the algorithm remains correct. Note: You can influence this in PostgreSQL by adjusting work_mem . A larger work_mem lets the build table fit in RAM, avoiding spill and dramatically speeding up hash joins on large datasets. Try It Live The widget below sets up two tables — products and order_lines — and runs an equi-join. SQLite's query planner is simple and will often choose a nested loop on small data, but the SQL and result are identical to what a hash join would produce. Focus on reading the query and understanding the equality condition the hash join would key on. Interactive SQL · Hash join scenario Now try aggregating — hash joins feed naturally into GROUP BY since both operations batch rows by a key: Interactive SQL · Join then aggregate Key Takeaways A hash join has two phases: build a hash table from the smaller table, then probe it with the larger table. Overall cost is O(M + N) — a massive improvement over an unindexed nested loop when both tables are large. Hash joins require the join condition to be an equality ( = ); non-equi joins cannot use this algorithm. Memory is the main resource constraint. When the build table overflows RAM, the engine spills to disk via grace hashing, which is slower but still correct. The query planner chooses the algorithm automatically. If you see a hash join in EXPLAIN output, it usually means the planner found no useful index and decided linear-scan cost was acceptable. ← Previous Nested loop joins Next → Merge joins

title: Merge joins
text: Merge join walks two sorted inputs in lockstep. O(M+N) once sorted. Ideal when data comes from an index or ORDER BY is needed. When two tables are already sorted on the join key, combining them is surprisingly cheap: you walk both lists in lockstep, advancing the pointer on whichever side is behind. That is the core idea of a merge join — sometimes called a sort-merge join because the engine will sort the inputs first if they are not already ordered. How the Algorithm Works Picture two sorted sequences of keys. The engine holds one pointer into each, starting at the top: Compare the two current keys. If they are equal, emit all matching row pairs, then advance both pointers (or hold one while scanning duplicates on the other side). If the left key is smaller, advance the left pointer. If the right key is smaller, advance the right pointer. Stop when either pointer runs off the end. Because both inputs are sorted, no row is ever revisited from the wrong direction. Each side is read exactly once, making the algorithm O(N + M) in the number of row comparisons — linear in the size of the inputs — once sorting is done. Note: The sort step itself is O(N log N). If an index already provides sorted order (a B-tree index on the join column is common), the engine skips the explicit sort and the whole join is linear. A concrete walk-through Suppose you are joining orders.customer_id to customers.id , both sorted: Customers (sorted by id) Orders (sorted by customer_id) 1 · Alice 1 · #101 2 · Bob 1 · #102 3 · Carol 3 · #103 5 · Dave 5 · #104 Steps: match 1 →emit (Alice, #101) and (Alice, #102); advance orders pointer to 3 . Compare 2 vs 3 — left is smaller, skip Bob. Compare 3 vs 3 — emit (Carol, #103). Compare 5 vs 5 — emit (Dave, #104). Done. Bob is skipped because he has no orders; 4 never appears. When the Planner Chooses a Merge Join The query optimizer picks a merge join when it expects the join to produce many matching rows and the inputs are large enough that a hash join's up-front memory cost is undesirable, or — crucially — when both sides are already sorted . Common triggers: The join columns are covered by B-tree indexes the planner can exploit. The query has an ORDER BY on the join key, so the sort is "free" (it satisfies two goals at once). The tables are large and available memory is limited: merge join streams data through rather than building an in-memory hash table. Compared to a nested-loop join (O(N × M)), merge join wins on large, unsorted datasets once the sort cost is amortized. Compared to a hash join , merge join wins when inputs are pre-sorted but loses when they are not and memory is plentiful. Join type Best when… Memory use Requires sorted input? Nested loop Small outer table or index on inner Low No Hash join Large unsorted inputs, plenty of RAM High (hash table) No Merge join Large inputs, sorted or index-covered Low (streaming) Yes (or pays sort cost) Seeing It in Action The widget below creates customers and orders tables already sorted by their join key. Run the query to see a straightforward inner join — then try adding an ORDER BY or changing to a LEFT JOIN to observe which customers appear even without orders. Interactive SQL · Merge join example Notice that Bob (id = 2) is absent — he has no matching orders. Switch JOIN to LEFT JOIN and Bob reappears with NULL in the order columns, which mirrors what a merge join must do when a left-side key has no match on the right. Duplicates and Skew One subtlety: when many rows share the same key value, the merge join must buffer all rows from one side for that key to pair them with every row on the other side. If a single key has thousands of matches (a "skewed" distribution), memory pressure can spike. Most engines handle this by spilling to disk, but it is worth knowing — a merge join's O(N + M) guarantee assumes the number of output rows is manageable. High-cardinality joins on skewed data can turn a theoretically cheap algorithm into a slow one. Note: You rarely choose a join algorithm directly in SQL. You write the query; the planner decides. But understanding the mechanics lets you write queries and design indexes that guide the planner toward the fastest plan — for example, creating an index on a frequently joined foreign key so the merge join path is always available. ← Previous Hash joins Next → Grouping and aggregation

title: Grouping and aggregation
text: GROUP BY splits rows into buckets by key, then each aggregate function folds each bucket's values into a single scalar — one output row per group. So far you have filtered rows with WHERE and sorted them with ORDER BY . Those operations work on individual rows. Aggregation is different: it collapses many rows into a single summary value — a count, a sum, an average, a minimum, or a maximum. Grouping lets you compute those summaries separately for each subset of your data. Together, GROUP BY and the aggregate functions are among the most practically useful tools in SQL. Aggregate Functions SQLite (and every major relational database) ships with five core aggregate functions: Function Returns COUNT(*) Number of rows COUNT(expr) Number of non-NULL values of expr SUM(expr) Total of all non-NULL values AVG(expr) Arithmetic mean of non-NULL values MIN(expr) / MAX(expr) Smallest / largest non-NULL value Used without GROUP BY , each function collapses the entire table into one row: SELECT COUNT(*) AS total_orders, SUM(amount) AS revenue, AVG(amount) AS avg_order FROM orders; That is useful, but often you want the same numbers broken down by category — by customer, by month, by product. That is where GROUP BY comes in. Grouping Rows with GROUP BY GROUP BY partitions the result set into groups, one per distinct combination of the grouping columns, and then applies the aggregate function independently to each group. SELECT customer_id, COUNT(*) AS order_count, SUM(amount) AS total_spent FROM orders GROUP BY customer_id; Think of it as: "For each unique value of customer_id , count and sum the rows that belong to it." The engine scans all the rows, buckets them by customer_id , and emits one output row per bucket. Note: Every column in the SELECT list must either be one of the GROUP BY columns or wrapped in an aggregate function. Selecting a column that is neither — for example SELECT customer_id, product_name, SUM(amount) while grouping only by customer_id — is an error in standard SQL (SQLite is lenient here, but the result is unpredictable). Filtering Groups with HAVING WHERE filters rows before grouping. To filter after aggregation — for example, "only show customers who placed more than two orders" — use HAVING : SELECT customer_id, COUNT(*) AS order_count FROM orders GROUP BY customer_id HAVING COUNT(*) > 2; HAVING operates on the aggregated result, so aggregate functions are valid inside it. WHERE cannot reference aggregate functions at all. The logical order of a SELECT query is: FROM — identify the source rows WHERE — filter individual rows GROUP BY — partition into groups HAVING — filter groups SELECT — compute output columns ORDER BY — sort the final result Try It Live The widget below has a small orders table with customers, products, and amounts. Run the default query, then experiment — try grouping by product , or add a HAVING clause to keep only high-revenue groups. Interactive SQL · GROUP BY and aggregates Now try filtering with HAVING . The widget below starts with the same dataset — edit the query to see only customers who spent more than $400 in total. Interactive SQL · HAVING clause Key Takeaways Aggregate functions ( COUNT , SUM , AVG , MIN , MAX ) reduce many rows to one summary value. GROUP BY applies those functions per group rather than across the whole table. Every non-aggregate column in SELECT must appear in GROUP BY . Use WHERE to filter rows before grouping; use HAVING to filter groups after aggregation. The logical execution order — FROM → WHERE → GROUP BY → HAVING → SELECT → ORDER BY — explains why aggregate expressions are legal in HAVING and ORDER BY but not in WHERE . ← Previous Merge joins Next → External sort

title: External sort
text: External merge sort: sort chunks that fit in work_mem, write sorted runs to disk, then merge them — only O(n) memory needed per pass regardless of total data size. Sorting a handful of rows in memory is trivial. Sorting a table that is larger than available RAM is not — and databases face exactly that challenge whenever you write ORDER BY on a big result set, or when the engine needs sorted input for a merge join or a GROUP BY . The solution is external sort , a classic algorithm that does the work in two phases, shuttling data between memory and disk. Why "External"? An internal sort (like quicksort) assumes all data fits in memory at once. An external sort is designed for the case where it does not. The database engine gets a fixed memory budget — say 4 MB — and must produce a fully sorted output from a 10 GB table without violating that budget.
