The key insight: sort small pieces in memory, write each piece to disk as a sorted run , then merge the runs together. You only need to hold a small slice of each run in memory at merge time. Phase 1 — Building Sorted Runs The engine reads as many rows as fit in the memory budget, sorts them (any in-memory sort works here), then writes them out as a run — a sorted temporary file on disk. It repeats until the whole input is consumed. Memory budget = B rows 1. Read B rows from the input table. 2. Sort those B rows in memory. 3. Write the sorted chunk to a temporary "run" file on disk. 4. Repeat until all rows are read. Result: ⌈N / B⌉ sorted runs, each of size ≤ B. For example, with 1 000 000 rows and a budget of 100 000 rows at a time, you produce 10 sorted runs. Phase 2 — Merging the Runs Now the engine opens all the run files simultaneously and does a k-way merge : it keeps a small buffer from each run in memory, always emitting the smallest current value across all buffers. When a buffer is exhausted, it refills from the corresponding run file. Open k run files (one buffer slot per run in memory). while any run has rows remaining: find the run whose next row has the smallest sort key emit that row to the output advance that run's buffer This is exactly the same idea as merging k sorted lists. At any moment, only a few rows per run need to be in memory, so the memory budget is respected. Multi-Pass Merging If there are too many runs to merge in one pass (more runs than memory can buffer), the engine does multiple passes: merge groups of runs into larger runs, reducing the total count, then merge again. In practice, two passes almost always suffice because modern databases use large memory budgets and produce relatively few initial runs. Note: PostgreSQL calls its memory budget work_mem . Doubling work_mem can cut external sort from two passes to one, which meaningfully speeds up large ORDER BY queries. What This Looks Like in a Query Plan Run EXPLAIN (SQLite) or EXPLAIN ANALYZE (PostgreSQL) on a query with ORDER BY and no suitable index. You will often see a note like "USE TEMP B-TREE FOR ORDER BY" (SQLite) or "Sort Method: external merge" (PostgreSQL) — that is the external sort at work. The best way to avoid external sort is an index whose key order matches the ORDER BY clause. When such an index exists, the engine reads rows in sorted order directly from the index with no sort phase at all. Try It Live The widget below creates a small sales table and lets you observe how ORDER BY changes the output. In a real engine on a large table, this ORDER BY would trigger external sort when no index covers the sort key. Try sorting by different columns or adding EXPLAIN in front of the query. Interactive SQL · ORDER BY and sort plans Now check what the query planner says. Notice the "USE TEMP B-TREE" note on the unindexed column, then create an index and re-run to see it disappear. Interactive SQL · Eliminating the sort with an index Key Takeaways External sort handles tables larger than RAM by sorting small runs in memory, writing them to disk, then merging them. Cost is roughly O(N log N) in data volume, with the constant dominated by disk I/O. More memory → fewer runs → fewer merge passes → faster sort. An index that matches the ORDER BY key eliminates the sort entirely — the engine reads already-ordered rows straight from the index. External sort is also used internally for sort-merge joins and some GROUP BY operations, not just explicit ORDER BY clauses. ← Previous Grouping and aggregation Next → Memory limits and spill to disk

title: Memory limits and spill to disk
text: When a sort or hash operation exceeds work_mem, the database spills to temporary disk files — each spill adds I/O overhead that can dramatically increase query latency. Every operation you have studied so far — hash joins, sort-merge joins, GROUP BY , ORDER BY — needs somewhere to hold intermediate data while it works. That "somewhere" is RAM. When the data fits, things are fast. When it does not, the engine has two choices: fail, or find a way to keep going by using disk as overflow storage. This overflow is called spill to disk , and understanding it explains why some queries are dramatically slower than others despite looking nearly identical. Why Memory Budgets Exist A database engine serves many queries at once. If every query could grab as much RAM as it wanted, a single heavy ORDER BY could starve every other session. So engines enforce a per-operation memory budget — a cap on how much RAM one sort, hash table, or aggregate can consume. Database Setting Default PostgreSQL work_mem 4 MB MySQL sort_buffer_size 256 KB SQL Server Query memory grant Estimated at plan time SQLite Controlled by host application Varies The defaults are intentionally conservative. Multiply the per-operation default by the number of concurrent connections and you get the worst-case memory pressure — 100 connections each using 4 MB of work_mem is 400 MB just for sort buffers. Note: In PostgreSQL, work_mem applies per sort or hash operation , not per query. A single complex query can have multiple such operations running simultaneously, each consuming up to work_mem . What Happens When You Exceed the Budget When intermediate data grows past the memory budget, the engine spills the overflow to a temporary file on disk. The exact mechanism depends on the operation: Sort spill: Sorted runs are written to temp files and merged (the external sort algorithm from the previous page). Hash join spill: The build-side hash table is partitioned; partitions that exceed memory are written to disk and processed in batches. Hash aggregate spill: Partial aggregation results are flushed to disk and combined in a second pass. In all cases the query still returns correct results. The penalty is speed, not correctness. The Cost Cliff Spill is not a gentle slowdown — it is often an order-of-magnitude jump in query time. Disk throughput is typically 10–100x lower than memory bandwidth, so operations that cross the spill threshold can jump from milliseconds to seconds. In-memory hash join: ~50 ms (hash table lives entirely in RAM) Spilling hash join: ~4 s (writes and reads temp files on disk) This cliff is worth watching for in production: a query that ran fine when the table had 1 million rows may become painful when it grows to 10 million and pushes the working set past the memory budget. Observing Spill in Query Plans Most databases surface spill information in their execution plans. Look for these signals: PostgreSQL EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS) — shows Batches: N on a hash join (more than 1 means spill) and Sort Method: external merge on sorts. SQLite EXPLAIN — shows USE TEMP B-TREE when a sort or grouping operation needs temporary on-disk storage. MySQL EXPLAIN — Using filesort and Using temporary in the Extra column signal on-disk temporary work. The widget below lets you inspect SQLite's plan for an aggregation with an unindexed sort. Run it as-is to see the plan, then try removing the EXPLAIN line to see the actual results. Interactive SQL · Spill signals in query plans Tuning Strategies The goal is to keep operations in memory or at least minimize how much spills to disk. 1. Raise the memory budget per session (carefully). For a heavy one-off query, raising work_mem can eliminate spill entirely. Do it per-session to avoid starving other connections: -- PostgreSQL: raise work_mem for this session only SET work_mem = '64MB'; SELECT customer_id, SUM(amount) AS total FROM orders GROUP BY customer_id ORDER BY total DESC; RESET work_mem; 2. Add indexes to eliminate sorts. If an ORDER BY matches an index key, the engine reads rows in order directly from the index — no sort phase, no spill possible. 3. Filter and project early. The fewer rows and columns that reach a sort or join, the smaller the working set. Select only the columns you need and push WHERE conditions as close to the source table as possible. 4. Pre-aggregate or partition. For very large aggregations, splitting the work by a natural partition (e.g., by date range or by shard) keeps each sub-query within the memory budget. The widget below lets you compare a wide-column sort against a narrow-column sort on the same data — fewer columns means a smaller working set. Interactive SQL · Wide vs. narrow working sets Reveal: Why does doubling work_mem sometimes cut query time in half? A sort that fits in twice the memory may go from two merge passes to one. Each pass reads and writes the entire dataset to disk, so eliminating one pass cuts I/O roughly in half. The relationship is not always that clean — it depends on how many initial sorted runs were produced — but large memory increases often produce disproportionately large speedups because they eliminate entire passes rather than just shrinking them. Key Takeaways Engines enforce per-operation memory budgets to share RAM fairly across concurrent queries. When intermediate data exceeds the budget, the engine spills to disk — results remain correct, but I/O cost skyrockets. Spill is a cost cliff , not a gradual slowdown; watch for it when data volumes grow past a threshold. Query plans expose spill through keywords like external merge , Batches > 1 , USE TEMP B-TREE , and Using filesort . The main remedies are: raise the memory budget per session, add indexes to avoid sorting, filter early, and select only the columns you need. ← Previous External sort Next → Join order matters

title: Join order matters
text: Join order dramatically affects performance — starting with the smallest tables keeps intermediate result sets small and reduces total comparisons. When you write a multi-table query, the database engine has to decide how to combine those tables internally — which table to read first, which to probe into next, and what algorithm to use at each step. This sequence of decisions is called the join order , and it can be the difference between a query that finishes in milliseconds and one that crawls for minutes on the exact same data. Why the sequence matters Every join produces an intermediate result set — a temporary "virtual table" in memory (or on disk if it's large). If you join a huge table early and a small table late, you carry that heavy intermediate result through every subsequent step. Flip the order and the intermediate results stay small the whole way through. Consider three tables: Table Rows orders 10,000,000 customers 500,000 promo_codes 50 Worst order: orders JOIN customers → 10 M intermediate rows → JOIN promo_codes Better order: promo_codes JOIN orders → ~50 matching rows → JOIN customers The data hasn't changed. The query hasn't changed. Only the join order changed — and the engine touches millions fewer rows. What the optimizer does Modern database engines include a query optimizer that analyzes your SQL before executing it. It considers statistics (row counts, value distributions, index availability) and picks the join order it estimates will be cheapest. You don't have to write tables in a magic order; the optimizer will often rearrange them. Note: The optimizer works from statistics, not live data. If your statistics are stale — because a table grew 10× since the last ANALYZE — the optimizer can choose a bad plan. Keeping statistics fresh is a routine DBA task. The optimizer's plan is visible via EXPLAIN (or EXPLAIN ANALYZE in PostgreSQL). This is the single most useful debugging tool for slow queries. When the optimizer gets it wrong The optimizer can be fooled by: Stale statistics — table grew but ANALYZE hasn't run. Data skew — a column value appears in 90 % of rows; average-based estimates are off. Complex predicates — functions on columns ( WHERE YEAR(created_at) = 2024 ) prevent index use and confuse cardinality estimates. Too many tables — join-order search space is exponential; optimizers prune it heuristically, sometimes badly. In these cases you can use hints (supported by MySQL, Oracle, SQL Server) or rewrite the query to force a better plan. Seeing join order in action The example below has two tables: a small categories table (5 rows) and a larger products table (12 rows). Try running the default query, then swap the table order in the FROM clause and re-run — SQLite will produce the same result either way, demonstrating that logical output is order-independent even when the physical plan may differ. Interactive SQL · Join order demo Now try the EXPLAIN command to see how SQLite actually plans the join — it may surprise you: Interactive SQL · Inspect the query plan Look at the detail column in the output. You'll see which table SQLite scans first (the outer loop) and which it looks up (the inner loop). Add the suggested index and re-run — notice how the plan changes. Key takeaways Join order determines how large intermediate result sets grow; smaller intermediate sets mean less work. The query optimizer rewrites join order automatically using table statistics. EXPLAIN / EXPLAIN shows you the plan the engine actually chose — always check it when a query is slow. Keep statistics current ( ANALYZE , UPDATE STATISTICS , etc.) so the optimizer can make good decisions. When the optimizer gets it wrong, you can guide it with index hints or by restructuring subqueries, but that should be a last resort after understanding why it chose a bad plan. ← Previous Memory limits and spill to disk Next → Cardinality and row counts

title: Cardinality and row counts
text: The optimizer uses table statistics (n_distinct, histograms) to estimate row counts at each step — accuracy of those estimates determines whether the plan is fast or slow. When you join two tables, the number of rows in the result is rarely what intuition suggests. Understanding cardinality — how many rows a join or aggregation produces, and why — is the single most useful mental model for reasoning about query performance and correctness. What "Cardinality" Means In database contexts, cardinality has two related meanings: Column cardinality — how many distinct values a column holds. A status column with three possible values ( active , inactive , pending ) has low cardinality. A user_id column with millions of unique values has high cardinality. Relationship cardinality — how many rows from one table match rows in another. This is the one that bites people in joins. The relationship types you met in Chapter 4 map directly to row-count behaviour: Relationship Example Typical result row count one-to-one user ↔ profile same as input one-to-many order → order_lines grows (multiplied) many-to-many student ↔ course can explode Note: A join does not cap rows at the size of either input table. If 1 order has 10 lines, joining orders to order_lines produces 10 rows for that order — not 1. Row Multiplication: The Classic Trap The most common surprise is a fan-out join — joining a one-to-many relationship when you only wanted summary data. Suppose you have orders and their line items, and you want the total revenue per customer. If you join first and aggregate second, every order row is duplicated once per line item before the SUM runs — which gives the right answer here , but only because aggregation collapses it back. Add a second one-to-many join (say, payments) and you can double-count badly. Try this widget to see fan-out in action. Notice how joining before aggregating inflates intermediate rows: Interactive SQL · Fan-out join Run it, count the rows, then modify the query to group by customer and sum o.amount — you will see why that double-counts Alice's order 1 (it appears twice, once per line item). Counting Rows Correctly SQLite (and every SQL engine) gives you COUNT(*) for total rows and COUNT(DISTINCT col) for unique values. These are not the same thing, and mixing them up is a frequent source of bugs. Interactive SQL · COUNT variants Edit the query to add GROUP BY user_id and watch how COUNT(*) changes meaning — it now counts events within each group , not across the whole table. How the Planner Uses Cardinality Estimates The query planner you read about in Chapter 6 relies on cardinality estimates to pick join algorithms and join order. It maintains statistics — histograms of value distributions, row counts per table — and uses them to guess how many rows each plan step will produce. A bad estimate cascades: if the planner thinks a join returns 100 rows but it actually returns 100,000, it may choose a nested-loop join that is catastrophically slow. That is why commands like ANALYZE (SQLite/Postgres) exist — they refresh statistics so estimates stay accurate as data grows. Key takeaway: Cardinality is not just an academic concept. It determines result correctness when you aggregate across joins, and it drives every performance decision the planner makes. Always ask "how many rows will this join produce?" before you write the GROUP BY . ← Previous Join order matters Next → Practical query tuning

title: Practical query tuning
text: The query tuning cycle: find the slow query, explain its plan, identify the bottleneck, apply a targeted fix, then verify improvement — repeat as needed. Writing a query that returns the right rows is step one. Writing a query that returns them fast is step two — and step two matters a lot once your tables grow past a few thousand rows. Query tuning is the craft of reading what the database is actually doing and nudging it toward something cheaper. Understand What the Engine Is Doing Before you change anything, look at the query plan. Most databases expose this through EXPLAIN (SQLite, MySQL, Postgres) or EXPLAIN ANALYZE (Postgres, which also runs the query and shows real timings). EXPLAIN SELECT o.id, c.name, o.total FROM orders o JOIN customers c ON c.id = o.customer_id WHERE o.total > 500; The output tells you which tables are scanned, which indexes are used, and the estimated cost of each step. The two things to watch for: Plan term What it means Usually bad when… SCAN (full table scan) Reads every row Table is large SEARCH (index scan) Jumps to matching rows — (this is what you want) TEMP B-TREE Sorts or groups with a temporary structure Happens on large result sets If you see a full scan on a column you filter or join on frequently, that column is a candidate for an index. Indexes: The Fastest Lever An index is a sorted auxiliary structure the engine maintains so it can find rows without reading the whole table. Adding one to a high-cardinality filter column often drops query time from seconds to milliseconds. -- Without this index, filtering by customer_id requires a full scan of orders. CREATE INDEX idx_orders_customer ON orders(customer_id); -- Composite indexes help when you filter on multiple columns together. CREATE INDEX idx_orders_customer_total ON orders(customer_id, total); Note: Indexes speed up reads but slow down writes because the index must be updated on every INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE. Don't index every column — focus on columns that appear in WHERE , JOIN ON , and ORDER BY clauses with many distinct values. Try it yourself. The widget below sets up a small orders table with and without an index. Run EXPLAIN to see the difference, then toggle the index and run again. Interactive SQL · Index vs. full scan Write Queries the Optimizer Can Help With The query optimizer rewrites your SQL before running it, but some patterns make its job harder: Avoid functions on indexed columns in WHERE clauses. Wrapping a column in a function prevents index use. -- Bad: the index on order_date cannot be used WHERE TO_CHAR(order_date, 'YYYY') = '2024' -- Better: let the column stand alone WHERE order_date >= '2024-01-01' AND order_date < '2025-01-01' Use EXISTS instead of COUNT when you only care whether rows exist. -- Wasteful: counts all matching rows just to check if any exist WHERE (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM orders WHERE customer_id = c.id) > 0 -- Better: stops at the first match WHERE EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM orders WHERE customer_id = c.id) Limit early when possible. If you only need the top 10 rows, add LIMIT 10 — the engine can often skip computing the full result. A Tuning Workflow When a query is slow, work through this sequence rather than guessing: Measure first. Record the baseline execution time. Tools like EXPLAIN ANALYZE (Postgres) or simply timing the query in your client work fine. Read the plan. Find the most expensive step — usually the widest scan or the largest sort. Add or adjust one index. Target the column in the expensive step. Re-run the plan. Confirm the engine uses the new index. Measure again. Verify the real-world improvement. Estimated costs and actual runtimes sometimes diverge. The widget below lets you explore a join with aggregation — a common pattern where indexes and GROUP BY interact. Try adding a WHERE clause or an ORDER BY total DESC LIMIT 5 to see how the plan changes. Interactive SQL · Aggregation and plan exploration Note: Query tuning is iterative. A change that helps on a small dataset may not matter — or may even hurt — at production scale. Always test against realistic data volumes when you can. ← Previous Cardinality and row counts Next → Chapter 8 Summary: What We Learned

title: Chapter 8 Summary: What We Learned
text: Page Topic Takeaway in One Sentence 071 Nested loop joins Simple and always available: for each row in the outer table, scan the inner table for matches. Excellent with a small outer + indexed inner. 072 Hash joins Build a hash table from one side, probe with the other. Best for large, unsorted inputs when equality join is needed. 073 Merge joins Requires both inputs sorted on the join key; walks both sorted streams in parallel. Excels for large, pre-sorted data. 074 Grouping and aggregation The engine can hash-group or sort-group; hash grouping is faster when groups fit in memory. 075 External sort When data exceeds memory, the engine uses multi-pass merge sort with temporary files — this is why sorting can be slow. 076 Memory limits and spill to disk Every operator has a memory budget; when exceeded, data spills to disk, causing a dramatic performance drop. 077 Join order matters Joining A then B vs B then A can differ by orders of magnitude — the optimizer's job is to find the best order. 078 Cardinality and row counts Accurate cardinality estimates are critical: wrong estimates cause the optimizer to choose the wrong join order or algorithm. 079 Practical query tuning Start with EXPLAIN, look for scans where you expected index seeks, check actual vs estimated rows, and add indexes selectively. Reveal: How do these ideas connect to the next chapter? Chapter 8 showed you how the query engine combines data from multiple sources — joining, grouping, and sorting at the operator level. But where does the data actually live? Chapter 9 drops down a level to the physical storage layer. You will learn about pages, records, and files — the actual bytes on disk. You will see the difference between row stores and column stores, how slotted pages allow variable-length records, and how a record ID (page + slot) gives O(1) access to any row. The operators from this chapter ultimately read from the structures in the next. ← Previous Practical query tuning Next → Why databases use pages

title: Why databases use pages
text: Databases use fixed-size pages (typically 8 KB) as the unit of I/O — this aligns with OS/HW behaviour, enables efficient caching, and simplifies space management. Every database stores its data somewhere on disk. But it does not write raw bytes one row at a time — instead, the storage engine groups data into fixed-size chunks called pages (sometimes called blocks ). Understanding why pages exist unlocks almost everything else about how databases read, write, index, and cache data. The Physics of Disk I/O Hard drives and SSDs do not let you fetch a single byte in isolation. The operating system and the hardware both transfer data in minimum-sized chunks aligned to disk sectors. Asking for one byte means reading an entire sector (typically 512 bytes) or filesystem block (typically 4 KB) anyway. Database designers turned this constraint into an advantage: if you are paying the I/O cost of a large chunk regardless, you should pack as many useful bytes into that chunk as possible. A database page is usually 8 KB (PostgreSQL's default), 16 KB (MySQL InnoDB's default), or a power-of-two size you choose at creation time. SQLite defaults to 4 KB. Database Default page size SQLite 4 KB PostgreSQL 8 KB MySQL (InnoDB) 16 KB SQL Server 8 KB Note: "Page" (database term) and "page" (OS virtual-memory term) are different things. In database literature, "page" always means a fixed-size storage unit on disk and in the buffer pool. What Lives Inside a Page A page is not a raw dump of rows. It has structure: a small header that records the page type, a checksum for corruption detection, and bookkeeping for the storage engine; then a slot array that points to where each record starts; and finally the records themselves. ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ Page header (type, LSN, checksum, …) │ ├─────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ Slot array [offset₁, offset₂, …] │ ├─────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ Free space │ ├─────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ Records (packed from the bottom up) │ └─────────────────────────────────────────┘ The slot array grows downward from the top; records grow upward from the bottom. Inserts are cheap until the free space in the middle is exhausted — at that point the page is full and the engine must find another page or split this one. A single page can hold many rows. At 8 KB with a 200-byte average row, one page holds roughly 40 rows. That means one disk read can fetch 40 rows simultaneously — a massive win over fetching rows one at a time. Pages as the Unit of Caching The real payoff comes in memory. Databases maintain a buffer pool (also called the buffer cache): a region of RAM where recently-used pages live. When a query needs a row, the engine checks the buffer pool first. If the page is already there, no disk I/O happens at all — the data is served from RAM at nanosecond speed instead of millisecond disk speed. Because the page is the unit of transfer, this caching works naturally: load one page, cache the whole page, serve every subsequent access to any row on that page from RAM. A hot table that fits in the buffer pool is effectively an in-memory table. This also explains why query performance often "falls off a cliff" once a working set grows larger than the buffer pool: pages start getting evicted before they are reused, and every access goes back to disk. Pages as the Unit of Locking and Transactions Pages matter beyond I/O. Many storage engines acquire page-level latches when reading or writing, ensuring that two threads do not corrupt the same page simultaneously. Write-ahead logging (WAL) records changes page by page. When the engine needs to roll back a transaction or recover from a crash, it replays or undoes changes at page granularity. In short, the page is the fundamental atom of the storage layer — the thing that gets read, written, cached, locked, logged, and recovered as one indivisible unit. Try the widget below to see page-size reasoning in action. The query counts how many rows of different sizes would fit on a single 4 KB page (leaving 100 bytes for the page header and slot overhead per row). Interactive SQL · Rows per page calculator Change the page sizes in the query (try 16384 for MySQL's default 16 KB) or add a row size that matches a table you are designing. Notice how a wide row can drop a page down to just one or two records — a strong signal to reconsider your schema or choose a larger page size. ← Previous Practical query tuning Next → Heap files and page layouts

title: Heap files and page layouts
text: A heap file is a linked collection of fixed-size pages; records are appended in insertion order across pages, with no inherent ordering. When a database writes a row to disk, it does not just append text to a file the way a spreadsheet might. It places the row inside a precisely structured unit called a page , and those pages are collected into a heap file . Understanding this physical layer explains why databases behave the way they do — why some queries scan millions of rows quickly, why deleting a row does not instantly reclaim space, and why "bloat" is a real operational concern. The Heap File: An Unordered Collection of Pages A heap file is the default storage structure for a table. "Heap" means no inherent order — rows are stored wherever there is space, not sorted by any key. When you insert a row, the engine finds a page with enough free space and writes the row there. When you delete a row, the engine marks it as dead but leaves the space on the page until it is reclaimed later (vacuumed or compacted). This is deliberately simple: Inserts are fast — just append to the last page or fill a gap. No insert-time sorting cost — ordering is the job of indexes, not the heap. Trade-off — a full-table scan reads every page, including pages with dead rows and free space. Most relational databases — PostgreSQL, MySQL (for non-InnoDB tables), SQLite — store tables as heap files by default. Note: InnoDB (MySQL/MariaDB's default engine) uses a clustered index instead, which stores rows in primary-key order inside a B-tree. The heap concept still applies to secondary structures in clustered-index engines, and many databases offer both options. Page Layout: What Is Inside One Page A page (also called a block ) is the smallest unit the storage engine reads from or writes to disk. Typical sizes are 4 KB, 8 KB, or 16 KB — matching OS and hardware page sizes for efficiency. Every I/O operation moves at least one full page, even if you only need one column of one row. A page is divided into three regions: ┌────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ Page Header (metadata: page ID, LSN, …) │ ├────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ Slot Array [slot 0 | slot 1 | slot 2 …] │ ← grows downward ├────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ Free Space │ ├────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ … record N │ … record 1 │ record 0 │ ← grows upward └────────────────────────────────────────────┘ Region Purpose Page header Page ID, checksum, free-space pointer, transaction info (LSN) Slot array Fixed-size array of (offset, length) pairs — one entry per record on the page Free space The gap between the slot array and record data; new records consume from here Record data Actual row bytes, packed from the bottom of the page upward The slot array is the key insight. Because slots are fixed-size and numbered, the engine can refer to a row by its (page_id, slot_number) — called a record ID (RID) or tuple ID (TID). When a row is updated and moves within the same page, only the slot entry changes; external references (like index entries pointing to this row) remain valid. Variable-Length Records Real rows have variable-length fields — VARCHAR , TEXT , BLOB . The engine stores fixed-width fields first (integers, dates, fixed-length chars), then variable-width fields after, with a small offset array at the start of the record so it can jump directly to any field without scanning the whole row. Dead Rows and Fragmentation When you DELETE or UPDATE a row, the old version is marked dead but stays on the page until the engine's background cleaner runs. This is intentional: Other transactions that started before the delete may still need to read the old version (MVCC — covered later). Immediately reclaiming space would require rewriting the page under concurrent readers. Over time, a heavily-updated table accumulates dead rows and fragmented free space spread across many pages. This is table bloat . A full-table scan must still read every page, even pages that are 80% dead rows. Databases like PostgreSQL run a background process called VACUUM to reclaim that space. Try It: Observing Row Storage SQLite exposes some of its page-level internals through PRAGMA commands. Run the query below to see how SQLite allocates pages as you insert data, and observe how page count grows with the table. Interactive SQL · Page allocation The page_count climbs as the table grows — each page is exactly page_size bytes on disk, read and written as a unit regardless of how much of the page is actually occupied by live rows. The Key Takeaway A heap file is simply a collection of fixed-size pages containing variable-size records, with no inherent row ordering. The slot array inside each page makes it possible to locate and reference rows efficiently without re-packing the page on every change. This design — simple, append-friendly, and MVCC-compatible — is the foundation on top of which indexes, vacuum, and the buffer pool all operate. ← Previous Why databases use pages Next → Row stores and column stores

title: Row stores and column stores
text: Row stores (NSM) keep all columns of a row together — ideal for OLTP. Column stores (DSM) group all values of a column together — ideal for analytical aggregates on few columns. How a database lays out your data on disk turns out to matter enormously — not just for performance, but for the kinds of questions your database can answer efficiently. Two fundamentally different strategies have emerged: row stores (also called row-oriented or N-ary storage, NSM) and column stores (column-oriented or DSM — decomposition storage model). Most databases you encounter pick one, and understanding why helps you choose the right tool for the job. How Row Stores Work A row store keeps all the fields of a single row together on the same page. If you have a table of orders, the page on disk looks roughly like: Page 42: [ order_id=1001, customer='Alice', amount=99.00, status='shipped' ] [ order_id=1002, customer='Bob', amount=14.50, status='pending' ] [ order_id=1003, customer='Carol', amount=230.75, status='shipped' ] ... Reading a single row is fast: one disk read fetches everything about that row in one shot. This is great for OLTP (Online Transaction Processing) workloads — inserting a new order, updating a status, or fetching one customer's record. PostgreSQL, MySQL, and SQLite all use row storage. How Column Stores Work A column store flips the layout: all values for a single column are stored together, separate from other columns. order_id column: [ 1001, 1002, 1003, ... ] customer column: [ 'Alice', 'Bob', 'Carol', ... ] amount column: [ 99.00, 14.50, 230.75, ... ] status column: [ 'shipped', 'pending', 'shipped', ... ] If you only need the amount and status columns to compute total revenue by status, the database reads only those two columns — it never touches order_id or customer . For wide tables with dozens of columns, this can cut I/O by an order of magnitude. Column stores also compress extremely well: similar values (like repeated status strings) pack tightly together. Databases like DuckDB, ClickHouse, Amazon Redshift, and Apache Parquet files use columnar layout. Trade-offs at a Glance Concern Row Store Column Store Single-row read/write Fast (one I/O) Slow (touches many column files) Aggregate over one column Slow (reads all columns) Fast (reads one column) Compression Moderate High (column values are similar) Typical workload OLTP (many small transactions) OLAP (few, large analytical queries) Examples PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQLite DuckDB, ClickHouse, Redshift Note: Many modern systems blur the line. PostgreSQL has columnar extensions (like pg_mooncake ). Some "hybrid" engines (like SingleStore or SQL Server with columnstore indexes) maintain both layouts and pick at query time. Seeing the Difference in Query Patterns The SQL you write looks the same regardless of storage layout — but the engine's internal behavior differs drastically. Consider these two queries against a million-row sales table: -- Query A: fetch one full order (row store wins) SELECT * FROM sales WHERE order_id = 999; -- Query B: aggregate one column across all rows (column store wins) SELECT status, SUM(amount) FROM sales GROUP BY status; Query A touches exactly one row — a row store finds it with one index lookup and one page read. A column store must stitch the row back together from multiple column segments. Query B scans every amount and status value but never touches customer , address , or any other column. A column store reads only two narrow columns and can vectorize the arithmetic. A row store drags every column off disk even though most of it is irrelevant. Try it yourself. This widget seeds a small sales table and lets you compare how different queries feel. Change SELECT * to SELECT SUM(amount) and notice how the second form is the shape a column store is optimized for: Interactive SQL · Row vs Column Query Shapes Run the first query, then uncomment the second. The aggregation only cares about two columns — exactly the pattern a column store exploits. SQLite here uses row storage, so both queries read all columns; in a real column store the aggregate would skip most of the data entirely. ← Previous Heap files and page layouts Next → Fixed-length and variable-length records

title: Fixed-length and variable-length records
text: Fixed-length records are all the same size, enabling O(1) offset calculation; variable-length records need length prefixes and a slot directory for lookup. Every row in a table must eventually live somewhere on disk. How the database engine lays out the bytes for that row is determined by whether its fields have fixed or variable lengths — a design decision with real consequences for storage efficiency, read speed, and update cost. Fixed-Length Records A fixed-length record is one where every field occupies exactly the same number of bytes, regardless of the actual data stored. If a CHAR(20) column is declared, the engine always writes 20 bytes — padding with spaces (or zeros) when the actual value is shorter. Column Declared type Bytes on disk Example value employee_id INTEGER 4 1042 department_id INTEGER 4 7 salary REAL 8 82500.00 status_code CHAR(1) 1 'A' Total record size: 17 bytes, always. Every record in this table is the same width. This uniformity is a big win for the storage engine: Random access by row number — to find record n , seek to byte offset n × 17 . No scanning needed. Simple updates in place — a new value for salary fits in the same 8 bytes, so the engine overwrites without moving anything. No metadata overhead — no need to store lengths or offsets per field. The cost is wasted space when values are short. A CHAR(200) column storing mostly short names wastes hundreds of bytes per row. Variable-Length Records A variable-length record contains at least one field whose on-disk size changes with the data — typically columns declared as VARCHAR(n) , TEXT , or BLOB . The engine stores only the bytes actually needed, plus a small amount of bookkeeping so it can find each field. A common layout stores a slot directory (or "record header") at the start of the record: a compact list of (offset, length) pairs — one per variable field — followed by the actual data bytes: [ offset_1 | len_1 | offset_2 | len_2 | ... | data_1 | data_2 | ... ] For example, storing name = 'Ali' and bio = 'Loves hiking' might look like: [ 4 | 3 | 7 | 12 | A | l | i | L | o | v | e | s | | h | i | k | i | n | g ] ^offset ^len ^offset ^len ^---name---^ ^-----------bio-----------^ Advantage Trade-off Compact storage — short strings use few bytes Cannot seek to a field by offset alone Handles unbounded text naturally Updates that grow a value may force a record to move Works well for sparse, irregular data Small per-field header overhead Note: Most real engines use a hybrid: fixed-length fields (integers, dates, floats) are stored at known offsets at the start of the record, and variable-length fields are appended after them. This keeps fast access for common numeric fields while still supporting text columns. Seeing the Difference in Practice SQLite stores TEXT and BLOB columns as variable-length values internally, while INTEGER and REAL use compact fixed-width encodings. The query below lets you compare storage for a table that mixes both kinds of columns. Try running the default query, then change the INSERT values to very long or very short strings and observe how length() changes — that variation is variable-length storage in action. Note: length() returns the number of characters , not bytes. For multi-byte encodings like UTF-8, a single character can occupy 1–4 bytes. In PostgreSQL, use octet_length() to get the actual byte count of a string. The difference matters for understanding storage: a 100-character string of emoji (4 bytes each) costs 400 bytes on disk, not 100. Interactive SQL · Fixed vs. Variable-Length Fields Notice that fixed_cols_bytes is identical for every row, while variable_total swings widely. That swing is exactly what the engine's slot directory has to track at runtime. Why This Matters for Query Performance The record layout affects more than storage size. When the engine scans a table page looking for rows that match a WHERE clause: With fixed-length records , it can jump directly to any row's field using arithmetic — no parsing required. With variable-length records , it must consult the slot directory for each row before it can read a specific field. This is one reason why columns used in hot filter conditions ( WHERE status = 'A' ) are often kept as fixed-width types, while free-text columns ( bio , notes ) are allowed to vary. The trade-off between compactness and access speed shapes table design from the very first CREATE TABLE . ← Previous Row stores and column stores Next → Slotted pages

title: Slotted pages
text: A slotted page has a header, a slot array growing downward, free space in the middle, and records growing upward from the bottom — each slot points to its record's offset and length. When a database engine reads or writes data, it works in fixed-size chunks called pages (typically 4 KB–16 KB). Each page holds multiple records, but those records are rarely the same length — a VARCHAR(1000) column might store 3 bytes in one row and 900 in another. The slotted page format is the near-universal answer to this problem: a layout that packs variable-length records tightly while still letting the engine jump directly to any record by number. The Layout A slotted page is divided into three regions that grow toward each other: ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ Page header (fixed, at the top) │ ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ Slot array → [slot 0][slot 1][slot 2]... │ grows ↓ ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ free space │ ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ ...← record N ← record 1 ← record 0 │ grows ↑ └──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ Page header — stores the page ID, free-space pointer, number of slots, and a checksum. Slot array — a compact array of (offset, length) pairs, one per record. Slot 0 points to record 0, slot 1 to record 1, and so on. The array grows downward from just below the header. Record area — records are packed upward from the bottom of the page. When a new record arrives it is written just above the last one; its position is recorded in the next slot. Because every record is addressed through a slot, external references (indexes, foreign keys, page-level pointers) only need to store (page_id, slot_number) — a record ID (RID) or tuple ID (TID) . The slot acts as an indirection layer. Why Indirection Matters Without the slot array, moving a record inside the page (to compact free space after a deletion) would require updating every index entry that points to the old byte offset. With slots, you just update the single (offset, length) entry in the slot array and leave every external pointer untouched. Operation Without slots With slots Delete a record Must compact immediately or leave a "hole" with a forwarding pointer Mark slot as free ( length = 0 ), compact later Update a record (grows) May need to move other records; all pointers break Move the record within the page, update one slot entry External index points to Byte offset (fragile) (page_id, slot_number) (stable) Note: When a record outgrows the page entirely after an update, the engine writes a forwarding record in the old slot — a tiny pointer to the new page. Queries that follow the original RID transparently follow one hop to the new location. Deletions and Compaction Deleting a record marks its slot as invalid and leaves the bytes as dead space. Over time, a page can become fragmented — many small gaps between live records. The engine reorganizes (compacts) the page lazily: it slides all live records to the bottom, rewrites the slot array with fresh offsets, and reclaims the free space in one contiguous block. Because only the slot array changes, no external pointer breaks. Explore this idea below. The query simulates what a slot directory might look like by tracking offset and length for each record on a hypothetical 8 KB page. Interactive SQL · Slot directory simulation Uncomment the second query to see how much space compaction would recover. In production engines, this reclaimed space becomes available for new inserts on the same page. PostgreSQL uses VACUUM to reclaim dead tuple space in both tables and indexes. In SQLite, tables are themselves B-trees, so page compaction happens automatically during normal operations; REINDEX only rebuilds indexes — it does not compact table pages. Key Takeaways A slotted page stores a slot array at the top and records packed from the bottom, with free space in the middle. Each record is referenced by a stable (page_id, slot_number) pair — not a raw byte offset — so records can be moved within the page without breaking indexes. Deletions are cheap (mark the slot); compaction is deferred and only touches the page itself. Almost every relational engine uses this layout or a close variant: PostgreSQL's heap pages, InnoDB's row format , SQLite's leaf pages for rowid tables all follow the same principle. ← Previous Fixed-length and variable-length records Next → Free space management

title: Free space management
text: Free space map (FSM) tracks how much room each page has left — the engine consults it to find the best-fit page for a new row without scanning. When a database deletes a row or updates a variable-length value to a shorter one, it does not immediately hand that space back to the operating system. Instead, the engine tracks the now-empty area internally so it can reuse it for future inserts. That bookkeeping is called free space management , and getting it right is the difference between a healthy database that stays compact and a bloated one that wastes disk and slows down every scan. Why Gaps Appear Pages (the fixed-size chunks your storage layer reads and writes) fill up row by row. Delete a row and you leave a hole: the bytes are free, but the page still occupies the same spot on disk. The same thing happens when you UPDATE a TEXT column from a long value to a short one — the leftover bytes sit unused inside the page. Before delete: ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ [row 1][row 2][row 3][row 4] free area │ └──────────────────────────────────────────┘ After deleting row 2: ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ [row 1][ ][row 3][row 4] free area │ └──────────────────────────────────────────┘ If the engine ignores these holes, it will keep appending new rows to the "free area" at the end. Eventually the page is full of both live rows and holes — the engine allocates a fresh page even though plenty of bytes are wasted inside existing ones. Over time, a heavily-updated table bloats to several times its logical size. How Engines Track Free Space Free Space Maps Most production engines maintain a free space map (FSM) — a compact index that records, for each page, roughly how many bytes are available. PostgreSQL, for example, keeps one FSM file per table. When an insert arrives, the engine consults the FSM to find a page with enough room, writes the row there, and updates the FSM entry. SQLite uses a similar structure called the freelist : a linked chain of pages that have been freed entirely (by dropping a table or deleting all rows from a page). Partial free space within a page is tracked in each page's header. Approach Granularity Used by Free space map (per-page byte count) Fraction of a page PostgreSQL Freelist (whole free pages) Whole page SQLite, InnoDB Page-level free-space percentage Rounded bucket SQL Server (PFS pages) Fragmentation and Compaction Even with a free space map, internal fragmentation builds up. Many small holes scattered across many pages means no single hole is big enough for a large row, even though the total free bytes would be sufficient. Engines handle this in two ways: In-page compaction (slot shifting): When a page is written, the engine slides live rows together and merges the holes at the end. This is cheap and happens automatically. Table-level vacuuming / reorganization: A background process rewrites pages, discarding deleted rows entirely and reclaiming space. PostgreSQL calls this VACUUM ; SQL Server calls it a rebuild or reorganize of the heap/index; SQLite exposes it as VACUUM . Note: SQLite's VACUUM rewrites the entire database file into a new, compact file and then replaces the old one. It reclaims space from dropped tables and deleted rows but requires enough disk space to hold two copies of the database at once during the operation. Observing Free Space in SQLite SQLite exposes page-level statistics through PRAGMA commands. Try the widget below to see how deletes affect the page count and how VACUUM reclaims space. Interactive SQL · Free space and VACUUM Run the SELECT above to see the baseline, then delete half the rows and re-run it to watch freelist_count grow. After VACUUM , the numbers reset to a compact state. The Cost of Ignoring Free Space Neglecting free space management has cascading effects: Bloated files — the database file grows without bound even as logical data stays flat. Slower full scans — a sequential scan reads every page, including ones that are mostly empty. Index bloat — B-tree index pages suffer the same fragmentation; a bloated index means more I/O per lookup. Stale statistics — many planners estimate row counts from page counts. A bloated table makes the optimizer think there is more data than there is, leading to bad plans. Scheduling regular VACUUM (or equivalent) runs — ideally during low-traffic windows — is one of the simplest operational habits that keeps a database healthy over months and years. ← Previous Slotted pages Next → Record identifiers

title: Record identifiers
text: A record identifier (RID) combines page and slot numbers — indexes store RIDs to locate rows without duplicating data. Every record stored in a database needs an address — a way for the engine to say "go fetch that specific row off disk." The previous page showed how slotted pages give each record a slot number. Now we zoom out: how does the rest of the database — indexes, the query executor, foreign key constraints — refer to a record? The answer is the record identifier , also called a RID , TID (tuple ID), or rowid depending on the system. What a Record Identifier Is A record identifier is the physical address of a row within the storage layer. In a slotted-page heap file it is almost always a two-part value: RID = (page_id, slot_number) page_id — which page in the file holds the record (often a file number + page number pair in multi-file engines). slot_number — which slot in that page's slot directory points to the record's actual byte offset. Because the slot directory is the indirection layer, the slot number stays stable even when the record is moved within the page during compaction. External references — indexes, in particular — store the RID, not the raw byte offset. Component Size (typical) What it means File / segment ID 1–2 bytes Which physical file on disk Page number 3–4 bytes Which page within the file Slot number 1–2 bytes Which slot in that page Total ~6 bytes The physical address of one row Note: PostgreSQL calls this a ctid (column tuple ID) and exposes it as a hidden system column you can query directly. SQLite uses a 64-bit integer rowid . InnoDB identifies rows by primary key internally — it is a clustered index, so there is no separate physical RID at all. RIDs in Indexes The link between logical data and physical rows runs through the index. A typical B-tree secondary index stores entries of the form: (indexed_value, RID) When you run a query like WHERE email = 'alice@example.com' , the engine: Walks the B-tree to find the leaf entry for that email value. Reads the RID stored there. Goes directly to (page_id, slot_number) in the heap file and fetches the full row. This two-step process is called a heap fetch or bookmark lookup . It is fast because the RID is an exact physical address — no scanning required. The widget below simulates the concept of an index that maps values to RIDs. Run it to see how the engine would resolve a lookup. Interactive SQL · Index-to-RID lookup Try changing the WHERE clause to look up a different email. Notice that the join on (page_id, slot_num) is the exact operation the storage engine performs when it follows a RID — your SQL just makes the two-step explicit. When RIDs Become Unstable RIDs are physical addresses, so anything that moves a row to a different page breaks them. This matters in two common situations: 1. VACUUM / page reorganization across pages. If the engine moves a row from one page to another (not just within the same page), it must leave a forwarding pointer in the old slot so that existing index entries still work. The next time an index uses the old RID, the engine follows the forwarding pointer to the new location — one extra I/O. After a while, stale forwarding pointers are cleaned up, and index entries are updated to the new RID. PostgreSQL calls this VACUUM ; SQL Server calls it a ghost record sweep. 2. Clustered (index-organized) tables. InnoDB (MySQL/MariaDB) and SQL Server's clustered indexes do not use a heap at all. Rows are stored inside the B-tree leaf pages, ordered by primary key. There is no separate heap RID; the primary key value itself is the row locator. Secondary indexes store the primary key value rather than a physical RID, which means: Secondary index lookups do two B-tree traversals (secondary index → primary key → row). Rows never have forwarding pointers — moving a row during a page split just updates the B-tree structure. Reveal: Why do secondary indexes in InnoDB store the primary key, not a physical RID? Because InnoDB rows live inside the primary-key B-tree, their physical location changes every time the B-tree splits or merges pages. If secondary indexes stored physical RIDs they would become stale constantly. Storing the primary key value instead means the address is always logically correct — the engine just does a second B-tree lookup to resolve it. This is why choosing a short, stable primary key matters so much in InnoDB: every secondary index entry carries a copy of it. Key Takeaways A record identifier (RID) is the physical address (page_id, slot_number) that pinpoints a row in a heap file. Secondary indexes store RIDs so they can jump directly to the right slot after a B-tree lookup. RIDs are stable within a page because of the slot-directory indirection layer, but become stale if a row migrates to a different page — the engine handles this with forwarding pointers. Clustered tables (InnoDB, SQL Server) skip the heap entirely: the primary key is the locator, and secondary indexes store the primary key value rather than a physical address. ← Previous Free space management Next → Updates and fragmentation

title: Updates and fragmentation
text: When an UPDATE makes a row too big for its original slot, the engine moves it to a new page and leaves a forwarding pointer — creating fragmentation in the old page. When a row is first inserted, the database writes it neatly into a page with room to spare. Updates, however, rarely arrive in the same tidy order — and over time they quietly degrade the physical layout of your data. Understanding why this happens, and what engines do about it, demystifies a whole class of performance problems you'll encounter in production. What Happens During an In-Place Update A page is a fixed-size block on disk (commonly 8 KB or 16 KB). Rows are packed into it sequentially. When you update a row that stays the same size — say, changing an integer column — the engine simply overwrites those bytes in place. Fast and clean. The trouble starts with variable-length columns (VARCHAR, TEXT, BLOB). If the new value is larger than the old one, the existing slot may not have room. Engines handle this in a few ways: Strategy What happens Overflow pointer Keep a short stub on the original page; write the larger value on another page and link them In-page compaction Shift other rows within the same page to make room (expensive, requires a page rewrite) Delete + re-insert Mark old slot dead, write a new row at the end of an available page Most systems use a combination. PostgreSQL, for example, uses TOAST (The Oversized Attribute Storage Technique) to store values that exceed ~2 KB off the main page. SQLite uses "overflow pages" linked from the B-tree leaf. Fragmentation: Two Flavors Repeated updates and deletes leave two kinds of waste behind. Internal fragmentation Dead row stubs and holes inside a page reduce the usable density. The page is still marked "in use," but a scan must skip over garbage to find live rows. The page cannot be released to the OS even though much of it is empty. Page 42 (8 KB) ┌───────────────────────────────────┐ │ row 1 (live) │ │ ████ dead slot (deleted row) │ │ row 3 (live) │ │ ████ dead slot (updated, old ver) │ │ row 5 (live) │ │ (free space) │ └───────────────────────────────────┘ External fragmentation Logically adjacent rows end up scattered across non-contiguous pages on disk. The engine has to chase page links or index entries across many physical locations, thrashing the buffer pool and increasing I/O. Note: On modern SSDs, random vs. sequential I/O matters less than it did on spinning disks — but external fragmentation still hurts because it increases the number of pages loaded into the buffer pool, wasting memory and cache capacity. How Engines Fight Back Vacuum / AUTOVACUUM (PostgreSQL) — A background process reclaims dead row versions left by MVCC (multi-version concurrency control). It marks pages as reclaimable and rewrites heavily-fragmented pages. Page reorganization (MySQL InnoDB) — OPTIMIZE TABLE rebuilds the table's clustered index in order, eliminating both types of fragmentation in one pass. The table is locked briefly (or, on newer versions, rebuilt online). SQLite's VACUUM command — Rewrites the entire database file into a fresh, compacted copy. Useful, but it requires exclusive access and roughly 2x free space on disk. You can see fragmentation effects live. The example below simulates repeated inserts and deletes, then counts how many "dead" rows remain occupying space alongside live ones. Interactive SQL · Fragmentation simulation Note: SQLite (used in this widget) keeps things simple — it does not implement MVCC, so deleted rows vanish immediately from queries. In PostgreSQL, those deleted versions stay on disk until VACUUM cleans them up, making fragmentation a much bigger operational concern. Practical Takeaways Monitor table bloat — Most engines expose statistics (e.g., pg_stat_user_tables in PostgreSQL, SHOW TABLE STATUS in MySQL) that estimate how much space is wasted. Schedule maintenance — Let AUTOVACUUM run; tune it for write-heavy tables; run ANALYZE after bulk loads. Avoid wide VARCHAR updates — If you frequently update a text column to much larger values, consider moving it to a separate table or using a fixed-size alternative where possible. Fragmentation is the physical side-effect of a logical world where data changes constantly. Every engine trades some write-time simplicity for read-time overhead; knowing which levers to pull puts you back in control. ← Previous Record identifiers Next → How rows are found on disk

title: How rows are found on disk
text: The full path from query to row data: index lookup yields RID → page/slot directory → byte offset → actual row bytes. When you run SELECT * FROM employees WHERE id = 712 , the database engine has to physically locate that one row on disk — possibly among millions. This chapter explains the machinery that makes that possible: how rows live inside pages, how the engine navigates from a query down to a specific byte offset, and what happens when there is no shortcut. Pages Are the Unit of I/O The engine never reads a single row directly from disk. It always reads a page — a fixed-size block, typically 4 KB to 16 KB depending on the engine. A page is the smallest unit the storage layer moves between disk and memory (the buffer pool). Inside each page, rows are stored as records — a sequence of fields, with a small header describing things like which columns are NULL, and the length of any variable-width data (like TEXT ). At the start of the page is a slot array : a compact list of offsets pointing to where each record begins within that page. ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐ Page 7 │ Page header (page id, free space, ...) │ │─────────────────────────────────────────│ │ Slot array: [slot 0 → offset 120, │ │ slot 1 → offset 185, │ │ slot 2 → offset 251, ...] │ │─────────────────────────────────────────│ │ ... free space ... │ │─────────────────────────────────────────│ │ Record 2 (row 3 of the table) │ ← offset 251 │ Record 1 (row 2 of the table) │ ← offset 185 │ Record 0 (row 1 of the table) │ ← offset 120 └─────────────────────────────────────────┘ A row's physical address is therefore a pair: (page_number, slot_number) . In PostgreSQL this is called a ctid . In other engines the concept is the same even if it goes by a different name. Finding a Row: Two Paths Path 1 — Table Scan (No Index) Without an index, the engine has no shortcut. It reads every page of the table from the first to the last, checks each record against the WHERE predicate, and returns any that match. This is called a sequential scan or full table scan . For large tables a sequential scan is expensive — but it is always correct . It is the fallback that guarantees any row can be found. Path 2 — Index Lookup An index is a separate on-disk structure (usually a B-tree) that maps a column's values to physical row addresses. When the engine uses an index, the process has two steps: Traverse the index to find the physical address (page, slot) for the matching key. Fetch the page that contains that slot, then read the record. Step 2 is called a heap fetch (or in PostgreSQL, a "heap access"). It is a random I/O — the index tells you exactly which page to load, so you load only that page rather than all of them. Access method Pages read (example: 1 row in 1 M-row table) When the engine chooses it Sequential scan All pages (~8,000 for 8 KB pages, 1 M rows) No usable index, or query returns many rows Index + heap fetch 2–4 pages (index levels + 1 data page) Selective filter with a matching index Index-only scan 1–3 pages (index only, no heap) All needed columns are in the index Note: An index-only scan skips the heap fetch entirely — it reads the answer directly out of the index leaf. This only works when every column the query needs is stored in the index itself. PostgreSQL additionally checks a visibility map to confirm the row's commit status before skipping the heap. Watching It Happen in SQLite SQLite stores each table as a B-tree of rows keyed by the rowid (an implicit 64-bit integer). A lookup by rowid is a B-tree traversal directly into the table — no separate heap fetch needed. A lookup on any other column without an index is a full scan. The query below seeds a small products table and looks up a row. Try running EXPLAIN variants to see SQLite describe the access path it chose. Interactive SQL · Heap scan vs. index lookup Uncomment and run the sku queries one at a time. Before the index exists, SQLite reports SCAN products — every page, every row. After CREATE INDEX , it reports SEARCH products USING INDEX idx_sku — it jumps straight to the matching leaf. Deleted Rows and Free Space When a row is deleted, the engine marks its slot as free but does not immediately reclaim the space on disk. The page now has a hole . Over time, pages accumulate holes and the engine can reuse that space for new inserts, a process called compaction or (in PostgreSQL) VACUUM . This means a row's physical address can become stale. Indexes that store physical addresses must handle this — PostgreSQL's B-tree indexes store ctid values and tolerate the need to re-check the heap. SQLite's B-tree tables store the data directly in the tree, so a deleted key simply disappears from the tree with no dangling pointer. Understanding this chain — from SQL predicate, to index lookup, to page number, to slot, to bytes on disk — is what separates guessing about performance from reasoning about it. ← Previous Updates and fragmentation Next → Chapter 9 Summary: What We Learned

title: Chapter 9 Summary: What We Learned
text: Page Topic Takeaway in One Sentence 081 Why databases use pages Pages (usually 4–16 KB) are the unit of I/O — reading/writing whole pages amortizes disk seek costs and aligns with OS and hardware. 082 Heap files and page layouts A heap file is an unordered collection of pages; rows are stored wherever there is space, identified by page + slot. 083 Row stores and column stores Row stores optimize for transactional workloads (read/write full rows); column stores optimize for analytics (scan few columns). 084 Fixed-length and variable-length records Fixed-length records are predictable and fast; variable-length records (TEXT, BLOB) need extra bookkeeping for offsets. 085 Slotted pages A slotted page has a header with slot array pointing to variable-length records — records can move within the page without changing their ID. 086 Free space management The engine tracks free space per page so INSERTs know where to go; fragmented free space wastes I/O. 087 Record identifiers A record ID (page number + slot number) gives O(1) access to any row — the basis of secondary index lookups. 088 Updates and fragmentation In-place updates can overflow a page; some engines use forwarding pointers or MVCC to avoid moving referenced rows. 089 How rows are found on disk Without an index: sequential scan. With a B-tree index: traverse root to leaf, read record ID, fetch the page and slot. Reveal: How do these ideas connect to the next chapter? Chapter 9 explained the physical layout of data — pages, slotted records, heap files. But how does the engine efficiently find a specific row among millions of pages? Chapter 10 answers that by exploring the internal data structures that power every database: B-trees for fast lookups, the Write-Ahead Log for durability, and the buffer pool for caching. You will understand write amplification, read amplification, and the background processes that keep a database healthy without you ever noticing. ← Previous How rows are found on disk Next → B-tree structure in detail

title: B-tree structure in detail
chapter: Chapter 10. Trees, Logs, and Internal Structures
text: Internal pages store keys and child pointers guiding search downward. Leaf pages store sorted key-rowID pairs, linked for efficient range scans. The tree stays balanced through splits. B-trees are the dominant index structure in relational databases — PostgreSQL, MySQL/InnoDB, SQLite, Oracle, and SQL Server all use them as the default for primary keys and secondary indexes. Understanding their shape explains why indexed lookups are fast, why range scans are cheap, and why unordered bulk inserts can stress a database. The Shape of a B-tree A B-tree organizes data into a balanced hierarchy of pages (also called nodes). Every path from the root to any leaf is exactly the same number of hops — that is what "balanced" guarantees. There are three kinds of nodes: [ 30 | 60 ] ← root (internal node) / | \ [10|20] [40|50] [70|80] ← internal nodes / | \ ... [1-9][11-19][21-29] ... ← leaf nodes (hold actual rows / row pointers) Node type What it stores Purpose Root Keys + child pointers Entry point; always kept in memory Internal Keys + child pointers Routing — no row data Leaf Keys + row data (or row pointers) Where actual lookups land The order (or branching factor ) of a B-tree is the maximum number of children per internal node — typically 100–500 in practice, because each node maps to one disk page (~8 KB) and keys are small. With a branching factor of 200 and 3 levels, a tree can index 200³ = 8 million rows while reading only 3 pages to find any one of them. Note: The variant used by most databases is actually a B+ tree : internal nodes store only keys (no row data), and all row data lives in the leaf nodes. Leaf nodes are also linked together in a doubly-linked list, making range scans very efficient. Finding a Row: The Search Algorithm A point lookup walks the tree top-down. At each internal node the engine binary-searches the keys to find the right child pointer, loads that child page, and repeats until it reaches a leaf. Search for key = 45: Root [30 | 60] → 30 < 45 < 60, follow middle child Internal [40|50] → 40 < 45 < 50, follow middle child Leaf [41|43|45] → found at slot 2 Cost: O(log_B n) page reads, where B is the branching factor and n is the number of rows. For a million-row table with B = 200, that is about log₂₀₀(1 000 000) ≈ 2.9 — so at most 3 page reads, regardless of table size. Range scans are equally efficient: find the starting leaf, then follow the leaf-level linked list forward until the range is exhausted — no need to climb back up the tree. Insertions and Page Splits Inserting a new key always targets a leaf. If the leaf has room, the key is inserted in sorted order and the page is written back. If the leaf is full , a page split occurs: A new sibling page is allocated. The keys are divided roughly 50/50 between the old and new page. The median key is promoted to the parent as a new separator. If the parent is also full, it splits too — the process can cascade all the way to the root. Leaf [10|20|30|40] is full. Insert 25: → Split into [10|20] and [25|30|40] → Promote 25 to parent Splits are the main reason heavily write-loaded tables periodically need index maintenance ( REINDEX , REBUILD , or the equivalent): many splits over time can leave pages that are only half-full, wasting space and adding extra I/O per scan. Note: Deletions can cause the mirror operation — an underflow where a page falls below a minimum fill threshold. Engines handle this by either merging two half-empty siblings or redistributing keys between them, then removing the separator from the parent. Exploring B-tree Behavior with an Index The best way to see a B-tree in action is to create an index and watch the query planner use it. The widget below sets up a small orders table with and without an index so you can compare lookup strategies. Interactive SQL · Index lookup vs. full scan Run the EXPLAIN above. When you see SEARCH orders USING INDEX idx_orders_customer , the engine is walking a B-tree — reading the root, descending internal nodes, and landing on a leaf — instead of scanning all 500 rows. Change the filter to a range ( customer_id BETWEEN 40 AND 50 ) and re-run; the same index handles it by scanning a contiguous leaf range. Fill Factor and Real-World Tuning Production engines expose a fill factor setting (PostgreSQL: fillfactor , SQL Server: FILL FACTOR ). A fill factor of 80% tells the engine to leave 20% of each leaf page empty at build time. This reserves space for future inserts on already-existing pages, reducing splits on write-heavy tables at the cost of a slightly larger index. For read-mostly tables, 100% packs data tighter and reduces I/O per scan. The key intuitions to carry forward: Height stays tiny — branching factor keeps trees 2–4 levels deep for virtually any realistic dataset. Leaves are linked — range scans never backtrack through internal nodes. Splits propagate upward — a write-heavy workload with many inserts in key order (auto-increment IDs) always appends to the rightmost leaf and rarely splits, while random-key inserts (UUIDs) cause frequent splits throughout the tree. ← Previous How rows are found on disk Next → Splits, merges, and rebalancing

title: Splits, merges, and rebalancing
chapter: Chapter 10. Trees, Logs, and Internal Structures
text: When a B-tree page fills up, it splits into two half-full pages. The parent gets a new separator key. This cascades upward — keeping the tree balanced automatically. B-tree indexes look elegant in diagrams, but the real database takes a constant beating of inserts, updates, and deletes. Every write can break the tree's balance. The engine fixes this automatically through splits , merges , and rebalancing — understanding them explains why index performance holds up over time, and why it sometimes quietly degrades. The Problem: Pages Fill Up (and Empty Out) A B-tree stores keys in fixed-size pages. Each page holds some maximum number of keys. Two things go wrong over time: Overflow: An insert targets a page that is already full. Underflow: A delete leaves a page nearly empty, wasting I/O and space. The B-tree invariant says every non-root node must be at least half full. Splits handle overflow; merges handle underflow; rebalancing is the lighter-weight middle path when a full split or merge is not strictly necessary. Splits: When a Page Is Full When an insert arrives at a leaf page that has no room, the engine performs a page split : Allocate a new sibling page. Move roughly half the keys from the full page into the new page. Promote the middle key up to the parent as a separator. Update the parent's child pointers to include the new page. Before split (page full, inserting 35): Parent: [ 20 | 50 ] / | \ ... [22|28|33|38] ... ← leaf full After split: Parent: [ 20 | 33 | 50 ] ← 33 promoted / | | \ ... [22|28] [35|38] ... The promotion can cascade: if the parent is also full, it splits too, pushing a key further up. In the worst case this chain reaches the root, which splits into two children and a new root is created — the only moment the tree grows taller. Note: Splits require writing at least two pages (the original and the new sibling) plus the parent. For safety, the engine journals these writes before applying them so a crash mid-split does not corrupt the tree. That is one reason WAL and B-trees are inseparable in practice. Merges: When a Page Is Too Empty When a delete leaves a page below the minimum fill threshold (usually 50%), the engine looks at the adjacent sibling: If the sibling has extra keys to spare , borrow one key from the sibling and adjust the separator in the parent. This is the "rebalance" or "rotate" case — cheaper than a full merge. If the sibling is also near-minimum , merging is the only option: pull all keys from one sibling into the other, and delete the now-empty page plus its separator from the parent. Deleting a separator from the parent can trigger another underflow, so merges can also cascade upward — shrinking the tree's height when the root is left with a single child. Before merge (leaf [7] underflows after delete): Parent: [ 10 ] / \ [7] [12|15] After merge: Parent: (root dissolves, tree shrinks) [7|12|15] ← single root leaf Rebalancing Without a Split or Merge A full split or merge touches at least three pages and always modifies the parent. Many engines try a cheaper fix first: redistribution (also called rotation). If a sibling page has slack, the engine shuffles one or two keys across the boundary and updates only the separator in the parent — no new pages allocated, no pages freed. Situation Engine action Pages written Page full, sibling has slack Redistribute (rotate) 2 leaves + parent Page full, sibling also full Split New page + original + parent Page underflows, sibling has slack Redistribute (rotate) 2 leaves + parent Page underflows, sibling also minimal Merge 1 page freed + parent Production engines prefer redistribution when possible because it keeps pages fuller on average, reducing tree height and improving cache hit rates. Note — PostgreSQL FILLFACTOR: You can reduce split frequency by reserving spare space inside each page during initial builds and index rebuilds. PostgreSQL's FILLFACTOR setting (default 100 for tables, 90 for B-tree indexes) leaves a percentage of each page free so that future inserts and updates have room without triggering immediate splits. For indexes with heavy random inserts, lowering the fill factor can dramatically reduce write amplification. Observing the Effect in SQLite You cannot watch splits happen directly, but you can see their footprint. The widget below builds a small table, then compares page usage before and after a bulk insert that forces the index to grow. Try adding more rows to push more splits. Interactive SQL · Index page growth As you add rows, page_count climbs in jumps — each jump corresponds to one or more split events allocating new pages. Why This Matters for Performance Splits and merges are the engine's way of staying honest, but they are not free: Write amplification: One logical insert can touch many pages (the target leaf, a new sibling, and every ancestor up to the root in a worst-case cascade). Page fragmentation: After many random inserts and deletes, pages may be only 60–70% full on average. A full sequential scan of the index reads more pages than a freshly built one would. Index rebuilds: REINDEX (SQLite/PostgreSQL) or ALTER INDEX ... REBUILD (SQL Server) rewrite the B-tree from scratch, packing pages to near 100% fill. This is the blunt-but-effective cure for fragmentation after heavy churn. Understanding splits and merges also sets up the next topic: LSM-trees (Log-Structured Merge-trees) were invented specifically to avoid the high random-write cost of in-place B-tree splits by batching all writes into sequential appends. ← Previous B-tree structure in detail Next → Write-ahead logging basics

title: Write-ahead logging basics
chapter: Chapter 10. Trees, Logs, and Internal Structures
text: Write-ahead logging: every change hits the sequential WAL first, guaranteeing that committed transactions survive even if data pages are lost. Before a database can promise that a committed transaction survives a crash, it needs to record what it intended to do before touching the data files on disk. That mechanism is write-ahead logging (WAL) — the foundation of durability in PostgreSQL, SQLite, MySQL/InnoDB, Oracle, SQL Server, and virtually every other production engine. The Core Idea: Write the Log First The "write-ahead" rule is simple: Before any change is written to the main data file, a record of that change must be written to the log. The log (also called the redo log, transaction log, or WAL file) is an append-only sequence of log records . Each record captures which page was affected, the modification itself (logical or physical), and a log sequence number (LSN) that orders records globally. PostgreSQL WAL records typically contain the changes made rather than full before-images; full-page images are a special case written only for the first modification to a page after a checkpoint. Note — PostgreSQL WAL Levels: PostgreSQL offers three WAL levels: minimal (no replication support, skips enough info to save WAL volume during bulk loads), replica (default; enough for streaming replication and archive recovery), and logical (adds logical decoding info for change-data-capture).
