Use minimal only when you do not need PITR or replication. WAL file (append-only, on disk) ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────── LSN 100 | txn 42 | BEGIN LSN 101 | txn 42 | UPDATE accounts SET balance=900 WHERE id=1 (old=1000) LSN 102 | txn 42 | UPDATE accounts SET balance=1100 WHERE id=2 (old=1000) LSN 103 | txn 42 | COMMIT ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────── ↑ flushed to disk before the COMMIT returns to the client When you issue COMMIT , the engine flushes log records through the COMMIT entry to durable storage ( fsync ). Only after that flush succeeds does it tell your application "committed." The data pages in the heap file may still be dirty in memory — that is fine, because the log has everything needed to replay the change. Why This Survives a Crash Suppose the server loses power one second after your COMMIT returns. The data page was still in the buffer pool and never reached disk. On restart, the database runs crash recovery : Scan the log from the last checkpoint forward. Redo every change that has a COMMIT record in the log — this replays the change into the data file. Undo any changes whose transaction never reached a COMMIT (they were in-flight at crash time). After recovery, the data files are exactly as if the crash never happened. Committed data is not lost; uncommitted data is rolled back cleanly. Phase What it does Reason Redo Re-applies committed log records to data pages Data page may not have been flushed Undo Reverses log records for uncommitted transactions Partial writes must not be visible Checkpoints: Keeping Recovery Fast If recovery always replayed the log from the very beginning, startup after a crash could take hours on a busy system. Checkpoints bound that cost. At a checkpoint the engine: Flushes all dirty data pages to disk. Writes a checkpoint record into the log noting the LSN at which this happened. On recovery, the engine only needs to start from the most recent checkpoint LSN — everything before it is already safe on disk. Most engines take checkpoints automatically every few seconds or every N megabytes of log written. ...older log records (already on disk in data files)... ↑ [CHECKPOINT at LSN 500] ↓ LSN 501 → LSN 502 → ... → LSN 610 [COMMIT txn 77] ↑ only this window needs redo Note: A very long-running transaction delays how far back the log can be truncated, because the undo information for that transaction must be retained. This is why engines warn about or limit idle-in-transaction sessions. Seeing WAL Behavior in SQLite SQLite has two storage modes: the classic rollback journal and WAL mode . In WAL mode, writes go to a separate .wal file instead of modifying the database in place. Readers see a consistent snapshot of the main file while a writer appends to the WAL concurrently — better read/write parallelism with no extra complexity for application code. Try the widget below to see transactions commit in WAL mode. Interactive SQL · WAL mode and transactions Try changing COMMIT to ROLLBACK and re-run — the two inserts inside the transaction will disappear, exactly as crash-recovery undo would remove them. The Trade-Off: Sequential Writes Win One reason WAL is so effective is that appending to a log is sequential I/O , which is far cheaper than random writes to scattered data pages. A single sequential fsync of a small log buffer can make dozens of random page updates durable in one shot. This is the fundamental performance bargain that makes WAL both safe and fast. ← Previous Splits, merges, and rebalancing Next → Checkpoints

title: Checkpoints
chapter: Chapter 10. Trees, Logs, and Internal Structures
text: Checkpoints flush dirty pages to disk and write a record into the WAL — after a crash, recovery only replays from the last checkpoint forward. Every write-ahead log (WAL) tells the story of what changed — but that story keeps growing. On crash recovery, the engine must replay log entries to reconstruct a consistent state. A checkpoint is how the engine periodically says "everything before this point is already on disk; start replay here." Without checkpoints, recovery time grows unbounded and logs never shrink. The Problem Checkpoints Solve Before any page is modified in the buffer pool, the engine writes a log record describing the change. On crash, it replays those records to reconstruct a consistent state. But the buffer pool holds many dirty pages — modified in RAM but not yet flushed to the data files. As that gap grows, so does recovery time. A checkpoint compresses the gap by forcing dirty pages to disk , then writing a checkpoint record into the log that notes the LSN (Log Sequence Number) at that moment. After a crash, the engine scans the log only from the most recent checkpoint forward. WAL (append-only) ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────► LSN 1000 LSN 4200 LSN 6800 │ │ │ [CKPT] [CKPT] crash here ↑ ↑ pages flushed pages flushed up to LSN 1000 up to LSN 4200 Recovery replays only: LSN 4200 → LSN 6800 Without the checkpoint at LSN 4200, recovery would have to replay from LSN 1000 — or earlier. What Happens During a Checkpoint A checkpoint is not instantaneous. In most engines it proceeds in phases: Phase What happens Begin Record the checkpoint-start LSN; capture the list of active transactions Flush dirty pages Write modified buffer-pool pages to the data files on disk Write checkpoint record Append a checkpoint-end record to the WAL containing the begin LSN and active-transaction list Recycle old WAL segments (optional) WAL segments older than the checkpoint start LSN can be archived, recycled, or reused — PostgreSQL renames old segments rather than creating new ones to reduce filesystem overhead The checkpoint record is the recovery's anchor. It tells the engine: "any transaction that committed before my begin LSN is already reflected in the data files." Note: During a fuzzy (or online ) checkpoint — the mode used by PostgreSQL and most modern engines — dirty pages are flushed gradually in the background without blocking ongoing writes. The engine tolerates some newly-dirtied pages appearing during the flush; the active-transaction list captured at checkpoint-begin handles them during recovery. Checkpoint Frequency: The Trade-off How often should the engine checkpoint? Checkpoint interval Recovery time Write amplification Very frequent Short (little replay needed) High (more flushes, more I/O) Infrequent Long (more log to replay) Low (pages batched efficiently) Most engines let you tune this. PostgreSQL exposes several settings: checkpoint_timeout (max seconds between checkpoints; default 5 min), max_wal_size (triggers a checkpoint when the WAL reaches this size; default 1 GB), min_wal_size (floor for WAL retention; old segments below this threshold are recycled rather than removed), and checkpoint_completion_target (fraction of the timeout interval to spread the flush over; default 0.9). SQLite checkpoints its WAL when it hits 1,000 pages by default, and also exposes a manual PRAGMA wal_checkpoint . The right setting depends on your workload. OLTP systems with many small writes benefit from frequent checkpoints to keep recovery fast. Bulk-load jobs often disable or defer checkpoints temporarily for throughput, then checkpoint once at the end. Observing Checkpoint State The widget below simulates what a checkpoint must track: which buffer-pool pages are dirty (must be flushed) versus already clean. It also shows a checkpoint history log. Try modifying the queries to explore both tables. Interactive SQL · Checkpoint state simulation Key Takeaways A checkpoint flushes dirty pages and plants a WAL anchor — crash recovery replays only from that point forward. Modern engines use fuzzy checkpoints to flush pages gradually in the background without stalling writes. Checkpoint frequency trades recovery speed against write I/O: checkpoint more often for fast recovery, less often for higher throughput. In SQLite WAL mode, PRAGMA wal_checkpoint exposes the mechanism directly — a good starting point before diving into PostgreSQL's max_wal_size or InnoDB's innodb_io_capacity tuning. ← Previous Write-ahead logging basics Next → Buffer pools

title: Buffer pools
chapter: Chapter 10. Trees, Logs, and Internal Structures
text: The buffer pool caches pages in RAM: hash lookup finds frames, active queries pin them, and LRU replacement evicts the least recently used. Every database page you read about in the previous sections — heap pages, B-tree nodes, WAL records — lives on disk. But disk is slow: even a fast NVMe drive is thousands of times slower than RAM. The buffer pool is the database engine's main memory cache. It sits between your queries and the disk, and almost every read or write passes through it. What a Buffer Pool Is The buffer pool is a fixed-size region of RAM that the database manages itself, divided into frames. Each frame holds exactly one page (typically 8 KB or 16 KB, matching the on-disk page size). When the engine needs a page, it checks the buffer pool first: Cache hit — the page is already in a frame. Return it immediately. Cache miss — the page is not in memory. Find a free frame (or evict an existing one), read the page from disk into that frame, then return it. A small hash table maps (file_id, page_number) → frame index, so lookups are O(1). Disk Buffer Pool (RAM) ┌──────────┐ ┌────────┬────────┬────────┬────────┐ │ page 1 │◄──── miss ────│ frame0 │ frame1 │ frame2 │ frame3 │ │ page 2 │ │ page 7 │ page 2 │ page 9 │ free │ │ ... │ └────────┴────────┴────────┴────────┘ │ page N │ ▲ ▲ └──────────┘ query A query B Because most real workloads access a small "hot" set of pages repeatedly, a modest buffer pool can absorb the vast majority of disk I/O. PostgreSQL defaults to 128 MB ( shared_buffers ); production systems routinely allocate tens of gigabytes. Dirty Pages and the Write Path When a query modifies a page (an INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE), the engine changes the in-memory copy and marks that frame dirty . The dirty page is not written to disk immediately — this is intentional. Batching writes is far more efficient than flushing every change one at a time. Dirty pages are eventually written to disk by a background process (PostgreSQL calls it the bgwriter ; InnoDB has its own page cleaner ). The write-ahead log (WAL) you read about earlier ensures that even if the server crashes before a dirty page is flushed, the change can be replayed from the log. That is the contract: the log is durable, the buffer pool is not . Note: A page must never be evicted from the buffer pool while a transaction that modified it has not yet written its log record to disk. This ordering rule — "log before evict" — is a cornerstone of crash recovery. Eviction and Replacement Policy When the buffer pool is full and a new page must be loaded, the engine must choose a victim frame to evict. The classic policy is LRU (Least Recently Used) : evict the page that was accessed least recently. LRU works well for random access patterns, but it can backfire during a sequential scan. If a query scans a huge table, every page gets loaded exactly once, flooding the pool and evicting hot pages that other queries need. Most engines handle this with clock sweep (PostgreSQL) or a midpoint insertion variant of LRU (InnoDB), which gives new pages a lower priority until they are accessed a second time. Policy Good for Weakness LRU Mixed OLTP workloads Sequential scans flush hot data Clock sweep General use, low overhead Approximate, not strictly LRU LRU-K (k=2) Mixed + analytical More bookkeeping 2Q / midpoint Scan-heavy workloads Tuning required Seeing Buffer Pool Pressure in Practice Buffer pool behavior is mostly invisible — until the pool is too small. When hit rates drop, queries that used to be fast suddenly wait on disk I/O. Most databases expose cache statistics you can query directly. Try the interactive example below. Run it on any real PostgreSQL instance: Interactive SQL · Buffer hit rate simulation A healthy OLTP system typically runs at 95–99% hit rate. Try adding a WHERE heap_blks_read > 100 clause to find tables that are under the most disk I/O pressure — these are candidates for larger buffer pool allocations or index tuning. ← Previous Checkpoints Next → Dirty pages and flushing

title: Dirty pages and flushing
chapter: Chapter 10. Trees, Logs, and Internal Structures
text: Dirty pages in the buffer pool are modified in RAM but not yet on disk — the background writer periodically flushes them, coordinated with WAL guarantees. Every database engine keeps a pool of memory pages — the buffer pool — so it can serve reads from RAM instead of disk. When a transaction modifies data, it changes the in-memory copy of a page first. That page is now out of sync with what is on disk. A page in that state is called a dirty page . Getting it back to disk is called flushing . Understanding this cycle is the key to understanding write performance, crash recovery, and checkpoint mechanics. What Makes a Page "Dirty" Think of the buffer pool as a shared whiteboard. Each slot on the whiteboard holds one page of data (commonly 8 KB or 16 KB). When a query updates a row, the engine: Loads the page from disk into a buffer pool slot (if it isn't already there). Modifies the in-memory copy. Marks the slot with a dirty bit — a single flag saying "this page differs from what's on disk." The page stays dirty in RAM, potentially for seconds or minutes, while other transactions can also read and modify it. This is intentional: writing every change immediately to disk would serialize all writes and kill throughput. Buffer Pool (in RAM) ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ Slot 1 │ page 47 │ dirty=0 │ (matches disk) │ │ Slot 2 │ page 12 │ dirty=1 │ ← modified in txn │ │ Slot 3 │ page 88 │ dirty=1 │ ← modified in txn │ │ Slot 4 │ page 5 │ dirty=0 │ (matches disk) │ └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ ↕ ↕ (fsync on flush) Disk Disk The WAL (covered in the previous section) is what makes it safe to leave dirty pages in RAM: even if power fails, the log already contains enough information to replay the change. The dirty page is merely a performance optimization — it defers the more expensive random-write I/O. When Dirty Pages Get Flushed Flushing is triggered by several conditions: Trigger Why it happens Checkpoint Engine periodically forces all dirty pages to disk to bound recovery time Buffer pool pressure A slot is needed for a new page; if the evicted slot is dirty, it must be flushed first Explicit CHECKPOINT DBA or replication forces a sync point Shutdown Clean shutdown flushes everything fsync at COMMIT (SQLite default) Full sync on every commit — safe but slow Checkpoints are the dominant path on a busy system. At checkpoint time, the engine scans the buffer pool for all dirty pages and writes them to the data file, then records a checkpoint LSN in the WAL. Any log records before that LSN are now redundant for recovery purposes. Note: Flushing a dirty page requires an fsync call — a system call that tells the OS to push data all the way through its own write cache to physical storage. Without fsync , a crash can lose changes even after the OS acknowledged the write. This is why fsync=off is a dangerous configuration shortcut. The Write Path, Step by Step Here is the full lifecycle of a dirty page for a simple UPDATE : 1. Transaction calls UPDATE 2. Engine pins the target page in the buffer pool 3. WAL record written: old value + new value + LSN ← log first! 4. In-memory page modified; dirty bit set 5. Transaction COMMITs → WAL flushed/fsynced to disk 6. Client receives "OK" ... time passes ... 7. Checkpoint fires → dirty pages written to data file 8. Checkpoint LSN written to WAL 9. Old WAL segments before this LSN can be recycled Step 3 before step 4 is the write-ahead rule. Steps 5 and 7 are separate fsync calls — one to protect durability of the commit, one to advance the checkpoint so recovery is fast. Watching the Pattern with SQLite PostgreSQL's CHECKPOINT command forces dirty pages to be flushed to disk. The widget below creates a small table and inserts some rows (dirtying pages). Run CHECKPOINT to flush WAL frames to the main data files. Interactive SQL · Dirty pages and checkpointing Try adding more INSERT statements before the checkpoint command and observe the effect. In PostgreSQL, CHECKPOINT forces all dirty buffers to be written to disk immediately. The Cost of Too Many (or Too Few) Dirty Pages Holding many dirty pages in RAM is good for write throughput — batching reduces I/O. But there are limits: Too many dirty pages means a checkpoint takes a long time and recovery after a crash takes longer. Too few dirty pages (flushing too eagerly) means more random I/O and lower write throughput. Production engines expose tuning knobs for this trade-off. PostgreSQL has checkpoint_completion_target (spreads flushing over time) and bgwriter_lru_maxpages (controls background flush rate). SQLite's cache_size pragma controls how many pages the buffer pool holds before eviction pressure forces dirty pages out. Getting these right is a significant part of database performance tuning. ← Previous Buffer pools Next → Crash recovery overview

title: Crash recovery overview
chapter: Chapter 10. Trees, Logs, and Internal Structures
text: ARIES-style crash recovery in five steps (InnoDB, SQL Server, DB2). PostgreSQL skips the UNDO phase — instead it relies on REDO-only WAL replay and MVCC visibility rules to hide uncommitted changes. When a database crashes — power cut, OS panic, process killed — it must restart and end up in a consistent state . That means committed transactions are fully visible, and any transaction that never finished is completely gone. Getting this right is called crash recovery , and it is one of the most carefully engineered parts of any serious database engine. The Problem: Writes Are Not Atomic by Default A single INSERT or UPDATE may touch multiple disk pages: the row itself, one or more index entries, maybe a free-space map. Those writes land at different times. If power fails in the middle, the database wakes up with half-finished work spread across the disk. Consider a bank transfer: BEGIN; UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance - 100 WHERE id = 1; UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance + 100 WHERE id = 2; COMMIT; If the engine crashes after the first UPDATE but before COMMIT , account 1 has lost $100 and account 2 has gained nothing. Crash recovery must undo that partial write. Note: Even a COMMIT acknowledgment can arrive while some dirty pages are still in the OS buffer cache, not yet on disk. Recovery must handle that too. The Write-Ahead Log (WAL) The standard solution is the write-ahead log (WAL). Before any page is modified on disk, a description of that change is appended to a sequential log file. "Write-ahead" means the log entry is flushed to durable storage before the corresponding data page is written. The log contains records like: LSN Transaction Operation Table Old value New value 101 T42 UPDATE accounts balance=500 balance=400 102 T42 UPDATE accounts balance=300 balance=400 103 T42 COMMIT — — — 104 T55 INSERT orders — (row data) LSN (Log Sequence Number) is a monotonically increasing ID that orders every log record. Two recovery rules follow directly from this structure: REDO : If a committed transaction's changes did not make it to the data pages before the crash, replay those log records to put them back. All recovery systems do this. UNDO : If an uncommitted transaction left partial changes on the data pages, reverse its log records to erase the partial work. Used by ARIES engines (InnoDB, SQL Server, DB2). PostgreSQL handles this differently — see below. Checkpoints Replaying the entire log on every restart would be too slow. Engines periodically write a checkpoint — a log record that says "at this point, all changes up to LSN X are safely on disk." Recovery only needs to process records after the last checkpoint, keeping restart time bounded regardless of how long the database has been running. PostgreSQL Recovery: REDO-Only (Not ARIES) PostgreSQL does not implement ARIES. There is no Analysis phase, and — critically — there is no backward UNDO pass. PostgreSQL’s recovery works differently: Read the checkpoint from pg_control to find the last known-safe LSN. REDO-only replay — replay WAL forward from the checkpoint LSN to the end of the log, reapplying every record. Full-page images (FPI) embedded in WAL records provide a known starting point for each page, so REDO can begin from the checkpoint without needing an earlier consistent snapshot. Commit log (clog / pg_xact) — after REDO finishes, the commit log determines which transactions actually committed. Uncommitted transactions are not rolled back via undo records; instead, their row versions simply never become visible to any snapshot. MVCC visibility — every row version carries an xmin (creating transaction) and xmax (deleting transaction). When an uncommitted transaction’s xmin never appears in the commit log, that row version is invisible to all queries. No explicit backward unwinding of changes is performed. This design simplifies the recovery code significantly and avoids the complexity of maintaining undo logs during normal operation. The cost is that WAL records must be self-contained enough to replay forward without knowing the prior state of every page — which is where full-page images come in. PostgreSQL note — Full-page images and pg_control: After a checkpoint, the first modification to each data page writes a full copy of that page into the WAL (an FPI). This ensures REDO always has a clean starting image for every page. pg_control , located at a fixed offset in the PGDATA directory, stores the checkpoint LSN, the WAL segment to start replay from, and critical timeline information. If pg_control is lost or corrupted, PostgreSQL cannot recover — it has no way to know where to begin scanning the WAL. This is why pg_backup_start() / pg_backup_stop() and file-system-level snapshots must preserve pg_control . ARIES: The Algorithm Some Other Engines Use InnoDB (MySQL), SQL Server, and DB2 implement a recovery protocol called ARIES (Algorithm for Recovery and Isolation Exploiting Semantics), developed at IBM in the 1990s. ARIES has three phases: Analysis — scan the log forward from the last checkpoint to figure out which transactions were active at crash time and which pages were dirty (modified but not flushed). REDO — replay the log forward from the earliest relevant LSN, reapplying every update, even for transactions that will later be rolled back. This restores the database to its exact state at the moment of the crash. UNDO — walk the log backward and undo the changes of every transaction that never committed. The counter-intuitive step is REDO-before-UNDO: ARIES first reconstructs the crash state (including uncommitted garbage), then cleanly removes it. This approach handles edge cases such as transactions that were in the middle of rolling back when the crash occurred. Note: SQLite uses a simpler journal-based mechanism (rollback journal or WAL mode) that achieves the same safety guarantees with less complexity, trading some throughput for simplicity. The core ideas — log before you write, track what committed — are the same. Try It: Simulating a Transaction Log The widget below models a tiny transaction log table. The query identifies which transactions committed and which were left incomplete — exactly what the Analysis phase of ARIES does (in ARIES engines like InnoDB or SQL Server). Try adding a COMMIT row for T55 and re-run to see it change status. Interactive SQL · Transaction log analysis Key Takeaways Concept What it does Write-ahead log (WAL) Records every change before touching data pages; enables both redo and undo LSN Orders log records so recovery knows what happened and in what sequence Checkpoint Limits how far back recovery must scan; keeps restart time practical REDO phase Replays the log to reconstruct the exact state at crash time UNDO phase (ARIES) Reverses uncommitted transaction changes (InnoDB, SQL Server, DB2); PostgreSQL uses MVCC visibility instead Crash recovery is what lets a database make the "durable" and "atomic" promises in ACID. The WAL is the mechanism; the recovery strategy — whether ARIES-style REDO+UNDO or PostgreSQL's REDO-only with MVCC visibility — is how each engine uses it correctly even when the crash happens at the worst possible moment. ← Previous Dirty pages and flushing Next → Space amplification

title: Space amplification
chapter: Chapter 10. Trees, Logs, and Internal Structures
text: Space amplification: the ratio of what the database actually consumes on disk vs. your logical data size — dead rows, fragmentation, and metadata inflate it. Every database stores more bytes on disk than the "pure" data actually requires. That gap — the ratio of bytes consumed to bytes of useful data — is called space amplification . It is one of the three fundamental trade-offs in storage system design (alongside read amplification and write amplification), and understanding it will help you reason about why different engines behave so differently in production. Where the Extra Space Comes From Space amplification is not a bug; it is an unavoidable consequence of how storage engines work. Several forces push it upward: Source What it looks like on disk Dead row versions (MVCC) Old row images kept until vacuum/GC runs B-tree internal pages Branch nodes that hold keys but not full rows Fragmentation holes Gaps left after deletes inside heap/B-tree pages WAL / redo log A separate copy of every change, before it lands in the main file LSM compaction overhead Multiple sorted runs holding overlapping key ranges Index structures One or more extra files per indexed column A freshly vacuumed, freshly rebuilt table has the lowest amplification. After months of steady writes and deletes, that number climbs — sometimes dramatically. Measuring it The simplest definition: space amplification = bytes used on disk ───────────────────── bytes of live data A ratio of 1.0 is impossible in practice (even a one-row table needs a page header). A ratio of 1.1 is excellent. Ratios above 3–4× are common in write-heavy LSM-based stores during heavy compaction lag, and PostgreSQL tables bloated from MVCC can exceed 2× without regular AUTOVACUUM. B-Trees vs. LSM-Trees The two dominant storage engine families handle the amplification trade-off very differently. B-tree engines (PostgreSQL, MySQL InnoDB, SQLite) update data in place inside fixed-size pages. When a row is deleted or superseded, the old bytes sit as dead space inside the page until a maintenance pass (VACUUM, OPTIMIZE TABLE) reclaims them. Because pages are reused rather than appended, the total file size grows slowly — but internal fragmentation accumulates. LSM-tree engines (RocksDB, Cassandra, LevelDB) never overwrite data. Every write is appended to a new file (an SSTable or segment). This eliminates in-place fragmentation but creates a different problem: multiple files may contain different versions of the same key. Until compaction merges them, all those versions occupy disk space simultaneously. Note: RocksDB's space amplification in the worst case (before compaction) can be up to the number of levels × the size of a single level, because each level can hold a full copy of overlapping key ranges. In practice, RocksDB targets roughly 1.1× space amplification after a full compaction pass. Practical Levers You can actively manage space amplification: Run VACUUM / AUTOVACUUM (PostgreSQL) — reclaims dead tuples; VACUUM FULL rewrites the file entirely, shrinking it to minimum size. OPTIMIZE TABLE (MySQL) — rebuilds the clustered index and reclaims free pages. SQLite VACUUM — rewrites the entire database file into a fresh, compacted copy. Requires exclusive access and 2× free space. Trigger compaction (RocksDB-based systems) — force a manual compaction if you know a large batch of deletes just landed and you cannot wait for the background job. Right-size fill-factor — PostgreSQL lets you set a per-index fillfactor (default 90). Lowering it pre-reserves space on each page for updates, reducing future page splits and fragmentation. Seeing Space Amplification in SQLite SQLite exposes page-level accounting through PRAGMA statements. The widget below creates a table, inserts rows, deletes half of them, and compares the page count before and after VACUUM . Watch how the page count drops after reclamation — that's space amplification shrinking. Interactive SQL · Space before and after VACUUM After you run VACUUM; and re-run the SELECT, freelist_count drops to 0 and space_amplification falls back toward 1.0 — the reclaimed pages have been returned. On a real database the effect is identical; only the scale differs. Key Takeaways Space amplification is the ratio of disk bytes consumed to bytes of live data. A value of 1.0 is impossible; 1.05–1.3 is healthy; values above 2–3× signal a maintenance problem. B-tree engines accumulate internal fragmentation (holes inside pages); LSM engines accumulate version overlap across SSTable levels. Regular maintenance (VACUUM, compaction, OPTIMIZE TABLE) is not optional on write-heavy workloads — it is what keeps amplification from compounding over time. Space amplification is always a trade-off against write speed: engines that minimize space costs (aggressive in-place updates) tend to pay more in write I/O, and vice versa. ← Previous Crash recovery overview Next → Read amplification and write amplification

title: Read amplification and write amplification
chapter: Chapter 10. Trees, Logs, and Internal Structures
text: Read and write amplification: B-trees favor reads (low RA), LSM-trees favor writes (low WA) — every engine trades off one amplification for another. Every storage structure makes trade-offs. B-trees are fast for point reads but impose overhead on writes; LSM-trees (Log-Structured Merge-trees) absorb writes cheaply but do extra work at read time. The two metrics that capture these trade-offs with precision are read amplification and write amplification — ratios that measure how much extra I/O the engine does compared to what the application logically requested. The Two Amplification Factors Read amplification (RA) is the number of disk pages (or I/O operations) the engine reads to satisfy a single logical read. Write amplification (WA) is the number of bytes (or I/O operations) actually written to disk for every one byte the application asked to write. A ratio of 1 is the theoretical minimum. Real systems always exceed it. Structure Read amplification Write amplification Heap file (no index) High — full table scan possible Low — append only B-tree (with index) Low — O(log N) pages Moderate — splits and WAL LSM-tree Moderate — multi-level merge Low at ingestion, high during compaction Sorted file (static) Very low — binary search Very high — full rewrite on change Neither amplification can be driven to 1 simultaneously. This is a hard limit sometimes called the read-write-space amplification trilemma : optimise any two and the third gets worse. Read Amplification in Practice Consider a B-tree index on a table with one million rows. To fetch one row by primary key, the engine traverses from the root down to a leaf — say, 3–4 levels — then reads the actual heap page. That is 4–5 I/Os for a single logical row. If the row is not in the buffer pool, each level is a separate disk read. An LSM-tree can be worse: because data lives in multiple immutable sorted files (called SSTables or segments) across several levels, a point read may have to check every level before finding the key. Bloom filters reduce this in practice, but the worst case is proportional to the number of levels. Sequential scans flip the picture. A heap file scanned top-to-bottom has near-zero read amplification — every byte read is a byte the query needs. An index-driven random scan of many rows, on the other hand, can trigger one random I/O per row, making amplification extremely high. Note: Read amplification is why databases add covering indexes. A covering index stores all the columns a query needs right in the index leaf, eliminating the extra heap page fetch entirely (no "table lookup" step). Write Amplification in Practice When you INSERT a row, the database does more than write those bytes once: Write the row to the WAL (write-ahead log) — so it can be replayed on crash. Modify the B-tree index page in the buffer pool — possibly triggering a page split that rewrites two or more pages. Eventually flush dirty pages from the buffer pool to the data file. Periodically checkpoint, rewriting WAL records to the main file. Each step multiplies the original write. On write-heavy OLTP with many indexes, total WA of 5–10× is common. On NVMe SSDs, WA is a critical concern because every SSD cell has a finite write endurance — high WA wears out the drive faster and reduces throughput. LSM-trees trade high read amplification for low write amplification at ingestion time. Writes go into a sorted in-memory buffer (the memtable). When it fills, it flushes as a new immutable file. The catch: background compaction merges and rewrites those files repeatedly, so the long-run write amplification of an LSM-tree is not zero — it is simply deferred and made sequential, which is friendlier to SSDs. Measuring Amplification with a Model The query below models a simplified B-tree read path. It shows how many page reads are needed to find one row as the table grows, based on the tree height formula ceil(log_b(N)) where b is the branching factor and N is the number of rows. Run it as-is, then try changing branching_factor and rows to see how height — and therefore read amplification — changes. Interactive SQL · B-tree read amplification model Notice that doubling the row count barely moves the amplification number — logarithmic growth is very flat. This is why B-trees remain practical even at hundreds of millions of rows. Note: Real read amplification is lower when pages are already in the buffer pool. The numbers above are worst-case (cold cache). A warm cache turns many of those I/Os into cheap RAM reads, effectively reducing amplification to 1 for hot data. Understanding amplification helps you reason about index design, storage engine choice, and hardware sizing. The right trade-off depends on whether your workload is read-heavy (minimise RA), write-heavy (minimise WA), or space-constrained (minimise space amplification — a third factor worth exploring on its own). ← Previous Space amplification Next → Chapter 10 Summary: What We Learned

title: Chapter 10 Summary: What We Learned
chapter: Chapter 10. Trees, Logs, and Internal Structures
text: Page Topic Takeaway in One Sentence 091 B-tree structure in detail Internal nodes hold keys + child pointers; leaf nodes hold keys + values (or record IDs). Height stays low via high fan-out. 092 Splits, merges, and rebalancing When a node overflows, it splits; when under-full, it merges with a neighbor — keeping the tree balanced automatically. 093 Write-ahead logging basics All changes are logged sequentially to the WAL before they touch the main data files — the WAL is the single source of durability. 094 Checkpoints A checkpoint flushes all dirty pages to disk and truncates the WAL — balancing recovery time against I/O overhead. 095 Buffer pools The buffer pool caches frequently accessed pages in memory; its size and eviction policy (LRU, Clock) dominate performance. 096 Dirty pages and flushing A dirty page has been modified in memory but not yet written to disk; the background writer flushes them gradually. 097 Crash recovery overview On restart, replay the WAL from the last checkpoint to redo committed work and undo uncommitted work. 098 Space amplification Space amplification = (physical storage used) / (logical data size). Wasted space from fragmentation, page overhead, deleted rows. 099 Read amplification and write amplification Read amp = bytes read / bytes requested; write amp = bytes written / bytes of user data. Both degrade performance. Reveal: How do these ideas connect to the next chapter? Chapter 10 equipped you with the internal mechanics: B-trees organize data, the WAL guarantees durability, and the buffer pool keeps hot pages in memory. But these structures exist to serve a higher purpose: correctness. Chapter 11 introduces transactions — the mechanism that ensures your writes are atomic, consistent, isolated, and durable (ACID). You will learn why grouping operations into transactions is essential, what happens when you COMMIT or ROLLBACK, and how the WAL you just studied makes the D in ACID possible. ← Previous Read amplification and write amplification Next → Why transactions exist

title: Why transactions exist
text: Without transactions, a mid-operation crash corrupts data permanently. With transactions, a ROLLBACK undoes the partial work — the atomicity guarantee. Databases handle many operations at once — web servers fire queries in parallel, background jobs update rows while users are reading them, and a single user action often requires several writes that must all succeed or all fail together. Without a coordination mechanism, these overlapping operations would corrupt data in ways that are subtle, hard to reproduce, and catastrophic in production. Transactions are that mechanism. The Two Problems Transactions Solve Transactions exist because of two distinct failure modes that appear the moment a database does real work: 1. Partial failure. A bank transfer needs two updates: subtract from account A and add to account B. If the process crashes after the first write but before the second, money has vanished. No application-level retry logic can reliably fix this after the fact — you need the database to guarantee that both writes either happen together or neither happens at all. 2. Concurrent interference. Two sessions reading and writing the same rows at the same time can produce nonsensical results. Session 1 reads a balance, then Session 2 updates it, then Session 1 writes based on its stale read. The second update overwrites the first — a lost update . Without isolation, these races happen silently. A transaction wraps a group of operations into a single logical unit that the database treats atomically. The application marks the boundary with BEGIN and either COMMIT (make it permanent) or ROLLBACK (undo everything). ACID: The Four Guarantees Textbooks describe the guarantees a transaction provides with the acronym ACID . Each property addresses a specific failure: Property What it guarantees Which failure it prevents Atomicity All-or-nothing: every operation in the transaction commits, or none do Partial failure / crash mid-write Consistency The database moves from one valid state to another; constraints are never broken Invalid data entering the system Isolation Concurrent transactions do not interfere with each other Lost updates, dirty reads, race conditions Durability A committed transaction survives crashes; it is written to stable storage Data loss after a confirmed commit Note: Consistency in ACID is the odd one out — it depends on the application correctly defining what "valid" means via constraints, foreign keys, and business rules. The database enforces the rules, but you choose what the rules are. A Concrete Walk-Through Imagine a ticketing system. Selling the last ticket requires two steps: decrement seats_remaining and insert a row into bookings . If those steps are not wrapped in a transaction, a concurrent sale could decrement the counter twice while only one booking row is created — or the server could crash between the two writes, leaving the counter decremented with no booking to show for it. BEGIN; UPDATE events SET seats_remaining = seats_remaining - 1 WHERE id = 42 AND seats_remaining > 0; -- guard against overselling INSERT INTO bookings (event_id, user_id, booked_at) VALUES (42, 7, '2024-06-01 14:00:00'); COMMIT; If either statement fails — the seat count was already 0, a constraint fires, or the server dies — the entire transaction rolls back. No partial state leaks into the database. Try the widget below. It sets up the two tables, runs the transaction, and lets you query the result. Experiment by changing COMMIT to ROLLBACK to see that neither write survives. Interactive SQL · Atomic ticket sale Change COMMIT to ROLLBACK and re-run — both tables return to their original state, as if the sale never happened. Why Not Just Be Careful in Application Code? It is tempting to think careful application logic could handle this. It cannot, reliably, because: Crashes are unscheduled. Code cannot "be careful" across a power outage or OOM kill. Concurrency is non-deterministic. Two threads running the same careful code can still interleave in harmful ways. Retries create duplicates. Re-running a failed operation without atomicity can insert the same row twice or double-charge a customer. Transactions push the correctness guarantee down into the storage engine, where it can be enforced unconditionally — regardless of what the application does. That is the fundamental reason they exist. ← Previous Read amplification and write amplification Next → The meaning of atomicity

title: The meaning of atomicity
text: Atomicity: either all steps in a transaction succeed together, or none do. A mid-transaction crash triggers automatic rollback — no partial writes are ever visible. When a database says an operation is atomic , it means exactly what the word suggests from chemistry: indivisible. Either the whole operation happens, or none of it does. There is no halfway state that other parts of the system can observe. This single guarantee turns out to be the foundation on which everything else about transactions is built. The Classic Example: A Bank Transfer The go-to illustration of atomicity is a money transfer, and it earns that status because the problem is painfully obvious. Suppose Alice wants to send $200 to Bob. In the database that means two writes: Subtract $200 from Alice's balance. Add $200 to Bob's balance. If the database crashes — or the network drops, or the application throws an exception — right between step 1 and step 2, something terrible happens: Alice is $200 poorer and Bob received nothing. The money has vanished from the ledger. -- Without atomicity, a crash here leaves the database in a broken state: UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance - 200 WHERE name = 'Alice'; -- <-- crash, power cut, or error here --> UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance + 200 WHERE name = 'Bob'; Atomicity is the promise that this intermediate state can never be made permanent. The database guarantees that both writes commit together, or neither one does. What "All or Nothing" Actually Means Atomicity does not mean the two statements run in a single CPU instruction. Under the hood the engine executes them one at a time. What it means is that the engine tracks the full set of changes belonging to one transaction and, when something goes wrong before the transaction finishes, it rolls back every change in that set — restoring the database to the state it was in before the transaction started. The mechanism that makes this possible (the write-ahead log, covered in Chapter 10) records every intended change to durable storage before it is applied to the actual data pages. If the system crashes mid-transaction, recovery replays the log on restart, finds the incomplete transaction, and undoes its partial changes. Note: Atomicity is one of the four ACID properties. The others — Consistency, Isolation, and Durability — each add a further guarantee. But atomicity is the prerequisite: if you cannot guarantee all-or-nothing, the other three have nothing to stand on. Atomicity in Practice: BEGIN / COMMIT / ROLLBACK Applications control transaction boundaries with three SQL statements: Statement Effect BEGIN Start a new transaction; changes are now grouped COMMIT Make all changes in the transaction permanent ROLLBACK Discard all changes in the transaction; return to previous state The interactive example below lets you see atomicity in action. A ROLLBACK after two updates leaves the balances completely unchanged, as if the updates never ran. Interactive SQL · Atomicity with ROLLBACK Try it: change ROLLBACK to COMMIT and re-run. Now the transfer sticks and the balances reflect the move. Change it back to ROLLBACK and the database returns to its original state every time. What happens inside a failed COMMIT A COMMIT can also fail — for example if a disk write error occurs while flushing the log. In that case the database treats the transaction as if ROLLBACK had been issued: no partial changes escape. From the application's perspective the commit either succeeds completely or raises an error and leaves the data untouched. Why This Matters Beyond Transfers Bank transfers get all the press, but atomicity matters any time multiple rows or tables need to change as a single logical unit: Creating an order and decrementing inventory in the same operation. Inserting a user account and sending a welcome-email record to a queue table. Deleting a parent row and all its child rows (when cascades are not available). In every case, the application needs confidence that readers will never see a universe where one half of the update happened and the other did not. Atomicity is that confidence, stated as a hard guarantee by the database engine. ← Previous Why transactions exist Next → Consistency in practice

title: Consistency in practice
text: Consistency in a multi-table transaction: both debit and credit succeed (COMMIT) or both roll back — the database never shows an intermediate invalid state. The C in ACID stands for Consistency, but it is the most misunderstood of the four properties. Unlike Atomicity, Isolation, and Durability — which the database engine enforces mechanically — Consistency is partly your responsibility. The engine provides tools; you decide what "correct" means for your data. What Consistency Actually Means Consistency says: a transaction takes the database from one valid state to another valid state. "Valid" is defined by the constraints, rules, and invariants you have declared. Two sources contribute to those rules: Source Examples Who enforces it Database-level constraints NOT NULL , UNIQUE , FOREIGN KEY , CHECK The engine — automatically Application-level invariants "total debits must equal total credits", "a user's age must be positive" Your code — inside the transaction The engine can only enforce rules it knows about. Any invariant you leave implicit lives entirely in application code. If two code paths update the same data, both must respect the invariant — or you end up in an inconsistent state even though every individual query "succeeded." Note: Consistency in ACID is intentionally vague compared to the other three letters. It is a design goal, not a specific mechanism. The mechanisms that support it are constraints, transactions, and careful schema design. Constraints as a Safety Net Database constraints are the most reliable consistency tool because the engine checks them atomically — either the entire transaction satisfies every constraint, or it is rolled back. Common constraint types and what they guard: NOT NULL — a required field is never missing. UNIQUE — no duplicate values in a column (or group of columns). PRIMARY KEY — implies NOT NULL + UNIQUE ; identifies each row. FOREIGN KEY — a value in one table must exist in another; prevents orphaned rows. CHECK — arbitrary condition on one or more columns ( quantity > 0 , end_date >= start_date ). Try the widget below. It creates an orders table with several constraints, then attempts a few inserts — some valid, some not. Run each INSERT separately to see which ones the engine rejects and why. Interactive SQL · Constraints in action Uncomment each blocked insert and re-run to see the constraint violation messages. The database refuses to move to an invalid state — even if your application code forgot to check. Application-Level Invariants Some rules cannot be expressed as a single-column constraint. Consider a double-entry bookkeeping system where every transfer must debit one account and credit another by the same amount. The database cannot enforce this with a CHECK constraint on a single row — it spans multiple rows. The correct approach is to perform both writes inside a single transaction. If either write fails, the whole transaction rolls back, and the invariant holds. Interactive SQL · Maintaining a balance invariant Now try changing the transfer to $600 (more than Alice has). The CHECK(balance >= 0) constraint will fire when the first UPDATE runs, the transaction will be aborted, and neither balance changes. The constraint on the column protects the row-level rule; the transaction protects the cross-row invariant. What the Engine Cannot Do For You Even with perfect schema design, some consistency problems fall outside what the database can catch automatically: Stale reads in application logic — your code reads a value, makes a decision, then writes. If another transaction changed the value between the read and the write, your decision was based on outdated data. The fix is locking ( SELECT ... FOR UPDATE ) or optimistic concurrency checks — covered in the next page on isolation. Business rules encoded only in code — if "a premium customer may have at most 10 open orders" lives only in an API handler, a direct SQL connection bypasses it entirely. Data imported from outside — bulk loads often bypass triggers and may skip constraint checks if the import tool disables them for performance. The practical lesson: treat database constraints as a last line of defense , not the only line. Validate at the application layer first, then let the database confirm. If they disagree, the database wins — a constraint violation is a clear signal that your application logic has a gap. Note: Some engines (PostgreSQL, Oracle, SQL Server) support deferrable constraints — foreign key and uniqueness checks can be postponed until COMMIT rather than checked after each statement. This is useful when inserting rows that temporarily violate a constraint mid-transaction (such as inserting a parent and child row in reverse order). ← Previous The meaning of atomicity Next → Isolation and interference

title: Isolation and interference
text: Two concurrent transactions on the same row — without isolation, B overwrites A's change (lost update); with isolation, they serialize correctly. When two transactions run at exactly the same time, they can step on each other's work in surprising ways. Isolation is the database property that prevents one transaction from seeing or disrupting the in-progress work of another. Getting isolation wrong leads to subtle, hard-to-reproduce bugs — the kind that only appear under load. Why Concurrent Transactions Interfere A database serves many clients simultaneously. Without any coordination, two transactions reading and writing the same rows can interleave their operations in ways that produce incorrect results. These bad outcomes have standard names: Phenomenon What happens Dirty read Transaction A reads a row that Transaction B has modified but not yet committed. If B rolls back, A has seen data that never officially existed. Non-repeatable read A reads a row, then B commits an update to that row, then A reads the same row again — and gets a different value. Phantom read A runs a range query (e.g. WHERE amount > 100 ). B inserts a new row that matches. A runs the same query and now gets an extra row it didn't see before. Lost update A and B both read the same value, both compute a new value based on it, and both write back — one write silently overwrites the other. None of these require malicious intent; they happen naturally when two clients race on overlapping data. Isolation Levels SQL defines four standard isolation levels. Each one prevents a different subset of the phenomena above. Higher isolation means fewer anomalies but more contention (transactions may have to wait for each other). Isolation level Dirty read Non-repeatable read Phantom read Read Uncommitted Possible Possible Possible Read Committed Prevented Possible Possible Repeatable Read Prevented Prevented Possible Serializable Prevented Prevented Prevented Read Committed is the default in PostgreSQL and Oracle. Repeatable Read is the default in MySQL/InnoDB. Serializable gives you the strongest guarantee: the result is identical to some sequential (non-overlapping) execution of the same transactions, as if they had run one after another. Note: SQLite's default mode is Serializable for writers (it uses a write lock for the entire database), which eliminates most anomalies but limits write concurrency. SQLite's WAL mode softens this for readers. How engines enforce isolation Two main strategies are used in practice: Locking — a transaction acquires shared or exclusive locks on rows (or pages) before touching them. Other transactions that need conflicting locks must wait. MVCC (Multi-Version Concurrency Control) — the engine keeps multiple timestamped versions of each row. Readers see a snapshot of the database as it existed at the start of their transaction, so writers don't block readers and vice versa. PostgreSQL, MySQL/InnoDB, and Oracle all use MVCC. SQLite's WAL mode provides a simpler multi-reader, single-writer mechanism rather than full MVCC. Seeing Interference in Action The widget below simulates a classic lost update scenario. Two "sessions" both read a bank balance, add a deposit, and write back. Run the setup, then study what the final balance should be versus what it actually is. Interactive SQL · Lost update simulation Run the setup, then execute the UPDATE statements one at a time followed by the final SELECT . The discrepancy column reveals the lost $200 — Alice deposited first, but Bob's concurrent update overwrote her result. This is exactly the kind of corruption that isolation levels prevent. Key Takeaways Lost updates happen when two transactions read the same value, modify it, and write back without coordination — one write silently overwrites the other. The SQL standard defines four isolation levels: Read Uncommitted , Read Committed , Repeatable Read , and Serializable — each trades off correctness for concurrency. Read Committed is the default in most engines; it prevents dirty reads but still allows lost updates and non-repeatable reads. Repeatable Read gives each transaction a consistent snapshot of the database, preventing non-repeatable reads and phantom reads (depending on the engine). However, Snapshot Isolation alone does NOT prevent lost updates — two transactions can read the same snapshot, modify it, and both commit, causing one write to silently overwrite the other. Preventing lost updates requires SELECT ... FOR UPDATE , optimistic locking, or true Serializable isolation. Serializable provides the strongest guarantee — transactions behave as if they ran one at a time — at the highest performance cost. Choosing an isolation level is always a tradeoff: stricter = fewer anomalies but less concurrency. ← Previous Consistency in practice Next → Durability and persistence

title: Durability and persistence
text: A committed transaction must survive anything the real world throws at it: a power cut, a crashed process, a rebooted server. Durability is the ACID property that makes this promise. Once the database says "committed," that data is permanently recorded — not just sitting in memory waiting to be written, but safely on stable storage. What Durability Actually Guarantees Durability draws a hard line at the moment of COMMIT . Before that moment, nothing is promised — the database is free to keep changes in memory. After that moment, the changes must be recoverable even if the machine loses power the very next millisecond. This is a stronger claim than it sounds. Modern hardware has several layers of memory that can lose their contents on power loss: Layer Survives power loss? CPU registers & cache No OS page cache (RAM) No Storage controller write cache (no battery) No Storage controller write cache (battery-backed) Yes SSD / HDD physical media Yes A naive implementation that just writes to the OS page cache and calls COMMIT is not durable: the OS has not yet flushed those pages to disk. A crash before the flush erases the data. Real database engines call fsync() (or the platform equivalent) to force the OS to push data all the way to the physical medium before acknowledging a commit. Note: fsync() is expensive — it can take milliseconds. This is exactly why batching many transactions into a single fsync (group commit) is a common optimization. The durability guarantee is maintained; you are just sharing the cost. Write-Ahead Logging: The Engine of Durability Chapter 10 introduced the write-ahead log (WAL). It is the key mechanism behind durability. The rule is simple: before any data page is changed on disk, the intended change is written to the log first. On commit, the engine only needs to flush the log record — not the full data pages. Log writes are sequential and therefore fast. The data pages can be flushed later, lazily, during a checkpoint. If a crash happens after a commit but before the data pages are flushed, recovery is straightforward: the engine reads the log on restart and redoes the committed changes, bringing the data pages up to date. No data is lost. WAL ensures durability: the log is fsynced before COMMIT returns. Data page flushes happen later, lazily. Durability vs. Availability Durability is about surviving crashes, not about being available after a crash. A single-server database that loses its storage drive is durable right up until that moment — all previously committed data was intact — but it is now unavailable (and the data on that drive may be gone). True long-term durability at scale requires replication: a second server (or more) holds a copy of the data. The most durable configurations replicate the WAL itself, so a standby can replay it to a current state. Some systems require a commit to be acknowledged by at least two nodes before returning success — a practice called synchronous replication . This trades some write latency for a stronger guarantee: even if the primary fails immediately after committing, the standby already has the log record. Strategy Survives process crash Survives machine failure Survives data-center loss WAL + fsync (single node) Yes No No Async replication Yes Usually No Sync replication (multi-node) Yes Yes No Geo-redundant sync replication Yes Yes Yes Seeing Durability Boundaries in SQL PostgreSQL writes WAL records and calls fsync based on synchronous_commit setting. The default on mode forces WAL flush after every commit; off skips it entirely (fast, but unsafe on power loss). Try the widget to see the current synchronous_commit setting, and experiment with how BEGIN / COMMIT define the unit that gets durably written: Interactive SQL · Durability and COMMIT boundaries After running the query, try replacing COMMIT with ROLLBACK — the inserts disappear entirely, because the uncommitted changes were never made durable. Note: In application code, always check that your database driver is not silently operating in autocommit mode with synchronous=OFF . That combination is the most common cause of data loss after unexpected restarts in development and testing environments. ← Previous Isolation and interference Next → Transaction boundaries

title: Transaction boundaries
text: Every transaction has a beginning and an end. Those two moments — where you open a transaction and where you close it — are its boundaries . Choosing the right boundaries is one of the most practical skills in database programming: too narrow and your data can be left in a half-written state; too wide and you hold locks longer than necessary, slowing everything else down. Opening and Closing a Transaction In SQL, you mark the boundaries explicitly with three statements: Statement Meaning BEGIN (or START TRANSACTION ) Opens a new transaction. Everything after this is part of the same unit. COMMIT Closes the transaction successfully. All changes become permanent and visible to others. ROLLBACK Closes the transaction by undoing every change made since BEGIN . BEGIN; UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance - 100 WHERE id = 1; -- debit Alice UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance + 100 WHERE id = 2; -- credit Bob COMMIT; -- both changes land together, or neither does If the process crashes after the UPDATE s but before the COMMIT , the database engine replays or discards the partial work on recovery — Alice is not debited without Bob being credited. Note: Many database drivers operate in auto-commit mode by default, which means every individual statement is its own transaction. You only need BEGIN when you want to group multiple statements into one unit. Where to Draw the Line The golden rule: a transaction should span exactly the work that must succeed or fail together — no more, no less. Consider transferring money between accounts. These two updates are causally linked — one cannot succeed without the other. They belong inside the same transaction boundary. On the other hand, logging the transfer to an audit table is a separate concern; if the log write fails, you may not want to roll back the transfer itself. A common mistake is keeping transactions open while waiting for user input or making a network call. The transaction holds locks during that wait, blocking other clients. Keep the boundary tight: fetch what you need, close the transaction, then interact with the user, then open a new transaction to write the result. A committed transaction makes all its changes durable; a rolled-back transaction leaves the database unchanged. Savepoints: Partial Rollbacks Sometimes you want to undo only part of a transaction. SQL provides savepoints for this: BEGIN; INSERT INTO orders (customer_id, total) VALUES (42, 99.00); SAVEPOINT before_promo; UPDATE promotions SET uses = uses + 1 WHERE code = 'SAVE10'; -- suppose this update finds 0 rows (invalid code) ROLLBACK TO SAVEPOINT before_promo; -- undo just the promo update -- the INSERT above is still in play COMMIT; -- only the order insert is committed A ROLLBACK TO SAVEPOINT does not end the transaction — it rewinds to a checkpoint inside it. You can then continue working and eventually COMMIT or ROLLBACK the whole thing. Note: Savepoints are standard SQL and supported by PostgreSQL, MySQL, and SQLite. They are especially useful in application code that builds up complex writes where one optional step might fail. Trying It Yourself The widget below lets you experiment with BEGIN , COMMIT , and ROLLBACK . Notice that without an explicit BEGIN , each statement auto-commits. Try wrapping the two UPDATE s in a transaction and rolling back — then check whether the balances changed. Interactive SQL · Transaction boundaries Reveal: What happens if you ROLLBACK instead of COMMIT? Both UPDATE statements are undone. Alice keeps her 500 and Bob keeps his 300 — exactly as if neither statement had run. The database guarantees that a rolled-back transaction leaves no trace. ← Previous Durability and persistence Next → Commit and rollback

title: Commit and rollback
text: Every transaction lives in one of two states: in progress or ended . Ending a transaction is a deliberate choice — you either tell the database to make all your changes permanent ( COMMIT ) or to erase them entirely ( ROLLBACK ). Those two commands are the on/off switch for every guarantee that transactions provide. What Commit and Rollback Actually Do When you open a transaction with BEGIN , the database engine starts tracking every change you make — which rows you inserted, updated, or deleted. Until you commit, those changes live in a private workspace that only your session can see. Other sessions see the old data; you see your in-progress version. COMMIT tells the engine: I am done, make this real. The engine flushes the changes to durable storage (typically by writing a record to the write-ahead log first), releases any locks, and makes the new data visible to every other session. Once a commit returns successfully, the changes survive crashes. ROLLBACK tells the engine: Discard everything since BEGIN. The engine undoes every change in the transaction — new rows are deleted, modified rows are restored to their previous values — and the database is left exactly as it was before the BEGIN . No trace of the aborted work remains. A transaction either commits (changes become durable and visible) or rolls back (changes vanish). Implicit vs. Explicit Transactions Most databases support two modes: Mode How it works When to use Explicit You write BEGIN … COMMIT / ROLLBACK yourself Multi-statement operations that must be atomic Auto-commit Every statement is its own one-statement transaction Quick, standalone reads and single-row writes Note: Auto-commit is on by default in most database clients. Every INSERT , UPDATE , or DELETE you run without an explicit BEGIN is automatically committed the moment it succeeds. This is convenient but means there is no safety net — a mistaken DELETE without a WHERE clause is permanent as soon as it runs. Always use explicit transactions when multiple statements need to succeed or fail together. Automatic Rollback on Error You do not always have to call ROLLBACK yourself. If a statement inside a transaction causes an error (a constraint violation, a type mismatch, a deadlock), the database marks the transaction as aborted . Any further statements in the same transaction will be rejected, and when you eventually call COMMIT — or when the connection drops — the engine rolls back automatically. BEGIN; INSERT INTO orders (id, customer_id, total) VALUES (101, 9, 250.00); -- This statement fails: customer_id 999 does not exist (foreign key violation). INSERT INTO orders (id, customer_id, total) VALUES (102, 999, 75.00); -- The transaction is now aborted. Even calling COMMIT here rolls back. COMMIT; -- behaves like ROLLBACK — neither row is saved This all-or-nothing behavior is what "atomicity" means in practice: a half-finished transaction cannot be committed. Trying It Live The widget below sets up a small accounts table. Run the block as-is to see a successful commit. Then change COMMIT to ROLLBACK and re-run — notice that the balance stays at its original value, as if the update never happened. Interactive SQL · Commit vs. rollback Now try introducing an error. Remove the second UPDATE entirely and add a line that references a non-existent row ( WHERE id = 99 ). The first update succeeds in isolation, but swap COMMIT for ROLLBACK and confirm that Alice's balance is untouched — demonstrating that you control the outcome right up until the commit. Savepoints: Partial Rollback Full rollback is all-or-nothing, but SQL also provides savepoints — named checkpoints within a transaction that you can roll back to without aborting the whole thing. BEGIN; INSERT INTO audit_log (msg) VALUES ('started'); SAVEPOINT before_risky_step; UPDATE inventory SET qty = qty - 1000 WHERE product_id = 5; -- might be wrong -- Oops, that was too many. Roll back just to the savepoint: ROLLBACK TO SAVEPOINT before_risky_step; UPDATE inventory SET qty = qty - 10 WHERE product_id = 5; -- correct amount COMMIT; -- the audit log insert AND the corrected update are committed Savepoints are useful in long transactions where one optional step can fail without invalidating earlier work — common in batch imports and stored procedures. ← Previous Transaction boundaries Next → Multi-step updates

title: Multi-step updates
text: Most real database work isn't a single INSERT or a lone SELECT — it's a sequence of related writes that must all succeed together to leave the database in a meaningful state. Transferring money, placing an order, or cancelling a reservation all involve touching multiple rows (sometimes across multiple tables) in a specific order. This is what makes multi-step updates both powerful and dangerous. Why multiple steps are needed A single SQL statement can change many rows at once, but it can only change one thing at a time — one table, one kind of change. Real operations cross those boundaries. Consider an e-commerce order: Insert a new row into orders . Insert one row per product into order_items . Decrement the stock count for each product. Deduct the total from the customer's credit balance. Each step reads work done by the step before it. If step 3 fails (e.g., a stock count would go negative and violate a constraint), leaving steps 1 and 2 already committed would corrupt the database — you'd have a "ghost" order with items but no reserved stock. Transactions wrap the steps The database's answer is the transaction : a named boundary inside which all steps are executed together. You open a transaction with BEGIN , do your work, and then either: COMMIT — finalize all changes at once, making them visible to others. ROLLBACK — undo every change made inside the transaction, as if it never happened. BEGIN; INSERT INTO orders (customer_id, total) VALUES (42, 149.99); UPDATE inventory SET stock = stock - 1 WHERE product_id = 7; -- If anything above failed, we'd ROLLBACK here instead: COMMIT; The guarantee is atomicity : either both the INSERT and the UPDATE commit together, or neither does. There is no in-between state that other sessions can observe. On success, both steps commit atomically. On failure, a ROLLBACK leaves the database unchanged. What can go wrong (and how the DB helps) There are three main reasons a multi-step update might need to abort: Cause Example Database response Constraint violation Stock goes below 0 (CHECK constraint) Statement fails; transaction can be rolled back Application error Code detects bad input after step 1 Application calls ROLLBACK explicitly System failure Process crashes mid-transaction Engine uses its write-ahead log to undo uncommitted work on restart In all three cases, the database's logging infrastructure — the write-ahead log (WAL) — records what changed and lets the engine reverse it cleanly. You don't have to write "undo" code yourself. Note: In SQLite (and most databases), if you don't open an explicit BEGIN , each statement runs in its own auto-committed transaction. That's fine for isolated reads, but it means two consecutive UPDATE statements are not atomic with each other. Try it yourself The widget below models a ticket-booking scenario: a customer buys a seat, which should simultaneously record the booking and reduce available seats. Run it as-is to see both steps succeed, then try triggering a rollback by changing the seat_count update to subtract more seats than exist — the CHECK constraint will fire. Interactive SQL · multi-step booking Try this: change seats_available - 1 to seats_available - 99 and re-run. The CHECK constraint fires, the statement fails, and because the INSERT and UPDATE are in the same transaction, the booking is never recorded either. Then try removing the BEGIN / COMMIT wrapper — now the two steps are separate transactions, and the INSERT commits even when the UPDATE fails. ← Previous Commit and rollback Next → Real examples of broken logic

title: Real examples of broken logic
text: Theory is clean; production is messy. The concurrency anomalies from the previous chapter — dirty reads, lost updates, phantoms — sound abstract until you see exactly where in real application code they appear. This page walks through three scenarios that developers encounter repeatedly in the wild, showing the broken pattern and what actually goes wrong. Scenario 1: The Double-Spend (Check-Then-Act Race) A classic mistake is to check a condition in one query and act on it in a second query , without holding any lock between them. Another transaction can slip in between. Consider a wallet system. The application code reads the balance, decides whether the user can afford a purchase, then deducts the amount: -- Step 1 (application reads balance) SELECT balance FROM wallets WHERE user_id = 9; -- returns 50 -- ... application checks: 50 >= 30? Yes. Proceed ... -- Step 2 (application deducts) UPDATE wallets SET balance = balance - 30 WHERE user_id = 9; If two purchase requests arrive simultaneously for the same user — both for $30 against a $50 balance — both sessions can execute Step 1 before either executes Step 2. Both see $50, both decide to proceed, and both subtract $30, leaving the balance at $20 instead of $20... wait: $50 − $30 − $30 = −$10 . The wallet goes negative. The fix is to move the check into the write statement itself and use a transaction with appropriate isolation: BEGIN; UPDATE wallets SET balance = balance - 30 WHERE user_id = 9 AND balance >= 30; -- guard is atomic with the write -- Check rows affected; if 0, the guard failed — rollback COMMIT; Because the check and the deduction happen in a single atomic operation, no other transaction can read a stale balance and slip past the guard. Both sessions read balance = 50 before either writes, so both authorize the purchase — the wallet goes negative. Scenario 2: The Invisible Inventory Oversell E-commerce systems hit this constantly. The application checks stock > 0 , then inserts an order row, in two separate statements outside any transaction: -- (No BEGIN) SELECT stock FROM products WHERE id = 77; -- returns 1 -- application: stock > 0, so insert the order INSERT INTO orders (product_id, qty, user_id) VALUES (77, 1, 301); UPDATE products SET stock = stock - 1 WHERE id = 77; -- (No COMMIT — autocommit fires after each statement) With 200 users hitting "Buy Now" simultaneously, dozens of sessions can each see stock = 1 before any of them decrements it. The result: one physical item, dozens of confirmed orders, angry customers, and a support nightmare. Note: Wrapping all three statements in a single transaction and verifying the UPDATE affected exactly one row (i.e., stock was not already 0) is the minimum fix. On high-traffic systems you also want SELECT ... FOR UPDATE (in PostgreSQL/MySQL) or optimistic locking to queue writers properly. SQLite serializes all writers automatically, which sidesteps this in single-node setups. Scenario 3: Constraint Bypass via Missing Transaction Suppose you have a rule: every employee row must reference a valid department , and every department must have at least one employee. Enforcing this requires a brief moment where the constraint is technically violated — during a transfer between departments. -- Moving the last employee out of dept 10 into dept 20. -- Without a transaction this is dangerous: UPDATE employees SET dept_id = 20 WHERE id = 55; -- RIGHT HERE: dept 10 now has zero employees (constraint violated!) -- If the process crashes here, data is corrupt. UPDATE departments SET head_count = head_count - 1 WHERE id = 10; UPDATE departments SET head_count = head_count + 1 WHERE id = 20; Inside a transaction, the database defers constraint checks until COMMIT , so the transient violation is never visible to other sessions and is rolled back automatically if anything fails: BEGIN; UPDATE employees SET dept_id = 20 WHERE id = 55; UPDATE departments SET head_count = head_count - 1 WHERE id = 10; UPDATE departments SET head_count = head_count + 1 WHERE id = 20; COMMIT; -- constraints verified here; all-or-nothing Try It: The Lost Inventory Update The widget below demonstrates the oversell pattern. The initial stock is 1. Two "sessions" both read stock, both see it as available, and both decrement — ending at −1. Experiment by rewriting the update to use WHERE stock > 0 as a guard and observe what changes. Interactive SQL · Inventory oversell Now try adding AND stock > 0 to both UPDATE statements. The second update will silently affect 0 rows — indicating the sale should be rejected — and the stock will stop at 0 instead of going negative. In a real application you would check changes() (SQLite) or ROW_COUNT() (MySQL) after the update and roll back if no rows were modified. The thread connecting all three scenarios is the same: the window between a read and its dependent write is where races and partial failures hide . Transactions close that window by making the entire sequence atomic and isolated. The examples here are simplified, but the bugs they represent have caused real financial losses, oversold concerts, and corrupted payroll records in production systems. ← Previous Multi-step updates Next → Chapter 11 Summary: What We Learned

title: Chapter 11 Summary: What We Learned
text: Page Topic Takeaway in One Sentence 101 Why transactions exist Without transactions, concurrent writes and crashes leave data in inconsistent states — transactions make correctness automatic. 102 The meaning of atomicity All-or-nothing: either every operation in a transaction happens, or none do — no partial updates survive. 103 Consistency in practice The database enforces constraints (PK, FK, CHECK) at commit time; application-level invariants are your responsibility. 104 Isolation and interference Isolation prevents one transaction's intermediate state from being visible to another — the I in ACID is the hardest to get right. 105 Durability and persistence Once a transaction commits, its effects survive crashes and power loss — guaranteed by the WAL. 106 Transaction boundaries Group logically related reads and writes into one transaction; keep transactions short to reduce lock contention. 107 Commit and rollback COMMIT makes changes permanent; ROLLBACK undoes all changes since the transaction began. 108 Multi-step updates Money transfers, order placements, inventory decrements — all require multiple writes that must be atomic. 109 Real examples of broken logic Dirty reads, lost updates, and phantom reads cause real-world bugs: double-spending, inconsistent reports, corrupted data. Reveal: How do these ideas connect to the next chapter? Chapter 11 introduced transactions and ACID — the contract that keeps your data correct. But the I in ACID (Isolation) is the hardest letter to get right, and it is the bridge to Chapter 12. You will learn how databases handle concurrent transactions: locks, MVCC, isolation levels, and the anomalies that occur when isolation is too weak. If Chapter 11 was about why transactions exist, Chapter 12 is about how they coexist without stepping on each other. ← Previous Real examples of broken logic Next → The lost update problem

title: The lost update problem
text: The lost update problem: two transactions read the same value, each computes a new value based on what they read, and the second write silently overwrites the first. Fix: SELECT FOR UPDATE or optimistic locking. Imagine two bank tellers both pulling up the same account at the same moment, each counting the balance, each adding a deposit, and each typing in a new total — without knowing the other exists. One write lands first; the second write replaces it. A deposit has vanished into thin air. That is the lost update problem , one of the most common and costly concurrency bugs in database applications. What Makes an Update "Lost" An update is lost when two transactions follow this pattern on the same row : Both transactions read the current value. Each computes a new value based on what it read. Each writes its result back. The second write silently overwrites the first — as if the first never happened. The critical point: neither transaction does anything wrong in isolation. The bug emerges only from the interleaving of their steps.
