Transaction size Typical undo time Notes A few rows Milliseconds Barely noticeable at restart Thousands of rows Seconds to tens of seconds May surprise users after a crash Bulk loads / long batches Minutes This is why large ETL jobs are risky without checkpoints Note: Some databases let you monitor undo progress. In PostgreSQL, pg_stat_activity shows transactions in state idle in transaction (aborted) and the pg_locks view can show blocking. Long undo operations are a real operational concern, not just theory. Explore It with a Simulated Log The widget below models the undo decision for a crashed transaction. It uses a table of log records; the query identifies which records need to be undone (no matching COMMIT) and lists them newest-to-oldest — the exact traversal order the engine would use. Interactive SQL · Simulating undo order Try adding INSERT INTO wal_log VALUES (330, 9, 'COMMIT', NULL, NULL, NULL); at the top of the setup area, then re-run — transaction 9 disappears from the undo list because it is now committed. Key Takeaways Uncommitted changes that reached disk are the target of the undo phase. They are not automatically safe just because the data file was written. The WAL's before-images make undo possible: every log record stores the value to restore. Undo runs newest-to-oldest within each transaction, so that dependent changes are reversed in the right sequence. CLRs protect undo from being interrupted: if the engine crashes mid-undo, it knows exactly where to resume. Savepoints use the same undo machinery, allowing partial rollbacks inside a still-running transaction. ← Previous Crash-safe commits Next → Why recovery shapes design

title: Why recovery shapes design
text: Everything you have read about the write-ahead log, redo/undo passes, checkpoints, and steal/force policies has one thing in common: none of it was optional. Each mechanism exists because the database made a promise — durability and atomicity — and keeping that promise under any crash scenario forces specific, concrete design choices. This page steps back and shows how those choices ripple through the entire storage engine. The Promise That Drives Everything When a client receives COMMIT OK , the database has guaranteed two things: Atomicity — either all changes from that transaction are visible, or none are. Durability — the committed data will survive any subsequent crash. Both guarantees must hold even if a power failure occurs one microsecond after COMMIT returns. That constraint — survive any crash — is the forcing function behind nearly every design decision in a storage engine. The durability promise is the root cause of four major storage engine design constraints. How Recovery Requirements Shape Specific Choices Logging forces sequential I/O at commit time A naive approach to durability would flush every dirty data page to disk on each COMMIT . That is correct, but the pages touched by a transaction are scattered across many disk locations — random I/O is orders of magnitude slower than sequential I/O on spinning disks, and still expensive on SSDs. The WAL sidesteps this by making the log the only thing that must reach disk at commit time. Log records are appended sequentially to a single file — one fast sequential write replaces dozens of random page writes. The data pages can follow later, batched and reordered by the I/O scheduler. Recovery is what makes this safe: if the engine crashes before the pages catch up, redo reconstructs them from the log. Key insight: the existence of recovery enables a faster commit path. Without a recovery mechanism, you would be forced into the slow, synchronous approach. Buffer pool freedom comes at the cost of undo Steal policy (evicting dirty pages before commit) keeps the buffer pool from bloating under large transactions. But it means committed-looking data might hit disk before its transaction commits — so recovery must be able to undo those writes. The WAL's before-images (undo records) exist precisely to support this. Remove the recovery requirement and you could use no-steal — simpler, but impractical for any transaction that touches more pages than fit in RAM. Checkpoint frequency is a latency vs. recovery-time trade-off Checkpoints flush dirty pages and write a marker to the log. They serve recovery: on restart, the engine only needs to replay the log from the last checkpoint, not from the beginning of time. But checkpoints are expensive — they cause a burst of I/O. DBAs tune checkpoint frequency to balance two competing costs: Checkpoints Recovery time Checkpoint I/O cost More frequent Short (less log to replay) Higher ongoing I/O Less frequent Long (more log to replay) Lower ongoing I/O PostgreSQL exposes checkpoint_completion_target and max_wal_size precisely to tune this trade-off. Data structures must be crash-safe by construction A B-tree node split writes to multiple disk locations. If a crash interrupts the split halfway, the tree is structurally corrupt — and no amount of WAL replay will help if the tree's own metadata pages are inconsistent. This forces engines to either: Double-write the page to a safe location before writing it in place (MySQL InnoDB's double-write buffer), or Log the structural change at a granular level so recovery can redo the partial split safely (PostgreSQL full-page writes after a checkpoint). The choice of index structure is therefore not just a performance decision — it is a recoverability decision. A structure that cannot be repaired by log replay cannot be used in a crash-safe engine without extra protection. Seeing the Log-Then-Data Constraint in SQLite SQLite makes these trade-offs visible. The widget below lets you simulate the order in which log entries would need to appear relative to a data write. Explore which log sequence numbers (LSNs) on the page would need to be flushed before a given data page is safe to evict. Interactive SQL · Recovery design constraints Try changing a flushed_to_disk value to 0 and re-running — the status will flip to "Must flush log first", showing exactly the WAL constraint that prevents data loss. The Cascading Effect Recovery is not a feature bolted onto the side of a database. It is a foundational constraint that determines: What the log must record (before and after images) How the buffer pool can behave (steal is allowed; no-force is allowed) How often checkpoints run (bounded recovery time) What data structures are permissible (must survive partial writes) What "COMMIT" actually does (log flush, not necessarily page flush) Every time you tune wal_level , innodb_flush_log_at_trx_commit , or synchronous_commit , you are adjusting exactly where your system sits on the durability-vs-performance axis — a dial that only exists because the recovery system was designed to make it tunable. Note: SQLite in WAL mode, PostgreSQL with synchronous_commit = off , and MySQL with innodb_flush_log_at_trx_commit = 2 all weaken the durability guarantee deliberately — trading the risk of losing a few seconds of commits on a crash for significantly higher write throughput. Understanding recovery is what lets you make that trade consciously. ← Previous Recovering unfinished work Next → Chapter 13 Summary: What We Learned

title: Chapter 13 Summary: What We Learned
text: Page Topic Takeaway in One Sentence 121 What can go wrong Power loss, OS crash, disk failure, or a transaction abort mid-write can leave data corrupt — recovery handles all of these. 122 The write-ahead log WAL is an append-only log of all changes; written sequentially (fast), used to reconstruct state after a crash. 123 Redo and undo Redo repeats committed changes lost from the buffer pool; undo reverses uncommitted changes that made it to disk. 124 Checkpoints and restart A checkpoint marks a safe point; recovery starts from the last checkpoint and replays the WAL forward from there. 125 Steal and no-steal Steal: dirty pages from uncommitted transactions may be written to disk (requires undo). No-steal: only committed pages are flushed (simpler). 126 Force and no-force Force: committed pages are flushed at commit time (slow). No-force: flush lazily (fast, but requires redo on crash). 127 Crash-safe commits A transaction is committed when its COMMIT record is flushed to the WAL — not when data pages hit disk. 128 Recovering unfinished work ARIES (and similar algorithms) use three passes: analysis (identify dirty pages), redo (replay history), undo (roll back losers). 129 Why recovery shapes design Recovery requirements drive WAL structure, buffer pool policies, and checkpoint frequency — they cannot be an afterthought. Reveal: How do these ideas connect to the next chapter? Chapter 13 closed Part III by showing how a single database survives crashes. But what happens when one machine is not enough — when your data is too large, your traffic too high, or your users too spread out? Chapter 14 opens Part IV: Distributed Systems. You will learn the difference between replication and sharding, why partition keys matter, and the fundamental truth that distribution trades simplicity for scale. Everything you learned about single-node reliability now gets extended across multiple nodes. ← Previous Why recovery shapes design Next → Why distribute a database

title: Why distribute a database
chapter: Chapter 14. From One Machine to Many
text: For most of this guide, a single machine ran everything: one disk, one set of CPUs, one database engine answering every query. That model works remarkably well until it doesn't. This chapter explains the pressures that push databases beyond a single node — and why "just get a bigger server" is rarely the right answer for long. The Three Limits of a Single Machine A single-node database fails in three distinct ways. 1. Capacity: you run out of storage or RAM A relational table with a billion rows will eventually exceed what one disk can hold efficiently. In-memory databases run out of RAM even sooner. At some point there is simply no bigger machine available, or the cost of buying one exceeds the value of the data it holds. 2. Throughput: you run out of write bandwidth Each additional write must flow through one set of I/O paths. A busy e-commerce site during a flash sale might need to record tens of thousands of orders per second. A single spinning disk tops out around 200 random writes/s; even an NVMe SSD maxes out in the low hundreds of thousands. When write load exceeds what one node can absorb, queries queue up and latency spikes. 3. Availability: one machine means one point of failure Hardware fails. Kernels crash. Power is cut. A single-node database goes down when its host goes down — sometimes for minutes, sometimes for hours. For many applications, even five minutes of downtime per month is unacceptable. Single-node limits (left) and how distributing across multiple nodes addresses each one (right). Scaling Up vs. Scaling Out The instinctive response to hitting a limit is to buy better hardware — more RAM, faster CPUs, bigger disks. This is called vertical scaling (or "scaling up"). It works, and should often be your first move, because a single powerful machine is much simpler to operate than a cluster. But vertical scaling has a ceiling: Approach What it buys you Hard limit Vertical (scale up) More resources on one machine Cost and hardware maximums Horizontal (scale out) More machines in a cluster Theoretically unbounded, but complex Once you cannot fit the workload on one machine at a reasonable cost, you scale out : add more machines and let the database engine spread work across them. This is what "distributed database" means — a system that looks like one database to your application but runs on many physical or virtual nodes. Note: The cloud has blurred this line. Services like Amazon RDS offer instances with 128 vCPUs and 4 TB RAM. Vertical scaling now goes much further than it did on-premise. Still, for global companies processing millions of events per second, even the largest cloud instance is not enough. Latency and Geography There is a fourth motivation that pure capacity arguments miss: the speed of light . A database server in Frankfurt takes roughly 150 ms to respond to a user in Sydney — not because the software is slow, but because the network round-trip across half the planet takes that long. For interactive applications, 150 ms per query is painful. For five sequential queries it becomes a second of visible lag. Distributing a database geographically — placing nodes close to the users who query them — can cut that 150 ms down to 10–20 ms. This is geo-distribution , and it is a key reason why global web services run database clusters across multiple continents. What You Gain (and What You Give Up) Distribution is not free. The rest of Part IV covers the tradeoffs in detail, but here is the preview: Benefit Cost More storage Data must be partitioned — deciding where each row lives is non-trivial Higher throughput Coordinating writes across nodes adds latency and complexity Fault tolerance The system must detect failures and route around them automatically Geo-distribution Keeping copies of data consistent across continents is a hard problem (CAP theorem) The goal of the next chapters is to give you the vocabulary and mental models to reason about those tradeoffs — starting with the two core techniques: replication (keeping copies of the same data on multiple nodes) and sharding (splitting data across nodes so each node owns a slice). Reveal: When is a single-node database still the right choice? Almost always — for new projects. Distributed databases are complex to operate, debug, and reason about. If your dataset fits on one machine (even a moderately sized cloud instance) and your write throughput is below the node's I/O ceiling, a single well-tuned database with regular backups and a read replica is simpler, cheaper, and more reliable than a cluster. Distribute only when a real, measured limit forces you to. ← Previous Why recovery shapes design Next → Replication and sharding

title: Replication and sharding
chapter: Chapter 14. From One Machine to Many
text: Replication copies the same data everywhere (for fault tolerance and read scale). Sharding splits data across nodes (for write scale and data volume). Often used together. When a single database server can no longer keep up — because there's too much traffic, too much data, or you can't afford it going down — you spread the work across multiple machines. Two fundamental techniques make this possible: replication (copying the same data to more than one node) and sharding (splitting different data across different nodes). They solve different problems and are often used together. Replication: Copies for Resilience and Read Scale Replication means keeping identical (or near-identical) copies of your data on multiple servers. The most common setup is leader–follower (also called primary–replica or master–slave): Leader accepts all writes. Followers receive a stream of changes from the leader and apply them, keeping their own copy up to date. Reads can be served by any follower, spreading the load. Leader–follower replication: all writes go to the leader, which streams changes to followers; reads can be served by any node. Synchronous vs. Asynchronous Replication This distinction is critical: Mode How it works Tradeoff Synchronous Leader waits for follower to confirm before ack'ing the write Durable — follower is always up to date; but write latency increases Asynchronous Leader ack's the write immediately; follower catches up later Faster writes; follower may lag behind (replication lag) Replication lag means a follower might serve slightly stale data. If a user writes a record and immediately reads it from a follower, they might not see their own write. This is a form of eventual consistency — followers will catch up, but there's no guarantee of when. Note: Most production setups use asynchronous replication for performance but keep at least one synchronous follower for durability. PostgreSQL calls these "synchronous standby" replicas. Failover If the leader crashes, a follower can be promoted to leader. This is called failover . Automated failover is convenient but tricky: a misconfigured system can elect two leaders simultaneously ("split-brain"), causing data divergence. Reliable failover requires careful coordination — tools like Patroni (Postgres) and Orchestrator (MySQL) handle this. Sharding: Splitting Data for Write Scale Replication gives you more read capacity and redundancy, but every node still holds the entire dataset. When data grows too large for one machine, or write throughput exceeds what one leader can handle, you need sharding (also called horizontal partitioning ). Sharding divides rows across nodes by a shard key . For example, a users table sharded by user_id might place users 1–1 000 000 on shard A, users 1 000 001–2 000 000 on shard B, and so on. -- Conceptual view: each shard holds a slice of the full table -- Shard A SELECT * FROM users WHERE user_id BETWEEN 1 AND 1000000; -- Shard B SELECT * FROM users WHERE user_id BETWEEN 1000001 AND 2000000; A router (sometimes called a proxy or query coordinator) sits in front of the shards, inspects the shard key in each query, and forwards it to the right node. Choosing a Shard Key The shard key determines how evenly work is distributed: Strategy Idea Watch out for Range Consecutive key values go to the same shard Hot spots if recent data is read heavily (e.g., time-series by date) Hash Hash of the key determines the shard Even spread; range queries must hit all shards Directory A lookup table maps keys to shards Flexible; the lookup table itself can become a bottleneck Hot spots are the main danger: if one shard receives most of the traffic (because a celebrity's posts are all on shard 3, for instance), the system is no better than before. What Sharding Complicates Cross-shard joins : If two related rows live on different shards, the database can't do an ordinary join — the application or router must fetch from both and combine results. Cross-shard transactions : Maintaining ACID guarantees across shards requires distributed protocols like 2-phase commit (2PC), which adds latency and failure modes. Re-sharding : As data grows, you may need to split shards. Moving data between nodes without downtime is operationally complex. Note: These complexities are why sharding is usually a last resort. Vertical scaling (a bigger single machine), read replicas, and caching can defer the need for sharding considerably. Replication + Sharding Together Real systems often combine both: each shard has its own leader and one or more followers. This provides both horizontal data scale (sharding) and read scale plus redundancy (replication) simultaneously. Reveal: If you shard by user_id and a query filters only by email, which shards must the router consult? All of them. Because the router doesn't know which shard holds a given email address, it must broadcast the query to every shard and merge the results. This is called a scatter-gather query and is much more expensive than a single-shard lookup. It's a key reason why choosing the right shard key — one that matches your most common query patterns — matters so much. The interactive example below lets you simulate a simple sharding scheme: a hash on user_id mod 3 decides which logical shard each row belongs to. Interactive SQL · Simulating sharding with hash mod ← Previous Why distribute a database Next → Shared-nothing systems

title: Shared-nothing systems
chapter: Chapter 14. From One Machine to Many
text: The previous page introduced replication and sharding as two techniques for spreading data across machines. Shared-nothing architecture is the design philosophy that unifies them — and it's the reason modern distributed databases can scale to thousands of nodes without a single point of contention. What "Shared Nothing" Means In a shared-nothing system, every node is completely independent. Each node has: Its own CPU and memory — no shared RAM bus. Its own local disk — no shared storage array or SAN. A network connection to other nodes — the only communication channel. No node can directly read another node's memory or disk. To get data from a peer, a node must send a network message and wait for a reply. This stands in contrast to two older alternatives: Architecture What is shared Bottleneck Shared memory RAM across CPUs Memory bus; OS lock contention Shared disk Storage (e.g., SAN/NAS) I/O bandwidth; distributed locking Shared nothing Nothing (network only) Network latency; cross-node coordination The key insight: networks scale horizontally and cheaply. Adding a node adds independent capacity rather than competing for a fixed shared resource. A shared-nothing cluster: three nodes, each with private CPU, RAM, and disk, communicating only through a network switch. Why It Scales — and Where It Hurts Shared-nothing's big win is linear scalability for workloads that map cleanly onto shards. If each node handles 10 000 writes/second and you need 50 000, add four more nodes. No shared bottleneck limits you. But the architecture moves complexity into the application or middleware layer: Cross-node queries are expensive. A JOIN between two tables that live on different nodes requires one node to pull data across the network. Some systems push part of the computation down to the remote node (a "push-down"); others ship entire result sets and join in memory. Either way, it costs network round trips. Distributed transactions are hard. ACID guarantees across two nodes require a coordination protocol — usually two-phase commit (2PC) . If any node fails mid-transaction, the others may block waiting for a decision. Many shared-nothing systems weaken isolation guarantees to avoid this cost, offering eventual consistency instead. Rebalancing is painful. When you add a node, some data must migrate from existing nodes to the new one. During migration the system must continue serving queries, which means careful bookkeeping about which version of a row is authoritative. Note: Systems like Google Spanner and CockroachDB have engineered around some of these pain points using Paxos-based consensus and globally synchronized clocks — but they don't eliminate the fundamental costs of coordination; they just manage them more gracefully. Shared Nothing in Practice Most databases you'll encounter in production are at least partially shared-nothing: PostgreSQL with Citus / Patroni — Postgres extended with a sharding coordinator layer. Cassandra / DynamoDB — pure peer-to-peer shared-nothing; no single leader. Snowflake / Redshift — shared-nothing compute nodes but with shared cloud object storage (a hybrid, sometimes called "shared-disk for storage, shared-nothing for compute"). Vitess — shared-nothing sharding middleware on top of MySQL, used at YouTube. The label "shared-nothing" sometimes gets blurred when cloud object storage (S3, GCS) plays the role of a durable shared layer beneath independent compute nodes. That hybrid trades some of the coordination simplicity for operational convenience — you can resize compute without migrating data. The query below lets you see scatter-gather behavior (querying all shards) vs. a targeted single-shard lookup, using the same user_id % 3 sharding scheme from the previous page. Notice how the CASE expression mimics what a router would do. Interactive SQL · Single-shard vs. scatter-gather Reveal: Why does a query on `status` hit all shards even when there are only a few distinct statuses? The shard key is user_id , not status . The router has no way to know which shards contain rows with status = 'shipped' without asking every one of them. This is the scatter-gather problem: any filter on a non-shard-key column requires a broadcast to all nodes, and the coordinator must merge the partial results. It's one reason choosing a shard key that matches your hottest query patterns is so important. ← Previous Replication and sharding Next → Partition keys

title: Partition keys
chapter: Chapter 14. From One Machine to Many
text: When you split a large table across multiple nodes (sharding), every row has to land somewhere . The partition key — sometimes called the shard key — is the column (or columns) whose value decides which node a row lives on. Choosing it well is one of the most consequential design decisions in a distributed system, because you can't easily change it later without moving every row. How a Partition Key Works The database (or a routing layer in front of it) takes the partition key value, runs it through a partitioning function , and maps the result to a shard. Three strategies are common: Strategy How rows are assigned Good for Watch out for Range Consecutive key values go to the same shard (e.g. user_id 1–1 M on shard A) Range scans, time-series Hot spots when new data concentrates on one shard Hash hash(key) mod N → shard index Even spread of random writes Range queries must scatter to all shards List Explicit mapping of values to shards (e.g. country → shard) Geographic locality Manual maintenance; skewed if one bucket is huge The diagram below shows all three strategies applied to the same orders table: Range, hash, and list partitioning applied to an orders table — each strategy routes rows to shards differently. Hot Spots: The Main Enemy A hot spot happens when one shard receives a disproportionate share of traffic. This defeats the whole point of sharding — you've still got one machine doing all the work. Common causes: Monotonically increasing keys (like auto-increment IDs or timestamps) with range partitioning: all new inserts land on the last shard. Skewed data with list partitioning: 80% of your orders might come from one region. Famous entities : a celebrity's account being accessed millions of times per second hits only one shard. Hash partitioning largely solves the monotonic-key problem because the hash scrambles the distribution. But it creates a new tradeoff: you lose the ability to do efficient range scans. Note: Some systems (like Apache Cassandra) add a random suffix to hot partition keys — for example, a celebrity user ID becomes user:42:0 , user:42:1 , … user:42:9 — then aggregate across them at read time. This is called write sharding or salting . Queries and the Partition Key The partition key shapes every query in your system: Key-aligned query (filter on the partition key): the router sends the query to exactly one shard. Fast and cheap. Scatter-gather query (filter on a non-partition column): the router broadcasts to all shards, waits for each to reply, then merges the results. Cost scales with the number of shards. This is why picking the partition key to match your most frequent query pattern matters so much. If 90% of your queries look up orders by user_id , partitioning on user_id makes those fast. If you instead partition on order_id and query by user_id , every lookup becomes a scatter-gather. The interactive example below lets you simulate both cases. The setup creates 12 orders across 4 logical shards, partitioned by user_id % 4 . Try the two queries and notice which shard each row must come from. Interactive SQL · Partition key alignment Partition Keys in Practice Real distributed databases make the partition key explicit in their data model: Apache Cassandra : you declare a PRIMARY KEY ((partition_key), clustering_cols) . The double-parenthesis part is the partition key; the clustering columns sort data within the partition. DynamoDB : every table has a mandatory partition key (and an optional sort key ). Queries without the partition key require a full-table scan via a Global Secondary Index. CockroachDB / Spanner : use range-based partitioning under the hood, but expose it through SQL PARTITION BY clauses on indexes. The recurring theme: you choose the partition key at schema-design time, and the system optimizes the data placement around it. Change it later and you're re-writing the whole table — which in a distributed system means shuffling gigabytes or terabytes across the network. Get it right up front. Reveal: Why can't you just index every column and avoid the scatter-gather problem? You can add secondary indexes, but in a distributed system a secondary index on a non-partition column is itself partitioned — and usually differently from the base table. A query on that column either hits a global secondary index (a separate distributed index that must be kept in sync with every write, adding latency and consistency complexity) or it still scatters to all shards. Either way there's a cost. Primary key lookups remain the only truly cheap, single-shard operation. ← Previous Shared-nothing systems Next → Cross-node queries

title: Cross-node queries
chapter: Chapter 14. From One Machine to Many
text: Once your data is spread across multiple nodes — whether by sharding, federation, or a mix of both — a new class of problem appears: what happens when a single query needs data that lives on more than one machine? That's a cross-node query , and handling it correctly is one of the harder engineering challenges in distributed databases. Why Cross-Node Queries Are Hard On a single machine, a join is straightforward: the engine reads both tables from local disk, builds a hash table or merge buffer in RAM, and returns the result. When the two tables are on different servers, none of that applies. The engine must: Decide which node coordinates the query (the query coordinator ). Send sub-queries or data requests to the relevant nodes ( remote scan ). Receive partial results over the network. Combine (merge, join, aggregate) those partial results locally. Return the final answer to the client. Every one of these steps adds latency, and the network — unlike a local memory bus — can be slow, lossy, and unpredictable. The Scatter-Gather Pattern The most common execution model for cross-node queries is scatter-gather : Scatter : The coordinator broadcasts a sub-query to all (or several) shards in parallel. Gather : Each shard executes its piece and sends back partial results. Merge : The coordinator combines the partial results into a final answer. Scatter-gather: the coordinator fans out a sub-query to every relevant shard in parallel, then merges the partial results before replying to the client. Scatter-gather works well for simple aggregations ( COUNT , SUM , MAX ) because each shard computes a partial aggregate and the coordinator combines them cheaply. It works poorly for large cross-shard joins , because the coordinator may have to receive millions of rows from multiple shards and join them in memory — doing the heavy lifting that each individual node could have done locally. Cross-Shard Joins in Practice Consider an e-commerce system where orders is sharded by order_id and customers is sharded by customer_id . A query like: SELECT c.name, COUNT(o.order_id) AS order_count FROM customers c JOIN orders o ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id GROUP BY c.name; ...is a cross-shard join. A given customer's row lives on one shard; their orders may be scattered across several others. Distributed databases handle this in a few ways: Strategy How it works Best when Broadcast join Coordinator sends one (small) table to every shard; each shard joins locally One side of the join is small enough to fit in memory Repartition join Both tables are reshuffled so matching keys land on the same node, then joined Neither side is small; keys are well-distributed Co-location Data is designed upfront so related rows share a shard key (e.g., customer_id ) Query patterns are known at design time Co-location is the most efficient — it eliminates network traffic entirely for the join — but it requires thinking about your access patterns before you shard. Note: Systems like Google Spanner, CockroachDB, and YugabyteDB have built-in query planners that choose between these strategies automatically. Older hand-rolled sharding setups leave this to the application layer. Distributed Aggregations Aggregates split cleanly across shards when they are decomposable — meaning the coordinator can combine partial results from each shard without seeing every row: COUNT(*) → sum the per-shard counts. SUM(amount) → sum the per-shard sums. MAX(price) → take the max of per-shard maxes. AVG(score) → not directly decomposable . Each shard must return (sum, count) separately; the coordinator divides the totals. A naive per-shard average averaged together gives the wrong answer. COUNT(DISTINCT …) is especially painful: exact distinct counts require shipping all values to one place (or using an approximation like HyperLogLog). The widget below lets you explore how a coordinator might combine partial aggregates. The two CTEs simulate results coming back from two different shards; the final query merges them the way a real coordinator would. Interactive SQL · Merging partial aggregates The Cost to Keep in Mind Cross-node queries introduce three costs that don't exist locally: Network latency — even fast data-center networks add milliseconds per hop. Data transfer volume — shuffling millions of rows between nodes for a join is expensive. Coordination overhead — the coordinator must track partial results, handle partial failures, and retry timed-out shards. The practical takeaway: design your shard key so that the most frequent, most latency-sensitive queries touch as few shards as possible . Cross-node queries are sometimes unavoidable, but they should be the exception, not the rule. Reveal: Why can't you just run ORDER BY … LIMIT 10 on each shard and merge the tops? Because the true global top 10 might not appear in any single shard's top 10. Imagine three shards each returning their top 10 rows — that's 30 rows total. The coordinator must sort all 30 and take the top 10. The safe rule: each shard must return the top N × number_of_shards rows (or the coordinator must do a full merge-sort). This is why OFFSET -based pagination is especially expensive in sharded systems — deep pages require fetching and discarding huge numbers of rows from every shard. ← Previous Partition keys Next → Rebalancing data

title: Rebalancing data
chapter: Chapter 14. From One Machine to Many
text: When you spread data across multiple nodes, the distribution is rarely perfect forever. Nodes are added to handle growth, removed when they fail or are retired, and some partitions naturally attract more traffic than others. Rebalancing is the process of moving data between nodes to restore an even distribution — keeping every machine carrying a fair share of the load without taking the cluster offline. Why Data Drifts Out of Balance In Chapter 13 you saw how partitioning splits a dataset into shards assigned to specific nodes. The assignment that was sensible last month may be a problem today for several reasons: Trigger What happens Node added The new node sits idle while existing nodes stay overloaded. Node removed / failure Its partitions must move somewhere or they become unreachable. Hot partition One key range attracts disproportionate traffic (e.g., a viral user). Data growth A partition that started small may now dwarf the others. The goal of rebalancing is always the same: spread both data and request load roughly evenly, move as little data across the network as possible, and keep the cluster serving reads and writes throughout the process. Strategies for Rebalancing Fixed number of partitions Create far more partitions than nodes from the very beginning — say, 1000 partitions for a 10-node cluster. Each node owns ~100 partitions. When you add an eleventh node, simply move a few whole partitions to it; no data inside a partition is reorganized. Adding a node moves whole partitions (P4, P8, …) to the new node — no partition is split or reorganized internally. Trade-off: you must choose the total number of partitions upfront. Too few and you cannot distribute finely; too many and the overhead of tracking them grows. Dynamic partitioning Used by databases like HBase and RethinkDB. Partitions split automatically when they exceed a size threshold (say, 10 GB) and merge when they shrink. The cluster tracks partition boundaries in real time, so you never have to pre-commit to a partition count. Consistent hashing (virtual nodes) Systems like Cassandra and DynamoDB assign each node multiple positions ("virtual nodes" or vnodes ) on a hash ring. When a node is added, it takes over a few vnodes from its neighbors. When a node leaves, its vnodes are handed to the remaining nodes. The key insight: only the data belonging to those specific vnodes moves — everything else stays put. Note: Consistent hashing dramatically reduces the fraction of keys that need to move when the cluster changes size. With a naive modulo hash ( key % N ), changing N from 10 to 11 remaps almost every key. With consistent hashing, only ~1/N of the keys move. Automatic vs. Manual Rebalancing Most production databases offer a choice: Automatic rebalancing — the cluster decides when and how to move data (e.g., Cassandra's nodetool repair , MongoDB's balancer). Convenient, but a rebalance can spike network and disk I/O exactly when the cluster is already under load. Manual rebalancing — an operator triggers moves explicitly. This gives you control over timing (schedule it during off-peak hours) at the cost of operational complexity. Many systems land in the middle: the cluster detects imbalance and proposes a rebalancing plan, but a human must approve or schedule it. What Moves During a Rebalance During a rebalance the cluster streams partition data from one node to another. While the transfer is in progress: The source node continues serving reads and writes for the partition. New writes are also forwarded to the destination node so it stays current. Once the transfer is complete, the routing table is updated and the destination node becomes the primary owner. This "copy then switch" approach keeps the partition online throughout the move — clients never see a gap in availability. Reveal: Why not just stop writes during a rebalance? Stopping writes would guarantee the source and destination end up identical, which sounds clean — but it means downtime. Real clusters can't afford to go offline every time a node is added or removed. Instead, databases track which writes arrived after the copy started (via a replication log or version counter) and apply those delta writes to the destination before doing the final cutover. The window where both copies must agree is shrunk to milliseconds. Key Takeaways Strategy Partition count Best for Fixed partitions Chosen upfront, never changes Predictable workloads, simple ops Dynamic partitioning Grows/shrinks with data Datasets with unpredictable size Consistent hashing / vnodes Scales with node count Large clusters, frequent membership changes Rebalancing is not a one-time event — it is an ongoing background process in any long-lived distributed database. Understanding it helps you predict when your cluster will be most vulnerable to latency spikes and gives you the tools to schedule that risk intelligently. ← Previous Cross-node queries Next → Hot partitions

title: Hot partitions
chapter: Chapter 14. From One Machine to Many
text: When you shard a database, the goal is to spread work evenly across nodes. But data and traffic are rarely uniform. Some keys attract vastly more reads and writes than others, and the shard that holds those keys ends up doing the lion's share of the work. This is a hot partition (also called a hot shard ) — and it's one of the most common performance traps in distributed databases. What Makes a Partition Hot? A partition gets hot when a disproportionate fraction of requests land on it. The cause is almost always a skewed shard key — one whose distribution in real traffic doesn't match the distribution of key values. Three classic patterns: Pattern Example Why it's hot Celebrity / viral row A tweet from a pop star; a Reddit post on the front page Millions of reads on a single post_id or user_id Monotonic key order_id auto-increment, date-range sharding All new writes go to the latest shard; older shards sit idle Low-cardinality shard key Sharding by country_code with 80% of users in one country One shard is permanently overloaded by design The monotonic-key case deserves emphasis: if you range-shard by a timestamp or an auto-incrementing ID, every new write hits the same shard. The "last" shard becomes a sequential bottleneck no matter how many shards you add. A hot partition absorbs most traffic while other shards sit idle. Adding shards doesn't fix skew — it only dilutes the cool ones further. Diagnosing a Hot Partition Hot partitions announce themselves through symptoms that look like general overload but are actually localized: One node pegs CPU or disk I/O while others are under-utilized. Query latency spikes on a specific key range, not system-wide. Write queues or lock waits accumulate on one shard only. Most distributed databases expose per-shard metrics. In systems like Cassandra or DynamoDB you can see partition-level read/write units; in PostgreSQL-based sharding setups (like Citus), you can query shard statistics directly. How to Fix a Hot Partition There's no single cure — the right fix depends on whether traffic is skewed on reads, writes, or both. Add a Random Suffix (Write Skew) For write-heavy hot keys, shard key salting spreads one logical key across multiple physical partitions. Instead of writing everything for user_id = 42 to one shard, you append a random suffix (0–N) and distribute writes: -- Conceptual: write to one of 4 sub-shards at random INSERT INTO events (partition_key, user_id, event_type, ts) VALUES ( '42_' || (ABS(RANDOM()) % 4), -- e.g. '42_2' 42, 'page_view', CURRENT_TIMESTAMP ); The downside: reads must now query all N sub-shards and merge results. This trades write scalability for read complexity. Cache Hot Keys (Read Skew) When a handful of rows are read by everyone (a trending post, a product on sale), add an application-level cache (Redis, Memcached) in front of the database. The shard never sees the bulk of reads. Re-shard with a Better Key If the root cause is a poor shard key choice, the real fix is to pick a different one — typically a hash of the key rather than a range. Hash sharding scatters keys pseudo-randomly, so no single shard accumulates sequential writes or popular key ranges. Note: Re-sharding live data is expensive — it means migrating rows between nodes without downtime. This is why choosing a good shard key up front is so important. Many teams treat shard key selection as an architectural decision that's very difficult to undo. Use a Time-Based Expiry Strategy For time-series or event data, some databases use time-bucketed partitions : instead of keeping all recent writes on the "last" shard, new partitions are pre-created and rotated on a schedule. InfluxDB, TimescaleDB, and AWS DynamoDB time-series patterns all use variants of this idea. The widget below lets you explore key skew in a small dataset. Notice how user_id % 3 (hash sharding) distributes rows evenly, while sorting by a created_at timestamp reveals how monotonic writes would pile onto the latest partition. Interactive SQL · Simulating hot partitions The query makes the skew visible: user 999 owns nearly all events, so one shard absorbs most requests regardless of how many shards you add. That's the essence of the hot partition problem — it's a data distribution issue, not a capacity issue. ← Previous Rebalancing data Next → Failure becomes normal

title: Failure becomes normal
chapter: Chapter 14. From One Machine to Many
text: On a single machine, a hardware failure is a crisis — the whole system is down. Spread your database across dozens or hundreds of machines and the math changes completely: at that scale, something is almost always broken. A disk dies, a network switch reboots, a power supply flickers, a kernel panic takes out one node at 3 a.m. Distributed databases are built on the recognition that partial failure is the default state , not an edge case to handle someday. Why Failure Is Inevitable at Scale Consider a back-of-the-envelope calculation. If a single server has 99.9% uptime (about 8.7 hours of downtime per year), a cluster of 100 such servers will see at least one node unavailable roughly 9.5% of the time — nearly one day in ten. Real hardware failure rates, network partitions, and software bugs make the true number higher. Cluster size Prob. all nodes healthy Expected outages/year on one node 1 node 99.9% ~8.7 hours 10 nodes 99.0% at least one node down ~87 hours 100 nodes 90.5% constantly managing incidents Note: These numbers assume fully independent failure probabilities, which is optimistic. Shared power, shared network racks, and correlated software deployments mean real clusters see correlated failures — multiple nodes going down for the same reason at the same time. The implication is stark: a distributed database that requires all nodes to be healthy before it can serve requests is not a real distributed database. It must make progress even when some nodes are unavailable. The Failure Taxonomy Not all failures look the same, and a good distributed system must reason about each kind differently. Three failure modes a distributed database must handle: a crash-stop node goes silent permanently; a partition isolates a live node temporarily; a Byzantine node sends incorrect but plausible responses. Crash-stop (fail-stop): The node halts and sends no further messages. This is the easiest failure to reason about — silence means dead. Most crash recovery protocols assume this model. Network partition: The node is alive and healthy, but network links are severed. It can still serve local reads and accept writes — it just cannot talk to the rest of the cluster. This is far more common than outright crashes, and far more dangerous, because the isolated node and the main cluster may both believe they are the authoritative copy. Byzantine failure: The node behaves arbitrarily — sending stale data, returning wrong answers, or replying to some nodes but not others. This is the hardest model to handle and requires protocols (like Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance) that assume up to ⌊(n−1)/3⌋ traitors. Most databases in trusted data-center environments skip Byzantine tolerance entirely and focus on crash and partition failures. What "Handling" Failure Means A distributed database cannot simply prevent failures. Instead it must detect them and respond without stalling the whole system. The two core tools are: Timeouts and heartbeats. Nodes send periodic "I'm alive" pings. If a heartbeat is missed for longer than a threshold (say, 10 seconds), the coordinator marks the node suspected failed and stops routing requests to it. The tricky part: a slow network looks identical to a dead node from the outside, so the timeout threshold is always a trade-off between false positives (declaring a slow node dead) and slow detection (waiting too long to give up). Replication as the survival strategy. The reason a single-node outage doesn't bring down the whole cluster is that data is replicated across multiple nodes. If node A holds copies of data that node B and C also hold, then A's failure is survivable — B and C can continue serving reads and accepts writes. How many simultaneous failures a cluster can tolerate depends on its replication factor and the quorum rules it uses (covered in the next pages). Note: Replication and fault tolerance come with a catch: you now have multiple copies of data , and those copies can disagree. Every distributed database has to decide what to do when two nodes have conflicting versions of the same row — a question that leads directly into consistency models and the CAP theorem, coming up soon. Try the query below to model a simple heartbeat table and see how a coordinator might identify which nodes are suspected failed versus healthy. Interactive SQL · Detecting failed nodes via heartbeats Try changing the timeout thresholds (30, 10) or adjusting a node's last_seen_at timestamp to see how the coordinator's view shifts. Notice that from the coordinator's perspective, a partitioned node (alive but unreachable) looks identical to a crashed one — both just stop sending heartbeats. ← Previous Hot partitions Next → New tradeoffs at scale

title: New tradeoffs at scale
chapter: Chapter 14. From One Machine to Many
text: Running a database on a single machine is a well-understood problem. You tune your indexes, optimize your queries, and add more RAM when things slow down. The moment you distribute data across multiple machines, though, a new class of constraints appears — constraints that don't come from hardware limits or bad SQL, but from the fundamental physics of networked systems. Understanding these tradeoffs is the foundation for every design decision in distributed databases. The Three Pressures: Consistency, Availability, Partition Tolerance In 2000, Eric Brewer observed that distributed systems face three desirable properties, and — as formally proven by Gilbert and Lynch in 2002 — no system can guarantee all three simultaneously when a network partition occurs. This is the CAP theorem. Property What it means Example guarantee Consistency (C) Every read sees the most recent write (or an error) All nodes agree on the current account balance Availability (A) Every request gets a response — no timeouts, no errors The system answers even if some nodes are down Partition tolerance (P) The system keeps working even when network messages are lost or delayed The cluster survives a broken network link between data centers The catch: partition tolerance is not optional . Networks do drop packets and split clusters — that's the real world. So every practical distributed database must choose between C and A when a partition occurs: CP systems (e.g., HBase, etcd, Zookeeper) refuse to answer rather than return stale data. During a partition they become unavailable to preserve consistency. AP systems (e.g., Cassandra, DynamoDB in eventual-consistency mode) keep answering but may return data that isn't yet fully up to date. They stay available and reconcile divergence later. Note: CAP is often oversimplified. Real systems don't sit neatly in one bucket — most offer tunable behavior. Cassandra's consistency level ( ONE , QUORUM , ALL ) lets you slide along the CP–AP spectrum per query. Think of CAP as a map of the territory, not a rigid classification. The CAP triangle: distributed systems must pick two of three. Since partitions are unavoidable, the real choice is between consistency and availability during a split. Latency vs. Throughput at Scale Scaling out adds a second pressure that CAP doesn't fully capture: latency . On one machine, a write commits and is immediately visible to the next read — the two operations share the same memory bus. Across machines they communicate over a network, and that changes the math dramatically. Operation Single machine Same data center (LAN) Cross data center (WAN) Memory read ~100 ns — — Disk read (SSD) ~100 µs — — Network round-trip — ~0.5 ms 30–150 ms Synchronous replication ~1 ms ~2–5 ms 30–150 ms per write Synchronous replication — waiting for every replica to confirm a write before acknowledging the client — guarantees consistency but adds latency proportional to the slowest replica. Asynchronous replication acknowledges the client immediately and propagates the write in the background, keeping latency low but leaving a window where replicas can diverge. This is the core distributed latency trade-off: strong consistency costs time; low latency risks staleness . The Quorum Shortcut Most production systems don't choose between "wait for all replicas" and "wait for none." They use a quorum : wait for a majority to confirm, then return. With a replication factor of 3 (three copies of every piece of data), the rules are: W = 2 — a write is confirmed once 2 of 3 replicas acknowledge it. R = 2 — a read consults 2 of 3 replicas and returns the newest value. W + R > N (2 + 2 > 3) — because write and read sets must overlap, at least one replica in every read has seen every confirmed write. This gives you consistency without requiring all nodes. The surviving node that missed a write will receive it during anti-entropy (background reconciliation). You tolerate one replica failure with no impact on reads or writes. Reveal: What happens if W + R ≤ N? If write and read quorums don't overlap, it's possible to read from replicas that haven't yet received a recent write — you get stale reads even though the write was "confirmed." For example, with N=3, W=1, R=1: a write goes to replica 1, a subsequent read hits replica 2, which hasn't gotten the write yet. The reader sees old data. This is the intended behavior of eventual-consistency (AP) configurations — you trade freshness for speed. Operational Complexity: The Hidden Cost Beyond CAP and latency, distributing a database introduces a third class of tradeoffs that are easy to underestimate: operational complexity . A single-node database has one failure mode (it's down or it's up). A distributed database has many: Which node is authoritative for a key right now? How do you add a node without downtime? How do you handle a network split that lasted 90 seconds — which writes from each side win? How do you debug a query that touches 12 shards and one of them returned an error? Every guarantee that was "free" on one machine — serializability, a single clock, a single write-ahead log — becomes a protocol that must be designed, implemented, tested, and operated. This is why the first rule of distributed systems is: don't distribute until you must . Vertical scaling (bigger machine) stays simpler longer than most people expect, and sharding or replication should only appear when a single machine genuinely cannot meet your requirements. Try the query below to explore how quorum math works across different replication configurations. Interactive SQL · Quorum calculator Edit the W and R values in the setup, or add your own row, to see how consistency and fault-tolerance shift. Notice that you can never get both write_fault_tolerance and read_fault_tolerance to be as high as N - 1 at the same time — that's CAP and quorum math in action. ← Previous Failure becomes normal Next → Chapter 14 Summary: What We Learned

title: Chapter 14 Summary: What We Learned
chapter: Chapter 14. From One Machine to Many
text: Page Topic Takeaway in One Sentence 131 Why distribute a database A single machine hits limits: data size, throughput, or availability — distribution spreads load and tolerates failures. 132 Replication and sharding Replication = copies of the same data (availability, read scale). Sharding = splitting data across nodes (write scale, capacity). 133 Shared-nothing systems Each node owns its own CPU, memory, and disk — scales horizontally but requires careful data placement. 134 Partition keys The partition key determines which node owns a row; choose it to spread load evenly and support your most frequent queries. 135 Cross-node queries Queries that span partitions require scatter-gather or distributed joins — significantly slower than single-node queries. 136 Rebalancing data Adding or removing nodes requires moving partitions; consistent hashing minimizes the amount of data moved. 137 Hot partitions A partition that receives disproportionate traffic becomes a bottleneck — choose partition keys to avoid hotspots. 138 Failure becomes normal With many nodes, some are always down — the system must handle partial failures gracefully. 139 New tradeoffs at scale CAP theorem, latency vs consistency, operational complexity, and network costs all become front-line concerns. Reveal: How do these ideas connect to the next chapter? Chapter 14 painted the big picture of distributed databases: replication for availability and read scale, sharding for write scale and capacity. Chapter 15 zooms in on replication specifically — the most common approach to keeping data available. You will learn leader-follower topologies, synchronous versus asynchronous replication, how failover works, and the replication lag that every read-replica application must handle. Replication is conceptually simple but operationally demanding. ← Previous New tradeoffs at scale Next → Leader-follower replication

title: Leader-follower replication
text: Leader-follower: one node accepts all writes and streams changes to followers. Followers serve reads. On leader failure, a follower is promoted to become the new leader. When a single database server fails, everything that depends on it fails too. Replication solves this by keeping copies of your data on multiple machines. Leader-follower replication (also called primary-replica or master-slave replication) is the most widely deployed pattern — used by PostgreSQL, MySQL, MongoDB, and many others. How It Works The idea is simple: one node is designated the leader (primary). All writes go to the leader. The leader records every change and streams those changes to one or more followers (replicas). Followers apply the changes in the same order and stay in sync. Clients send all writes to the leader. The leader streams a replication log to followers, which serve read queries. The mechanism used to stream changes varies by database: Method How it works Used by WAL shipping Send the raw write-ahead log segments PostgreSQL (streaming replication) Row-based binlog Log each row's before/after values MySQL (row-based replication) Logical log Log high-level operations decoded from WAL PostgreSQL logical replication Trigger-based Application triggers write to a change table Older or heterogeneous setups Synchronous vs. Asynchronous Replication The crucial question is: when does the leader confirm a write to the client? Asynchronous replication — the leader commits and replies immediately, then sends the log to followers in the background. Fastest, but if the leader crashes before followers catch up, the un-replicated writes are lost. Synchronous replication — the leader waits for at least one follower to acknowledge before replying. The write is durable on two machines, but every commit pays the network round-trip latency. Most systems use a hybrid: one synchronous follower (guaranteeing durability) and the rest asynchronous (for throughput). PostgreSQL calls this synchronous_standby_names . Note: "Semi-synchronous" replication in MySQL similarly waits for at least one replica to write the event to its relay log — not necessarily apply it — before the leader acks the client. Failover When the leader fails, one follower must be promoted to become the new leader. This process — failover — involves: Detecting that the leader is gone (heartbeat timeout). Electing the most up-to-date follower as the new leader. Reconfiguring clients to send writes to the new leader. Old followers starting to replicate from the new leader. Failover sounds straightforward but hides serious edge cases: The old leader may still be alive (network partition, not a crash) — now you have two nodes that both think they're leader , called split-brain . Both accept writes, and the data diverges. The promoted follower may be behind: any writes acknowledged by the old leader but not yet replicated are simply lost if you discard them, or must be re-applied somehow. Tools like Patroni (PostgreSQL) and MHA (MySQL) automate failover while managing these hazards. Read Scaling and Replication Lag Because followers serve reads, adding replicas lets you scale read throughput horizontally. This is why read-heavy applications (analytics dashboards, product listings) commonly run queries against replicas. The catch is replication lag — followers apply changes after the leader, so a read from a follower may return slightly stale data. The time gap is usually milliseconds on a healthy LAN, but can grow under load or network issues. Note: This creates a weaker consistency guarantee called eventual consistency : followers will eventually catch up, but there is no guarantee about when . Chapter 16 examines consistency models in depth. A common symptom is "read your own writes" breakage: a user submits a form (write goes to leader), then immediately reloads (read hits a lagging follower) and sees the old data. The fix is to route writes and the reads immediately following them to the leader, or use a replication position token to wait until a follower has caught up to the write. Below is an interactive query that simulates what a replication lag monitoring view might look like — databases like PostgreSQL expose pg_stat_replication for this purpose. Interactive SQL · Replication lag simulation ← Previous New tradeoffs at scale Next → Synchronous vs asynchronous replication

title: Synchronous vs asynchronous replication
text: When a leader node accepts a write, it must eventually send that change to its replicas. The critical question is: when does the leader tell the client "done"? Does it wait until at least one replica has confirmed the write, or does it acknowledge immediately and let replication happen in the background? That choice — synchronous versus asynchronous — shapes every durability and latency tradeoff in a replicated system. The Two Modes Synchronous replication means the leader waits for the replica to confirm it has received (and usually persisted) the write before acknowledging success to the client. The client's COMMIT does not return until the replica says "got it." Asynchronous replication means the leader acknowledges the client as soon as the write is durable locally. The replica will catch up eventually — maybe milliseconds later, maybe seconds later if the network is busy — but the client is not held waiting. Synchronous replication holds the client until the replica acknowledges; asynchronous replication acknowledges immediately and replicates in the background, creating a lag window. Tradeoffs at a Glance Synchronous Asynchronous Durability on crash Strong — replica already has the data Weaker — unconfirmed writes can be lost Write latency Higher — adds one network round-trip Lower — no waiting for replica Replica availability Required — a slow replica stalls writes Optional — leader proceeds regardless Read-your-writes on replica Guaranteed immediately after commit Not guaranteed; reads may be stale Replication lag Zero (by definition) Variable; can be seconds or more Note: Most production systems use a hybrid approach: one synchronous replica for durability, and additional asynchronous replicas for read scale. PostgreSQL calls this "synchronous_standby_names" — you nominate one standby that must confirm each commit; all others replicate asynchronously. Why Lag Matters in Practice With asynchronous replication, a replica lags behind the leader by some number of seconds. This creates real problems: Read-your-writes violation. A user posts a comment, then immediately reloads the page. If the read hits a replica, they may not see their own comment. Failover data loss. If the leader crashes before a write is replicated, promoting the replica means that write is silently gone. Stale metrics. Analytics queries on a lagging replica undercount recent events. The lag is not constant. Under low load, a healthy replica might be milliseconds behind. During a network hiccup, writes queue up and lag can spike to minutes. A replica that is too far behind is called unavailable for consistent reads in some systems, and can be taken out of the read pool automatically. Semi-Synchronous Replication MySQL's default (since 5.7) is semi-synchronous : the leader waits for at least one replica to acknowledge receipt, but not necessarily to flush to disk. This is a middle ground — you avoid the scenario where no replica has the data, without fully blocking on a disk flush. If no replica acknowledges within a timeout, MySQL falls back to asynchronous mode automatically. The distinction matters: Receive-and-buffer (semi-sync): replica has the data in memory but maybe not on disk. Still survives a leader crash if the replica doesn't crash simultaneously. Receive-and-fsync (fully synchronous): replica has flushed to disk. Survives even if both machines lose power simultaneously. Use this widget to explore what a replication lag monitor might look like. The query identifies which replicas are dangerously behind. Interactive SQL · Replication lag monitor Try changing replica_lsn values or switching a replica's mode from 'async' to 'sync' to see how the status column shifts. Notice that only the synchronous replica is guaranteed to be current — the others reflect the tradeoff you accept when you prioritise write throughput over read consistency. Reveal: If the leader crashes right now, which replicas can safely be promoted without data loss? Only replica-1 (sync mode, zero lag). Replica-2 through replica-4 have not yet applied all writes — promoting any of them would silently lose the writes they haven't received. In a real system, the failover controller must check each replica's log position and pick the one closest to the leader, then accept that some commits may be rolled back. ← Previous Leader-follower replication Next → Read replicas

title: Read replicas
text: When a database's write volume is modest but read volume is enormous — think a news site, an analytics dashboard, or a product catalog — the bottleneck isn't the leader's ability to accept writes, it's the leader's CPU and I/O being consumed by thousands of concurrent SELECT queries. Read replicas are the standard answer: spin up additional followers, route read traffic to them, and let the leader focus on writes. What a Read Replica Is A read replica is a follower that is configured to accept SELECT queries from application clients. It continuously replays the leader's replication stream to stay current, but it refuses INSERT , UPDATE , and DELETE — those always go to the leader. Read replica topology: the primary handles all writes and streams changes to replicas; reads are distributed across replicas. Most relational databases support this out of the box. PostgreSQL uses streaming replication ; MySQL calls them read replicas in both the upstream and managed flavors (Amazon RDS, Google Cloud SQL); MongoDB exposes secondaries in a replica set for reads via readPreference . Replication Lag and Stale Reads Because replication is almost always asynchronous , there is a window — often milliseconds, sometimes seconds under load — between when the primary commits a write and when a replica applies it. During that window a read from the replica returns stale data . Scenario What can go wrong User changes their email, then immediately views their profile Profile page reads from replica — shows the old email An order is placed, then an analytics query counts today's orders Count may be off by one or more A row is deleted, then a read checks existence Row might still appear on a lagging replica This is read-your-own-write inconsistency — a common gotcha. Strategies to handle it: Route sensitive reads to the primary. After a user writes, send their own reads to the primary for a short window (e.g., 1–2 seconds), then fall back to replicas. Track replication position. The primary returns a log sequence number (LSN in Postgres, binlog position in MySQL) after each write. The app can wait until a replica has reached that position before reading from it. Accept staleness where it's fine. Analytics, dashboards, and search indexes often tolerate seconds or even minutes of lag without any real harm. Note: PostgreSQL's pg_stat_replication view lets you inspect each replica's lag in bytes and time. SELECT * FROM pg_stat_replication; on the primary reveals the current replication state. When to Add Read Replicas Read replicas shine in read-heavy workloads where writes are a small fraction of total queries. A rule of thumb: if your primary's CPU is high but write throughput is modest, adding a replica and pointing reporting queries at it often cuts primary load dramatically. They are also commonly used for operational isolation — running long analytical queries against a replica so they cannot slow down the primary serving user-facing traffic. This is sometimes called an analytics replica or reporting replica and may even have a slight delay forced on it ( recovery_min_apply_delay in Postgres) to guard against accidental deletes. What read replicas do not solve: Write bottlenecks. Every replica still applies every write. If writes are the problem, you need sharding or a different architecture. Reduced durability by themselves. An async replica is not a substitute for backups — if the primary crashes before a write is replicated, that write is lost. Observing Replication Lag in SQLite (Simulated) Real replication lag is a runtime phenomenon, but you can reason about it with a simple model. The widget below seeds two "snapshot" tables — one representing the primary at time T, one representing a lagging replica at time T−1 — and lets you compare what each would return. Interactive SQL · Simulating stale reads from a lagging replica The numbers diverge because the replica hasn't applied the latest writes yet. In a real system this gap closes within milliseconds — but for reads that must be accurate the moment after a write, that window matters. Reveal: If you need to guarantee a user sees their own write, what is the simplest routing rule? Send reads that belong to the same user session that just wrote to the primary, at least for a brief window after the write. Everything else can go to replicas. This is sometimes implemented with a sticky session flag or a short TTL cache entry: "user 42 wrote at T, route their reads to primary until T + 2 s." It's not elegant, but it's the most straightforward way to avoid read-your-own-write anomalies without sacrificing replica scalability for all other users. ← Previous Synchronous vs asynchronous replication Next → Replication lag

title: Replication lag
text: In an ideal world, every replica would mirror the primary's data the moment a write is committed. In practice there is always a delay — replication lag — the gap in time between when a write lands on the primary and when it becomes visible on a replica. Understanding lag is essential because your application may read from replicas for performance reasons, and stale reads can silently break correctness guarantees. Why Lag Exists Replication is an asynchronous pipeline. After the primary writes and flushes a change to its own log (e.g., the WAL in PostgreSQL or the binlog in MySQL), it ships that log entry to each replica. The replica must then receive, write, and apply the change before it becomes queryable. Every step in that pipeline adds time: Pipeline stage Typical source of delay Primary → network Bandwidth saturation, TCP overhead, cross-region latency Network → replica disk Write I/O on the replica host Apply (replay) Replica CPU replaying writes serially (single-threaded replay is common) Long transactions The replica cannot advance past an open transaction on the primary Under low load, lag may be milliseconds. Under heavy write bursts — bulk imports, large transactions, schema changes — it can balloon to seconds or minutes. A write committed at t=0 on the primary is not visible on the replica until ~t=150ms, creating a replication lag window. Consequences: Anomalies You Will See Lag is not just a performance footnote — it changes what reads return. Stale reads — A user writes a row, then immediately queries a replica that hasn't applied the write yet. The row appears missing. This is the most common complaint in read-replica architectures. Read-your-own-writes violation — Your application writes to the primary but reads from a lagged replica. The user who just submitted a form sees the old data when they're redirected to the confirmation page. Monotonic reads violation — A user refreshes twice. The first request hits Replica A (up to date), the second hits Replica B (further behind). The data appears to go backwards . Note: These anomalies are the reason databases define consistency levels — a formal vocabulary for trading off freshness against performance (more in the next chapter on consistency models). Measuring Lag Most databases expose lag metrics directly: -- PostgreSQL: check replica lag from the primary SELECT client_addr, state, write_lag, flush_lag, replay_lag FROM pg_stat_replication; -- MySQL / MariaDB: check lag on the replica itself SHOW REPLICA STATUS\G -- Look at: Seconds_Behind_Source Monitoring tools like Prometheus + pg_exporter or Datadog can alert when lag exceeds a threshold (e.g., > 30 seconds). Mitigating Lag There is no single fix — the right approach depends on your workload: Strategy How it helps Trade-off Synchronous replication Zero lag — primary waits for replica ACK Writes are slower; replica failure blocks primary Read from primary for critical reads Always fresh Loses the scale-out benefit of replicas Sticky sessions / read-after-write routing Route a user's reads to primary just after they write Adds routing complexity Parallel replay Replicas apply changes on multiple threads Reduces CPU bottleneck; not all engines support it fully Smaller transactions Shorter apply time per event Requires application redesign Note: PostgreSQL's synchronous_commit = remote_apply setting guarantees a replica has applied (not just received) a WAL record before the primary acknowledges the commit. This eliminates lag for those writes at the cost of higher commit latency. The following widget simulates the kind of data discrepancy a lagged replica can create. The events table represents the primary state; replica_snapshot represents what a replica with lag might have seen at a slightly earlier point in time. Try querying both to see the difference. Interactive SQL · Replica lag simulation Notice that a read-your-own-writes check on the replica would miss first_login entirely — user 42 appears not to have logged in yet, even though the primary already recorded it. ← Previous Read replicas Next → Failover and promotion

title: Failover and promotion
text: When a leader node goes down, the system has to keep running. Failover is the process of detecting that the leader is unavailable and promoting one of the followers to become the new leader. Done well, it's nearly invisible to users; done poorly, it causes data loss, split-brain, or extended downtime. Understanding the mechanics tells you why "just promote the replica" is harder than it sounds. What Happens During Failover A typical automated failover sequence looks like this: Detect failure — a health-check agent notices the leader has stopped responding (TCP connection refused, heartbeat timeout, etc.). Agree on a new leader — surviving nodes (or an external coordinator) run an election. The most up-to-date follower is the best candidate. Promote the winner — that follower begins accepting writes; it is now the new leader. Redirect clients — application connections are pointed at the new leader (via a virtual IP, DNS update, or proxy like HAProxy/PgBouncer). Rejoin old leader — when the former leader recovers, it becomes a follower of the new leader and re-syncs. Failover timeline: the leader crashes, a follower wins the election, gets promoted, and clients reconnect to the new leader. Note: The window between the crash and the promotion — typically a few seconds to a minute — is when the system is unavailable for writes. Tuning the heartbeat timeout controls how fast failure is detected, but too-short timeouts cause false positives (network blips look like crashes). The Tricky Parts Replication Lag and Data Loss Asynchronous followers might not have received all of the leader's writes at the moment of the crash. Any writes that were acknowledged by the old leader but not yet replicated are lost when the follower becomes the new leader. The gap is measured in terms of the replication lag — how far behind the follower was. Replication mode Data loss risk Write latency Fully synchronous Zero — follower confirmed every write Higher (waits for ack) Semi-synchronous Low — at least one replica is current Moderate Fully asynchronous Possible — lagging replica may miss writes Lowest PostgreSQL's synchronous_commit = on guarantees that at least one synchronous standby has the write before the transaction is confirmed to the client. Split-Brain If the old leader recovers but doesn't know it was replaced, you end up with two nodes both believing they are the leader . Both accept writes; the data diverges. This is one of the most dangerous failure modes in distributed systems. Safe systems avoid split-brain by: Using fencing tokens — only the current leader holds a valid token; an old leader's writes are rejected. Requiring a quorum — a node can only act as leader if the majority of nodes agree it is. Using STONITH ("Shoot The Other Node In The Head") — the cluster forcibly powers off a suspected split-brain node before promoting another. The "False Positive" Problem A node that is actually alive but merely slow (under GC pause, network congestion) can be incorrectly declared dead. Promoting a replacement wastes effort and risks split-brain. Most tools use a combination of: Multiple independent health checkers Requiring a minimum number of missed heartbeats before declaring failure Human confirmation for manual failover in sensitive environments Tools That Handle Failover You rarely implement failover logic yourself. Popular tools per database: Database Failover tool Mechanism PostgreSQL Patroni, repmgr etcd/Consul/ZooKeeper for quorum; promotes best replica MySQL / MariaDB Orchestrator, MHA Topology-aware; tracks replication graph Redis Redis Sentinel Majority vote among Sentinel processes MongoDB Replica set Built-in; primary election via Raft-like protocol These tools watch node health, run elections, update connection routing, and re-attach the old leader as a follower — all automatically. The interactive example below lets you explore replication lag in a simulated replica log. The replica_log table mimics what a follower has received; notice which rows are missing compared to the leader. Interactive SQL · Simulating replication lag and missing writes ← Previous Replication lag Next → Conflict handling

title: Conflict handling
text: When multiple nodes in a replicated system can accept writes, you gain availability and geographic flexibility — but you also open the door to write conflicts . A conflict occurs when two nodes accept different writes to the same row (or key) before they have had a chance to synchronize. Both writes are valid locally, but they contradict each other globally. Resolving that contradiction without losing data or corrupting state is the central challenge of multi-master (multi-primary) replication. What a Conflict Looks Like Imagine an order management system replicated across two data centers — US and EU. A customer's loyalty-points balance starts at 200. At 10:00:00, the EU node records a purchase and subtracts 50 points → balance becomes 150. At 10:00:01, the US node records a different purchase and subtracts 80 points → balance becomes 120. Neither node knew about the other's write when it happened. When replication catches up, both nodes see two conflicting updates. The correct answer — 70 points remaining — is not what either node computed. A naive resolution (e.g., "last write wins") silently drops one transaction. Two nodes write different updates to the same row before replicating — a classic write conflict. Note: Single-leader replication avoids this entirely because only one node accepts writes.
