Monotonic reads — once you see a value, you will never see an older one (prevents time going "backwards" within a session). Linearizability — every operation appears to take effect instantaneously at some point in real time; every reader, not just the writer, sees writes immediately. Most production systems offer read-your-writes by default (or as a tunable option) because the user-facing impact of violating it is severe. Linearizability is reserved for cases where the cost — a round-trip to the leader for every read — is worth paying. Reveal: Why can't we just always read from the leader? Reading from the leader trivially satisfies read-your-writes (and even linearizability for single-object reads), but it defeats the main reason you added replicas in the first place: offloading read traffic. In read-heavy workloads — reporting dashboards, social feeds, product catalogs — the majority of load is reads. If all reads return to the leader, replicas sit mostly idle and the leader becomes the bottleneck. A targeted policy (route to leader only after a write, then fall back to replicas) captures most of the benefit with minimal correctness compromise. ← Previous Eventual consistency Next → Monotonic reads

title: Monotonic reads
text: Imagine you load your social-media feed, see a post from a friend, then refresh the page — and the post is gone. You didn't delete it. Nobody else did. The server just happened to route your second request to a replica that hadn't caught up yet. This eerie "time-travel" effect is exactly what monotonic reads prevents. What "Monotonic" Means In mathematics, a monotonic sequence only moves in one direction — it never goes backwards. Applied to reads in a distributed database, the guarantee is: Monotonic reads: If a client reads a value at version V , all subsequent reads by that same client will see version V or a later one — never an older one. Without this guarantee a single user can observe the database "rolling back" between requests, even though nothing was actually rolled back. The data was simply served from different replicas at different points in their replication lag. Why This Happens: Replication Lag Most distributed databases replicate data from a primary node to one or more replicas. Reads can be served by any replica to spread the load. But replicas are not always perfectly in sync — there is a replication lag measured in milliseconds to seconds. A client reads v2 from Replica A, then reads v1 from a lagging Replica B — a monotonic-reads violation. The client did nothing wrong. The load balancer simply sent the second request to a slower replica. How Databases Enforce Monotonic Reads There are two common approaches: Approach Mechanism Trade-off Sticky sessions Route all reads from a given client to the same replica Simple; replica failure disrupts the session Read-version tracking Client tracks the last seen log position; only reads from replicas that have reached that position More resilient; adds coordination overhead Read-your-writes sessions Guarantee a superset: you always see your own writes AND past reads Stronger, but further limits replica selection Sticky Sessions in Practice The simplest fix is to pin each client to one replica for the duration of a session — often by hashing the user ID or session token. If that replica is unavailable, the system either fails over gracefully or briefly serves a potentially stale read. Many databases expose this as a session-level setting. In PostgreSQL you can send reads to a specific server; in MongoDB you use session consistency with a causalConsistency: true option; in DynamoDB strongly consistent reads always go to the primary, avoiding the issue entirely. Read-Version (LSN) Tracking More sophisticated systems pass the client's log sequence number (LSN) — the position in the replication log of the last change they saw — along with every request. A replica checks whether it has applied at least that LSN before serving the read. If it hasn't caught up yet, it either waits or hands the request to a more advanced replica. Note: This is the mechanism behind causal consistency in systems like MongoDB and CockroachDB. The session automatically carries a "read timestamp" so the cluster can always route to a node that is sufficiently up to date. Seeing It in a Simulated Form The following widget lets you explore what monotonic reads protect. The replica_log table simulates two replicas at different applied positions. The query finds which replica a client can safely read from given their last-seen version. Interactive SQL · Replica selection by LSN Try changing the >= 2 threshold to >= 3 — now only replica_a qualifies, because replica_b hasn't applied LSN 3 yet. This is exactly how LSN-based routing enforces monotonic reads. The Takeaway Monotonic reads is a session-level consistency guarantee that prevents a client from observing the past after they have already seen the present. It does not require global coordination or linearizability — it only promises that for one client , time moves forward. That makes it a practical and widely implemented guarantee even in eventually-consistent systems. ← Previous Read-your-writes Next → Causal consistency

title: Causal consistency
text: Imagine you post a message in a group chat, then immediately reply to it. A friend who joins a moment later sees your reply — but not the original message. From their view, your reply is a non-sequitur. This is exactly the kind of anomaly causal consistency is designed to prevent. Causal consistency is a consistency model that sits between two extremes: it is weaker than linearizability (no global wall-clock ordering required), but stronger than eventual consistency (causally related operations must appear in order). The key insight is that not all operations are related — causal consistency only constrains operations that are causally linked . What "Causally Related" Means Two operations are causally related if one could have influenced the other: Write → Read: If process A writes a value and process B reads it, B's subsequent writes depend on that value. Read → Write: If B reads a value and then writes based on what it saw, that write is causally downstream. Transitivity: If A causes B, and B causes C, then A causes C — even if A and C happen on different nodes. Operations with no path between them are concurrent and causally independent. A causally consistent system may deliver concurrent operations in any order; it only enforces ordering within causal chains. Note: Databases track causality using vector clocks or version vectors — per-node counters that let every node determine whether one event "happened before" another without relying on synchronized wall clocks. A Concrete Example Consider three users — Alice, Bob, and Carol — sharing a distributed document store with two replicas. Causal chain: Alice's post causes Bob's read, which causes Bob's reply. Carol must see Alice's post before Bob's reply. Under causal consistency, Carol is guaranteed to see "Hello!" before "Hi Alice!" — because Bob's reply causally depends on Alice's message. If Carol happened to be on a replica that received Bob's write first, the replica must delay delivering it until Alice's message has also arrived. Without causal consistency (e.g., under plain eventual consistency), Carol could see "Hi Alice!" arrive first — a confusing and broken experience. Causal Consistency vs. Other Models Model Global order? Causal order? Concurrent ops order? Availability Linearizability Yes (wall clock) Yes Yes Lower (quorum needed) Sequential consistency Yes (logical) Yes Yes Lower Causal consistency No Yes No (any order) Higher Eventual consistency No No No (any order) Highest Causal consistency is the strongest model that remains available under network partitions, according to the PACELC framework. This makes it attractive for geo-distributed systems where you cannot afford the latency of a global quorum. How Databases Implement It Real systems track causal dependencies using version vectors (also called dependency vectors). Each client carries a vector summarizing the set of writes it has already observed. When the client issues a new operation, it sends this vector along. The server only executes the operation after it has applied all causally prior writes. Client vector at Carol's read: { replica_A: 5, replica_B: 3 } This means Carol has seen all writes up to sequence 5 on replica A and up to sequence 3 on replica B. The server will not show her a write with replica_A: 6 until she has also seen replica_A: 5. Notable systems that offer causal consistency: MongoDB (since v3.6) — causally consistent sessions via afterClusterTime DynamoDB — offers strongly consistent reads (per-request opt-in) and client-side version tokens for read-your-writes, but has no native causal consistency API . Causally ordering reads across multiple keys requires application-level tracking with DynamoDB Streams or external coordination. AntidoteDB — built from the ground up around causal+ consistency CockroachDB — uses hybrid logical clocks (HLC) to approximate causal ordering The Trade-off Worth Knowing Causal consistency is not free. Version vectors grow with the number of nodes and clients; tracking fine-grained causal histories at scale is expensive. Many systems therefore implement causal+ (causal consistency plus convergent conflict resolution) rather than perfect causal consistency, accepting a small window where concurrent writes must be merged by the application. The practical question to ask is: "Does the correctness of this operation depend on another operation the user just performed?" If yes, causal consistency — or stronger — is warranted. If operations are genuinely independent (e.g., two users updating unrelated records), eventual consistency may be enough and will give you better write throughput. Reveal: Would causal consistency prevent a "lost update" where two users increment the same counter concurrently? No. Causal consistency only orders operations that are causally related — one must have observed the other. If two users each read the counter value (say, 10) and both write 11 without seeing each other's write, those operations are concurrent and causally independent. Causal consistency allows both writes and the system converges to 11, losing one increment. Preventing this requires a stronger model (e.g., serializable transactions) or a CRDT like a grow-only counter that merges concurrent increments correctly. ← Previous Monotonic reads Next → CAP as a tradeoff lens

title: CAP as a tradeoff lens
text: The CAP theorem is one of the most cited — and most misunderstood — ideas in distributed systems. State it plainly: a distributed system cannot simultaneously guarantee all three of Consistency, Availability, and Partition tolerance . When a network partition occurs, it must sacrifice one. That's the theorem. But CAP's real value isn't the proof — it's as a thinking tool that forces you to ask the right questions when designing or choosing a distributed database. The Three Properties, Precisely Before using CAP as a lens, you need the exact definitions, because informal shorthand leads to bad decisions. Property What it means C — Consistency Every read returns the most recent write (or an error). All nodes see the same data at the same time. This is linearizability , not the "C" in ACID. A — Availability Every request to a non-failed node receives a response — not an error, not a timeout. The response may not reflect the very latest write. P — Partition tolerance The system continues operating when network partitions split nodes into groups that cannot communicate. Note: "Partition tolerance" is not really optional. Real networks drop packets, switches reboot, and cables get cut. Designing as if partitions cannot happen is designing for a world that doesn't exist. So in practice, the real choice is: when a partition strikes, do you sacrifice C or A? The Forced Choice During a Partition Picture two data center regions, East and West, both serving your application. A backbone failure severs the link between them. Requests are still arriving at both sides. What does each region do? During a partition, the West region must choose: refuse requests (CP — consistent but unavailable) or serve stale data (AP — available but inconsistent). CP systems (Consistent + Partition-tolerant) refuse to answer rather than answer wrongly. HBase, Zookeeper, and etcd work this way. A client might get an error or block waiting for the partition to heal. The data you do get is guaranteed to be current. AP systems (Available + Partition-tolerant) keep answering, even if the answer might be stale. Cassandra and CouchDB lean here. A client always gets a response; after the partition heals, the system reconciles conflicting writes. The price is that two clients in different regions can briefly see different truths. CAP as a Lens, Not a Binary Switch The danger in treating CAP as a strict "pick two" menu is that it suggests a one-time architectural decision. Reality is more nuanced: Granularity matters. A single system can make different tradeoffs per operation. DynamoDB lets you choose strongly consistent or eventually consistent reads per request. Partitions are rare but not zero. Most of the time there is no partition, and the CP/AP distinction is invisible. The system's behavior under normal conditions (latency, throughput, staleness) often matters more day to day. The "C" in CAP is strict. Linearizability is a strong guarantee. Many systems offer weaker consistency (causal, session, monotonic read) that sits between the two extremes — enough consistency for many applications, with less availability cost. Note: CAP was formalized by Eric Brewer in 2000 and proved by Gilbert and Lynch in 2002. Brewer himself later acknowledged it oversimplifies: the PACELC model extends CAP by also considering the latency vs. consistency tradeoff even when no partition exists — because in practice, replicating synchronously costs latency whether or not the network is split. Applying the Lens: Which Tradeoff Fits Your Application? Application type Partition behavior you likely want Why Bank account balance CP — refuse the request A stale balance can cause overdrafts Shopping cart contents AP — serve stale, merge later A missing item for a moment is acceptable Leader election (Zookeeper) CP — must have one leader Two leaders = split-brain disaster DNS record lookup AP — serve cached answer Stale IP for seconds is fine; unavailability is not Inventory "in stock" flag Depends — often AP with compensation Small oversell risk beats cart errors at checkout The right question when evaluating a distributed database isn't "is this CP or AP?" It's: what happens to my users and my data when a partition strikes? If incorrect data causes harm (financial, safety, legal), lean CP. If unavailability causes harm (lost revenue, poor UX), lean AP. Reveal: Why can't we just fix the network and always have all three? The CAP proof shows the conflict is fundamental, not an engineering limitation. During a partition, any message between the two sides is lost — by definition. So no amount of clever engineering can let West instantly learn about East's write. West must either wait (losing availability) or proceed with what it knows (losing consistency). Faster networks shrink the window of inconsistency in AP systems, but they cannot eliminate the logical impossibility during an active partition. ← Previous Causal consistency Next → Latency vs correctness

title: Latency vs correctness
text: Every distributed database forces you to make a choice that has no perfect answer: how fast do you want to be, and how wrong are you willing to be? These two goals pull in opposite directions. Stronger correctness requires more coordination between nodes — and coordination costs time. This chapter puts a name and a framework on that tension. What "correctness" means here In the context of distributed databases, correctness is about what a reader sees relative to what a writer has stored. The gold standard is linearizability : every operation appears to take effect instantaneously at a single moment in time, and every subsequent read reflects every prior write. It behaves as if the entire distributed system were one perfectly reliable single-node database. Achieving linearizability across nodes requires a coordination round before a read or write can complete: nodes must agree on ordering. That round-trip — even at the speed of light — takes time. A client in Tokyo reading from a leader in Frankfurt waits at least ~120 ms for one coordination round-trip, no matter how fast the hardware is. Note: This is not an engineering limitation you can optimize away. The speed of light, geographic distance, and the laws of physics set a hard floor on coordination latency. It is a fundamental constraint, not a bug. The spectrum: from fast-but-stale to slow-but-safe Consistency is not binary. Real systems expose a dial with several named positions. Important: the models below address two different dimensions. Linearizability is about recency — what order reads and writes appear in relative to real time. Serializability is about isolation — whether concurrent transactions produce results equivalent to some serial execution. They are orthogonal: a system can be serializable but not linearizable (e.g., snapshot isolation), or linearizable but not serializable (e.g., linearizable key-value store). The table lists them together for comparison, not because they are points on a single axis. Level What the reader sees Typical extra latency Example Linearizable Latest write, globally ordered High (coordination round) etcd, Zookeeper, Spanner Serializability Transactions appear in some serial order Medium–high CockroachDB, Postgres with SSI Read-your-writes Your own writes are visible to you Low–medium Sticky sessions, read-after-write routing Monotonic reads You never see data go backwards in time Low Session-level replica pinning Eventual Replica may be arbitrarily behind Near-zero Cassandra default, DynamoDB default reads Moving down the table: reads get faster and more available, but the gap between "what a reader sees" and "what is actually stored" widens. Visualizing the tradeoff The diagram below shows how a write to a leader propagates to two replicas under different consistency policies, and when a client read can safely return. An eventual read can return instantly (possibly stale); a linearizable read must wait until all required replicas confirm the write — incurring real latency. Why the tradeoff is unavoidable: a quick proof Suppose you want a read to always return the most recent write. The reader must somehow know what the most recent write is. In a distributed system, that knowledge requires communicating with other nodes. Communication takes time. Therefore: strong correctness implies latency. This is not a matter of clever engineering — it is a consequence of the network partition and message-delay models that underpin all distributed systems. The PACELC model (an extension of CAP) makes this explicit: even when the system is not partitioned, there is a tradeoff between latency and consistency (the "ELC" part: Else, Latency vs Consistency). Choosing your point on the dial The right choice depends on the cost asymmetry for your specific workload: High cost of staleness (bank balance, inventory that can go negative, authentication tokens) → pay the latency for linearizability or at least read-your-writes. Low cost of staleness (view counts, search index freshness, analytics dashboards) → accept eventual consistency and enjoy lower latency and higher availability. Mixed workloads → most production systems use tunable consistency per operation. DynamoDB lets you request strongly-consistent reads for critical paths while defaulting to eventual reads elsewhere. Try this widget to see how quorum reads encode the latency/correctness tradeoff in a concrete numeric model. A write quorum W and read quorum R satisfy R + W > N (where N is the replica count) to guarantee overlap — but larger quorums mean waiting for more nodes. Interactive SQL · Quorum overlap calculator Experiment: edit a row to set W=1, R=1 with N=3 and watch the guarantee disappear. That is exactly what "eventual consistency" looks like in quorum arithmetic — the read and write sets need not overlap at all. Key takeaways Coordination costs time. Every guarantee stronger than eventual consistency requires at least one synchronous round-trip between nodes. The PACELC model captures the permanent latency/consistency tradeoff that exists even without network partitions. Staleness has a price too. Choosing low latency means accepting the risk of serving outdated data — and that risk must be weighed against its business cost. Most real systems make the dial tunable per query or operation, rather than committing the whole database to one point on the spectrum. ← Previous CAP as a tradeoff lens Next → Conflict-free approaches

title: Conflict-free approaches
text: When two replicas accept concurrent writes to the same piece of data, you eventually have to reconcile them. Most strategies either pick a winner (last-write-wins) or abort one of the writes (pessimistic locking). Conflict-free approaches take a third path: they design the data structure so that concurrent writes can always be merged automatically — no coordination needed, no lost updates, no aborts. The central idea is called a CRDT — a Conflict-free Replicated Data Type. What Makes a Data Type "Conflict-Free"? A CRDT is a data type with two properties: All states can be merged. Given any two states that replicas have diverged into, there is a well-defined merge (or join ) operation that produces a single valid result. Merge is commutative, associative, and idempotent. It does not matter in what order replicas exchange their states, or whether the same update is received twice — the final result is always the same. If those properties hold, replicas can accept writes locally (no coordination, no waiting for a quorum) and gossip their state to each other in the background. Convergence is guaranteed by the math, not by a protocol. Note: CRDTs come in two flavors. State-based CRDTs (CvRDTs) ship their entire state to peers, who merge it with their own. Operation-based CRDTs (CmRDTs) ship only the operations; the network must deliver each operation at least once. State-based are simpler to reason about; operation-based produce smaller messages. Common CRDT Types Each CRDT trades off expressiveness for simplicity. Here are the most widely used ones: CRDT What it models Merge rule G-Counter Monotonically increasing counter Per-node max, then sum PN-Counter Counter that can increment and decrement Two G-Counters (P − N) G-Set Grow-only set Union 2P-Set Set with removals (element CANNOT be re-added once removed) Union of add-set and remove-set LWW-Register Single value with last-write-wins Keep entry with highest timestamp OR-Set Set with add/remove (element can be re-added) Tag each add with a unique token; remove by token RGA / Logoot Ordered sequence (collaborative text) Assign unique position IDs to each character The G-Counter in Detail A G-Counter is the simplest useful CRDT. Each node keeps its own slot in a vector and only ever increments its own slot. The global count is the sum of all slots. Node A vector: [5, 0, 2] (A=5, B=0, C=2) → total = 7 Node B vector: [4, 3, 2] (A=4, B=3, C=2) → total = 9 Merge (element-wise max): [5, 3, 2] → total = 10 No matter what order A and B exchange messages, the merged vector — and therefore the global count — is always the same. An ordinary integer counter would silently lose increments under concurrent updates; the G-Counter cannot. G-Counter merge: each node increments only its own slot; merging takes the element-wise maximum, so no increments are ever lost. Where CRDTs Are Used in Practice CRDTs are not just academic. Several production systems rely on them: Redis (via RedisJSON and Redis Streams) uses CRDT-like structures in its active-active geo-replication mode. Riak (Basho) pioneered operational CRDT support — counters, sets, maps, registers — as first-class database types. Apache Cassandra uses LWW registers for most values, and offers a counter column type backed by a PN-Counter. Figma / Google Docs / Notion use sequence CRDTs (like RGA or LSEQ) to let multiple users edit the same document simultaneously without conflicts. Limitations to Keep in Mind CRDTs are a powerful tool, but they are not a universal answer: Not every operation can be made conflict-free. A bank balance that must never go negative cannot be modeled as a PN-Counter without coordination — you need a protocol to prevent overdrafts. Tombstones accumulate. In G-Set and 2P-Set variants, deleted elements leave behind markers ("tombstones") that must be garbage-collected over time. Semantic conflicts still happen. A CRDT for a shopping cart will correctly merge two concurrent "add item" operations, but if both users concurrently applied a coupon that is only valid once, the merge produces two applied coupons — which is semantically wrong even if structurally valid. The key question: "Is the merge semantics of this data type the same as the business semantics?" When the answer is yes, CRDTs give you coordination-free availability with guaranteed convergence. When it is no, you still need transactions or application-level conflict resolution. Note: PostgreSQL has no native CRDT support, but its JSONB and HSTORE types can serve as building blocks for implementing CRDT-like merge semantics in application code. For example, a G-Counter can be modeled as a JSONB object mapping node IDs to counts, with the merge operation implemented as a user-defined function using element-wise GREATEST . This is not a replacement for true CRDTs, but it can be sufficient for moderate-scale multi-master or edge-sync scenarios where the conflict resolution logic is simple and well-understood. Try the widget below to see how a simple CRDT-like merge works in SQL — simulating two nodes that each recorded increments independently, then sync. Interactive SQL · G-Counter merge simulation Reveal: Can a PN-Counter go negative? Yes — a PN-Counter is simply two G-Counters: one tracking all increments (P) and one tracking all decrements (N). The observed value is P − N. Because each component only grows, the merge is conflict-free. But nothing prevents the result from being negative; if that represents an invalid business state (like a bank balance), you must add coordination (e.g., a reservation protocol) on top of the CRDT to enforce the constraint. ← Previous Latency vs correctness Next → Picking the right guarantee

title: Picking the right guarantee
text: By now you have a toolbox of consistency guarantees — linearizability, causal consistency, read-your-writes, eventual consistency, and more. The hard part is not understanding them individually; it is knowing which one to reach for when you sit down to design a real system. This page builds a practical decision framework. The Core Tradeoff: What Are You Paying? Every stronger consistency guarantee costs you something. The two main currencies are latency and availability . Latency: Stronger guarantees require coordination between nodes (waiting for acknowledgments, running consensus rounds). Every extra round-trip adds milliseconds. Availability: If a node or network link goes down, strongly consistent systems may refuse reads and writes rather than risk returning stale or split data. Weaker systems can keep serving requests from whichever nodes are reachable. This is the heart of the CAP theorem and its more nuanced successor PACELC: you are always trading between these forces. There is no free lunch. A Taxonomy of Guarantees (Weakest to Strongest) Guarantee What it promises Typical cost Eventual consistency All replicas converge eventually ; no ordering guarantee Lowest latency, highest availability Monotonic reads Once you read a value, you never see an older one Slight: route reads to same replica or epoch Read-your-writes You always see your own writes Low: session affinity or sticky routing Causal consistency Causally related ops appear in order to all readers Medium: vector-clock tracking overhead Snapshot isolation Reads see a consistent point-in-time snapshot Medium: MVCC storage overhead Linearizability Every op appears instantaneous on a global timeline High: consensus round per write Serializability Transactions appear to execute one-at-a-time High: locking or OCC validation Note: "Serializability" is the gold standard for transactions; "linearizability" is the gold standard for single-key operations. Databases that offer both (e.g., Google Spanner's "external consistency") are the strongest category but also the most expensive to operate. A Decision Framework The right guarantee depends on three questions: Can users tolerate seeing stale data — even briefly? Shopping cart contents, social media feeds, DNS lookups: yes. Bank balances, inventory counts, seat reservations: generally no. Do operations have causal dependencies? A comment that replies to a post must be seen after the post. If operations are independent (e.g., per-user settings), causality enforcement is wasted effort. Is the operation a single key or a multi-key transaction? Single-key ops can often get away with lighter guarantees than multi-key updates that must be atomic together. Decision flowchart: answer three questions to land on the right consistency guarantee. Mixing Guarantees in One System Real applications rarely pick one guarantee for everything. A common pattern is tiered consistency : Use serializable transactions for the checkout flow (money, inventory). Use read-your-writes for the user's own profile and order history. Use eventual consistency for product recommendations and review counts. Most distributed databases let you dial this per operation. DynamoDB lets you choose between eventually consistent and strongly consistent reads per request. Cassandra exposes consistency levels ( ONE , QUORUM , ALL ) on each query. CockroachDB defaults to serializable but lets read-only transactions relax to follower reads. The mistake to avoid is defaulting to the strongest guarantee everywhere . Serializable distributed transactions require consensus, which means network round-trips on every write. At scale, that latency compounds. Using eventual consistency where correctness genuinely allows it is not cutting corners — it is engineering. Try exploring how isolation levels affect what concurrent transactions can see. This SQLite example simulates two transactions racing to update the same counter: Interactive SQL · Consistency tradeoffs Reveal: Which guarantee prevents the lost-update shown above? Serializable isolation (or at minimum repeatable read with a compare-and-swap) prevents the lost update. Under serializability, the two concurrent transactions are ordered: the second one either waits for the first to commit and then reads the updated balance, or it detects the conflict and aborts. Under read committed or eventual consistency , both transactions can read the same stale balance and overwrite each other — one update disappears. Practical Checklist Before choosing a consistency level, answer these: What is the worst-case user impact of a stale read? Annoying (show it stale) or catastrophic (double-spend)? How often do concurrent writers touch the same data? Low contention? Lighter guarantees are safer to use. High contention? Conflicts will happen; you need a strategy. What is your read/write ratio? Read-heavy workloads benefit most from eventual consistency and replica reads; write-heavy workloads pay the coordination cost either way. Can you use application-level idempotency to compensate? Retrying a payment is safer if the payment is idempotent (keyed on a client-generated ID). That lets you use at-least-once delivery with weaker guarantees without double-charging. Picking the right guarantee is not a one-time decision. It is a recurring conversation between correctness requirements and performance constraints — one you will revisit as your system grows. ← Previous Conflict-free approaches Next → Chapter 17 Summary: What We Learned

title: Chapter 17 Summary: What We Learned
text: Page Topic Takeaway in One Sentence 161 Strong consistency After a write completes, all subsequent reads return the new value — as if there is only one copy of the data. 162 Eventual consistency If no new updates are made, eventually all replicas converge to the same value — but reads may see stale data in the meantime. 163 Read-your-writes A user should always see their own updates immediately — requires routing reads to the same replica that handled the write. 164 Monotonic reads A user should never see data go backwards in time — each read should return data at least as fresh as the previous read. 165 Causal consistency Operations that are causally related must be seen in the same order by everyone; unrelated operations can be seen in any order. 166 CAP as a tradeoff lens During a network partition, you must choose between Consistency (correct answers) and Availability (any answer at all) — not both. 167 Latency vs correctness Stronger consistency guarantees require more coordination, which adds latency — this is a fundamental tradeoff, not a bug. 168 Conflict-free approaches CRDTs, last-write-wins, and application-level merging let systems avoid coordination while still making progress. 169 Picking the right guarantee Not every part of your application needs strong consistency — match the guarantee to the business requirement. Reveal: How do these ideas connect to the next chapter? Chapter 17 closed Part IV by showing that consistency is a spectrum, not a binary choice. Now Part V opens by asking a different question: the relational model is not the only model. Chapter 18 introduces key-value databases — the simplest database family. You will learn put/get/delete semantics, the difference between in-memory and persistent key-value stores, and when sub-millisecond latency matters more than rich query capabilities. The consistency tradeoffs you just studied play out concretely in these systems. ← Previous Picking the right guarantee Next → The key-value model

title: The key-value model
text: A key-value database is the simplest data model: a persistent dictionary. O(1) lookups by key, but no querying inside values, no joins, and no schema enforcement. At its core, a key-value database is the simplest data model imaginable: a giant, persistent dictionary. You hand it a key , it hands back a value . That is the entire contract. No schemas to define, no joins to write, no query planner to second-guess. This simplicity is both its superpower and its boundary. The Model in One Sentence Every record is a key → value pair stored in a hash-map-like structure. The key is a unique identifier (a string or binary blob). The value is whatever you want — a string, a number, a serialized JSON object, a binary blob, a counter. The database stores it and retrieves it without inspecting its contents. Concept What it is Example Key Unique lookup handle — you choose the format session:u4291:token Value Opaque payload — any bytes you want to store {"userId":4291,"expires":1751000000} Namespace / prefix A convention (not enforced by all engines) for grouping keys cache: · session: · rate: Note: "Opaque" means the database does not parse or index the value's contents. It stores bytes in, bytes out. If you need to search inside values, you must handle that in your application or choose a different model (e.g., a document store). Structure and Key Design Because there is no schema and no query language beyond GET key / SET key value , key naming becomes your schema . Teams adopt conventions like colon-separated namespaces to create logical groupings: session:user:4291 → {"token":"abc123","created":1750900000} rate_limit:user:4291:day → 47 product:sku:NB-001 → {"name":"Notebook","price":2.99} cache:homepage:v3 → <rendered HTML> This is a deliberate design choice. The database is blind to the colon; it is pure convention. But a consistent scheme lets you reason about key space, set expiry by prefix in some engines, and avoid collisions across different parts of an application. A key-value store maps unique string keys to opaque values of any type. The highlighted row shows an active session lookup. Core Operations The API is deliberately tiny. Most key-value stores expose only a handful of verbs: Operation Meaning GET key Retrieve the value for a key (or null if absent) SET key value Write or overwrite a key-value pair DEL key Delete a key EXISTS key Check whether a key is present EXPIRE key seconds Schedule automatic deletion after a TTL Some engines add richer primitives (atomic counters, lists, sorted sets), but the lookup-by-exact-key model stays at the center. There is no WHERE price < 5 — you either know the key or you do not. Note: The EXPIRE / TTL (time-to-live) feature is what makes key-value stores ideal for caches and sessions: data evicts itself automatically, so you never accumulate stale records. Where the Model Fits (and Where It Does Not) Strong fits: Session storage — look up session:<id> on every request; expire automatically after inactivity. Caching — store pre-computed results under a predictable key; invalidate or let them expire. Rate limiting — increment an integer counter under rate:<user>:<window> ; check before serving. Feature flags — feature:<flag_name> → true / false , read on every render. Poor fits: Queries that filter or search inside values (a document store or relational DB handles this better). Relationships between records that you need to traverse (a graph DB or relational FK handles this). Ad-hoc analytics across the full dataset (a column store or data warehouse handles this). The key-value model is intentionally narrow. It trades expressiveness for speed and operational simplicity — and in the right problem domain, that trade is an excellent one. SQLite is not a key-value store, but you can simulate the model to make the mechanics concrete. Try looking up, inserting, and deleting pairs in the widget below. Interactive SQL · Simulating a key-value store ← Previous Picking the right guarantee Next → Fast lookups and simple APIs

title: Fast lookups and simple APIs
text: Key-value databases trade away almost everything a relational database offers — rich queries, joins, schemas — in exchange for one thing: blazing-fast access to a single value by its key . That sounds like a drastic trade-off, but for the right workloads it is exactly the right deal. This page explains how key-value stores work internally, what their APIs look like in practice, and why that simplicity is a feature, not a limitation. The Core Idea: A Hash Map at Database Scale The mental model is a hash map (or dictionary) in memory — the same data structure you use in code every day. Given a key, you get the value. That's it. There is no schema, no schema migration, no foreign key to validate, no planner choosing a join strategy. Under the hood, most key-value stores use one of two physical structures: Hash table — O(1) average lookup; used by Redis for its in-memory store. Keys and values live in RAM; persistence is a separate concern (append-only log or periodic snapshot). B-tree or LSM-tree — used when the dataset is too large for RAM and durability is the priority. RocksDB (which powers many embedded key-value layers) uses an LSM-tree for high-throughput writes. A GET request is hashed directly to a memory slot — no table scan, no query planner, just one pointer dereference. The API: Five Operations and You're Done The entire key-value API typically fits in five commands. Redis, Memcached, and virtually every embedded key-value library share this shape: Operation What it does Redis example SET key value Store or overwrite a value SET session:4291 "{...}" GET key Retrieve a value (or nil) GET session:4291 DEL key Remove a key entirely DEL session:4291 EXISTS key Check presence without fetching EXISTS session:4291 EXPIRE key seconds Schedule automatic deletion EXPIRE session:4291 3600 Note: The EXPIRE / TTL (time-to-live) feature is one of the most useful primitives in any key-value store. It lets you implement caches, session expiry, and rate-limit windows without a background cleanup job — the store handles deletion automatically. Redis extends this core with richer value types — lists, sorted sets, hyperloglogs — but even then, every command addresses a single key. You still never write a WHERE clause. Key Naming Conventions Because there is no schema to structure your data, teams impose order through key naming patterns : <namespace>:<entity>:<id>[:<field>] session:user:4291 cache:homepage:en ratelimit:ip:203.0.113.5:2024-06 leaderboard:game:chess:weekly The colon is just a convention (Redis calls them "keyspaces"), but it matters in practice: it lets you reason about what's in the store, set TTLs per category, and scan a namespace for debugging. When Simple Wins Key-value stores dominate four workload categories where their simplicity is genuinely optimal: Session storage — web sessions are looked up by session ID on every request. A hash map in Redis takes ~100 µs; a relational query scanning an indexed table takes ~1–5 ms. At thousands of requests per second, the difference is material. Caching — rendered HTML fragments, API responses, and computed aggregates are stored under a cache key and expire automatically via TTL. Rate limiting — increment a counter keyed by ratelimit:<user>:<window> ; if it exceeds the limit, reject the request. The INCR and EXPIRE commands make this atomic and trivial. Feature flags — store feature:<flag_name> as "on" or "off" (or a JSON config blob) and read it on startup or per-request. Instant rollouts without a database migration. The common thread: you always know the full key up front . The moment you need to ask "give me all sessions for users in Germany" — a query across values — a key-value store forces a full scan and you should reach for a different tool. Try the widget below to simulate a session-store workflow using SQLite's key-value-like lookup pattern. Notice how the query is always an exact key match — no joins, no filters on value contents. Interactive SQL · Key-Value Lookup Pattern ← Previous The key-value model Next → Range scans and ordered keys

title: Range scans and ordered keys
text: Pure hash-based key-value stores are magnificent for point lookups — one key, one value, done. But the moment you ask "give me all events from the last hour" or "list every user whose ID falls between 1000 and 2000," a hash table has nothing useful to offer. That is where ordered key-value stores step in: by keeping keys sorted, they make range queries a first-class operation. Why Order Matters for Ranges In a hash table, adjacent keys in the hash space have nothing to do with adjacent keys in the logical space. The hash of "user:1000" might land in slot 7, while "user:1001" lands in slot 412. To find all keys between two values you must scan every slot — O(n) work regardless of how narrow the range is. An ordered store keeps keys in lexicographic (sorted) order on disk and in memory. Keys that sort near each other are stored physically near each other. A range query then becomes a single seek to the start key followed by a sequential read to the end key — far cheaper than a full scan. A hash table scatters keys randomly across slots; an ordered store keeps them sorted so a range query reads a contiguous segment. Key Design: Putting Order to Work Because the sort is lexicographic (byte-by-byte, like dictionary order), you can engineer ranges directly into the structure of your keys. This is the single most important design skill in ordered key-value stores. Composite keys and natural ranges Consider an event log for a multi-tenant application. By composing the key as: <tenant_id>:<ISO-8601-timestamp>:<event_id> ...you get three useful range queries for free: Query goal Key prefix / range All events for tenant acme SCAN "acme:" … "acme:~" Events for acme in March 2024 SCAN "acme:2024-03-" … "acme:2024-03-~" Single event by exact ID GET "acme:2024-03-10T14:22:01Z:evt_991" The key insight: ISO 8601 timestamps sort correctly as plain strings ( "2024-03" < "2024-04" lexicographically), so no special date logic is needed. Numeric IDs need zero-padding for the same reason ( "007" < "010" whereas "7" > "10" ). Note: RocksDB, LevelDB, and FoundationDB — three widely-used ordered key-value stores — all expose this exact SCAN(start, end) primitive. Redis sorted sets offer a similar capability through ZRANGEBYLEX . The prefix scan pattern A prefix scan retrieves every key that starts with a given string. Implementations typically express the open end of the range by appending the highest sentinel character after the prefix: start = "acme:2024-03-" end = "acme:2024-03~" -- '~' (ASCII 126) sits above all printable alphanum chars This pattern appears everywhere — from namespace isolation in multi-tenant stores to per-user data shards in distributed systems. Simulating an Ordered Key-Value Store in SQLite SQLite's TEXT type compares strings lexicographically with < and > , making it a perfect sandbox for practicing ordered key patterns. The widget below seeds an event log and lets you explore range queries. Try the default query first, then modify the BETWEEN bounds to narrow or widen the range. Notice that the query only touches the rows it returns — no full scan needed when there is an index on the key column. Interactive SQL · Ordered Key Range Scan The tilde ~ (ASCII 126) sits just above all printable alphanumeric characters, making it a reliable "end of prefix" sentinel for open-ended scans. PostgreSQL Range Scans on B-tree Indexes PostgreSQL uses B-tree indexes as its default index type, and they are the workhorse for range queries. Key behaviors to know: PostgreSQL B-trees store index entries in sorted key order within each leaf page, and leaf pages are linked together in a doubly-linked list. A range scan performs a single seek to the start key (via B-tree traversal, O(log n)), then follows the leaf-page chain forward — reading multiple rows from each page — until the end key is reached. This is O(log n + k) where k is the result size. The Index Scan node reads matching index entries and fetches heap tuples. If the table is clustered on the index key ( CLUSTER command) or correlation is high, the heap fetches benefit from sequential I/O. With low correlation, a Bitmap Index Scan may be chosen instead — it builds a bitmap of matching pages and fetches them in physical order to reduce random I/O. Covering indexes ( INCLUDE clause, PostgreSQL 11+) allow an Index Only Scan that never visits the heap at all, dramatically improving range scan throughput — especially on wide tables. For time-series or append-only workloads, BRIN indexes (Block Range INdex) can be far more compact than B-trees while still supporting range scans efficiently, because naturally ordered data already clusters nearby values in the same blocks. Note: Use EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS) to see whether a query is using an Index Scan, Index Only Scan, or Bitmap Scan — and how many buffer hits it costs. The difference between an Index Scan (heap fetches per row) and an Index Only Scan (no heap fetches) can be 5–50× in throughput for large ranges. Maintaining sort order is not free. Ordered stores typically use a B-tree or an LSM-tree (log-structured merge-tree), both of which incur more write overhead than a simple hash table. Random inserts in a B-tree require page splits; LSM-trees batch writes but run background compaction to merge sorted runs. Property Hash KV (e.g. Redis) Ordered KV (e.g. RocksDB) Point lookup O(1) average O(log n) Range scan O(n) — full scan O(log n + k) where k = result size Write throughput Very high High (LSM) / moderate (B-tree) Key ordering None Lexicographic Typical use case Caching, sessions Event logs, time-series, sorted leaderboards Choose an ordered key-value store when your queries are naturally range-shaped — time windows, alphabetical sweeps, prefix lookups — and you are willing to encode that shape into your key design. Choose a hash store when you always know the exact key and need the lowest possible latency for point reads. Key Takeaways Hash tables scatter keys randomly — range queries require a full scan. Ordered stores keep keys in lexicographic order so range queries read a contiguous segment in O(log n + k). Key design is everything: timestamps, zero-padded numbers, and composite prefixes let you engineer range access patterns up front. ISO 8601 timestamps and zero-padded integers are self-ordering as plain strings — use them freely. The prefix-scan sentinel pattern ( prefix~ ) is a widely used idiom for expressing open-ended ranges. ← Previous Fast lookups and simple APIs Next → Common storage engines

title: Common storage engines
text: Under the hood, every key-value database is built on a storage engine — the component responsible for actually writing bytes to disk and reading them back efficiently. The simple GET / SET API you use as a developer is just a thin shell around one of a small number of proven physical data structures. Choosing a storage engine involves a real trade-off between write speed, read speed, memory use, and crash safety. This page covers the three engines you will encounter most often. Hash Tables: Pure Speed in RAM The most direct implementation of a key-value store is a hash table in memory. A hash function maps each key to a slot in an array; slot collisions are resolved by chaining or open addressing. Lookup, insert, and delete are all O(1) average. Redis uses this approach for its primary data store. Every key lives in a single in-memory hash table (called dict ), which means reads and writes are measured in microseconds — no disk I/O on the hot path. Persistence is handled separately: Redis writes a snapshot file (RDB) periodically, or appends every write to an append-only file (AOF). If the process crashes between snapshots, you can lose recent writes — an acceptable trade-off for a cache, less so for a primary database. Note: Memcached also uses an in-memory hash table but offers no persistence at all. It is a pure cache layer; a restart empties the store completely. Limitations: The dataset must fit in RAM. You cannot range-scan (iterate keys from a to z ) efficiently, because hash tables scatter keys unpredictably across buckets. For most key-value workloads this is fine — you always know your exact key. B-Trees: Ordered and Durable A B-tree stores keys in sorted order across a balanced tree of fixed-size pages (typically 4 KB or 16 KB), written directly to disk. Every internal node contains key ranges that guide the search; leaf nodes hold the actual key-value pairs. Lookup is O(log n) but with very few disk reads in practice — a three-level tree holds millions of keys while touching only three pages. LMDB (Lightning Memory-Mapped Database) is the canonical key-value engine built on a B-tree variant. It uses copy-on-write semantics: updates never overwrite live pages, they write a new version of the page and atomically flip the root pointer. This makes LMDB ACID-safe with zero write-ahead logging overhead. BerkeleyDB and many embedded databases take a similar approach. B-trees excel at range queries — because keys are sorted, iterating all keys from session:a to session:z is a sequential page scan. They also offer predictable read performance. The downside: random writes are expensive because updating a key often requires reading its page from disk, modifying it, and writing it back — causing many small random I/Os under a heavy write load. LSM-Trees: Write-Optimized for High Throughput The Log-Structured Merge-tree (LSM-tree) flips the B-tree trade-off: it sacrifices some read performance to make writes dramatically faster. This makes it the engine of choice for workloads with high write rates (time-series, event logs, metrics). The core idea has three stages: MemTable — incoming writes go into a sorted in-memory buffer (often a red-black tree or skip list). Reads check here first. Write-ahead log (WAL) — every write is also appended to a sequential log on disk for crash recovery. SSTables — when the MemTable fills up, it is flushed to disk as an immutable, sorted file called an SSTable (Sorted String Table). Background compaction periodically merges SSTables to reclaim space and remove stale versions. RocksDB (developed at Meta, forked from LevelDB) is the dominant LSM-tree engine. RocksDB powers TiKV , Pebble (CockroachDB), and many others. Apache Cassandra uses its own independent LSM-tree implementation inspired by the Bigtable paper. WiredTiger , the default storage engine in MongoDB, is B-tree based by default but offers an optional LSM-tree mode for write-heavy workloads. LSM-tree write path: writes land in the WAL and MemTable; when the MemTable is full it flushes to L0 SSTables on disk; background compaction merges levels. PostgreSQL note: PostgreSQL's default heap storage suffers from write amplification due to its MVCC model (old row versions remain until vacuumed). The zheap storage engine effort aims to address this with in-place updates and undo logs, reducing both write amplification and vacuum overhead. Additionally, BRIN indexes (Block Range INdexes) mitigate storage overhead for very large, naturally ordered tables by storing summary statistics per block range rather than per row — a useful tool when B-tree index size becomes the bottleneck. Read amplification is the LSM trade-off: a read might need to check the MemTable, then several L0 files, then L1, and so on until it finds the key (or confirms it is absent). Bloom filters on each SSTable reduce this cost by letting the engine skip files that definitely do not contain the key. Choosing Between Them Engine Write speed Read speed Range scans Dataset size Examples Hash table Very fast (RAM) Very fast (RAM) No Fits in RAM Redis, Memcached B-tree Moderate Fast, predictable Yes Disk-backed LMDB, BerkeleyDB LSM-tree Very fast (sequential) Moderate (bloom filters help) Yes Disk-backed, huge RocksDB, LevelDB Note: Most production key-value systems are not single-engine. Redis adds persistence layers on top of its hash table. RocksDB is embedded inside larger systems (TiKV, Pebble) that add distributed replication on top. The engine is one layer in a larger stack. The interactive widget below lets you observe a key behavior that distinguishes engines: with a B-tree-style sorted store, range scans are cheap; with a hash table, they require a full scan. Simulate both patterns and compare their EXPLAIN output. Interactive SQL · B-tree range scan vs hash scan Key Takeaways Hash tables store all keys in RAM for O(1) reads; dataset must fit in memory and range scans require a full scan. B-trees store sorted keys in fixed-size pages on disk; give O(log n) reads and support range queries with predictable latency. LSM-trees funnel all writes through a sequential path (WAL + MemTable → SSTables); maximize write throughput at the cost of read amplification, mitigated by bloom filters. Every engine has a maintenance phase (log compaction, page splits, or SSTable compaction) — understanding it prevents operational surprises. Production systems often layer engines: a fast in-memory hash table as a cache in front of a durable LSM or B-tree store behind. ← Previous Range scans and ordered keys Next → Caching vs persistence

title: Caching vs persistence
text: The previous pages showed how key-value stores trade query richness for raw speed. But speed means different things depending on what you are protecting against. A cache optimizes for fast reads — losing data on a crash is acceptable because the data can be recomputed. A persistent store optimizes for durability — data must survive crashes, restarts, and power cuts. The same key-value engine can serve both roles, but the configuration choices are very different. Two Different Contracts The core distinction is not about which software you run — it is about what you promise about the data . Property Cache mode Persistent store mode Data lives in RAM only RAM + durable storage Survives a crash? No (acceptable) Yes (required) Source of truth? No — origin DB holds real data Yes — this IS the real data Typical latency < 1 ms 1–5 ms (with fsync) Eviction policy Yes — LRU, LFU, etc. No (or capacity-based only) Examples Redis (cache-only config), Memcached Redis (AOF + RDB), RocksDB, etcd Note: Memcached is cache-only by design — it has no persistence mechanism at all. Redis was originally cache-first but added persistence options over time; today it is commonly used in both modes. The Cache Contract When you use a key-value store as a cache, the golden rule is: the cache is a copy, not the original . Your application writes the canonical data to a relational or document database, then populates the cache as a fast-path shortcut: 1. Request arrives: GET cache:product:9182 2. Cache hit → return cached value immediately 3. Cache miss → query origin DB, write result to cache, return value Because the data is always reconstructible from the origin, you can afford: No persistence — if the server reboots, you lose cached entries. Traffic hits the origin DB until the cache warms back up, then life continues normally. Eviction — when memory is full, the store discards least-recently-used (or least-frequently-used) keys automatically. You configure maxmemory and maxmemory-policy in Redis. The Persistence Contract When the key-value store is the system of record — think distributed configuration, session tokens that cannot be silently lost, or a job queue — you need durability guarantees comparable to a relational database. Redis offers two persistence mechanisms that can be combined: RDB (Redis Database) snapshots — Redis forks itself and writes a point-in-time binary snapshot to disk at configured intervals (e.g., every 60 s if 1000 keys changed). Fast to load on restart; you can lose up to one snapshot interval of writes. AOF (Append-Only File) — every write command is appended to a log file. On restart Redis replays the log. With appendfsync always , you get at most one command lost; with appendfsync everysec (the common default) you lose at most one second of writes. Cache mode accepts data loss on crash and relies on the origin DB to refill. Persistent mode replays the append-only log and recovers within seconds. Eviction: The Cache-Only Feature One concept that only makes sense in cache mode is eviction — the store automatically deletes old entries when it is under memory pressure. No relational database does this; in a source-of-truth store it would be catastrophic. In a cache it is entirely expected. Redis ships with several eviction policies: Policy What gets evicted allkeys-lru The least-recently-used key across all keys allkeys-lfu The least-frequently-used key across all keys volatile-lru LRU, but only among keys that have a TTL set volatile-ttl The key whose TTL will expire soonest noeviction Reject writes when full (good for persistent use) A persistent key-value store should use noeviction (or set a very large maxmemory and alert on it). Silently deleting data from a system of record is a bug, not a feature. Choosing Your Mode — and Mixing Both In practice, many production systems run two separate Redis instances : one configured for caching (fast, eviction enabled, no AOF) and one for persistence (AOF enabled, noeviction , replicated). This avoids the accidental footgun of cache eviction wiping out durable data. The decision tree is simple: Can you reconstruct the data if it disappears? → Cache mode is fine. Would losing this data cause incorrect behavior or lost work? → Enable persistence (AOF + RDB), set noeviction , and treat it as a database. Try the widget below. It models a cache table with TTL-style expiry and a persistent store without expiry. Notice how you can simulate a cache miss by querying for a key with an expired entry, versus a persistent store where no expiry field exists. Interactive SQL · Cache vs Persistent Store Note: Real Redis TTL eviction is handled inside the engine — you never write the WHERE expires_epoch > now() check yourself. The SQL widget above makes the mechanism visible by modeling it explicitly. The key insight is the same: cache entries are transient, persistent entries are not. Key Takeaways A cache is a copy of data that lives elsewhere — data loss on crash is acceptable and expected. A persistent key-value store is the source of truth — durability guarantees (AOF, RDB) are required. Eviction (automatically deleting keys under memory pressure) is a cache feature; it is dangerous in a persistence context. Redis supports both modes through configuration; Memcached is cache-only by design. Many production systems run separate instances for caching and persistence to prevent accidental data loss from eviction. ← Previous Common storage engines Next → When key-value is enough

title: When key-value is enough
text: Key-value stores are the simplest database model you will encounter: every piece of data is stored under a unique key, and you retrieve it by that key — nothing more, nothing less. No joins, no schemas, no query planner. That simplicity is their superpower, but also their limit. Knowing when to reach for one — and when not to — is a practical skill every developer needs. The Core Mechanic A key-value store is conceptually a giant hash map persisted to disk (and often to memory). Every operation reduces to three primitives: Operation Meaning SET key value Store a value under a key GET key Retrieve the value for a key DEL key Remove a key and its value The value can be anything: a string, a serialized JSON blob, a binary image, a counter. The store doesn't care — it treats the value as an opaque byte sequence. Your application gives it meaning. An application issues SET and GET commands; the store maps opaque keys to opaque values with no schema enforced. When It Fits Perfectly Key-value thinking works best when your access pattern is: "I know exactly which record I want." You have the key; you want the value; done. Common real-world fits: Session storage — look up session:<token> on every HTTP request. Every web framework has done this with Redis for years. Caching — store expensive computed results under a cache key, set a TTL, let them expire automatically. Rate limiting — increment a counter keyed by rate:<user>:<window> . One atomic operation, sub-millisecond latency. Feature flags — flag:dark-mode:user:99 → true . Simple reads, updated rarely. Leaderboards / counters — sorted sets (a Redis extension) or plain counters indexed by key. The pattern they all share: a single lookup by a known identifier , with no need to filter, sort, or join across records. Note: Popular key-value engines include Redis, Memcached (memory-only, no persistence), DynamoDB (when used in simple key-lookup mode), and RocksDB (the embedded engine powering many larger systems like Kafka and TiKV). When It Falls Short Key-value stores are a poor fit the moment you need to ask questions about the data rather than just fetch a known record: "Show me all users who signed up last week" — requires scanning, no SQL WHERE clause exists. "Which orders belong to customer 42?" — foreign-key relationships don't exist natively. "Sum revenue by country" — aggregation requires reading every relevant key. You can work around these limitations (prefix scans, secondary indexes in some engines), but at that point you are reimplementing what a relational or document database already does well. A useful rule of thumb: Access pattern Best fit Exact lookup by ID / token Key-value Filter / range / sort on attributes Relational or document Graph traversal Graph DB Full-text search Search engine (Elasticsearch, etc.) Try It: Simulating a Key-Value Store in SQLite A key-value store is conceptually a two-column table. This widget lets you explore that idea — insert, read, and expire entries just like a simple cache would. Interactive SQL · Key-Value simulation Notice how every lookup uses WHERE key = ... — an exact match on the primary key. That single-row retrieval is exactly what makes key-value stores so fast: the engine never scans; it hashes or B-tree-walks straight to the record. Reveal: What happens if you try to query kv_store for all sessions belonging to user 1? You would need to scan every row and parse the JSON value to find "uid":1 — or use a LIKE '%uid":1%' hack. A real key-value store has no query planner for that; you'd have to maintain a separate index key like user:1:sessions → ["abc1"] yourself. That's the trade-off: blazing speed for known-key lookups, awkward workarounds for anything resembling a search. The bottom line: if your workload lives and dies by "give me the thing with this ID," a key-value store will outperform a relational database on latency, throughput, and operational simplicity. When you start needing to ask questions about the data, it's time to reach for a richer model. ← Previous Caching vs persistence Next → Limits of the model

title: Limits of the model
text: The key-value model's radical simplicity is its greatest strength — and its most important constraint. Once you step outside the narrow corridor of "look up a value by its exact key," the model offers you very little help. Understanding where the walls are is just as important as knowing where the model excels. The One Rule Everything Else Follows A key-value store has exactly one access path: the key . If you know the full key, you get constant-time retrieval. If you do not know the full key, you are in trouble. There is no query planner, no secondary index (in the base model), no WHERE clause. Every limitation below flows directly from this single design decision. Everything on the left is O(1) by design. Everything on the right requires inspecting values — the key-value model offers no help here. The Four Core Limits 1. You Cannot Query Inside Values The store treats every value as an opaque blob of bytes. It never parses, indexes, or interprets what is inside. If you store {"role":"admin","country":"DE"} as a JSON value, there is no command to ask "give me all keys where role is admin." You must either: Know the key in advance (which defeats the point of searching), or Scan every key , deserialize every value, and filter in your application. That scan is O(n) over your entire dataset — exactly what the hash-map design was built to avoid. 2. There Are No Relations A key-value store has no foreign keys, no join semantics, and no referential integrity. If you store a user profile under user:8 and a set of sessions under session:8:* , nothing in the database enforces the link. Delete the user and the sessions remain. There is no cascade, no constraint, no transaction that spans multiple logical entities by default. Note: Some systems (Redis Lua scripts, Redis transactions via MULTI/EXEC ) allow multi-key atomicity, but these are bolt-on features, not first-class relational semantics. They do not give you joins or foreign-key enforcement. 3. Range Queries Are Second-Class In a hash-based store, keys are scattered by hash — there is no natural ordering. Asking for "all rate-limit counters from the last hour" requires knowing every key in advance, or scanning the entire keyspace. Some key-value engines (like RocksDB or sorted key spaces in Redis with ZRANGE ) support lexicographic key ranges , which partially addresses this — but only if you have deliberately designed your keys so that the range you want maps to a contiguous prefix. This requires careful, upfront key design and cannot be retrofitted. 4. Consistency Across Multiple Keys Is Hard Operations on a single key are typically atomic. Operations across two or more keys are not, unless the engine provides an explicit multi-key transaction (which many do not, and those that do often sacrifice performance to offer it). This means patterns like "decrement inventory AND create an order" require extra design work — optimistic locking, application-level locking, or choosing a database with first-class transaction support. When You Hit a Wall: What to Reach For Limitation you are hitting Better tool Need to query inside values Document store (MongoDB, Firestore) Need joins and relations Relational database (PostgreSQL, MySQL) Need range scans and ordering Wide-column or relational store Need cross-entity transactions Relational DB with ACID transactions Need full-text search Search engine (Elasticsearch, Typesense) This is not a failure of key-value databases — it is a deliberate design trade-off. They give up expressiveness to achieve speed and operational simplicity. The moment your access patterns stop fitting the "I know the key" mold, that trade-off is no longer working in your favor. The widget below makes the wall concrete. Notice what happens when you try to search inside values or aggregate across keys — the query must scan every row, bypassing the primary key index entirely. Interactive SQL · Where the key-value model breaks down Reveal: Why can't we just add an index on value? A secondary index on the raw value column only lets you do exact or prefix matches on the entire serialized blob — it cannot index a field inside the JSON. To query efficiently on role or country , you would need to extract those fields into separate columns (breaking the key-value model) or use a document store that maintains indexes on nested fields natively. That is exactly why document databases exist: they are key-value stores that understand the structure of their values. ← Previous When key-value is enough Next → Real-world examples

title: Real-world examples
text: Theory is clean; production is messier and more interesting. Key-value databases power some of the highest-traffic systems on earth — and understanding which system engineers reach for, and why , makes the abstractions from earlier pages suddenly concrete. The Big Four in Production Four names dominate the key-value landscape. Each makes a different trade-off on the same fundamental model. System Primary Use Where Values Live Notable Feature Redis Cache, session store, pub/sub RAM (persisted to disk optionally) Rich value types (lists, sorted sets, streams) Memcached Pure cache layer RAM only — no persistence Simpler than Redis; scales horizontally by adding nodes Amazon DynamoDB Durable application data at cloud scale SSD (fully managed) Single-digit-millisecond latency; serverless billing etcd Distributed configuration & service discovery SSD (Raft-replicated) Strong consistency; used as Kubernetes' brain Each occupies a distinct niche. Redis is the default choice when you need speed plus some structure in the values. Memcached is the choice when you need a pure, stateless cache with nothing extra. DynamoDB is the choice when you need a key-value store that your ops team never has to touch. etcd is the choice when correctness matters more than throughput — it is optimized for configuration data that a cluster stakes its life on. Redis: The Swiss Army Key-Value Store Redis is the first tool most teams reach for. At its core it is still a key-value store — every command addresses a key — but it extends the value side with six distinct types: String → SET user:4291:name "Alice" # plain bytes or numbers List → RPUSH notifications:4291 "msg1" # ordered list, push/pop both ends Hash → HSET user:4291 email a@example.com # mini key-value inside a key Set → SADD online_users 4291 # unordered, no duplicates ZSet → ZADD leaderboard 9800 user:4291 # sorted set with a numeric score Stream → XADD events * action purchase # append-only log with consumer groups The killer feature is that all operations are atomic . INCR rate:user:4291:hour increments an integer counter in one round-trip with no race condition. ZADD leaderboard keeps a scoreboard sorted at all times without a SELECT … ORDER BY query. These primitives let application code stay simple while the data structure does the heavy lifting. Note: Redis is single-threaded for command execution. That sounds like a limitation, but it is the reason every command is inherently atomic — there is no need for locks. This design choice lets Redis sustain millions of operations per second on a single node. A Caching Layer in Practice The most common Redis deployment sits between your application and your relational database as a read-through cache . The idea: store the result of expensive queries under a predictable key; serve subsequent requests from RAM without touching the database. Cache-aside pattern: the app checks Redis first; on a miss it queries PostgreSQL and writes the result back to Redis with a TTL. The application code for this pattern is typically fewer than ten lines — check the cache, populate it on a miss, serve subsequent requests from RAM. A five-minute TTL on product data means stale data is bounded and the database sees a fraction of the traffic it would otherwise handle. DynamoDB: Key-Value at Cloud Scale While Redis is typically self-hosted (or managed via ElastiCache), Amazon DynamoDB is a fully managed key-value and document store where you provision throughput, not servers. Every table has a partition key (the key in "key-value") and an optional sort key. Reading by exact partition key is the primary fast path, exactly as in any key-value store. Teams building on AWS use DynamoDB for things like: User profiles — userId as partition key; one item per user. Game state — gameId → entire serialized game state blob. Shopping carts — userId as partition key, itemId as sort key. The trade-off: querying outside the key requires either a full scan (expensive) or a secondary index (you must declare it up front). The key-value access pattern is the happy path; anything else is a signal to reconsider the data model. etcd: Key-Value for Infrastructure etcd is a specialized key-value store optimized for one thing: storing small, critical configuration data that a distributed cluster agrees on. It is the storage layer behind Kubernetes — every pod definition, service endpoint, and cluster configuration lives as a key-value pair replicated across an etcd cluster using the Raft consensus protocol. Unlike Redis, etcd sacrifices raw throughput for strong consistency : every read is guaranteed to see the latest write. That guarantee is what lets a Kubernetes control plane trust that two nodes do not simultaneously believe they are the leader. Note: etcd is intentionally not a general-purpose cache. Its recommended dataset size is a few gigabytes; for anything larger, teams use a relational database or object store. The key-value model fits here because Kubernetes always looks up configuration by a known path — never runs ad-hoc queries across values. Putting It Together: Session Store Simulation The widget below simulates the session store and rate-limiting patterns that Redis handles in production. Notice how every operation is an exact key lookup or a counter increment — the workload is perfectly shaped for a key-value store. Interactive SQL · Session store + rate limiting Uncomment the second query to check a user's rate-limit counter, then try the third to see why a "find all expired sessions" scan is an anti-pattern — in a real key-value store, you would rely on TTL expiry instead of querying. Reveal: Why can't you just query Redis for all sessions belonging to a user? Redis stores keys in a flat hash table. To find all keys matching a pattern (like session:user:4291:* ) you would have to call SCAN with a match filter — which iterates every key in the store. At millions of keys this is slow and blocks other clients. The correct Redis pattern is to maintain a secondary index yourself: for example, a Set under user:4291:sessions that lists session IDs. You manage it explicitly; the key-value store does not do it for you. This is the fundamental trade-off: you get speed and simplicity, but you lose declarative querying. ← Previous Limits of the model Next → Design patterns

title: Design patterns
text: Key-value stores are deceptively simple — a key maps to a value, full stop. But that simplicity is exactly what makes them fast, and experienced engineers have developed a handful of recurring design patterns that extract surprising power from this primitive. Understanding these patterns tells you both when to reach for a key-value store and how to structure your data inside one. Pattern 1: Key Namespacing Because a key-value store is one flat namespace, you need a convention to group related keys. The standard approach is hierarchical prefixes separated by a delimiter ( : or / are most common). Key Value Purpose session:user:4291 {"token":"abc","exp":1719000000} User session cache:page:homepage <rendered HTML> Page cache rate:ip:203.0.113.42 47 Request counter lock:order:9988 worker-3 Distributed lock This lets a single store serve multiple concerns while keeping keys predictable and debuggable. You can scan all keys matching session:* to find active sessions, or delete cache:* to flush the cache layer — most key-value stores support prefix scans or pattern matching for exactly this. Note: Namespacing is a convention enforced by your application, not the database. The store treats session:user:4291 as an opaque string; it has no idea the : means anything. Document your key schema the same way you document a table schema. Pattern 2: TTL-Based Expiry Key-value stores almost always support setting a time-to-live (TTL) on a key. After the TTL expires the key is automatically deleted — no background job, no cron, no manual cleanup. This single feature powers three extremely common use cases: Session storage — set TTL to the session idle timeout; the session evicts itself when the user goes away. Caching — set TTL to how stale you can tolerate. The next miss regenerates the value and resets the clock. Rate limiting — store a request counter keyed on rate:{user_id} with a TTL of one minute. Increment on each request; reject if the counter exceeds the limit. The key self-resets each window. A key lives from write until its TTL fires. Reads after expiry get a cache miss; the application regenerates and re-writes the value. Pattern 3: Counter and Accumulator Keys Incrementing a counter looks trivial, but doing it safely under concurrent load is not. Key-value stores expose an atomic increment operation ( INCR in Redis, update_item with ADD in DynamoDB) that reads and writes the counter in a single, indivisible step — no read-modify-write race condition. Common uses: page-view counts, API call quotas, inventory quantities (decrement on purchase), vote tallies. Try it below. The rate_limit table simulates a simple counter store — each INSERT represents an incoming request, and the query checks whether any key has exceeded its limit. Interactive SQL · Rate-limit counter pattern Pattern 4: Fan-Out and Aggregation Keys Sometimes you want to read multiple related values together. Rather than fetching hundreds of individual keys, engineers pre-aggregate into a single key whose value is a small JSON blob or list — a pattern called fan-out on write . A social media feed is the classic example. Instead of computing "all posts from accounts Alice follows" at read time (expensive), each new post is written into every follower's feed key. A read is then a single key lookup. feed:user:4291 → ["post:88","post:74","post:31", ...] (pre-computed list) The trade-off: writes become heavier (one post → many writes), but reads stay O(1).
