This is only sensible when reads vastly outnumber writes and follower counts stay bounded. Note: Fan-out breaks down for celebrities or accounts with millions of followers — writing one post to 10 million feed keys in real time is impractical. Real systems use a hybrid: pre-compute feeds for ordinary users, compute on-the-fly for high-follower accounts. Reveal: Why not just store everything in one big key? A single monolithic key becomes a bottleneck: every reader contends for the same lock, the value grows unbounded, and a single write can invalidate everything. Splitting data across many fine-grained keys distributes load across the cluster and lets TTLs be set independently per logical unit. The art is finding the right granularity — too coarse and you lose parallelism; too fine and you need too many round-trips to assemble a response. ← Previous Real-world examples Next → Chapter 18 Summary: What We Learned

title: Chapter 18 Summary: What We Learned
text: Page Topic Takeaway in One Sentence 171 The key-value model A simple dictionary: put(key, value), get(key), delete(key). The simplest database model with the fewest guarantees. 172 Fast lookups and simple APIs With no query language or schema, key-value stores achieve sub-millisecond latency — ideal for caching and session storage. 173 Range scans and ordered keys Ordered key-value stores (like RocksDB) support range queries on sorted keys, useful for time-series and prefix scans. 174 Common storage engines LSM trees (write-optimized) and B-trees (read-optimized) are the two dominant key-value storage backends. 175 Caching vs persistence Some key-value stores prioritize speed and cache data (Redis); others prioritize durability and persist to disk (RocksDB). 176 When key-value is enough When access is always by primary key, no joins are needed, and data is simple — session tokens, config, counters. 177 Limits of the model No joins, no aggregations, no declarative queries. Complex queries require application code. Embedding logic in keys is a warning sign. 178 Real-world examples Redis for caching/rate-limiting, DynamoDB for simple workloads, etcd for configuration — each optimized for a specific pattern. 179 Design patterns Use TTL for auto-expiry, hash slots for partitioning, sorted sets for leaderboards — key-value systems reward clever key design. Reveal: How do these ideas connect to the next chapter? Chapter 18 covered the simplest database model: key-value. But what if your data has structure — nested objects, arrays, variable fields — and you want to store it without decomposing it into relational tables? Chapter 19 introduces document databases. You will learn JSON document storage, schema flexibility, embedding versus referencing, and when the document model is a natural fit versus when it becomes a trap. Key-value gave you raw speed; documents add structure without the rigidity of tables. ← Previous Design patterns Next → JSON as a data model

title: JSON as a data model
text: JSON stores a complete record as a single self-contained document with nested structure. Great for flexibility; less so for cross-document relationships. JSON — JavaScript Object Notation — started life as a lightweight wire format for sending data between a browser and a server. It turned out to be such a natural way to describe a real-world record that the database world adopted it as a primary data model . Understanding JSON as a data model means understanding both why it resonates and what it costs . What JSON Actually Is JSON has exactly six value types: PostgreSQL note: The JSON standard defines only one numeric type (IEEE 754 double-precision), but PostgreSQL's jsonb type stores numbers using the NUMERIC type internally — preserving arbitrary precision and avoiding floating-point rounding errors. This means 0.1 + 0.2 stored in a JSONB column retains exact decimal precision, unlike standard JSON implementations that would lose fidelity through float64 representation. Type Example String "Bangkok" Number 42 , 3.14 Boolean true , false Null null Array ["read", "write", "admin"] Object {"city": "Bangkok", "zip": "10110"} Objects and arrays can nest arbitrarily deep. That single rule — nesting is allowed — is what makes JSON expressive enough to represent almost any real-world entity without splitting it across multiple tables. { "id": "usr-8821", "name": "Priya Kapoor", "email": "priya@example.com", "address": { "street": "12 Sukhumvit Rd", "city": "Bangkok", "country": "TH" }, "roles": ["editor", "reviewer"], "metadata": { "created_at": "2024-01-15", "last_login": "2025-06-20" } } All the information about Priya lives in one document — no joins required to load it. This is the core promise of the document model. How JSON Differs from the Relational Row In a relational table every row must conform to the same fixed schema. Adding a new field means an ALTER TABLE migration, and absent values turn into NULL columns that waste space and complicate logic. A JSON document has no such contract. Two documents in the same collection can look completely different: Relational rows share a fixed schema; JSON documents carry only the fields they need. Nested Objects vs. Joins The deepest practical difference is how related data is stored. In a relational model, an order and its line items live in separate tables linked by a foreign key — you retrieve them together with a JOIN . In the document model, the line items are embedded inside the order document: { "order_id": "ORD-5512", "customer": "usr-8821", "items": [ { "sku": "NB-001", "qty": 2, "unit_price": 2.99 }, { "sku": "LP-042", "qty": 1, "unit_price": 999.00 } ], "total": 1004.98 } Fetching the full order is one read — no join. This is extremely efficient for document-centric access patterns : you almost always want the whole thing, and you rarely need to query deeply into the embedded arrays from the outside. Note: Embedding has limits. If you embed too much — for example, storing all comments inside a blog post document — the document grows unboundedly and updates become expensive. The general rule: embed data that is owned by the parent and always retrieved with it; reference data that has its own identity or is shared across documents. The Schema Flexibility Trade-off Schema flexibility is real and useful — you can add a new field to one document without touching any others. But it shifts the enforcement burden from the database to your application code. Without discipline: Documents in the same collection drift into inconsistent shapes. A typo like "emial" instead of "email" silently stores bad data. Queries that assume a field exists will silently miss documents that lack it. Most document databases now offer optional schema validation (MongoDB's JSON Schema, Firestore's security rules). Many teams adopt these after their first painful inconsistency bug. Reveal: When should you embed vs. reference a sub-document? Embed when: (1) the sub-data is owned by the parent and has no independent existence, (2) you always need the sub-data when you load the parent, and (3) the sub-data is bounded in size (e.g., a fixed list of tags, not an ever-growing history). Reference (store only an ID) when: the sub-entity is shared across many parents (e.g., a product referenced by thousands of orders), the sub-data grows unboundedly (e.g., user activity logs), or you need to query the sub-entity independently. Querying JSON: Paths and Filters Document databases expose a query language built around field paths — dotted notation that drills into nested objects. For example, in MongoDB you would write: // Find all orders where the first item's SKU is NB-001 db.orders.find({ "items.0.sku": "NB-001" }) // Find orders whose total exceeds 500 db.orders.find({ total: { $gt: 500 } }) Even relational databases have adopted JSON path syntax. SQLite's json_extract() and PostgreSQL's -> / ->> operators let you query into JSON columns with similar path expressions. Try it yourself below. Run this query to extract and filter fields stored as JSON text: Interactive SQL · Querying JSON fields Try changing the threshold ( > 100 ) or extracting a different path like $.items[1].sku . Notice that when an index is out of range, the -> operator returns NULL — the document model's version of a missing column. ← Previous Design patterns Next → Flexible schema

title: Flexible schema
text: Document databases are often described as "schemaless," but that label is misleading. A better framing: document databases have a flexible schema — one that the database doesn't enforce by default, but that your application implicitly relies on. Understanding what schema flexibility really means, and when it helps vs. hurts, is essential for working effectively with any document store. What "Schema" Means in Different Systems In a relational database, the schema is a first-class citizen: you declare it with CREATE TABLE , and the engine enforces it on every write. The database will reject a NULL in a NOT NULL column, a string where an integer belongs, or a value that violates a foreign key constraint. In a document database, there is no upfront declaration of field names or types. Each document is just a blob of JSON (or BSON, or similar). Two documents in the same collection can look entirely different: // Document A { "_id": "1", "type": "user", "name": "Priya", "email": "priya@x.com", "roles": ["admin"] } // Document B { "_id": "2", "type": "user", "username": "leo99", "tier": "pro", "credits": 450 } Both are valid. No migration needed. This is the flexibility that attracts developers working on rapidly evolving products. Old and new document shapes coexist in the same collection. The application handles version differences. The Spectrum from Fully Flexible to Enforced Schema flexibility is not binary. Most document database systems sit somewhere on a continuum: Level Description Who enforces? No validation Any JSON accepted Nobody — bugs silently stored App-level validation Code checks fields before write Application layer Optional DB schema Validation rules in DB config Database (at write time) Strict mode All fields must be declared Database (full enforcement) MongoDB calls its option JSON Schema Validation . You attach a $jsonSchema validator to a collection and the database rejects writes that violate it. Firestore uses security rules. CouchDB uses design documents with validate functions. Best practice: Start without strict validation when the schema is genuinely unknown. Add validation rules as soon as the schema stabilizes — usually before the first production deployment. Letting an inconsistent schema persist for months is the most common document-database regret. Schema-on-Read vs. Schema-on-Write These two terms capture the fundamental difference: Schema-on-write (relational databases): the schema is declared up front; the database validates and rejects bad data at write time. Schema-on-read (document databases by default): any data is accepted; the application imposes structure when it reads and parses the document. Neither is universally superior. Schema-on-write catches bugs early and makes data trustworthy. Schema-on-read allows faster iteration and accommodates heterogeneous data (e.g., events from many sources with different shapes). The key is knowing which mode you're in and making a deliberate choice. Practical Consequences of Flexibility The Good No migration needed for additive changes. Adding a new field to documents going forward requires zero database work. New code writes the field; old documents simply don't have it. Heterogeneous data fits naturally. An event log, a product catalog with wildly different product types, or data ingested from external APIs with variable shapes are all easier to store without forcing them into a fixed schema. Rapid prototyping is faster. Early in a project, the domain model changes constantly. Document databases let you iterate without running migrations. The Costly Application code becomes the schema. Every place that reads a document must defensively handle missing fields, wrong types, and version drift. This is hidden complexity. Querying partial collections is fragile. A query that filters on status = 'active' will silently miss documents that stored the status as is_active due to an old naming convention. Refactoring is hard. Renaming a field means writing a migration script that touches every document — exactly what you wanted to avoid. Without discipline, these migrations accumulate as debt. Versioning Documents A common pattern for managing schema evolution without painful migrations is document versioning : store a schema_version field in each document and handle all versions in the application. { "_id": "usr-001", "schema_version": 1, "name": "Priya", "email": "p@x.com" } { "_id": "usr-002", "schema_version": 2, "name": "Leo", "email": "l@x.com", "preferences": { "theme": "dark", "notifications": true } } Your application's loadUser() function reads schema_version and applies the appropriate parsing path — or migrates the document on first read (the lazy migration pattern). This keeps old documents working without a bulk rewrite. Reveal: What is lazy migration and when does it break down? Lazy migration (also called "migrate on read") means: when you load an old-version document, you upgrade it in memory and immediately save it back in the new shape. This spreads the migration cost over time rather than requiring a big-bang script. It works well when (1) you read every document eventually, (2) the migration is purely additive, and (3) writes are safe to do at read time. It breaks down when: old documents are rarely accessed (some will never be migrated), the migration requires data from external sources, or you need the entire collection to be in the new shape before deploying new code (you can't do a blue-green deploy safely if both shapes are possible). When to Use Flexible Schema Flexible schema shines when: The domain is genuinely heterogeneous (different document types with unrelated fields). You are early in development and the model changes weekly. You ingest data from external systems you don't control. It is a liability when: Multiple services or teams depend on the schema — lack of enforcement creates coordination failures. You need strong data quality guarantees for analytics or compliance. The "flexibility" is actually just inconsistency that has built up over time. Key Takeaways "Schemaless" means the database doesn't enforce structure — not that you don't have a schema. You always have one; it just lives in code. Schema-on-read gives speed and flexibility at the cost of moving enforcement responsibility to the application. Optional schema validation (MongoDB's $jsonSchema , Firestore rules) gives you the best of both worlds — use it. Document versioning with lazy migration is a practical pattern for evolving a schema without downtime. Schema drift is the main long-term risk. Invest in validation rules before your collection grows to millions of inconsistent documents. ← Previous JSON as a data model Next → Nested data and arrays

title: Nested data and arrays
text: One of the defining features of document databases is the ability to store nested objects and arrays directly inside a document — no separate tables, no join keys, no foreign-key relationships. This page explores how nesting works, why it is powerful, and where it creates problems that catch teams off guard. What Nesting Actually Means In JSON (and therefore in most document databases), a value can itself be an object or an array. That means a "row" is not a flat list of scalar values — it is a tree : { "_id": "order-9901", "placed_at": "2025-06-01T08:30:00Z", "customer": { "id": "cust-441", "name": "Aroha Ngata", "email": "aroha@example.com" }, "shipping_address": { "street": "14 Quay St", "city": "Auckland", "country": "NZ" }, "items": [ { "sku": "SHOE-9B", "size": 42, "qty": 1, "unit_price": 120.00 }, { "sku": "SOCK-3P", "size": null, "qty": 3, "unit_price": 8.50 } ], "discounts": ["SUMMER10", "LOYALTY5"], "total": 145.50 } The customer and shipping_address fields are nested objects. items is an array of objects. discounts is an array of strings. All of this ships as one blob — one read retrieves the complete entity. A document is a tree. A single read retrieves the entire tree — no joins needed. Arrays of Objects: The Most Common Pattern Arrays of embedded objects are ubiquitous in document databases. Common examples: Parent Document Embedded Array Order Line items Blog post Comments Invoice Tax breakdown entries User profile Notification preferences Product Variant sizes/colors The advantage: reading one document gives you everything. No JOIN orders LEFT JOIN order_items ON … . That's one round trip instead of two, and no join algorithm to plan. Querying Into Arrays Document databases provide operators that work against array contents. In MongoDB the $elemMatch operator filters on element-level conditions: // Find orders that contain a SHOE-9B item with qty >= 2 db.orders.find({ items: { $elemMatch: { sku: "SHOE-9B", qty: { $gte: 2 } } } }) SQLite exposes the same concept through json_each() — a table-valued function that unnests an array into rows you can filter with WHERE : SELECT o.order_id, e.value->>'$.sku' AS sku FROM orders o, jsonb_each(o.doc::jsonb->'items') e WHERE e.value->>'qty' >= 2; When Nesting Causes Problems Nesting is efficient when the embedded data is bounded and owned by the parent. It creates problems in three scenarios: 1. Unbounded Growth If you embed every comment inside a blog post, the post document grows without limit. Large documents: Require reading the entire blob even when you only want the title. Hit document size limits (MongoDB's 16 MB BSON limit, for example). Cause high write amplification — rewriting the full document just to add one comment. Fix: Move high-cardinality arrays (comments, events, log entries) to a separate collection and reference them. 2. Updating a Specific Element To update item[1].qty in a document, most databases require a positional operator (MongoDB's $ or $[filter] ), which can be tricky: // Increment qty for the SOCK-3P item db.orders.updateOne( { _id: "order-9901", "items.sku": "SOCK-3P" }, { $inc: { "items.$.qty": 1 } } ) In contrast, updating a row in a relational order_items table is a simple UPDATE … WHERE id = ? . 3. Querying Across Parents Arrays are great when your query starts from the parent. They break down when you need to ask "find all items where the item's supplier is X" — now you must scan every document and unnest every array. A separate items collection with an index on supplier_id handles this in milliseconds. Rule of thumb: If you need to query the array elements from the outside more than you need to load them as part of the parent, move them to their own collection. Practical Nesting Decision Table Scenario Embed (nest) Reference (separate collection) Tags, categories on a post Yes — few, bounded No Line items on an order Usually yes If items are shared across orders Comments on a post No — unbounded Yes Activity log on a user No — unbounded Yes Address on a user Yes — owned, 1-3 max No Product variants Yes — bounded If variants are a large catalog Interactive Example: Unnesting Arrays with SQLite Interactive SQL · Querying nested arrays with json_each Try changing 'SHOE-9B' to 'SOCK-3P' or 'HAT-1X' to query different items. Notice that json_each effectively "joins" the array elements back into rows — the same mental model used by document database array query operators. Key Takeaways Nesting is natural in documents: use nested objects and arrays for data that is owned by and always loaded with the parent. Arrays of objects eliminate joins for the common "load the parent with all its children" pattern. Watch out for unbounded arrays — every added element bloats the parent document and increases write cost. Querying into arrays is possible but expensive at scale; indexes on array elements help, but separate collections are often the right answer for high-cardinality child data. The decision to embed vs. reference is the most important modeling decision in a document database. ← Previous Flexible schema Next → Secondary indexes on documents

title: Secondary indexes on documents
text: Document databases are often portrayed as simple key-lookup stores — fetch a document by its _id and you're done. In practice, real applications need to filter, sort, and paginate on many different fields. That's where secondary indexes come in. This page explains how secondary indexes work in document databases, what makes them trickier than in relational systems, and how to reason about indexing nested fields and arrays. The Default: Primary Key Lookup Every document in a collection has a unique identifier — MongoDB's _id , Firestore's document ID, CouchDB's _id . The database maintains a primary index on this field automatically. Any query that specifies the full _id value is a direct lookup: O(log n) at worst, typically a B-tree traversal to a leaf. Everything else — filtering by status , sorting by created_at , finding by email — requires either a full collection scan or a secondary index. What a Secondary Index Looks Like A secondary index on a document field is structurally the same as a secondary index in a relational database: a B-tree (or hash, or other structure) keyed by the indexed field's value, with a pointer to the full document. Secondary index on "status": "active" → [doc-001, doc-004, doc-009, ...] "inactive"→ [doc-002, doc-007, ...] "pending" → [doc-003, doc-005, ...] When you query { status: "active" } , the engine walks the secondary index to find the matching document IDs, then fetches those documents from the primary store. Without the index, it scans every document in the collection. A secondary index narrows the query to matching document IDs; the engine then fetches full documents from the primary store. Indexing Nested Fields The ability to index fields inside nested objects is one area where document databases diverge from simple key-value stores. In MongoDB you create an index with dotted-path notation: // Index the city field inside the address sub-object db.users.createIndex({ "address.city": 1 }) // Query uses the index automatically db.users.find({ "address.city": "Auckland" }) The database walks the document tree to extract address.city at write time and inserts the value into the secondary index. Queries on that path then use the index instead of scanning documents. Note: Indexing is always a write-time cost. Every time a document is inserted or its indexed field changes, all affected indexes must be updated. More indexes = slower writes. Index only what your queries actually need. Multikey Indexes: Indexing Arrays When you index an array field, a document database creates one index entry per array element . This is called a multikey index (MongoDB's term) or array index. db.products.createIndex({ tags: 1 }) // Document: { _id: "p1", tags: ["footwear", "sale", "sport"] } // Creates three index entries: // "footwear" → p1 // "sale" → p1 // "sport" → p1 This lets you query { tags: "sale" } efficiently — the index is checked for the single value "sale" and returns p1 instantly. Trade-off: A document with a 50-element array creates 50 index entries. Arrays with many elements balloon index size and slow writes. Compound Indexes on Documents Just as in relational databases, you can create compound (multi-field) indexes: // Supports queries that filter on status AND sort by created_at db.orders.createIndex({ status: 1, created_at: -1 }) The same left-prefix rule applies: a compound index on (status, created_at) helps queries on status alone, but not queries on created_at alone. Covered Queries A covered query is one where the index contains all the fields needed to satisfy the query — no document fetch is required. This works the same way as in relational databases: // If index is { email: 1, name: 1 }, this query is covered: db.users.find({ email: "a@x.com" }, { name: 1, _id: 0 }) // Returns name from the index itself — never reads the document Covered queries can be orders of magnitude faster for read-heavy workloads. Index Types Available in Document Databases Index Type Description When to use Single-field One field, ascending or descending Most common; point lookups and range scans Compound Multiple fields Queries that filter/sort on multiple fields Multikey Array fields (one entry per element) { tags: "X" } style array queries Text Full-text tokenization Search-style keyword queries Geospatial 2D/2dsphere for lat/lng Location-based queries TTL Expire documents automatically Session data, logs with a retention window Partial Index only documents matching a filter Large collections where you only query a subset Sparse Omit documents where the field is missing Optional fields that appear in few documents Interactive Example Interactive SQL · Secondary index on a JSON field (simulated) The product_tags table simulates a multikey index — one row per tag per product, exactly as MongoDB stores multikey index entries. The compound query at the bottom shows how a join against the expanded index structure replaces a full document scan. Key Takeaways Secondary indexes in document databases work the same way as in relational databases: B-tree or hash structures pointing to documents by their primary key. Dotted-path syntax lets you index fields inside nested objects ( address.city ). Multikey indexes on arrays create one index entry per array element — powerful but can inflate index size. The left-prefix rule for compound indexes applies just as in SQL. Covered queries (all needed fields in the index) avoid document fetches entirely. Index sparingly: each additional index adds write overhead. Use partial or sparse indexes to limit index size when only a subset of documents needs the index. ← Previous Nested data and arrays Next → Querying document fields

title: Querying document fields
text: Querying a document database is different from writing SQL — but the underlying ideas are the same. You specify which documents you want (filtering), which fields to return (projection), and how to sort or paginate the results. This page walks through the query model, shows how path-based expressions work, and compares document query syntax to its SQL equivalent. The Query Model at a Glance In a relational database you describe what you want in SQL. In a document database you typically pass a query document (itself a JSON object) that describes the shape of documents you want to match. // "Find all orders where status is 'shipped' and total > 100" db.orders.find({ status: "shipped", total: { $gt: 100 } }) The outer object { status: "shipped", total: { $gt: 100 } } is the filter . Every field in the filter must match the target document. Multiple conditions are implicitly AND -ed together. This parallels a SQL WHERE clause: SELECT * FROM orders WHERE status = 'shipped' AND total > 100; Field-Path Expressions Because documents are trees, not flat rows, document databases need a way to address nested fields. The standard notation is dot-separated paths : Path What it targets status Top-level field status address.city city inside the address object items.0.sku sku of the first element of items items.sku sku of any element in items (array match) The last case — items.sku without an index — is special. It returns the document if any element in the items array has a matching sku . This implicit array unrolling is unique to the document query model and has no direct SQL equivalent without json_each . The path "items.sku" automatically iterates the array. Any matching element causes the document to be included. Comparison and Logical Operators Document databases provide a rich set of query operators. Using MongoDB notation as a common reference: Comparison Operator Meaning Example $eq Equal (default when no operator given) { age: 25 } $ne Not equal { status: { $ne: "cancelled" } } $gt / $gte Greater than / greater than or equal { total: { $gte: 100 } } $lt / $lte Less than / less than or equal { price: { $lt: 50 } } $in Value in a list { status: { $in: ["pending", "processing"] } } $nin Value not in a list { category: { $nin: ["archived"] } } Logical // OR: documents where status is "shipped" OR total > 1000 db.orders.find({ $or: [ { status: "shipped" }, { total: { $gt: 1000 } } ] }) // AND (explicit form): db.orders.find({ $and: [ { status: "active" }, { total: { $gt: 0 } } ] }) // NOT: db.orders.find({ status: { $not: { $eq: "cancelled" } } }) Array Operators // $elemMatch: all conditions must match the SAME element db.orders.find({ items: { $elemMatch: { sku: "SHOE-9B", qty: { $gt: 1 } } } }) // Without $elemMatch: conditions can match DIFFERENT elements // (usually not what you want) db.orders.find({ "items.sku": "SHOE-9B", "items.qty": { $gt: 1 } }) Common gotcha: Without $elemMatch , { "items.sku": "X", "items.qty": { $gt: 1 } } matches a document where some element has sku = "X" and some element (possibly a different one) has qty > 1 . Always use $elemMatch when you need both conditions to apply to the same array element. Projection: Choosing Which Fields to Return By default, a find() returns the entire document. For large documents, that wastes bandwidth and memory. A projection specifies which fields to include or exclude: // Include only name and email (suppress _id) db.users.find({ status: "active" }, { name: 1, email: 1, _id: 0 }) // Exclude a specific field (useful when document is mostly what you want) db.users.find({}, { password_hash: 0 }) You cannot mix inclusions and exclusions in the same projection (except for _id ). Either list the fields you want or the fields you don't want. Sorting and Pagination // Sort by total descending, then by created_at ascending db.orders.find({ status: "shipped" }) .sort({ total: -1, created_at: 1 }) .skip(20) .limit(10) skip + limit implements offset-based pagination, which is simple but inefficient for large offsets (the engine still scans and discards skip documents). For high-performance pagination on large collections, use cursor-based pagination : remember the last _id (or sort field value) from the previous page and use $gt to start the next query from there. SQL Translation Reference Document Query SQL Equivalent find({ status: "active" }) WHERE status = 'active' find({}, { name: 1 }) SELECT name FROM … find({}).sort({ price: -1 }) ORDER BY price DESC find({}).limit(10).skip(20) LIMIT 10 OFFSET 20 find({ tags: "sale" }) WHERE 'sale' = ANY(tags) aggregate([{ $group: … }]) GROUP BY … Interactive Example Interactive SQL · Document-style field queries with json_extract Key Takeaways Document queries use a filter document (key-value pairs) rather than SQL syntax; the concepts — filtering, projection, sorting, limiting — are identical. Dotted paths navigate nested objects; array paths implicitly match any element. $elemMatch ensures multiple conditions apply to the same array element — a common source of bugs when omitted. Projection limits returned fields, reducing bandwidth and memory for large documents. For paginating large results, prefer cursor-based pagination ( $gt on the last seen _id ) over skip to avoid scanning and discarding rows. ← Previous Secondary indexes on documents Next → Document updates and rewrites

title: Document updates and rewrites
text: Updating documents sounds straightforward — change a field, save the document. But the mechanics matter a great deal at scale. Document databases offer two fundamentally different update strategies: whole-document replacement and field-level operators . Choosing between them — and understanding the concurrency hazards of each — is essential for building correct applications. The Two Update Strategies Whole-Document Replacement The simplest model: read the document, modify it in your application, write the entire new document back. Every document database supports this. // Fetch the document const user = await db.users.findOne({ _id: "usr-441" }); // Modify in application memory user.last_login = new Date(); user.login_count += 1; // Write the entire new document back await db.users.replaceOne({ _id: "usr-441" }, user); Problem: This is a read-modify-write cycle . Between the read and the write, another concurrent request could modify the same document. Your write overwrites those changes. This is the classic lost update problem. Field-Level Update Operators Most document databases provide atomic update operators that modify only specific fields without reading the document first: // Atomic: increment login_count and set last_login — no read needed await db.users.updateOne( { _id: "usr-441" }, { $set: { last_login: new Date() }, $inc: { login_count: 1 }, $push: { recent_ips: "203.0.113.5" } } ) This executes as a single atomic operation on the server. No other write can interleave. The document is read, modified, and written as an indivisible unit. Read-modify-write exposes a lost-update window. Atomic operators eliminate it. Common Update Operators Operator What it does Example $set Set one or more fields $set: { status: "active" } $unset Remove a field from the document $unset: { temp_field: "" } $inc Increment a numeric field by N $inc: { views: 1 } $mul Multiply a numeric field by N $mul: { price: 1.1 } $rename Rename a field $rename: { "old": "new" } $push Append to an array $push: { tags: "sale" } $pull Remove matching elements from array $pull: { tags: "draft" } $addToSet Append if not already present $addToSet: { tags: "new" } $pop Remove first or last array element $pop: { items: -1 } $setOnInsert Only set fields if this is an insert (upsert) Used with upsert operations Upserts An upsert (update-or-insert) is a single atomic operation: if a document matching the filter exists, update it; if not, create it. This pattern is extremely useful for idempotent writes. // Upsert: find by external ID; update if exists, insert if not await db.users.updateOne( { external_id: "oauth-google-12345" }, { $set: { last_seen: new Date() }, $setOnInsert: { created_at: new Date(), plan: "free" } }, { upsert: true } ) $set applies on both insert and update. $setOnInsert applies only when a new document is created. Whole-Document Replacement: When Is It Safe? Replacement is fine when: Only one process ever updates this document (no concurrent writers). You use optimistic concurrency control — store a version counter or timestamp, check it in the filter, and retry if the update finds zero matches (the document was concurrently modified). // Optimistic concurrency: include version in filter const result = await db.records.updateOne( { _id: "rec-001", version: 5 }, // match only if version is still 5 { $set: { data: newData, version: 6 } } // increment version on write ) if (result.matchedCount === 0) { throw new Error("Concurrent modification — retry"); } Partial Updates on Arrays: Positional Operators Updating a specific element inside an array requires positional update operators: // Update qty of the item where sku matches db.orders.updateOne( { _id: "ORD-9901", "items.sku": "SOCK-3P" }, { $set: { "items.$.qty": 5 } } // $ refers to the matched element ) // Update all items where qty < 1 (MongoDB 3.6+) db.orders.updateOne( { _id: "ORD-9901" }, { $set: { "items.$[elem].backordered": true } }, { arrayFilters: [ { "elem.qty": { $lt: 1 } } ] } ) Note: The $ positional operator only works when the array field is included in the query filter. If the array is not filtered, use $[] to update all elements. Interactive Example Interactive SQL · Simulating document update patterns The jsonb_set() function updates fields in place without replacing the full document. The version column increments with each write, enabling optimistic concurrency checks. Key Takeaways Read-modify-write cycles are dangerous under concurrency; use atomic update operators ( $set , $inc , $push , etc.) to avoid lost updates. Atomic operators execute on the server as a single indivisible step — no concurrent write can interleave. Upserts combine insert-or-update into one atomic operation, eliminating the race condition of "check then insert." For whole-document replacement, protect against concurrent modification with optimistic concurrency (store a version field and include it in the filter). Positional array operators ( $ , $[] , arrayFilters ) let you surgically update specific elements inside embedded arrays. ← Previous Querying document fields Next → Embedding vs referencing

title: Embedding vs referencing
text: The single most consequential design decision in a document database is whether to embed related data inside a parent document or reference it via an ID and store it in a separate collection. Get it right and queries are fast, data is cohesive, and writes are simple. Get it wrong and you face unbounded document growth, expensive multi-collection lookups, or stale denormalized copies. Two Strategies Side by Side Embedding (also called denormalization): the related data lives inside the parent document. { "_id": "order-9901", "customer_name": "Aroha Ngata", "customer_email": "aroha@example.com", "items": [ { "sku": "SHOE-9B", "name": "Trail Shoe", "qty": 1, "unit_price": 120 }, { "sku": "SOCK-3P", "name": "Sport Sock", "qty": 3, "unit_price": 8.50 } ] } Referencing (also called normalization): the related data lives in a separate collection, linked by ID. // orders collection { "_id": "order-9901", "customer_id": "cust-441", "item_ids": ["item-221", "item-222"] } // customers collection { "_id": "cust-441", "name": "Aroha Ngata", "email": "aroha@example.com" } // order_items collection { "_id": "item-221", "order_id": "order-9901", "sku": "SHOE-9B", "qty": 1, "unit_price": 120 } Embedding is faster to read; referencing avoids duplication and keeps updates simple. Decision Framework The right choice depends on five questions: 1. How often is the related data accessed with the parent? Always together → embed. Eliminates the second read. Sometimes separately → reference. The child has its own access pattern. 2. Does the related data change frequently? Rarely changes → embed. A product name embedded in 10,000 orders is fine if the name changes once a year. Changes often → reference. A price that changes daily would require updating every order containing it. 3. Is the relationship one-to-one, one-to-few, or one-to-many? One-to-one or one-to-few → embed. A user's address (1–3 items) fits cleanly inside the user document. One-to-many (unbounded) → reference. An order with thousands of comments should not embed all comments. 4. Is the related data shared across multiple parents? Shared → reference. A Product referenced by many OrderItem s exists once; embedding would duplicate it everywhere. Owned exclusively → embed. An Address owned by one User is a good candidate for embedding. 5. What are the document size limits? MongoDB enforces a 16 MB BSON limit per document. Embedding large, growing arrays can hit this. Reference instead. The Decision Matrix Condition Embed Reference Always read together ✓ Related data is bounded (< ~20 items) ✓ Related data rarely changes ✓ Exclusively owned by parent ✓ Data is shared across many parents ✓ Related data changes independently and often ✓ Array could grow unboundedly ✓ Child needs to be queried on its own ✓ Hybrid Patterns In practice, the best design is often a mix: Partial embed (summary only): Store a summary inside the parent and the full record elsewhere. // Order embeds a lightweight customer summary { "_id": "order-9901", "customer": { "id": "cust-441", "name": "Aroha Ngata" }, // ... } // Full customer data lives in the customers collection Extended reference pattern: Embed the fields you need for display; reference the full entity for editing. This avoids joins for the common read path while keeping a single source of truth for writes. Referencing in a Document Database: No Joins A critical difference from relational databases: document databases have no built-in JOIN . MongoDB's $lookup stage in aggregation pipelines provides server-side joins, but these are executed within the aggregation framework rather than through a deeply optimized relational join engine. $lookup runs on the server, not at the application level, but it does not benefit from the decades of join optimization built into relational engines — it is generally slower and more resource-intensive than an equivalent SQL JOIN on indexed foreign keys. db.orders.aggregate([ { $match: { status: "shipped" } }, { $lookup: { from: "customers", localField: "customer_id", foreignField: "_id", as: "customer" }} ]) This works, but it is slower than a relational join because document databases are not designed to optimize cross-collection lookups. If you find yourself writing many $lookup stages, the relational model may be a better fit. Interactive Example Interactive SQL · Embedding vs referencing tradeoff Key Takeaways Embed data that is owned by the parent, always loaded with it, bounded in size, and rarely changes independently. Reference data that is shared, unbounded, frequently updated, or queried on its own. Embedding speeds up reads (one document = one query) at the cost of duplication and update complexity. Referencing avoids duplication but requires multiple reads or an application-level join ( $lookup ). Hybrid patterns (embed a summary, reference the full record) often give the best of both worlds. Document databases are not optimized for joins — if your data model needs many cross-collection lookups, re-examine whether a relational database is a better fit. ← Previous Document updates and rewrites Next → Strengths and weaknesses

title: Strengths and weaknesses
text: Every database family makes trade-offs. Document databases exchange relational power for document-centric simplicity — and that exchange is genuinely good in some situations and genuinely harmful in others. This page maps the strengths and weaknesses clearly so you can make an honest assessment for your use case. Core Strengths 1. Natural Fit for Hierarchical, Self-Contained Entities When a real-world entity is naturally a tree — an order with line items, a blog post with comments, a user profile with preferences — a document is the most direct representation. There is no impedance mismatch between the object in your code and the record in the database. // A user profile maps directly to a programming language object { "_id": "usr-441", "name": "Aroha Ngata", "address": { "city": "Auckland", "country": "NZ" }, "preferences": { "theme": "dark", "language": "en" }, "roles": ["editor", "reviewer"] } Compare this to a relational representation that might span users , user_addresses , user_preferences , and user_roles tables — four joins just to reconstruct the object. 2. Flexible Schema for Evolving Data Adding a new field requires no ALTER TABLE , no downtime, and no migration script for existing documents. For a product catalog with 20 product types that have different attributes (a t-shirt has size and color ; a digital game has platform and download_size ), the flexible schema is a natural fit. 3. Horizontal Scalability Document databases were largely designed for sharding from the start. Because a document is the natural unit of access and data, it is also the natural unit of distribution. Sharding on the document _id (or a shard key) partitions data across nodes without the cross-partition join problem that plagues relational sharding. 4. High Write Throughput for Independent Documents Because each document is self-contained, concurrent writes to different documents never conflict. There are no foreign-key checks, no cascading updates, no join-induced lock contention. Systems that write many independent records — event logs, IoT sensor readings, clickstream data — can often achieve high throughput with document databases. 5. Developer Velocity For many teams, the reduced ceremony of working without migrations is a genuine productivity win during early development. Frameworks like Mongoose (Node.js + MongoDB) make the start-to-first-query time very short. Document databases excel at schema flexibility, write throughput, and developer velocity; they struggle with complex cross-document queries and multi-entity consistency. Core Weaknesses 1. No Efficient Multi-Document Transactions (Historically) For most of their history, document databases offered atomicity only at the single-document level. MongoDB added multi-document ACID transactions in version 4.0 (2018), but they come with significant performance overhead and are generally discouraged as a primary pattern. The root issue: the document model assumes each document is an independent unit . When your business logic requires atomically updating two documents together (e.g., debit one account, credit another), you need either transactions (slow) or a redesign that embeds both accounts in one document (not always possible). 2. No Joins → Denormalization → Data Inconsistency Risk Document databases do not natively join collections. Denormalization (embedding data that is also stored elsewhere) is the common workaround. But every embedded copy is a potential source of inconsistency. If a customer's email changes and it was embedded in 10,000 order documents, you must update all 10,000 — or live with stale copies. The data integrity guarantee: Relational databases enforce referential integrity at the database level ( FOREIGN KEY ). Document databases enforce nothing — if you delete a customer and forget to update the orders that reference it, you have orphaned references with no database error. 3. Inefficient for Cross-Document Queries "Find all orders placed by customers in Auckland" — in a relational database this is a two-table join with indexes. In a document database where customer data is referenced (not embedded), it requires loading all customer documents first or using $lookup (an application-level join without relational-engine optimizations). Cross-collection queries are expensive. 4. Schema Drift Over Time Schema flexibility is a feature in the short term and a maintenance burden in the long term. Without discipline, collections accumulate inconsistent document shapes that break queries, confuse new engineers, and make refactoring painful. The relational constraint system that felt bureaucratic turns out to have been doing important work. 5. Aggregations Require a Pipeline Complex aggregations (GROUP BY, windowed functions, multi-step transformations) require learning an aggregation pipeline API that is significantly more verbose than SQL: // MongoDB aggregation equivalent of: // SELECT customer_id, SUM(total) FROM orders GROUP BY customer_id HAVING SUM(total) > 500 db.orders.aggregate([ { $group: { _id: "$customer_id", total: { $sum: "$total" } } }, { $match: { total: { $gt: 500 } } } ]) SQL's expressive GROUP BY + HAVING is more readable for most developers. Analytics workloads generally don't fit the document model well. Honest Comparison Table Capability Document DB Relational DB Schema enforcement Optional Strong by default Adding new fields Zero downtime ALTER TABLE (may lock) Nested / hierarchical data Native Joins required Cross-entity queries Expensive / awkward First-class JOIN Multi-entity atomicity Limited (recent additions) First-class Referential integrity Not enforced FOREIGN KEY Analytics / reporting Poor fit Excellent fit Horizontal sharding Designed for it Requires extension Developer ramp-up Fast for simple models Longer for schema design Key Takeaways Document databases excel when your data is naturally hierarchical, your access patterns are document-centric (load the whole thing at once), and your schema is evolving rapidly. They struggle when you need cross-entity queries, multi-entity transactions, or strong referential integrity guarantees. The lack of joins is not a bug — it is a deliberate trade-off that enables horizontal scalability and document-centric performance. But it becomes a problem when your data model is actually relational. Schema flexibility is most valuable early in a project. Late in a project, the absence of enforcement is a maintenance liability. Multi-document transactions exist in modern document databases but are an escape hatch, not a design target. ← Previous Embedding vs referencing Next → Good use cases

title: Good use cases
text: Document databases are not universally better or worse than relational databases — they are optimized for a specific set of access patterns. This page walks through the use cases where document databases genuinely shine, explains why they fit, and calls out the warning signs that suggest a relational database would serve better. The Core Criterion: Document-Centric Access A document database is a strong fit whenever your application: Almost always loads an entity in its entirety (not individual fields from many rows). Stores entities that naturally have variable structure (not all instances look alike). Rarely needs to query across entities via arbitrary cross-entity joins. When all three hold, the document model eliminates joins, removes the mismatch between application objects and database rows, and enables schema evolution without migrations. Use Case 1: Content Management Systems A CMS stores articles, blog posts, landing pages, FAQs, and product descriptions. Each content type has a different set of fields: // Article: has author, tags, body, reading_time { "_id": "article-991", "type": "article", "title": "...", "author": {...}, "tags": ["tech"], "body": "...", "reading_time": 5 } // FAQ: has question, answer, category { "_id": "faq-042", "type": "faq", "question": "...", "answer": "...", "category": "billing" } // Landing page: has hero, sections array, cta { "_id": "page-home", "type": "page", "hero": {...}, "sections": [...], "cta": {...} } Forcing these into a relational schema requires either a wide table with many NULLs, or an entity-attribute-value anti-pattern. A document collection holds all types naturally. Use Case 2: User Profiles and Settings User profiles are read in full on almost every authenticated request and grow organically over time: { "_id": "usr-8821", "email": "priya@example.com", "plan": "pro", "preferences": { "theme": "dark", "notifications": { "email": true, "sms": false }, "locale": "en-IN" }, "integrations": [ { "provider": "google", "connected_at": "2024-01-10" } ], "feature_flags": { "beta_dashboard": true } } Every new feature adds a new sub-field under preferences or feature_flags . With a document store this requires no migration. With a relational table, every new preference column requires an ALTER TABLE or a separate preference table with a join. Use Case 3: Product Catalogs with Variable Attributes E-commerce product catalogs are a classic document database use case precisely because products are heterogeneous: Product Type Unique Attributes Clothing size , color , material , gender Electronics voltage , wattage , dimensions , warranty_years Digital download file_format , download_size_mb , platform Perishable food ingredients , allergens , expiry_days , refrigeration In a relational database you must choose: one giant table with hundreds of mostly-NULL columns, or a separate table per product type (hard to query together), or EAV (complex to query, no type safety). A document collection stores each product's actual attributes naturally. Use Case 4: Event Logs and Activity Feeds High-volume append-only data where each event is a self-contained record: { "_id": "evt-1001", "type": "user.login", "user_id": "usr-441", "ip": "203.0.113.5", "at": "2025-06-20T08:14:00Z" } { "_id": "evt-1002", "type": "order.placed", "user_id": "usr-441", "order_id": "ORD-9901", "total": 250, "at": "2025-06-20T08:15:30Z" } { "_id": "evt-1003", "type": "email.sent", "template": "order_confirm", "recipient": "aroha@x.com", "at": "2025-06-20T08:15:31Z" } Events have different shapes. They are written once and rarely updated. They are read by _id or by user_id + time range. These are excellent conditions for a document store. Use Case 5: Real-Time Applications and Session Data Mobile apps and games often need to load a complete user or game state in one round trip: { "_id": "session-9abc", "player_id": "usr-882", "level": 14, "position": { "x": 120, "y": 340 }, "inventory": ["sword-bronze", "potion-health", "key-iron"], "stats": { "hp": 87, "mp": 50, "xp": 14200 }, "quest_flags": { "castle_unlocked": true, "boss_defeated": false } } The entire game state is one document. Saving progress is one write. Loading the game is one read. This is exactly the document model's sweet spot. Use cases arranged by fit. The further right you go, the more you are fighting the document model. What Signals a Poor Fit You are fighting the document model when: You find yourself writing many $lookup stages (joining collections). You want a relational database. You need multi-document transactions frequently. You are modeling relational data as documents. Your documents reference dozens of other collections. The data is naturally relational. Your analytics team cannot query the data without exporting it first. The document model is hostile to ad-hoc aggregation. You need schema enforcement for compliance or data quality. A relational database with CHECK constraints and NOT NULL is more appropriate. The heuristic: If your entity relationships look like a graph (many cross-entity links, arbitrary depths), or if your analytics team writes complex GROUP BY queries, the relational or graph model is likely a better fit. Document databases are for document-shaped data with document-shaped access patterns . Interactive Example Interactive SQL · Heterogeneous product catalog (document-style) Notice that color is NULL for electronics and warranty_years is NULL for clothing — but there are no wasted columns in the actual storage. Each document carries only its own attributes. A relational table storing all product types would have many NULL columns per row. Key Takeaways Document databases are the right tool when: your entities are hierarchical, read as a whole, variable in structure, and not frequently joined with other entity types. The best use cases are: CMS content, user profiles, product catalogs with heterogeneous attributes, event logs, and game/session state. Warning signs that you need a different tool: many cross-collection joins, frequent multi-document transactions, complex aggregation pipelines, or regulatory requirements for schema enforcement. "Use a document database because it's flexible" is not a sufficient reason — the flexibility is a trade-off, not a free lunch. Match the model to the access pattern. ← Previous Strengths and weaknesses Next → Chapter 19 Summary: What We Learned

title: Chapter 19 Summary: What We Learned
text: Page Topic Takeaway in One Sentence 181 JSON as a data model Documents are self-describing JSON/BSON objects — the structure is part of the data, not declared separately in a schema. 182 Flexible schema Different documents in the same collection can have different fields — great for rapid iteration, dangerous for long-term consistency. 183 Nested data and arrays Embedding related data inside a single document avoids joins — load an order with all its line items in one read. 184 Secondary indexes on documents Document databases support indexes on nested fields and arrays, but index maintenance on deeply nested data is expensive. 185 Querying document fields Query languages (MQL, N1QL) let you filter on arbitrary fields, but multi-collection joins are limited compared to SQL. 186 Document updates and rewrites Partial updates to nested fields are possible but may trigger full document rewrites — large documents amplify this cost. 187 Embedding vs referencing Embed data you always read together; reference data shared across documents — the same normalization tradeoff in document form. 188 Strengths and weaknesses Strengths: developer velocity, flexible schema, natural fit for JSON APIs. Weaknesses: weak joins, eventual consistency, limited aggregation. 189 Good use cases CMS content, user profiles, product catalogs with variable attributes, event logs, and game/session state — entities that are self-contained, read as a whole, and variable in structure. Reveal: How do these ideas connect to the next chapter? Chapter 19 showed you document databases — flexible, JSON-native, and developer-friendly. But what if your workload is not about flexible documents but about ingesting massive streams of sparse, wide-row data? Chapter 20 introduces column-family databases like Cassandra and HBase. You will learn about wide-column storage, LSM trees, compaction, and why these systems are built for append-heavy write workloads like time-series, IoT, and event logging. The design philosophy shifts from "store what the document looks like" to "store for how you will query." ← Previous Good use cases Next → Wide rows and sparse data

title: Wide rows and sparse data
text: Column-family databases take a fundamentally different approach to organizing data than the relational model. Instead of storing data in rigid tables where every row has exactly the same columns, they allow wide rows — rows that can carry hundreds or even thousands of columns — and they are sparse , meaning a row only stores the columns it actually has. This page explores what that means in practice, why it matters for certain workloads, and how the data model maps onto systems like Apache Cassandra and Apache HBase. What Is a "Wide Row"? In a relational table, every row has exactly the same set of columns. If a column has no value for a given row, you store NULL. The schema is fixed: add a column and you touch every row. A wide row works differently. Think of a row as an ordered map of column name → value pairs. One row might have 3 columns; the next row in the same table might have 3,000 columns. The columns themselves can differ between rows. There is no NULL stored for missing entries — a column simply does not exist for that row. This is a crucial distinction: Concept Relational Column-Family Schema Fixed — same columns for every row Flexible — each row defines its own columns Missing data NULL stored (wastes space) Column absent (nothing stored) Row width Narrow, bounded by schema Wide, potentially thousands of columns Column definition DDL ( ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMN ) Implicit — just write to it Suitable for Structured, uniform entities Event streams, sensor data, timelines The Sparsity Advantage Imagine you are modeling user activity on a platform. A user can perform 500 different action types. In a relational model you might add 500 boolean or timestamp columns. Most users perform a handful of action types, so 98% of those columns would be NULL. In a column-family database, you write only the columns for the actions a user actually performed. A user who did five things has a row with five columns. A power user who did 400 things has a row with 400 columns. Storage is proportional to the actual data, not the schema footprint. Key insight: Sparsity is the default, not an exception. Column-family stores are built to store tens of millions of rows each with a different column set efficiently. NULLs are not "stored as NULL" — they are simply absent. The Hierarchy: Keyspace → Table → Partition → Row → Column Column-family systems organize data in a consistent hierarchy. Using Cassandra's terminology: Keyspace (≈ database / schema in SQL) └── Table (≈ table, but with a wide-row data model) └── Partition (a logical group of rows with the same partition key) └── Row (identified by the primary key within a partition) └── Column (a name-value pair, only present if data exists) This hierarchy determines both how data is stored on disk and how it is distributed across nodes. A partition is the unit of distribution — all rows in the same partition live on the same node(s). Left: a relational table stores NULLs for every missing value. Right: a column-family row stores only the columns that exist — absent columns take zero space. Column Families In HBase (and Cassandra's early design), columns are grouped into column families — a fixed set of column groups defined at table creation. Within a column family, individual column qualifiers can vary per row. Table: user_activity Column Family: "events" Row "user:1001" → events:login=2024-01-15, events:purchase=2024-01-20 Row "user:1002" → events:login=2024-01-16, events:search=2024-01-16 Column Family: "profile" Row "user:1001" → profile:name=Alice, profile:country=US Row "user:1002" → profile:name=Bob Column families matter physically: each family is stored in a separate file (HFile in HBase, SSTable in Cassandra). Reads that only need one column family don't touch the files of another, which can dramatically reduce I/O. In modern Cassandra (version 3+), the data model is closer to SQL in syntax — you define columns in a CREATE TABLE statement — but the storage model remains sparse and wide rows are still possible through collection types and user-defined types. Modeling Wide Rows: A Practical Example Consider a system that stores time-series IoT sensor readings. Different sensors report different metrics. In a relational model: -- Relational: every sensor gets NULLs for metrics it doesn't report CREATE TABLE sensor_readings ( sensor_id TEXT, ts TIMESTAMP, temperature FLOAT, -- NULL for sensors that don't report this humidity FLOAT, -- NULL for sensors that don't report this pressure FLOAT, -- NULL for sensors that don't report this voltage FLOAT, -- NULL for sensors that don't report this PRIMARY KEY (sensor_id, ts) ); In a column-family model using CQL (Cassandra Query Language): -- Cassandra: only store what you have CREATE TABLE sensor_readings ( sensor_id TEXT, ts TIMESTAMP, metric TEXT, value FLOAT, PRIMARY KEY (sensor_id, ts, metric) ); -- Each "row" in the partition is effectively a column in the wide-row model Or using a map type to embed the sparse columns directly: CREATE TABLE sensor_readings ( sensor_id TEXT, ts TIMESTAMP, readings MAP<TEXT, FLOAT>, -- sparse: only present keys stored PRIMARY KEY (sensor_id, ts) ); Interactive SQL · Sparse vs Dense Storage What Column-Family Systems Are Not It is easy to conflate "column-family" with "column-store" (OLAP). They are different things: A column-store (like Parquet, DuckDB, Redshift) stores data column-by-column on disk, enabling compression and vectorized reads across all rows for a given column. It is optimized for analytics. A column-family store (Cassandra, HBase) groups data by row and partition key. Within a partition, columns are stored together. It is optimized for low-latency reads and writes by key . The word "column" in each name refers to completely different ideas. Key Takeaways Wide rows allow a single row to have thousands of columns, with different rows having different columns. Sparsity means only present columns are stored — there are no NULLs consuming space. The hierarchy is: keyspace → table → partition → row → column. Column families group physically related columns into separate files, enabling selective I/O. Column-family databases are distinct from column-stores (OLAP engines) — the word "column" has different meanings in each context. Best fit: event streams, sensor data, time-series, user activity tracking — workloads where schema uniformity across rows is not a requirement. ← Previous Good use cases Next → Partitioning and clustering keys

title: Partitioning and clustering keys
text: When you store data in a Cassandra-style column-family system, every table has a primary key — but that primary key works very differently from a SQL primary key. It has two distinct parts: the partition key and the clustering key . Understanding these two halves is the single most important thing to grasp about Cassandra data modeling, because they determine where data lives, how it is sorted, and whether your queries will be fast or catastrophically slow. Two Jobs, One Primary Key In a relational database, a primary key uniquely identifies a row. In Cassandra (and systems modeled after it, like ScyllaDB and Amazon Keyspaces), the primary key serves two completely different roles simultaneously: Partition key — determines which node stores the data. The partition key is hashed using a consistent hashing function (like Murmur3), and the hash value maps to a position on a ring of nodes. All rows with the same partition key end up on the same node(s). Clustering key — determines the sort order of rows within a partition. Rows in the same partition are stored sorted by the clustering key — this makes range scans within a partition extremely efficient. CQL (Cassandra Query Language) syntax makes this explicit: CREATE TABLE messages ( conversation_id UUID, -- partition key sent_at TIMESTAMP, -- clustering key sender_id UUID, body TEXT, PRIMARY KEY (conversation_id, sent_at) ); Here conversation_id is the partition key and sent_at is the clustering key. All messages in a conversation live on the same node, sorted by time. A query like "give me all messages in conversation X from the last 7 days" is a single-partition read — extremely fast. The Filing Cabinet Analogy Think of a partitioned table as a filing cabinet in a distributed office: The partition key is like choosing which drawer to open. Each drawer is in a specific office (node). You always go to the right office, open the right drawer — O(1) lookup. The clustering key is the order of files inside that drawer. Files are sorted alphabetically (or by timestamp, etc.), so finding a range is a fast binary search. If you ask "give me all files about project X from March to June", you open the right drawer (partition key = project X) and scan the sorted files between March and June. One disk seek, sequential read — that is the Cassandra sweet spot. The partition key routes data to a node via consistent hashing. The clustering key determines row order inside the partition, enabling efficient range scans without a full table scan. Composite Partition Keys Sometimes a single column produces partitions that are too large (a "hot partition") or you need a more specific routing unit. You can group multiple columns into a composite partition key: CREATE TABLE sensor_data ( region TEXT, sensor_id TEXT, recorded_at TIMESTAMP, value FLOAT, PRIMARY KEY ((region, sensor_id), recorded_at) ); Note the double parentheses: (region, sensor_id) is the composite partition key; recorded_at is the clustering key. Both region AND sensor_id must be provided in queries that use the partition key. This is the equivalent of a compound hash: hash(region + sensor_id) → node. You can now have millions of sensor IDs globally without any one sensor overwhelming a single partition. Clustering Key Direction You can control sort order per clustering column: CREATE TABLE events ( user_id UUID, event_time TIMESTAMP, event_type TEXT, PRIMARY KEY (user_id, event_time, event_type) ) WITH CLUSTERING ORDER BY (event_time DESC, event_type ASC); This means queries for "latest events for a user" read from the start of the partition (most recent first) without reversing or sorting — the storage is already in the optimal order. CQL vs SQL: What Changes Feature SQL (relational) CQL (Cassandra) Primary key purpose Uniqueness constraint Routing + sort order WHERE clause Any indexed column Must include partition key Range scans Any column with index Only on clustering columns, in order JOINs Supported Not supported (by design) Secondary indexes Efficient for many patterns Expensive — use sparingly Sort order ORDER BY at query time Defined at table creation Warning: A query that omits the partition key forces Cassandra to scatter the query to every node and gather results — called a full cluster scan or "scatter-gather". This is extremely expensive at scale and is why data modeling must start from access patterns, not from the entity model. Choosing Keys: The Golden Rules Partition key should distribute data evenly. If one partition key value appears far more often than others, you create a hot spot — one node handles a disproportionate share of traffic. Partition key should enable your most common query. You must know the partition key to do a fast read. Clustering key should reflect your range query. If you always ask "give me events between time A and time B", the clustering key should be the timestamp. Partitions should be bounded in size. Cassandra recommends keeping partitions under ~100MB. A partition that grows without bound becomes a problem. Interactive SQL · Partitioning and Clustering Simulation Key Takeaways The partition key determines which node stores the data via consistent hashing. All rows sharing a partition key live together. The clustering key determines sort order within a partition, enabling efficient range scans. Together they form the primary key: PRIMARY KEY (partition_key, clustering_key) . Composite partition keys group multiple columns for finer-grained routing: PRIMARY KEY ((col1, col2), clustering_col) . Every query should include the partition key; omitting it forces a full cluster scan. Clustering direction ( DESC / ASC ) is defined at table creation and baked into storage layout. Data modeling in Cassandra is query-driven: choose your partition and clustering keys based on the queries you need to serve, not based on the entity model. ← Previous Wide rows and sparse data Next → Write-optimized design

title: Write-optimized design
text: Column-family databases like Apache Cassandra are not just "NoSQL with a different API" — they are engineered from the ground up around one constraint: writes must be as fast as possible, even at petabyte scale . This shapes every design decision, from the on-disk format to the way updates and deletes work. This page explains the write-optimized architecture: why in-place updates are slow, what the append-only model looks like, and how data flows from a client write all the way to durable storage. Why In-Place Updates Are Slow In a traditional B-tree-based database (PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQLite), an update works roughly like this: Find the page on disk that contains the row. Lock the page. Modify the byte range in-place. Write the change to the WAL. Mark the page dirty in the buffer pool. Step 1 is the bottleneck at scale. On a spinning disk (HDD) , finding a page requires a seek — physically moving the read head to the right track. A typical HDD seek takes 5–10 milliseconds. At 200 updates/second per spinning disk, you're already near the physical limit. Even with SSDs (where there is no moving head), random writes cause write amplification : the SSD's flash translation layer must erase and rewrite entire flash blocks (128KB–1MB) to change a few bytes, wearing out the device faster and reducing throughput. The fundamental problem: in-place updates require random I/O , and random I/O is expensive whether you're on HDD or SSD. The Append-Only Alternative Column-family systems sidestep random I/O by making writes always sequential and append-only : A new value never overwrites the old one. Instead, the new value is appended as a newer version with a higher timestamp. Old versions are cleaned up later, in the background, during compaction . This means writes are fast because sequential I/O to an append-only log saturates disk bandwidth, rather than crawling through random seeks. A single SSD can sustain hundreds of thousands of sequential writes per second — orders of magnitude more than random writes. Key insight: "Update" in Cassandra is not a modification — it is an insert of a new version . The system resolves which version is current at read time (or during compaction) using timestamps. The Write Path: Commit Log + Memtable Every write in Cassandra follows a two-step path before it is acknowledged to the client: Client │ ├──► Commit Log (WAL) ── sequential append to disk ── durable immediately │ └──► Memtable ── in-memory sorted data structure ── fast, not yet on disk Commit log (Write-Ahead Log): The write is appended to an on-disk sequential log file. This is what makes the write durable — if the node crashes before the memtable is flushed, the commit log can replay all writes on restart. Memtable: The write is also inserted into an in-memory sorted structure (a memtable — think of it as an in-memory SSTable). Reads can be served from the memtable before data ever hits a permanent SSTable file. Once the write is safely in both the commit log and the memtable, Cassandra acknowledges success to the client. The entire operation involves zero random disk I/O — the commit log is sequential, and the memtable is in RAM. The Cassandra write path: every write hits the commit log (sequential, durable) and the in-memory memtable simultaneously. When the memtable is full it flushes to an immutable SSTable. Compaction periodically merges SSTables, removing stale versions. The Memtable Flush Cycle The memtable is bounded in size (configurable, typically 64MB–256MB per table). When it fills: The memtable is frozen — no more writes go to it. A new, empty memtable takes over immediately. Writes continue without interruption. The frozen memtable is flushed to disk as an immutable SSTable (Sorted String Table) file. Once the SSTable is fully written, the corresponding portion of the commit log can be discarded (since the data is now safely on disk). This cycle repeats continuously. The result: a steady stream of sequential disk writes, and an accumulation of SSTable files. What "No In-Place Update" Means for Reads and Deletes The append-only model has some non-obvious consequences: Updates are new versions. If you write (user_id=42, name='Alice') and then write (user_id=42, name='Alice Smith') , both versions exist somewhere — possibly in different SSTables or the memtable. A read for user_id=42 must check the memtable and potentially multiple SSTables, then take the most recent version by timestamp. This is why reads in Cassandra can be slower than writes — they may require merging multiple sources. Deletes are "tombstones." You cannot erase a byte in an immutable SSTable. Instead, a delete writes a special marker called a tombstone — a record saying "this key was deleted at time T". On a read, if a tombstone is found that is newer than any actual value, the key is treated as deleted. Tombstones are physically removed only when compaction runs. Compaction resolves versions. Compaction is the background process that reads multiple SSTables, merges them, keeps only the most recent version of each cell, discards tombstoned rows, and writes a new, clean SSTable. After compaction, reads are faster because fewer files need to be consulted. Comparing B-Tree vs LSM-Tree Write Paths Characteristic B-Tree (PostgreSQL, MySQL) LSM-Tree (Cassandra, HBase) Write mechanism In-place update in page Append to commit log + memtable Disk I/O type Random (seek to page) Sequential (append-only) Write durability WAL + in-place Commit log Update cost Seek + lock + modify Always O(1) append Read cost Single B-tree traversal Merge memtable + N SSTables Delete Mark as deleted (vacuum later) Write tombstone Background work Vacuum / autovacuum Compaction Write amplification Moderate (WAL + heap) High (compaction rewrites data) Read amplification Low (one path through tree) Higher (multiple SSTables) The fundamental trade-off: LSM-trees optimize write throughput by making reads slightly more expensive. B-trees optimize read performance by making writes involve random I/O. Choose based on your workload's write-to-read ratio. Why This Matters for Time-Series and Event Workloads Column-family databases excel at workloads where: Data is append-only by nature (events, logs, sensor readings, audit trails). Rows are never updated — only new readings are inserted. Write rate vastly exceeds read rate . Reads are always by partition key (e.g., "give me all events for device X in the last hour"). For these patterns, the write-optimized design is not a compromise — it is the ideal architecture. A single Cassandra node can sustain tens of thousands of writes per second. A cluster of dozens of nodes can sustain millions. Interactive SQL · Append-Only Write Pattern Key Takeaways In-place updates are slow because they require random I/O — seeking to the right page on disk before modifying it. Cassandra's write path is always sequential : commit log (WAL) + in-memory memtable. No random seeks on write. Every write is an append, never an overwrite . Updates create new versions with higher timestamps. Deletes write tombstones — logical markers, not physical erasure. The memtable flushes to an immutable SSTable when full; background compaction merges SSTables and removes stale versions. This design makes writes extremely fast at the cost of slightly more complex reads. Best fit: high-volume append workloads — events, IoT sensors, time-series, logs — where write throughput is the primary constraint. ← Previous Partitioning and clustering keys Next → LSM trees

title: LSM trees
text: The Log-Structured Merge-Tree (LSM-tree) is one of the most influential data structures in modern database engineering. It powers Cassandra, HBase, RocksDB, LevelDB, and indirectly every system built on top of them. The central insight: sequential writes to disk are orders of magnitude faster than random writes, so instead of modifying data in place, the LSM-tree converts all writes into sequential appends — and pays the cost of organization later, in the background. The Core Lifecycle of a Write When you write a key-value pair to an LSM-tree-backed database, five things happen in order: WAL append — the write is appended to the Write-Ahead Log (WAL), a sequential file on disk. This is the durability guarantee: if the process crashes, the WAL replays the write on restart. Memtable insert — the write is applied to the memtable , an in-memory data structure (often a red-black tree or skip list) that keeps all recent writes sorted by key. Memtable fills up — once the memtable reaches its size threshold (typically 64–256 MB), it is made immutable and a fresh, empty memtable takes its place. SSTable flush — the immutable memtable is flushed to disk as an SSTable (Sorted String Table) — a file where entries are written in key order and never modified again. Accumulation — SSTables pile up on disk. New flushes produce new SSTables at Level 0 (L0). Background compaction periodically merges them. Write("sensor:42:temp", 71.3) │ ├─ Append to WAL ──────────────────────── durability └─ Insert into Memtable (sorted, in RAM)── fast path │ │ (when full) ▼ SSTable (immutable, sorted on disk) Why "sorted"? Keeping data sorted by key is what makes compaction and range scans efficient. The memtable is a sorted structure in RAM; SSTables preserve that order on disk. SSTables: The On-Disk Format Each SSTable is a file containing: Section Contents Data blocks Key-value pairs, sorted by key Index block Sparse index: one entry per data block, storing the first key Bloom filter A probabilistic structure for fast key-existence checks Metadata Min key, max key, entry count, compression info SSTables are immutable : once written, they are never modified. Updates write a new entry with the same key at a higher sequence number. Deletes write a tombstone — a special marker that says "this key was deleted." The old value is reclaimed later, during compaction. Reading Data: The Multi-Level Check Reads are more expensive than writes in an LSM-tree. A read must check: Memtable — most recent data is here; O(log n) lookup in the sorted tree. Immutable memtables — any recently flushed but not yet fully compacted tables.
