L0 SSTables — can overlap in key range; all must be checked. L1, L2, … SSTables — at each level below L0, files are non-overlapping, so at most one file needs to be checked per level. In the worst case a read touches every level. Without mitigation, this gets slow as data accumulates. Bloom Filters to the Rescue A bloom filter is a probabilistic data structure attached to each SSTable. It answers the question: "Is this key in this file?" If it says "No" → the key is definitely not in this SSTable. Skip it. If it says "Yes" → the key is probably in this SSTable (may be a false positive). Check the file. A well-tuned bloom filter (1–10 bits per key) eliminates the vast majority of unnecessary SSTable reads. A read for a non-existent key barely touches disk at all. Read("sensor:99:temp") │ ├─ Check memtable → not found ├─ Check L0 SSTable 1 bloom filter → "No" → skip ├─ Check L0 SSTable 2 bloom filter → "Yes" → check file → not found (false positive) ├─ Check L1 SSTable bloom filter → "No" → skip └─ Check L2 SSTable bloom filter → "Yes" → check file → FOUND LSM-tree layer structure. Writes flow through WAL → Memtable → L0 SSTables. Compaction progressively merges data down through levels. Reads must check every level, but bloom filters skip most files. Read vs. Write Cost Comparison Metric LSM-tree B-tree Write Sequential append (fast) Random in-place update (slower) Read (hot key) Memtable hit → very fast Single B-tree traversal → fast Read (cold key) Must check multiple levels Single B-tree traversal Write amplification High (data written multiple times during compaction) Lower Read amplification Higher (multiple files) Lower Space amplification Higher (old versions accumulate until compaction) Lower The LSM-tree wins at write throughput — especially when writes are spread randomly across the key space. This is why it's the default for time-series workloads, event ingestion, and Cassandra's write-heavy use cases. Key Concepts in Practice Sequence Numbers Every write carries a monotonically increasing sequence number . When the same key appears in both L0 and L2, the version with the higher sequence number wins. This is how LSM-trees handle updates without ever overwriting old data. Write-Ahead Log and Crash Recovery The WAL is written before the memtable is updated. On crash: the database replays the WAL to reconstruct the memtable. Once the memtable is safely flushed to an SSTable, those WAL segments can be discarded. Memory Pressure Most production deployments set a memtable size limit (e.g., 128 MB in Cassandra). When it fills, the write is blocked briefly while the flush to disk completes. Tuning this limit is one of the most impactful knobs in write-heavy systems. Interactive SQL · Reading Across LSM Levels Key Takeaways LSM-trees convert all writes into sequential disk appends , making them dramatically faster than random in-place writes. Data flows through a pipeline: WAL → Memtable → L0 SSTables → L1 → L2 , with each level holding progressively older and larger chunks of sorted data. SSTables are immutable — updates and deletes create new entries with higher sequence numbers; old data is cleaned up by compaction. Bloom filters make reads efficient by quickly ruling out SSTables that don't contain the target key — turning O(N files) reads into O(levels) reads for existing keys. LSM-trees trade read amplification and write amplification for exceptional write throughput — the right trade-off for write-heavy workloads like time-series ingestion, event logging, and column-family databases. Understanding LSM-trees is essential for tuning Cassandra, RocksDB, and any system built on them: memtable size, bloom filter bits-per-key, and compaction strategy are the primary levers. ← Previous Write-optimized design Next → Compaction

title: Compaction
text: Without compaction, an LSM-tree-based database would eventually collapse under its own weight. SSTables would pile up indefinitely on disk, reads would slow to a crawl as they checked more and more files, and space would fill up with stale values and phantom deletes. Compaction is the background process that keeps all of this in check: it merges SSTables, eliminates superseded versions, and physically removes deleted records. Why Compaction Exists Consider what happens after a few hours of writes in a write-heavy system: Dozens or hundreds of L0 SSTables accumulate, all with overlapping key ranges. Every UPDATE creates a new entry — the old value still occupies disk space. Every DELETE writes a tombstone marker — the underlying data still exists. Bloom filters help, but they cannot eliminate the overhead of checking 50 files where 5 would do. Compaction is the answer: it reads a set of SSTables, merges their contents using a merge-sort algorithm, writes a new set of compacted SSTables, and discards the originals. Before compaction (L0): SST-1: [a:v1, c:v3, e:TOMBSTONE] SST-2: [a:v2, b:v1, c:v4] SST-3: [b:TOMBSTONE, d:v1] After compaction (L1): SST-A: [a:v2, b:TOMBSTONE, c:v4, d:v1] (e was tombstoned and had no older version → removed entirely) (b tombstone stays until lower levels are checked) Note: Tombstones are not immediately removed by every compaction pass. A tombstone can only be dropped when compaction is certain no lower level still holds the old value. Until then, the tombstone must be preserved — otherwise deleted data would "resurface." Size-Tiered Compaction (STCS) Size-tiered compaction is the simpler of the two dominant strategies and is the default in Apache Cassandra for write-heavy tables. How It Works SSTables are grouped into size tiers (e.g., files of similar size). When enough files accumulate in a tier (typically 4), they are merged into a single larger file. That larger file joins the next tier, and the process repeats. Tier File size (approx.) Trigger Tier 1 ~50 MB 4 files → merge into 1 Tier 2 ~200 MB 4 files → merge into 1 Tier 3 ~800 MB 4 files → merge into 1 Tier 4 ~3.2 GB 4 files → merge into 1 Trade-offs Strengths: Minimal write amplification — each byte is rewritten fewer times. Excellent write throughput; SSTables are merged infrequently. Weaknesses: Files within a tier can overlap in key range, so reads may still check multiple files. Temporarily requires up to 2× disk space (old files + new merged file coexist). Tombstones may persist for a long time if the triggering merge is delayed. Leveled Compaction (LCS) Leveled compaction takes the opposite philosophy: prioritize read performance by ensuring that within each level (L1 and below), SSTables have non-overlapping key ranges . This is the strategy used in LevelDB, RocksDB, and optionally in Cassandra. How It Works L0 SSTables are merged with L1 SSTables. The result is sorted and split into fixed-size files (e.g., 10 MB each) with non-overlapping ranges. When L1 grows beyond its size limit, files are promoted into L2 — merged with existing L2 files, non-overlapping maintained. Each level is roughly 10× larger than the one above. L0: [a-z, a-z, a-z] ← overlapping (just flushed) L1: [a-d][e-h][i-m][n-z] ← non-overlapping, 10 MB each L2: [a-b][c-d][e-f]...[y-z] ← non-overlapping, 10 MB each, ~10× more files Trade-offs Strengths: At most one file per level contains the target key → much faster reads. Tombstones are removed promptly because all data for a key range passes through compaction regularly. Space amplification is lower (~10% overhead vs 50–100% for STCS). Weaknesses: Higher write amplification — the same data is rewritten many times as it migrates through levels. Higher I/O pressure on write-heavy workloads. Before compaction, three overlapping SSTables hold stale values and tombstones. After compaction, one clean file contains only the latest version of each surviving key. Tombstones: Deletes as Writes In an LSM-tree, you cannot delete a record by erasing it — the SSTable is immutable. Instead, a delete writes a tombstone : a special marker that says "this key was deleted at sequence N." -- Application issues: DELETE FROM sensor_data WHERE id = 42; -- LSM-tree writes: [key=42, value=TOMBSTONE, seq=9901] -- Old data in SST: [key=42, value=71.3, seq=8801] ← still on disk -- After compaction: key=42 disappears entirely Tombstones carry a risk: if compaction is delayed and tombstones accumulate, tombstone-heavy reads become very slow — the database must wade through many tombstones to find the live data. In Cassandra this is known as "tombstone overload" and can trigger TombstoneOverwhelmingException . Operational rule: Monitor tombstone counts in Cassandra. If SELECT queries produce warnings about tombstones, your data model likely relies on too many deletes. Consider using TTL instead — TTL expiry is handled more efficiently than explicit tombstones. The Compaction I/O Tax Compaction is not free. While it runs, it: Reads all source SSTables from disk. Performs an in-memory merge sort. Writes the result back to disk. Deletes the source SSTables. This means compaction competes with your application's I/O . Under heavy write load, compaction may fall behind — L0 accumulates files faster than compaction can drain it. This is called compaction debt and results in degraded read performance. Tuning levers: compaction_throughput_mb_per_sec (Cassandra) — throttle compaction to limit I/O impact. Number of compaction threads — more parallelism helps; too many starve foreground I/O. min_sstable_size and sstable_size_in_mb — control trigger thresholds. STCS vs. LCS: Choosing a Strategy Criterion Size-Tiered (STCS) Leveled (LCS) Best for Write-heavy, time-series, append-only Read-heavy, low-latency lookups Read performance Worse (overlapping files) Better (one file per level) Write amplification Lower Higher Space overhead Up to 2× during compaction ~10% steady-state Tombstone cleanup Slower Faster Default in Cassandra (most tables) RocksDB, LevelDB Interactive SQL · Simulating SSTable Merge and Tombstone Removal Key Takeaways Compaction is what keeps LSM-trees healthy: without it, reads degrade, disk fills up with stale data, and tombstones accumulate. Size-tiered compaction (STCS) optimizes for write throughput at the cost of read performance — ideal for append-heavy workloads. Leveled compaction (LCS) optimizes for read performance and space efficiency at the cost of write amplification — ideal for read-heavy workloads. Tombstones are how deletes work in LSM-trees: the old data persists until compaction physically removes it. Excessive tombstones are a serious operational hazard. Compaction consumes real I/O; running behind on compaction is called compaction debt and can severely impact read latency. Choosing and tuning the right compaction strategy is one of the most impactful operational decisions when running Cassandra, RocksDB, or any LSM-backed database. ← Previous LSM trees Next → Time-series and event workloads

title: Time-series and event workloads
text: Column-family databases were not designed in a vacuum — they were designed to solve a specific category of problems that relational databases handle poorly at scale: high-volume, time-ordered data attached to a known entity . IoT sensor readings, application metrics, user activity streams, financial tick data, server logs — these workloads all share the same shape: a flood of writes keyed by an entity identifier plus a timestamp, later queried as a time range for one entity. Column-family databases turn this shape into a first-class data model. Why the Workload Matters Consider a fleet of 100,000 IoT sensors, each reporting temperature every 10 seconds. That is 10,000 writes per second and 864 million rows per day . The queries are almost always the same pattern: "give me all readings for sensor X between time A and time B." In a relational database, you would have: CREATE TABLE sensor_readings ( sensor_id INT, ts TIMESTAMP, value FLOAT, PRIMARY KEY (sensor_id, ts) ); This works at small scale. Under heavy write load, however, the B-tree index becomes a bottleneck: random writes to a hot index page cause contention and page splits. Queries across millions of rows for one sensor require expensive index traversals that scatter across disk. A column-family database — Cassandra being the canonical example — stores the data differently at the physical level: all readings for a single sensor live contiguously on disk , sorted by timestamp. The entire time-range query for one sensor becomes a single sequential scan of one partition. Partition Key + Clustering Key = Time-Series Superpower The Cassandra data model separates two key concepts: Concept Role Example Partition key Determines which node holds the data; groups rows physically together sensor_id Clustering key Determines the sort order within a partition ts (timestamp) -- Cassandra CQL CREATE TABLE sensor_readings ( sensor_id TEXT, ts TIMESTAMP, value FLOAT, unit TEXT, PRIMARY KEY (sensor_id, ts) ) WITH CLUSTERING ORDER BY (ts DESC); With this model: All readings for sensor:42 land in a single partition. Within that partition, rows are sorted by ts descending — the newest reading is always first. A query like WHERE sensor_id = 'sensor:42' AND ts > '2024-01-01' is served by a single partition scan — no cross-node coordination needed. Each sensor's readings live in a single partition, sorted by timestamp. A time-range query is a sequential scan — no index lookups needed, no cross-partition coordination. TTL: Auto-Expiring Old Events Column-family databases (and Cassandra specifically) support per-row or per-column TTL (Time-To-Live). When a row's TTL expires, the database automatically writes a tombstone and the data is removed on the next compaction. -- Insert with a 30-day TTL INSERT INTO sensor_readings (sensor_id, ts, value, unit) VALUES ('sensor:42', toTimestamp(now()), 71.3, 'celsius') USING TTL 2592000; -- 30 days in seconds TTL is invaluable for event data where you only need the last N days of readings. Without TTL, you'd need a background cleanup job that issues millions of DELETE statements — which writes an equal number of tombstones, causing the very problem you're trying to solve. Tip: Use TTL instead of explicit deletes for time-series cleanup. TTL expiry is handled more efficiently during compaction than user-issued tombstones, and it avoids the tombstone accumulation problem. The "Wide Row" Pattern The column-family data model stores all clustering key values for a partition as a "wide row" — conceptually, one row in the partition map that fans out into many columns. This is why the original name was "column-family" — in the physical layout, it is not a row-per-reading but a wide, sparse row per entity, with each timestamp becoming a column key. sensor:42 → { "2024-06-25 10:00:00": {"value": 71.3, "unit": "celsius"}, "2024-06-25 09:59:50": {"value": 71.1, "unit": "celsius"}, "2024-06-25 09:59:40": {"value": 70.9, "unit": "celsius"}, ... } This layout is physically co-located on disk — all values for sensor:42 are stored together, enabling the sequential scan that makes range queries fast. The Bucket Anti-Pattern and Partition Size Limits Cassandra has a practical limit: a single partition should not exceed a few hundred megabytes or tens of millions of rows . If a partition grows without bound, it becomes a hot spot on one node and reads slow down as the partition can no longer fit in the SSTable block cache. For time-series data, a naive partition key like sensor_id will grow indefinitely. The solution is time bucketing : add a time bucket to the partition key. -- Bad: unbounded partition PRIMARY KEY (sensor_id, ts) -- Better: bucket by day (each partition holds one day's readings) PRIMARY KEY ((sensor_id, bucket), ts) -- bucket = date_trunc('day', ts) → e.g., '2024-06-25' Trade-off of bucketing: Too small a bucket (e.g., per-minute) → many tiny partitions, range queries require scanning many partitions (multi-partition queries are expensive and unordered). Too large a bucket (e.g., per-year) → partitions grow too large, hot spots. For sensors writing every 10 seconds, a daily bucket gives ~8,640 rows per partition — a comfortable size. Comparing Relational vs. Column-Family for Time-Series Aspect Relational DB Column-Family (Cassandra) Write path Random B-tree update + index Sequential LSM append 10k writes/sec Requires careful sharding + tuning Handled natively Range query (one entity) Index range scan (random I/O) Sequential partition scan Range query (all entities) Full table scan or index Must query each partition separately TTL / auto-expiry Requires cron job Native per-row TTL Schema changes ALTER TABLE — may lock Add column (natively flexible) Aggregations (AVG, SUM) Strong, fast with index Limited — push to app or use materialized views The relational model struggles with writes at IoT scale and range queries that must aggregate across all entities — for example, "what was the average temperature across all sensors in the last hour?" That cross-partition query in Cassandra requires a full cluster scan; a relational database can answer it with a single indexed aggregate. Interactive SQL · Time-Series Range Queries User Activity and Event Logs The same model extends naturally to user activity: CREATE TABLE user_events ( user_id TEXT, occurred TIMESTAMP, event_type TEXT, payload TEXT, PRIMARY KEY (user_id, occurred) ) WITH CLUSTERING ORDER BY (occurred DESC) AND default_time_to_live = 7776000; -- 90 days TTL Queries like "show me the last 50 events for user U" are trivially fast — one partition, a limit on the scan. "Show me all users who clicked button X in the last hour" is not — that requires querying every user's partition or building a secondary index. Design principle: Model your data around your queries, not your entities. If your primary access pattern is "all events for user U sorted by time," the partition key is user_id and the clustering key is occurred . If you also need "all events of type X across all users," you need a separate table (or materialized view) with event_type as the partition key. Key Takeaways Column-family databases excel at high-volume, time-ordered data because the partition key groups all data for one entity together, and the clustering key sorts it by time — enabling fast sequential reads. A query for one entity's events over a time range is a single partition scan — no cross-node joins, no index lookups. TTL makes automatic event expiry a first-class feature: no cleanup jobs, no tombstone storms from mass deletes. The wide row pattern co-locates all timestamped events for an entity on disk; this is the source of the "column-family" name. Bucketing the partition key (e.g., (sensor_id, day) ) prevents partitions from growing unboundedly — the key operational safeguard for time-series tables. Column-family databases are weak at cross-entity aggregations ("average across all sensors") — for that, pair them with a stream processor (Flink, Spark) or a purpose-built time-series database (InfluxDB, TimescaleDB). ← Previous Compaction Next → Modeling for query patterns

title: Modeling for query patterns
text: In relational databases, you design a normalized schema that accurately models your domain, then write whatever queries you need against it. In column-family databases — particularly Cassandra and its derivatives — you must reverse that process: start from the queries you need to serve, then design tables around those queries . This is one of the most important conceptual shifts when moving to the column-family world, and getting it wrong causes severe performance problems at scale. The Golden Rule: One Table Per Query Pattern Cassandra's architecture explains why this rule exists. Data is physically partitioned across a cluster by the partition key . A query that touches only one partition hits one node and returns in single-digit milliseconds. A query that touches many partitions must scatter across the cluster, gather results, and merge them — an expensive operation that Cassandra tries to discourage by requiring ALLOW FILTERING for any query that cannot be routed by the partition key alone. The practical consequence: if you have two different access patterns, you almost always need two different tables — even if both tables hold the same logical data. Entity-first design uses one table for all queries; query-first design uses one table per access pattern. The right side stores duplicate data but every read is a single-partition lookup. Why ALLOW FILTERING Is a Red Flag In Cassandra Query Language (CQL), adding ALLOW FILTERING to a query tells the coordinator: "I know this is expensive — scan every partition anyway." This is roughly equivalent to a full table scan in SQL, except it is distributed: the coordinator must contact every node, pull all data, and filter in memory. -- This query is FAST in Cassandra: partition key in WHERE SELECT * FROM users_by_id WHERE user_id = 'u-4291'; -- This query requires ALLOW FILTERING — touches every partition SELECT * FROM users_by_id WHERE country = 'DE' ALLOW FILTERING; -- Equivalent to: SELECT * FROM users WHERE country = 'DE' (no index, full scan) Rule of thumb: If you find yourself writing ALLOW FILTERING in production code, you are missing a table. The solution is not to optimize the query — it is to create a dedicated table for that access pattern. The "One Table Per Query" Pattern The canonical Cassandra data modeling methodology proceeds in four steps: List your queries — Write down every access pattern your application needs. Be specific: "Find user by user_id", "Find user by email", "List all orders for a user, newest first". Design a table for each query — The partition key answers "which node?"; the clustering key answers "in what order within the partition?". Accept the duplication — The same logical entity may live in 3–5 tables. That is by design, not a mistake. Keep tables in sync at write time — When a user's email changes, you update both users_by_id and users_by_email . Cassandra's lightweight transactions (LWT) or application-level logic handles this. Partition Key Selection The partition key is the most important design decision in a column-family schema. A good partition key: Distributes writes evenly — All user IDs, for example, spread naturally across nodes via consistent hashing. A partition key of country would send all German users to a handful of nodes (a hot partition). Matches the query exactly — The query must supply the full partition key in its WHERE clause. If your query is "find by email", the partition key must be email . Avoids unbounded growth — A partition storing all events ever emitted for an account may eventually exceed the 2 GB recommended maximum. Composite partition keys like (user_id, year_month) bucket data by time to bound partition size. Partition Key Choice Cardinality Risk user_id (UUID) Very high Excellent distribution country ~200 values Hot partition — avoid status ( active / inactive ) 2 values Extreme skew — never use alone (user_id, year_month) High Good; bounds partition size Practical Example: User Profile Tables Imagine an application with two lookup requirements: "Get user profile by user_id" — used on every authenticated API request "Get user profile by email" — used during login In SQL, one table with two indexes handles both easily. In Cassandra, you create two tables: -- Table 1: primary lookup by user_id CREATE TABLE users_by_id ( user_id UUID, email TEXT, full_name TEXT, created_at TIMESTAMP, PRIMARY KEY (user_id) ); -- Table 2: lookup by email (for login) CREATE TABLE users_by_email ( email TEXT, user_id UUID, full_name TEXT, created_at TIMESTAMP, PRIMARY KEY (email) ); Both tables contain the same data, duplicated. A write must insert (or update) both. A read hits exactly one table and one partition — guaranteed single-node resolution. Clustering Keys for Ordered Data Within a partition, rows are physically ordered by the clustering key . This unlocks efficient range scans inside a partition: -- Orders for a user, most recent first CREATE TABLE orders_by_user ( user_id UUID, created_at TIMESTAMP, order_id UUID, total DECIMAL, PRIMARY KEY (user_id, created_at, order_id) ) WITH CLUSTERING ORDER BY (created_at DESC); -- Query fetches the partition for user_id, then slices by time range — fast SELECT * FROM orders_by_user WHERE user_id = 'u-4291' AND created_at >= '2024-01-01' AND created_at < '2024-02-01'; The clustering key range scan ( created_at >= ... AND created_at < ... ) reads a contiguous slice of sorted rows within a single partition — as efficient as a B-tree range scan in a relational database. The Trade-off in Plain Terms Dimension Relational (SQL) Column-Family (Cassandra) Schema design Model entities Model access patterns Ad-hoc queries Supported naturally Expensive or impossible Data duplication Minimized (normalization) Accepted (intentional) Write complexity One row per entity One row per table per entity Read latency Depends on indexes/joins Predictably low (single partition) Query flexibility High Low — must plan ahead The column-family approach makes reads blindingly fast and perfectly predictable at the cost of write complexity and data duplication. You are essentially materializing views at write time — doing the join work upfront, once, rather than at read time, repeatedly. Interactive SQL · Two Tables for Two Access Patterns Key Takeaways Model for queries, not for entities. In column-family databases, the query is the schema. Design tables around the access patterns your application will actually execute. ALLOW FILTERING is a full-cluster scan. Avoid it in production; its presence signals a missing table. One table per query pattern is not a smell — it is the correct approach. Duplication is the price of predictable latency. Partition key determines routing. Every query must supply the full partition key in WHERE ; everything else risks a full scan. Clustering keys provide ordering within a partition. Use them for time-range or rank-order access within a user's or device's data. Write fan-out is the cost. A single logical write may touch multiple tables. Keep tables in sync at write time; the reward is single-partition reads at query time. ← Previous Time-series and event workloads Next → What is hard in these systems

title: What is hard in these systems
text: Column-family databases like Cassandra were designed to solve specific problems — massive write throughput, linear horizontal scalability, geographic distribution — and they solve those problems well. But every architectural choice is a trade-off , and the things Cassandra sacrificed are exactly the things that relational databases give you for free. Understanding these limitations before you commit to a column-family database is not optional; discovering them in production is painful and expensive. 1. No Joins The most fundamental limitation: column-family databases have no JOIN operation . In a relational database, you normalize data into separate tables and reconstruct the full picture at query time with joins. Cassandra's architecture makes cross-partition joins prohibitively expensive — it would require coordinating reads across many nodes, destroying the single-partition guarantee that makes it fast. What you must do instead: Denormalize — embed all data a query needs into a single table row. If a user's name appears in five query results, store it five times. Application-side joins — fetch user records, then fetch related orders in a second query, and join in application code. This adds round-trips and moves complexity to the application layer. Mitigation: Design your schema so queries never need joins. This is query-first modeling (covered in the previous chapter). It works well when access patterns are stable and known upfront. 2. No Ad-Hoc Queries You cannot walk up to a column-family database with a new question and get an answer cheaply. All access patterns must be known at schema design time. If the business asks "how many users signed up from Germany last week?" and you do not have a users_by_country_and_week table, answering that question requires ALLOW FILTERING — a full cluster scan — or exporting data to a separate analytics system. -- Fine: known access pattern, partition key supplied SELECT * FROM users_by_id WHERE user_id = 'u-4291'; -- Dangerous: no partition key, must scan everything SELECT * FROM users_by_id WHERE country = 'DE' ALLOW FILTERING; Mitigation: Pair Cassandra with an analytics pipeline (e.g., export to a data warehouse for analytical queries). Cassandra handles the operational, high-throughput queries; the warehouse handles exploratory analysis. 3. No Multi-Row Transactions with Strong Isolation Cassandra offers tunable consistency — you choose how many replicas must acknowledge a write (ONE, QUORUM, ALL) — but it does not offer multi-row ACID transactions with serializable isolation. Two writes to different partition keys are never atomic. If you write to users_by_id and users_by_email and the second write fails, your tables are inconsistent. Cassandra does offer Lightweight Transactions (LWT) — a Paxos-based compare-and-swap for single-partition operations. But LWT is expensive (multiple round-trips) and limited to a single partition; it does not span tables. A low-cardinality partition key (like status = 'active') concentrates traffic on a single node. The cluster has plenty of capacity — it's just on the wrong nodes. Mitigation: Accept eventual consistency for most operations. Use LWT only where true compare-and-set is required (e.g., unique email registration). For cross-table consistency, implement compensating transactions or event-driven patterns (e.g., write to an outbox, process asynchronously). 4. Tombstone Accumulation When you delete a row in Cassandra, the database does not immediately remove it. Instead, it writes a tombstone — a marker saying "this was deleted at timestamp T." This is necessary because in a distributed system, delete information must propagate to all replicas; tombstones are the mechanism. Tombstones are retained until the gc_grace_seconds window passes (default: 10 days) and compaction runs. Until then, reads must scan through tombstones to reconstruct the current view. A table with many deletes — like a time-series table where old events are regularly purged — can accumulate millions of tombstones, making reads slow and causing timeouts. Mitigation: Use TTL ( INSERT ... USING TTL 86400 ) instead of explicit deletes. Cassandra handles TTL expiry efficiently. Tune gc_grace_seconds based on your replication propagation time. Design schemas that naturally avoid deletes (append-only patterns). 5. Hot Partitions If your partition key has low cardinality — that is, only a few distinct values — all traffic concentrates on a small number of nodes. A partition key of status with values active and inactive routes perhaps 90% of your traffic to two nodes. Adding more nodes to the cluster does not help; the bottleneck is not capacity, it is the partition key choice. Hot partitions also occur with high-velocity keys: a viral post, a global counter, or a popular product all route every read and write to the same node regardless of cluster size. Mitigation: Choose high-cardinality partition keys (UUIDs, user IDs, device IDs). Bucket low-cardinality values: instead of status , use (status, bucket) where bucket = random(0..N) . Reads must then query all N buckets and aggregate — but load is spread. For counters, use Cassandra's native counter tables, which distribute increment operations. 6. Schema Changes Are Painful In Cassandra, adding a column is cheap — it is a metadata-only operation. But changing the partition key or clustering key requires rewriting the entire table . Because the partition key determines physical data placement, any change to it requires reading all existing data and writing it to a new table with the new key structure. For a table holding terabytes of data, this is a multi-hour or multi-day migration. Mitigation: Design partition and clustering keys carefully upfront; treat them as nearly immutable. Blue/green migrations: create the new table, dual-write to both, backfill the old data, cut over reads, then drop the old table. Use tooling like Apache Spark to backfill large tables efficiently. 7. Operational Complexity Cassandra is a JVM-based application, and JVM systems have specific operational challenges: Concern Detail Mitigation Heap sizing Too small → frequent GC; too large → long GC pauses Tune -Xmx to 8–16 GB; offload hot data to off-heap memory GC pauses Old-generation GC can pause all threads for 1–30 seconds Use G1GC or ZGC; tune GC settings per workload Compaction Background compaction consumes CPU and I/O, slowing reads/writes Choose compaction strategy (STCS, LCS, TWCS) per table Repair Periodic anti-entropy repair is required to keep replicas consistent Schedule regular nodetool repair ; use automated tools (e.g., Reaper) Capacity planning Tombstones, compaction overhead, and replication factor multiply storage Plan for 2–3× raw data size on disk Interactive SQL · Simulating Skewed Partition Load Key Takeaways No joins means you must denormalize or make multiple application-side round-trips. This is not optional — it is baked into the architecture. No ad-hoc queries means all access patterns must be designed upfront. Pair Cassandra with an analytics system for exploratory queries. No multi-row ACID transactions means you must accept eventual consistency or use expensive LWT for single-partition compare-and-set. Tombstones accumulate on deletes — prefer TTL over explicit deletes for time-bound data. Hot partitions are a function of partition key cardinality, not cluster size. Choose high-cardinality keys and bucket when necessary. Changing partition or clustering keys is a full table rewrite — treat them as immutable by design. Operational complexity is real: compaction tuning, GC pressure, and regular repair all require dedicated expertise. ← Previous Modeling for query patterns Next → Good use cases

title: Good use cases
text: Column-family databases like Cassandra were not built to replace relational databases — they were built for workloads that relational databases handle poorly at scale. When the write rate is extremely high, when data must be distributed across many nodes or data centers, and when access patterns are well-understood and stable, column-family databases deliver performance and availability that would require heroic effort to achieve with SQL. This chapter maps the specific workload characteristics that make a column-family database the right choice, and explains why each use case fits the data model so naturally. Use Case 1: Time-Series and IoT Sensor Data Why it fits: IoT devices write data continuously — one row per measurement, per device, per second. A fleet of 100,000 sensors each writing once per second generates 100,000 writes per second. Cassandra's LSM-tree storage engine is write-optimized : it buffers writes in memory and flushes them sequentially to disk, avoiding the expensive random-write penalty of B-tree structures. The data model is a natural fit: partition by device (or device + time bucket to bound partition size), cluster by timestamp. Every write goes to the correct partition, and reading the last N readings for a device is a cheap in-partition range scan. CREATE TABLE sensor_readings ( device_id UUID, recorded_at TIMESTAMP, temperature FLOAT, humidity FLOAT, PRIMARY KEY (device_id, recorded_at) ) WITH CLUSTERING ORDER BY (recorded_at DESC); What would be painful in SQL: A single PostgreSQL instance at 100,000 inserts/second would require extensive tuning, partitioning, and eventually sharding. Horizontal scaling of writes in relational databases is hard; in Cassandra it is the default mode. Use Case 2: User Activity and Event Logs Why it fits: Application event logs — page views, clicks, API calls — are append-heavy and partitioned naturally by user. Each user's activity stream is a wide row: the partition key is user_id , the clustering key is event_timestamp . Fetching a user's recent activity is a single-partition, range-bounded read. CREATE TABLE user_activity ( user_id UUID, event_time TIMESTAMP, event_type TEXT, metadata TEXT, PRIMARY KEY (user_id, event_time) ) WITH CLUSTERING ORDER BY (event_time DESC); -- Retrieve the last 50 events for a user — single partition read SELECT event_type, event_time, metadata FROM user_activity WHERE user_id = 'u-4291' LIMIT 50; Write volume is high and bursty; reads are always by user. Cassandra distributes users across nodes evenly (user UUIDs hash well). No joins are needed — each user's data is self-contained in their partition. What would be painful in SQL: A relational events table grows without bound. Partitioning by user_id in PostgreSQL or MySQL helps but requires manual management. Cassandra handles the distribution automatically. Use Case 3: Messaging Inbox Why it fits: A messaging system needs to store all messages for a recipient and retrieve them sorted by timestamp — newest first for inbox rendering, oldest first for conversation scrollback. This is a classic wide-row pattern: partition by recipient_id , cluster by sent_at . Each user's inbox is a partition. Messages within the partition are sorted by timestamp. Loading a user's inbox is a single-partition read — no cross-node coordination required. What would be painful in SQL: A single messages table with hundreds of millions of rows requires careful indexing and partitioning. Pagination by timestamp per recipient involves complex queries. Cassandra's data model makes this the default, not the exception. Use Case 4: Product Catalog with Variable Attributes Why it fits: Products in an e-commerce catalog have radically different schemas: a laptop has processor speed and RAM; a shirt has size and color; a book has ISBN and author. Relational databases handle this with EAV (Entity-Attribute-Value) tables — notoriously ugly — or JSONB columns. Cassandra supports sparse columns : you can define hundreds of columns in a table, but each row only stores the columns it actually has. Unused columns consume no storage. This makes Cassandra a natural fit for wide, sparse product data where each category has a different set of attributes. What would be painful in SQL: Adding a new product category attribute requires an ALTER TABLE or a schema workaround. With Cassandra, adding a column is a metadata-only operation. Use Case 5: Leaderboards and Counters Why it fits: Cassandra offers native counter columns — a special column type where UPDATE ... SET score = score + 1 is an atomic, distributed increment. Counter columns are designed for high-frequency increments from multiple nodes without read-before-write overhead. Leaderboards pair well with Cassandra when the ranking dimension maps to a clustering key. For example, partition by game_id and cluster by score DESC to always have the top scores for a game in order within a partition. CREATE TABLE game_scores ( game_id TEXT, score BIGINT, user_id UUID, PRIMARY KEY (game_id, score, user_id) ) WITH CLUSTERING ORDER BY (score DESC); Limitation to note: Native counters cannot be mixed with regular columns and have their own consistency quirks. For complex leaderboard logic, sorted sets in Redis may be simpler. Use Case 6: Recommendation Systems (Wide-Row User Preferences) Why it fits: A recommendation system needs to store a large set of user preferences, ratings, or interaction signals per user. In Cassandra, this is a natural wide row: partition by user_id , cluster by item_id or interaction_time . You can store tens of thousands of item interactions per user in a single partition and scan them efficiently. Batch reads for model training can scan partitions sequentially using token-range queries, which align with Cassandra's storage layout. Decision Matrix Use this table to evaluate whether a column-family database fits your workload: Characteristic Use Column-Family Avoid Column-Family Write volume Very high (100k+ writes/sec) Moderate (SQL handles it) Write pattern Append-heavy, few updates Heavy update/delete workload Read pattern Known, stable access patterns Ad-hoc, exploratory queries Data structure Wide rows, sparse columns Normalized, heavily joined entities Scale requirement Horizontal, multi-datacenter Single node or vertical scaling Consistency need Eventual / tunable Strong ACID transactions Analytics need Operational reads only Complex aggregations, reporting Team expertise Cassandra/wide-column specialists Generalist SQL developers Schema stability Partition key fixed at design Frequent schema evolution Interactive SQL · Messaging Inbox Model Workload Characteristics vs. Database Fit The clearest signal for choosing a column-family database is the combination of high write volume and well-understood, stable read patterns . When both conditions hold, the query-first design constraint is a feature, not a limitation — you cannot accidentally write an expensive query because the schema does not allow it. The clearest signal to stay with SQL is the need for ad-hoc queries or multi-entity transactions . Analytical workloads, reporting dashboards, and any logic requiring joins across entity types all belong in relational or analytical databases. Key Takeaways Time-series and IoT are the canonical column-family use case: high write volume, per-device partitioning, time-ordered clustering keys. User activity logs and event streams fit naturally: append-heavy, partitioned by user, time-sorted within partitions. Messaging inboxes are a textbook wide-row use case: partition by recipient, cluster by timestamp — inbox reads are single-partition. Sparse product catalogs benefit from Cassandra's ability to store only the columns a row actually uses, avoiding EAV complexity. Counters and leaderboards are supported by native counter columns and clustered-key ranking. The decision matrix comes down to: high write volume + stable access patterns = column-family wins; ad-hoc queries + multi-row transactions = stay relational. Column-family is a specialist tool. When it fits, it is dramatically better than SQL. When it does not fit, it is dramatically worse. ← Previous What is hard in these systems Next → Chapter 20 Summary: What We Learned

title: Chapter 20 Summary: What We Learned
text: Page Topic Takeaway in One Sentence 191 Wide rows and sparse data Column-family stores organize data into rows with many sparse columns — each row can have millions of columns but only populated ones are stored. 192 Partitioning and clustering keys The partition key distributes data across nodes; the clustering key sorts rows within a partition — both determine query patterns. 193 Write-optimized design Column-family databases use LSM trees for write-optimized storage — sequential writes, background compaction for reads. 194 LSM trees Write to an in-memory memtable, flush to sorted SSTables on disk, compact SSTables in the background to reduce read amplification. 195 Compaction Merging and sorting multiple SSTables into fewer files — critical for read performance but consumes I/O and CPU. 196 Time-series and event workloads Column-family databases excel at high-ingest, append-heavy workloads: metrics, logs, IoT sensor data, user events. 197 Modeling for query patterns Design tables around the queries you need, not around normalizing data — denormalization is expected and necessary. 198 What is hard in these systems No joins, limited ad-hoc queries, complex compaction tuning, and data modeling requires deep understanding of access patterns. 199 Good use cases Time-series and IoT sensor data, user activity and event logs, messaging inboxes, sparse product catalogs, leaderboards and counters, and recommendation systems. Reveal: How do these ideas connect to the next chapter? Chapter 20 covered column-family databases optimized for wide, sparse writes. But some data is not wide or sparse — it is deeply connected. Chapter 21 introduces graph databases. You will learn how nodes and edges model relationships directly, why a 5-hop traversal is O(edges-followed) instead of exponential, and when index-free adjacency beats foreign-key joins. If column-family stores excel at ingesting streams, graph databases excel at navigating networks. ← Previous Good use cases Next → Nodes, edges, and properties

title: Nodes, edges, and properties
text: A graph: nodes are entities, edges are named/directed relationships. Graph DBs make traversals ("friends-of-friends who bought X") first-class operations. A graph database stores data as a network of nodes (entities) and edges (relationships). Instead of spreading related data across multiple tables joined by foreign keys, graph databases make relationships first-class citizens — each edge is a named, directed, typed connection that can carry its own properties. This model is a natural fit for any domain that is fundamentally relational in the non-SQL sense: social networks, supply chains, knowledge graphs, fraud rings, and recommendation engines. The Labeled Property Graph Model The dominant graph model today is the Labeled Property Graph (LPG) , used by Neo4j, Amazon Neptune (Gremlin mode), and Memgraph. It has four building blocks: Concept Description Example Node An entity (vertex) A person, a company, a product Label A type tag on a node (multiple allowed) :Person , :Employee Edge A directed, typed relationship between two nodes KNOWS , WORKS_AT Property A key-value attribute on a node or edge name: "Alice" , since: 2019 Note: Edges are always directed (they have a start node and an end node), but graph engines typically let you traverse them in either direction at query time. The direction is semantic — (Alice)-[:FOLLOWS]->(Bob) means Alice follows Bob, not the other way around. Nodes A node is a record with an identity, zero or more labels, and zero or more properties. Labels are like table names but lighter-weight: a node can carry multiple labels simultaneously ( :Person:Employee ), and labels carry no schema constraints by default. Node id=1 labels: [:Person] properties: name: "Alice" age: 34 city: "Singapore" Node id=2 labels: [:Person] properties: name: "Bob" age: 29 city: "Hanoi" Node id=3 labels: [:Company] properties: name: "Acme Corp" founded: 2010 Edges An edge connects exactly two nodes with a mandatory type and optional properties. Every edge has: A start node (tail) An end node (head) A relationship type (uppercase by convention in Cypher) Zero or more properties Edge id=10 type: KNOWS start: node 1 (Alice) end: node 2 (Bob) properties: since: 2021 Edge id=11 type: WORKS_AT start: node 1 (Alice) end: node 3 (Acme Corp) properties: role: "Engineer" since: 2022 Edge id=12 type: WORKS_AT start: node 2 (Bob) end: node 3 (Acme Corp) properties: role: "Designer" since: 2023 A small labeled property graph: two Person nodes and one Company node, connected by typed, directed edges that carry their own properties. How This Differs from Relational Tables In a relational database, relationships are implicit. Two tables are connected by matching values — a foreign key in one row that equals a primary key in another. The relationship itself has no physical representation; it only comes into being when a query performs a join. In a graph database, relationships are explicit and stored directly . Each edge record points directly to its start and end nodes. There are three important consequences: No join computation at query time. Following an edge is a pointer traversal, not an index lookup and comparison across two table scans. Relationship properties are natural. To store when a friendship started in a relational model you need a junction table with an extra column. In a graph, you just add a since property to the edge itself. Schema flexibility. Nodes with the same label don't need identical property sets. Alice might have a phone property; Bob might not. No NULL column overhead, no ALTER TABLE . Relational vs Graph: Storing a Friendship Network Relational approach: -- Three tables minimum for a "person knows person with timestamp" CREATE TABLE persons (id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT, age INTEGER); CREATE TABLE friendships ( person_a INTEGER REFERENCES persons(id), person_b INTEGER REFERENCES persons(id), since INTEGER, PRIMARY KEY (person_a, person_b) ); Graph approach (Cypher, Neo4j): CREATE (a:Person {name: "Alice", age: 34}) CREATE (b:Person {name: "Bob", age: 29}) CREATE (a)-[:KNOWS {since: 2021}]->(b) The graph version has no join table, no nullable foreign keys, and the relationship is a named, typed first-class object. When you ask "who does Alice know?" the engine walks the edges stored on Alice's node record — it never scans the friends table. Simulating the Graph Model with Relational Tables The widget below uses SQLite to emulate a property graph using three tables: nodes , node_props , and edges . This is exactly how a naive relational graph layer works — and why dedicated graph databases exist. Interactive SQL · Property Graph in Relational Tables Notice how answering a single one-hop question — "where does Alice work?" — requires joining four tables. In a native graph database, the same question is resolved by following pointers stored directly on Alice's node record, with no join computation. Why Graph Databases Don't Use Foreign Keys Foreign keys enforce referential integrity, but they don't store physical pointers. When PostgreSQL evaluates JOIN persons ON friendships.person_b = persons.id , it looks up an index on persons.id for each row in friendships . That index lookup has a cost that scales with the size of both tables. Graph databases use a technique called index-free adjacency : each node stores a direct linked list of its edges. Traversing one hop is an O(1) pointer dereference regardless of how many nodes exist in the graph. A social network with 1 billion users doesn't make "find Alice's friends" slower — only the number of Alice's friends matters, not the global population. Concept Relational Graph (LPG) Relationship storage Implicit (matching FK values) Explicit (edge record with pointers) Relationship properties Junction table column Edge property Multi-label entity NULL columns or sub-type tables Multiple labels on one node Hop cost Index lookup per join Pointer traversal (O(1) per hop) Schema enforcement Strict column schema Optional constraints Key Takeaways Nodes are entities with labels and properties; edges are directed, typed relationships that can carry their own properties. The Labeled Property Graph model makes relationships first-class objects — stored explicitly, not inferred at query time. Graph databases use index-free adjacency : nodes hold direct pointers to their edges, making each hop O(1) regardless of graph size. The relational equivalent of a graph requires junction tables and multi-table joins — workable for shallow queries but expensive for multi-hop traversals. Graph databases excel when your questions are about the connections themselves — paths, reachability, clusters — rather than aggregate statistics over flat records. ← Previous Good use cases Next → Traversals and path queries

title: Traversals and path queries
text: If nodes and edges are the nouns and verbs of a graph, traversal is the grammar that makes them useful. A traversal starts at one or more nodes and follows edges outward — hop by hop — collecting or filtering nodes along the way. This is fundamentally different from a relational join: instead of comparing columns across two flat tables, the database follows physical pointers embedded in each node's record, expanding outward through the network. How Graph Traversal Works Every traversal begins with a start set — one or more seed nodes, usually identified by a property filter (e.g., name = "Alice" ). From there the engine expands outward by following edges. Two classic strategies: Breadth-First Search (BFS): Explore all neighbors at depth 1 before moving to depth 2. Finds the shortest path and all nodes reachable within a given number of hops. Memory-intensive because it must track the entire frontier. Depth-First Search (DFS): Follow one path as deep as possible before backtracking. Lower memory footprint than BFS; useful for finding any path or exhausting all paths. Most graph query languages let you specify path patterns without choosing the algorithm explicitly — the query planner picks the appropriate strategy based on the query shape (shortest path → BFS; all paths → DFS or iterative deepening). Cypher Path Patterns In Cypher (Neo4j's query language), you describe paths declaratively: -- One-hop: direct friends of Alice MATCH (a:Person {name: "Alice"})-[:KNOWS]->(friend) RETURN friend.name -- Two hops: friends-of-friends MATCH (a:Person {name: "Alice"})-[:KNOWS*2]->(fof) RETURN fof.name -- Variable-length: all nodes reachable within 1–3 hops MATCH (a:Person {name: "Alice"})-[:KNOWS*1..3]->(reach) RETURN DISTINCT reach.name -- Shortest path between Alice and Carol MATCH p = shortestPath( (a:Person {name: "Alice"})-[:KNOWS*]-(c:Person {name: "Carol"}) ) RETURN length(p), [n IN nodes(p) | n.name] AS path The * notation means "any number of hops"; *1..3 bounds it. shortestPath() activates a BFS internally. Index-Free Adjacency The reason graph traversal is fast in a native graph database comes down to index-free adjacency : each node record contains a direct pointer (or a linked list of pointers) to its adjacent edges. Following one hop is an O(1) memory dereference — the engine reads the pointer and jumps to the edge record, then reads the pointer to the target node. Contrast this with a relational join. Even with an index, each hop requires: A B-tree index lookup on the foreign key value A row fetch from the joined table Repeat for each matching row The index lookup is O(log N) in the size of the indexed table — and for multi-hop queries it compounds: three hops means three nested O(log N) lookups. In a graph with millions of nodes the difference between pointer-traversal and index-lookup per hop is orders of magnitude. BFS traversal from Alice: the frontier expands one depth level at a time. Nodes at depth 1 (Bob, Carol) are discovered before depth 2 (Dave, Eve), which are discovered before depth 3 (Frank). This guarantees the shortest-hop path is found first. Types of Path Queries Query type Description Example use case Single-hop Direct neighbors "Who does Alice follow?" Fixed-depth Exactly N hops "Friends-of-friends (depth 2)" Variable-length Between min and max hops "Nodes within 3 hops of Alice" Shortest path Minimum-hop route between two nodes "Degrees of separation between Alice and Frank" All paths Every route, possibly bounded "All supply chain routes from factory to store" Weighted shortest path Dijkstra / A* over edge weights "Cheapest flight route" Note: Unbounded all-paths queries ( MATCH p=(a)-[*]->(b) ) can be exponential in the number of cycles in the graph. Always bound variable-length traversals in production queries. Multi-Hop Traversal: The Relational Challenge Relational databases can answer multi-hop queries using recursive CTEs (Common Table Expressions). The SQL standard introduced WITH RECURSIVE precisely for this use case. Here is a three-hop friend-of-friend query: WITH RECURSIVE reachable(id, depth) AS ( -- Base case: start from Alice (id=1) SELECT end_id, 1 FROM edges WHERE start_id = 1 AND type = 'KNOWS' UNION ALL -- Recursive case: follow one more hop SELECT e.end_id, r.depth + 1 FROM reachable r JOIN edges e ON e.start_id = r.id AND e.type = 'KNOWS' WHERE r.depth < 3 ) SELECT DISTINCT id FROM reachable; This works, but it has a critical scalability problem: each recursive step is a full join over the entire edges table . The query planner re-evaluates the join predicate for every row at every depth level. For a graph with millions of edges, a 5-hop traversal requires five sequential full-table joins. The cost grows as O(E × depth) where E is the number of edges — compared to O(neighbors × depth) for a native graph database using index-free adjacency. Simulating Multi-Hop Traversal with Recursive CTEs The widget below runs a real recursive CTE in SQLite, simulating BFS over a small social graph. Watch the query find friends-of-friends and try changing the depth limit. Interactive SQL · Multi-Hop Traversal via Recursive CTE Notice the path NOT LIKE '%name%' cycle guard — a clumsy workaround for what a native graph database handles natively with visited-node tracking. Also notice that each recursive step re-joins the entire knows table, regardless of how few edges are actually relevant to the current frontier. Why Recursive CTEs Get Expensive Three compounding problems make recursive CTEs unscalable for deep graph traversal: 1. Full-table join per level. Each recursive step joins against every edge in the edges table, then filters to relevant rows. A native graph store follows only the edges stored on the current node — irrelevant edges are never touched. 2. No native visited-node tracking. SQL has no built-in cycle detection. You must maintain a path string or visited-set array using non-standard extensions ( ARRAY in PostgreSQL, string hacks in SQLite). This adds CPU and memory overhead proportional to path length. 3. Materialization between steps. Most SQL engines materialize the intermediate result of each recursive step before moving to the next. For wide graphs, this creates large temporary tables that must be written to disk. A native graph engine — Neo4j, TigerGraph, or Amazon Neptune — maintains the traversal frontier in memory as a set of node pointers, expanding it level by level without any join or materialization. For 6-degree-of-separation queries over a 100M-node social graph, a native graph store answers in seconds; a recursive CTE may time out. Shortest Path Shortest-path queries are the canonical graph workload. In Cypher: MATCH p = shortestPath( (a:Person {name: "Alice"})-[:KNOWS*]-(f:Person {name: "Frank"}) ) RETURN length(p) AS hops, [n IN nodes(p) | n.name] AS path The engine runs Dijkstra's algorithm (or BFS for unweighted graphs) using index-free adjacency. It terminates as soon as it finds the destination — it doesn't need to explore the entire graph. The relational equivalent requires running the recursive CTE to completion across all reachable nodes, then filtering — you cannot terminate early because SQL has no mechanism to stop recursion when a target is found. Key Takeaways Graph traversal follows edges hop by hop, using BFS for shortest paths and DFS for all-path enumeration. Index-free adjacency gives native graph databases O(1) per-hop cost: each node stores direct pointers to its adjacent edges, so traversal never touches a global index. Recursive CTEs let relational databases answer multi-hop queries, but each recursive step is a full join over the edge table — cost scales with total edge count, not just the local neighborhood. Shortest-path queries in native graph engines terminate early (Dijkstra / BFS); recursive CTE equivalents must exhaust all reachable nodes first. For queries that are "shallow and wide" (1–2 hops over many nodes), relational joins are often competitive. For "deep and targeted" queries (5+ hops, shortest paths, cycle detection), native graph traversal wins decisively. ← Previous Nodes, edges, and properties Next → Modeling relationships directly

title: Modeling relationships directly
text: Relational databases model relationships as a derived concept — you reconstruct them at query time by joining tables. Graph databases flip that design on its head: relationships are first-class citizens stored alongside nodes , ready to traverse at near-zero cost regardless of dataset size. This page shows exactly what that means in practice and why it matters for highly connected data. The Relational Way: Relationships as Tables In a relational database, relationships live inside data — encoded as foreign keys or resolved through join tables. Consider a simple social graph where people can follow each other. -- Relational schema for a social graph CREATE TABLE users ( id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL ); CREATE TABLE follows ( follower_id INTEGER REFERENCES users(id), followee_id INTEGER REFERENCES users(id), PRIMARY KEY (follower_id, followee_id) ); This is clean and normalized. A single hop ("who does Alice follow?") is one join. But add another hop and you join the same tables again: -- Two hops: "friends of friends" of Alice (id = 1) SELECT DISTINCT u3.name FROM users u1 JOIN follows f1 ON u1.id = f1.follower_id JOIN users u2 ON f1.followee_id = u2.id JOIN follows f2 ON u2.id = f2.follower_id JOIN users u3 ON f2.followee_id = u3.id WHERE u1.id = 1; Three hops adds another pair of joins. The query grows linearly with depth, and each new join forces the database to re-scan the follows table — which can have hundreds of millions of rows in a real social network. Key problem: In a relational database, the cost of a relationship traversal scales with the size of the join table , not the degree of the start node. If follows has 500 million rows, every hop through it is expensive. The Graph Way: Relationships as Stored Edges A property graph database like Neo4j stores data as nodes and edges . An edge is not a lookup — it is a physical pointer from one node to another. Traversal means following a pointer, not scanning a table. Each node stores a list of its own edges. To find Alice's followees, the database reads Alice's node and follows her outgoing edge pointers — it never touches any data belonging to other users. Depth-2 traversal doubles the pointer-following work, not the scan width. Node: Alice → edge ptr → Bob's node → edge ptr → Carol's node → edge ptr → Dave's node → edge ptr → Eve's node No index needed. No join table. The relationship itself is the path. Top: Relational friend-of-friend requires two full scans of a massive join table. Bottom: Graph traversal follows physical pointers on Alice's node — unrelated data is never touched. Property Graphs: Nodes and Edges with Attributes Most graph databases implement the property graph model . Both nodes and edges can carry arbitrary key-value properties — there is no need for a separate attribute table. Element What it stores Example Node Entity + label(s) + properties (:Person {name:"Alice", age:31}) Edge Directed relationship + type + properties [:FOLLOWS {since:"2022-01"}] Properties on edges are a critical advantage over join tables. In a relational schema, adding a followed_since date to a many-to-many relationship requires altering the join table or adding a new one. In a property graph you simply add the property to the edge. // Property graph notation (Cypher-like) (Alice:Person {age:31}) -[:FOLLOWS {since:"2022-01"}]-> (Bob:Person {age:28}) Index-Free Adjacency The technical term for what makes graph traversal fast is index-free adjacency : each node directly references its neighbors. When you load Alice's node, you get Alice's edge list; no secondary lookup is needed. Compare this with relational adjacency, where finding neighbors always requires querying an index on follower_id in the follows table. That index lookup is O(log n) at best, repeated for every hop, across a table that grows with every new relationship in the entire system. In a property graph: Hop cost = number of edges on the current node (local) In relational = log(total rows in join table) per hop (global) For sparse graphs with millions of users and billions of edges, this difference is orders of magnitude in traversal speed. Note: Index-free adjacency does not help with global aggregations like "count all edges." For those, a relational database may still be faster. Graph databases shine on local traversal from a known start node. Simulating the Join Explosion The widget below runs the "friend of friend" query in SQLite so you can see how many rows get scanned and how the SQL grows. Extend it to three hops by copying the pattern — notice the query must be rewritten each time depth increases. Interactive SQL · Friend-of-Friend Join Explosion Notice that adding depth means rewriting the query — in a graph database the depth is a parameter, not a structural change to the query. When to Choose a Graph Database Graphs are the right tool when your most important queries traverse relationships of unknown or variable depth : Social networks — friend recommendations, mutual connections, influence propagation Fraud detection — ring detection, money-laundering chains, account linkage graphs Knowledge graphs — entity relationships, ontology traversal, recommendation engines Network topology — routing, dependency resolution, impact analysis If your queries are mostly lookups by a single attribute and joins are shallow (1–2 hops, bounded), a relational database handles the job more simply. The graph model earns its keep when relationship traversal is the central operation. Key Takeaways Relational databases encode relationships as data (foreign keys, join tables); graph databases encode them as structure (physical edge pointers). Index-free adjacency means hop cost is proportional to the local degree of a node, not the global size of any table. Edges in a property graph are first-class: they carry a type and arbitrary properties, avoiding the need for auxiliary tables. The "friend-of-friend" query illustrates the core asymmetry: adding a hop in SQL requires a new join; in a graph query language it is a single parameter change. Graph databases are not a replacement for relational systems — they are purpose-built for workloads where traversing deeply connected data is the primary access pattern. ← Previous Traversals and path queries Next → Graph query languages

title: Graph query languages
text: Graph databases need query languages that can express path patterns, variable-depth traversals, and relationship conditions naturally — things SQL was never designed for. Several languages have emerged, each with a different philosophy. This page covers the major contenders: Cypher , Gremlin , SPARQL , and the emerging ISO standard GQL . Why SQL Falls Short for Graphs SQL can express graph queries — recursive CTEs (Common Table Expressions) let you walk edges — but the syntax is verbose, the depth must often be bounded at compile time, and the query planner is not optimized for pointer-chasing. A three-hop path query in SQL is already painful; a variable-length path query is nearly unwritable. Graph query languages solve this by treating path patterns as first-class syntax. Cypher: Pattern Matching with ASCII Art Cypher is the query language of Neo4j (open-sourced as openCypher). Its defining feature is an ASCII-art notation for graph patterns that reads almost like a diagram drawn in text. (a:Person)-[:KNOWS]->(b:Person) Round brackets () denote nodes; square brackets [] denote relationships; - and -> indicate direction. You read this exactly as it looks: "a Person node connected via a KNOWS edge to another Person node." Core Cypher Clauses Clause Purpose SQL analogue MATCH Declare a pattern to find FROM + JOIN WHERE Filter matched results WHERE RETURN Specify output SELECT CREATE Insert nodes or edges INSERT MERGE Upsert — create if not found INSERT ... ON CONFLICT ... DO NOTHING + UPDATE SET Update properties UPDATE … SET DELETE Remove nodes or edges DELETE Cypher Query Examples Find all people Alice knows: MATCH (alice:Person {name: "Alice"})-[:KNOWS]->(friend:Person) RETURN friend.name; Friend of friend (2 hops) — depth is a parameter, not a join: MATCH (alice:Person {name: "Alice"})-[:KNOWS*2]->(fof:Person) WHERE fof.name <> "Alice" RETURN DISTINCT fof.name; The *2 means exactly 2 hops. Use *1..3 for variable depth (1 to 3 hops). There is no rewrite needed — just change the number. Create a new relationship with a property: MATCH (a:Person {name: "Alice"}), (b:Person {name: "Bob"}) MERGE (a)-[:FOLLOWS {since: "2024-01"}]->(b); Shortest path between two nodes: MATCH p = shortestPath( (alice:Person {name:"Alice"})-[:KNOWS*]-(target:Person {name:"Eve"}) ) RETURN length(p), [n IN nodes(p) | n.name] AS path; A Cypher MATCH pattern reads like an ASCII diagram of the graph. The pattern (alice:Person)-[:KNOWS]->(bob:Person) binds node variables to matching subgraphs. Gremlin: Imperative Traversal Steps Gremlin is the query language of Apache TinkerPop, a graph computing framework used by Amazon Neptune, JanusGraph, and others. Where Cypher is declarative (describe the pattern, let the engine plan), Gremlin is imperative : you describe the exact traversal steps. // Start at Alice, follow outgoing KNOWS edges, return names g.V().has('name', 'Alice').out('KNOWS').values('name') // Friend of friend g.V().has('name', 'Alice').out('KNOWS').out('KNOWS').values('name').dedup() // Variable depth: 1 to 3 hops g.V().has('name', 'Alice').repeat(out('KNOWS')).times(2).values('name') // Shortest path g.V().has('name','Alice'). repeat(both().simplePath()). until(has('name','Eve')). path(). limit(1) Gremlin steps chain like a pipeline: each step produces a traverser that the next step filters or transforms. This is familiar to developers comfortable with functional pipelines. Gremlin vs Cypher: Cypher is easier to read for graph patterns; Gremlin gives finer procedural control and works across multiple backends. Choose based on your database (Neo4j → Cypher; Neptune/JanusGraph → Gremlin). SPARQL: Querying RDF Graphs SPARQL targets a different graph model: RDF (Resource Description Framework) , used in semantic web and linked data. In RDF, all data is triples: (subject, predicate, object) . A graph is a bag of triples. PREFIX foaf: <http://xmlns.com/foaf/0.1/> SELECT ?friendName WHERE { ?alice foaf:name "Alice" . ?alice foaf:knows ?friend . ?friend foaf:name ?friendName . } SPARQL is critical for knowledge graphs , ontology reasoning, and linked open data (Wikidata, DBpedia). For property graph use cases, Cypher or Gremlin are more common. GQL: The ISO Standard GQL (Graph Query Language) was ratified as ISO/IEC 39075 in 2024 — the first international standard for graph databases, filling the same role SQL plays for relational systems. GQL is heavily influenced by Cypher and incorporates ideas from PGQL and G-CORE. -- GQL (ISO 39075) — syntax resembles Cypher MATCH (p:Person)-[:KNOWS]->(friend:Person) WHERE p.name = 'Alice' RETURN friend.name; GQL's significance: vendors can implement it without a license, and tooling can target a single standard. It is still early (2024–2025 adoption), but most major graph databases have announced GQL roadmaps. Language Comparison Language Model Style Primary DB Standardized Cypher Property graph Declarative / pattern Neo4j, Memgraph, AgensGraph openCypher (open spec) Gremlin Property graph Imperative / pipeline Neptune, JanusGraph, TinkerPop Apache TinkerPop SPARQL RDF triples Declarative / pattern Virtuoso, GraphDB, Blazegraph W3C standard GQL Property graph Declarative / pattern Emerging across vendors ISO/IEC 39075 (2024) Simulating Cypher-Style Queries with SQL The widget below uses SQLite to simulate a Cypher MATCH pattern using relational tables. The nodes and edges tables represent the property graph; the SQL JOIN replicates what MATCH (a)-[:KNOWS]->(b) does. Notice how the SQL grows with each additional hop — the core pain point that Cypher solves. Interactive SQL · Simulating Cypher MATCH with Relational Tables In Cypher, the 2-hop query above becomes MATCH (a {name:"Alice"})-[:KNOWS*2]->(c) RETURN DISTINCT c.name — a single pattern change. In SQL, you must add two more JOINs. Key Takeaways Cypher (openCypher / Neo4j) uses ASCII-art pattern syntax and is the most readable graph query language for property graphs; variable-depth traversal is a first-class feature. Gremlin (Apache TinkerPop) takes an imperative pipeline approach, giving developers fine-grained control over traversal strategy and working across multiple graph backends. SPARQL is the W3C standard for RDF / semantic web graphs and underpins knowledge graphs and linked open data. GQL (ISO/IEC 39075, 2024) is the emerging international standard for property graph queries, designed to be to graphs what SQL is to relational databases. The core advantage of all graph query languages over SQL: variable-depth traversal is a parameter, not a structural rewrite . [:KNOWS*1..5] in Cypher replaces five pairs of joins in SQL. Adoption is still fragmented, but GQL standardization will drive convergence over the next decade. ← Previous Modeling relationships directly Next → When joins become graph walks

title: When joins become graph walks
text: Relational databases are extraordinarily good at joins — as long as you don't need too many of them in sequence. The moment a query requires following relationships three, four, or five hops deep , the join machinery starts to buckle. This page explains why, using a LinkedIn-style "people you may know" query as a concrete example, and shows exactly where the performance cliff appears. The Fan-Out Problem Imagine a social network. Every person has, on average, 5 connections . You want to find everyone within 3 hops of a given user — a common "recommendations" pattern. Hops People reached SQL joins required 0 1 (yourself) 0 1 5 1 self-join 2 25 2 self-joins 3 125 3 self-joins 4 625 4 self-joins 5 3 125 5 self-joins Each hop multiplies the intermediate result set. That multiplication is not just a count of rows returned — it is the number of index lookups the database engine must perform at each stage. With realistic branching factors (LinkedIn users average ~hundreds of connections), the intermediate join tables balloon into millions of rows before deduplication. Each hop multiplies the working set. With average branching factor 5, hop 3 reaches 125 candidates — and SQL must materialise every intermediate row. What SQL Actually Does A single hop is easy: -- 1-hop: direct friends SELECT b.user_id AS friend FROM connections c JOIN users b ON c.to_user = b.user_id WHERE c.from_user = 42; Two hops: join again against the result: -- 2-hop: friends of friends SELECT DISTINCT fof.user_id FROM connections c1 JOIN connections c2 ON c1.to_user = c2.from_user JOIN users fof ON c2.to_user = fof.user_id WHERE c1.from_user = 42 AND fof.user_id <> 42; The query planner sees connections twice. With 10 million rows in connections , each B-tree lookup is O(log N) ≈ 23 comparisons. After the first join, you might have 500 rows; the second join then fires 500 × O(log N) index lookups. At three hops, 2 500 lookups. The cost grows multiplicatively with both branching factor and depth. Recursive CTEs: The SQL Workaround SQL:1999 introduced recursive CTEs ( WITH RECURSIVE ) to express variable-depth traversal without repeating self-joins: WITH RECURSIVE reachable(user_id, depth) AS ( -- anchor: start at user 42 SELECT to_user, 1 FROM connections WHERE from_user = 42 UNION ALL -- recursive step: follow one more edge SELECT c.to_user, r.depth + 1 FROM reachable r JOIN connections c ON c.from_user = r.user_id WHERE r.depth < 3 -- max 3 hops ) SELECT DISTINCT user_id FROM reachable WHERE user_id <> 42; This is better — you don't hard-code the number of joins — but the engine still performs a B-tree lookup per edge in the recursive step. The intermediate table grows with every iteration, and each lookup carries the O(log N) cost of navigating the index. Interactive SQL · 2-hop friend-of-friend with recursive CTE Why Graph DBs Are Different: Index-Free Adjacency A native graph database stores each node with direct physical pointers to its neighbouring nodes . There is no index to traverse — the pointer is the edge. Operation Relational DB Graph DB Find node O(log N) B-tree lookup O(1) pointer follow Follow 1 edge O(log N) index join O(1) pointer dereference Follow k edges O(k × log N × branch_factor) O(k × branch_factor) — linear in edges visited 3-hop traversal on 1 M nodes ~millions of comparisons ~125 pointer follows (above example) Index-free adjacency is the term Neo4j uses for this design. Each node record in the storage file contains the address of its first relationship. Each relationship record contains the IDs of both endpoints and the addresses of the next relationships for each endpoint. Traversing is literally following linked-list pointers — no global index involved. The consequence: traversal time is proportional to the subgraph visited, not to the total graph size . Finding 3-hop neighbours of one person takes the same time whether the graph has 1 000 or 1 000 000 000 nodes — as long as the local neighbourhood is the same size. When Should You Switch? The crossover point is roughly: Depth ≤ 2, low cardinality: SQL with a good index is fine. A two-table join on indexed foreign keys is fast and familiar. Depth 2–3, moderate cardinality (thousands of rows in intermediate result): Recursive CTE works but starts to hurt on large graphs. Consider a graph DB if queries are frequent. Depth > 3 or variable depth (shortest path, reachability): Relational cost explodes. Graph DB is the natural tool. Many-to-many at any depth, plus frequent schema changes on relationship types: Graph DB wins on both performance and modelling flexibility. A common real-world trigger: fraud detection graphs, recommendation engines, access-control hierarchies, knowledge graphs, and route planning — all require traversal beyond 2 hops regularly. What Graph Query Languages Gain Cypher (Neo4j) expresses the same 3-hop query concisely: MATCH (you:Person {id: 42})-[:KNOWS*1..3]-(others) WHERE others <> you RETURN DISTINCT others.name The *1..3 syntax means "follow KNOWS edges between 1 and 3 times." The engine walks pointers rather than joining index pages. The planner never materialises intermediate result sets in the same way — it streams node IDs along paths. Key Takeaways Each additional SQL join multiplies the intermediate row count by the branching factor — cost is superlinear in depth. Recursive CTEs solve variable-depth queries syntactically, but the engine still pays O(log N) per edge at runtime. Index-free adjacency in graph databases replaces every join with a pointer follow — O(1) per hop regardless of graph size. Traversal cost in a graph DB scales with the number of edges visited, not with total data size. The practical switch point is depth > 2–3 or highly connected many-to-many relationships that change frequently. ← Previous Graph query languages Next → Performance characteristics

title: Performance characteristics
text: Understanding why graph databases are fast for some queries — and slow for others — requires looking at how they physically store and traverse data. This page digs into the storage layout, complexity analysis, and real-world trade-offs that determine when a graph engine outperforms a relational one and when it does not. Index-Free Adjacency: The Core Performance Mechanism The defining feature of native graph storage is index-free adjacency : each node record holds a direct pointer to its first relationship record, and each relationship record chains to the next relationship for each of its two endpoints. No global index is consulted during traversal. Each node record points directly to its first relationship. Relationship records form a doubly-linked list per endpoint — traversal is pure pointer-chasing, no index needed. In Neo4j's original fixed-size record format (up to Neo4j 4.x), every node record is exactly 15 bytes and every relationship record is exactly 34 bytes . Because records are fixed-size, random access by ID is arithmetic: offset = id × record_size . Both node and relationship lookups are O(1) file seeks. Version note: Neo4j 5.x+ introduced a more flexible record format with different internal sizing.
