A 10× compression ratio means: 10× more data fits in L3 cache — the CPU can process data at cache bandwidth (~500 GB/s) instead of memory bandwidth (~50 GB/s). 10× more values fit in each vector — a 4 KB vector now holds 4,096 compressed values instead of 400 uncompressed strings. SIMD operates on compressed codes — integer comparison on 8-bit dictionary codes is faster than string comparison, and SIMD can compare 32 of them per instruction. The engine can often skip decompression entirely for filter predicates: a WHERE country = 'CA' translates to WHERE dict_code = 1 , and the bit-packed integer comparison is faster than the original string comparison. Key insight: A query that reads 100 GB of uncompressed data might only read 8 GB after columnar compression. Vectorized execution then processes those 8 GB at full SIMD throughput. The combined effect is not additive — it is multiplicative. This is why DuckDB can aggregate a 1-billion-row TPC-H dataset in seconds on a laptop. Compression Ratios in Practice Real-world compression ratios vary by column characteristics: Column type Typical cardinality Technique Compression ratio Status / enum ( PENDING , PAID ) 2–10 Dictionary + bit-pack 10–40× Country code ~250 Dictionary + 8-bit 4–13× Timestamp (sorted) High, monotonic Delta + bit-pack 4–8× Numeric amounts High, varied Bit-pack or generic 1.5–3× Free-text strings Very high Generic (zstd) 2–4× The sweet spot for both compression and vectorization is low-cardinality, homogeneous categorical columns — exactly the kind of columns that dominate dimension tables and fact table foreign keys in analytical schemas. Interactive Example: Aggregating Compressed Data The query below demonstrates the kind of workload that benefits most from columnar compression and vectorized execution. Notice that the GROUP BY country, product_category forces the engine to process every row, but the low cardinality of those columns means the data compresses aggressively and the inner-loop comparison is a cheap integer equality check. Interactive SQL · Columnar Aggregation Patterns In a real columnar engine, the country and product_category columns would be dictionary-encoded at storage time. The aggregation loop runs entirely on integer codes, never touching the original strings. With vectorized execution, the inner GROUP BY loop processes 1,024 rows per iteration using SIMD integer operations — far faster than a row-at-a-time loop comparing raw strings. Key Takeaways Columnar data compresses well because storing homogeneous values in sequence creates statistical regularity: repeated values, monotonic sequences, and small cardinalities. RLE replaces runs of identical values with (value, count) pairs and enables aggregate queries to operate directly on compressed data without decompression. Dictionary encoding maps strings to small integers, enabling fast equality filters and dramatically reducing storage for low-cardinality columns. Delta encoding and bit-packing shrink numeric columns by storing only what varies and using only the bits required. The Volcano model (row-at-a-time) incurs millions of function calls and cannot use SIMD; vectorized execution amortizes overhead across fixed-size batches (typically 1,024 values) that fit in CPU cache and enable SIMD parallelism. Compression and vectorization multiply each other's benefits : compressed data fits more values per cache line, and SIMD operates on compact integer codes faster than on raw strings. The combination is why modern analytical engines (DuckDB, Snowflake, BigQuery) can scan billions of rows per second on modest hardware — the CPU spends nearly all its time doing useful arithmetic, not shuffling bytes or dispatching function calls. ← Previous Columnar storage Next → Star schemas and fact tables

title: Star schemas and fact tables
text: Analytical queries ask different questions than transactional ones. Instead of "fetch order #4821," they ask "what was total revenue by region last quarter?" To answer that efficiently, the data must be organized around measurements and their context — not around the entities of daily operations. The star schema is the most widely used approach to this kind of organization, and understanding it unlocks almost everything about how data warehouses are designed. The Star Schema A star schema places one large fact table at the center, surrounded by several smaller dimension tables that radiate outward like points of a star. Every row in the fact table records a single business event — a sale, a page view, a support call — along with numeric measurements and foreign keys into the dimension tables that describe who, what, when, where, and why . Star schema with fact_sales at center and four dimension tables: date, customer, product, and store. Arrows run from fact foreign keys to dimension primary keys. Fact Tables The fact table is the heart of the schema. Every row represents a single grain — the finest unit of measurement the schema tracks. Choosing the grain is one of the most important design decisions you will make. Column Type Role sale_id BIGINT Surrogate primary key date_key INT FK → dim_date product_key INT FK → dim_product customer_key INT FK → dim_customer store_key INT FK → dim_store sale_amount DECIMAL(12,2) Additive measure quantity INT Additive measure discount_pct DECIMAL(5,2) Semi-additive measure Additive measures (revenue, quantity) can be summed across any dimension. Semi-additive measures (inventory balance, discount percentage) can be summed across some dimensions but not all — summing a discount percentage across customers makes no sense. Fact tables are typically very wide (50–200 columns) and very deep (hundreds of millions to billions of rows). They dominate warehouse storage and are the primary target of columnar compression. The Grain The grain is stated as a sentence: "One row per line item on a sales order." If the grain is ambiguous or mixed, aggregations produce wrong answers. Common grains: One row per daily product inventory snapshot One row per web session event One row per financial transaction Finer grain = more flexibility but more storage. Coarser grain = less storage but less analytical depth. Dimension Tables Dimension tables hold the descriptive context around each fact. They are typically small — thousands to a few million rows — and deliberately denormalized . Instead of a separate city table joined through a region table joined through a country table, all geography is flattened into a single dim_geography table. -- Example dimension table: all location attributes in one flat table CREATE TABLE dim_store ( store_key INT PRIMARY KEY, -- surrogate key (not operational ID) store_id VARCHAR(20), -- natural / business key store_name VARCHAR(100), city VARCHAR(100), state VARCHAR(50), region VARCHAR(50), country VARCHAR(50), sq_footage INT, opened_date DATE ); Surrogate Keys Dimension tables use surrogate keys — system-generated integers — rather than natural business keys. This insulates the warehouse from operational system changes (a product SKU can change; the surrogate key never does) and enables slowly changing dimensions . Slowly Changing Dimensions (SCDs) When a customer moves from New York to London, which city should historical sales show? SCD strategies handle this: Type Strategy Trade-off Type 1 Overwrite old value Simple; loses history Type 2 Insert new row with new key Full history; table grows Type 3 Add "previous value" column Limited history; compact Type 2 is the most common: the dimension table gets valid_from and valid_to dates, and the fact table's foreign key points to the specific version of the row that was current at event time. Why Denormalization is Intentional In OLTP design, normalization reduces redundancy and update anomalies. In OLAP design, the calculus flips: Joins are expensive on billions of rows. Denormalization replaces runtime joins with storage — and storage is cheap. The goal is to touch as few column segments as possible per query. A normalized "snowflake" variant does exist (discussed below), but the flat star schema is often preferred because: Fewer joins means simpler query plans Columnar engines can filter and aggregate a dimension column with a single segment read Query writers don't need to know the operational normalization structure The Snowflake Schema In a snowflake schema , dimension tables are themselves normalized into sub-dimensions. dim_product might have a foreign key to dim_category , which joins to dim_department . fact_sales └── dim_product └── dim_category └── dim_department Advantages: Less storage redundancy, easier dimension maintenance. Disadvantages: More joins at query time, more complex SQL, harder for BI tools to auto-generate queries. Most modern warehouses prefer the star schema or a hybrid. Snowflake schemas are more common when dimensions are extremely large or when the hierarchy has many levels. Interactive Example: A Star Schema Query Interactive SQL · Star schema join and aggregation Comparing Schema Styles Property Star Schema Snowflake Schema 3NF (OLTP) Join depth Shallow (1 hop) Deep (2+ hops) Varies Query complexity Low Medium High Storage Higher (redundancy) Lower Lowest Update anomalies Possible Fewer None BI tool compatibility Excellent Good Poor Scan performance Excellent Good Poor Key Takeaways A star schema has one fact table at the center surrounded by flat dimension tables — shaped like a star. Fact tables store measurements (additive or semi-additive) plus foreign keys; they are wide and deep, often billions of rows. Dimension tables store descriptive attributes; they are small, denormalized, and accessed via surrogate keys. The grain defines what each fact row represents — getting it right is the first step in warehouse design. Denormalization is intentional in OLAP: fewer joins means faster full-scan aggregations on columnar storage. The snowflake schema normalizes dimension tables for reduced redundancy, at the cost of more joins. Slowly changing dimensions (SCD Type 2) preserve history by inserting new rows rather than overwriting old ones. ← Previous Compression and vectorized execution Next → Batch loading

title: Batch loading
text: Row-by-row INSERT statements are the natural way to write data in application code — one record arrives, one record goes in. For analytical databases that need to absorb millions or billions of rows on a schedule, that approach collapses under its own weight. Batch loading — ingesting large volumes of data in a single coordinated operation — is the dominant pattern for feeding data warehouses, and understanding why it is so much faster reveals a lot about how databases work internally. Why Not Just INSERT? A standard INSERT statement triggers a chain of work for every single row: Parse and plan the SQL statement Acquire row-level locks Write a log record (WAL entry) for the new row Locate a page with free space in the heap Write the row into that page Update all affected indexes Commit (fsync the log) Repeat this ten million times and the overhead dwarfs the actual data transfer. Parsing alone can consume more CPU than the data write. Bulk load bypasses most of this. A COPY command or bulk loader reads a prepared file, sorts it to match index order, writes data pages directly, and builds indexes in one pass at the end — skipping per-row logging and lock acquisition entirely. Method Rows/second (typical) Notes INSERT (single row) ~1 000 Full transaction overhead per row INSERT (multi-row VALUES) ~50 000 Amortizes parse cost COPY / bulk load ~500 000–5 000 000 Bypasses row-by-row logging Parallel bulk load 5 000 000+ Multiple files loaded concurrently Numbers vary wildly by hardware and table complexity, but the order-of-magnitude difference is consistent. ETL vs ELT Two dominant patterns exist for getting data from source systems into a warehouse: ETL — Extract, Transform, Load Data is extracted from source systems, transformed in a separate processing layer (cleaning, joining, reshaping), and then loaded into the warehouse in its final form. The transformation happens outside the warehouse. ELT — Extract, Load, Transform Raw data is loaded into the warehouse first (into staging tables), and the transformation runs inside the warehouse using SQL. Modern columnar warehouses are so fast at SQL that this is often cheaper than building a separate processing cluster. Trend: ELT has grown in popularity with cloud warehouses (BigQuery, Snowflake, Redshift) because warehouse compute is elastic and cheap, while maintaining a separate Spark or Flink cluster adds operational complexity. The ETL Pipeline Classic ETL pipeline: data flows from source systems through extract, transform, and bulk-load stages before landing in the warehouse. COPY and Bulk Load APIs Most databases ship a dedicated bulk-load mechanism: Database Command / API PostgreSQL COPY table FROM '/path/file.csv' CSV MySQL LOAD DATA INFILE '/path/file.csv' BigQuery bq load --source_format=CSV ... Snowflake COPY INTO table FROM @stage/file.csv Redshift COPY table FROM 's3://bucket/prefix' IAM_ROLE '...' These commands share a common design: they read a prepared file (CSV, Parquet, ORC, JSON), validate or cast data types, and write directly to data pages — often in parallel across multiple CPU cores or nodes. Staging Tables A common pattern is the staging table — a temporary table that mirrors the target schema (or the raw source format) and receives the bulk load before transformation: -- Step 1: bulk load raw data into staging COPY raw_sales_staging FROM 's3://pipeline/2024-06-25/sales.csv' IAM_ROLE 'arn:aws:iam::123456789:role/redshift-s3-role' FORMAT CSV IGNOREHEADER 1; -- Step 2: transform and upsert into production table INSERT INTO fact_sales (date_key, product_key, store_key, sale_amount, quantity) SELECT d.date_key, p.product_key, s.store_key, stg.amount, stg.qty FROM raw_sales_staging stg JOIN dim_date d ON stg.sale_date = d.full_date JOIN dim_product p ON stg.sku = p.sku JOIN dim_store s ON stg.store_id = s.store_id; -- Step 3: truncate staging for next run TRUNCATE raw_sales_staging; Staging tables also serve as a safety net: if the transformation fails, the raw data is still there and can be reprocessed. Why Bulk Load Is Faster: The Mechanics 1. No Per-Row Write-Ahead Logging Standard INSERTs write a WAL record for every row before touching the data page. Bulk loaders bypass this — they write the data pages first, then optionally write a single summary log entry for the entire operation. On crash recovery, the entire load is either rolled back or redone as one unit. 2. Sorted File Ingestion When data arrives pre-sorted by the clustering key (for columnar stores) or the B-tree key (for indexes), the loader can write pages sequentially without random seeks. This is why preparing sorted Parquet files before loading into Redshift or BigQuery dramatically improves performance. 3. Deferred Index Building Indexes are disabled (or accumulate changes in a buffer) during the load, then built in one pass at the end. Building a B-tree index from scratch on sorted data is an O(n) scan; updating it row by row during insert is O(n log n) with heavy random I/O. 4. Compression-Aware Writes Columnar warehouses encode each column using the best compression algorithm (RLE, dictionary, delta) for that column's value distribution. Bulk loaders analyze the full column batch and choose the optimal encoding; per-row inserts cannot do this because they see one value at a time. Micro-batching and Streaming Ingestion Pure batch loading means data is available only after each batch completes — potentially hours of lag. Several hybrid approaches reduce latency: Micro-batching: Run the pipeline every 5–15 minutes instead of nightly. Still uses bulk load mechanics, just more frequently. Streaming insert APIs: Snowflake, BigQuery, and Redshift all offer streaming insert APIs that accept individual rows or small payloads with sub-second latency, but at lower throughput and higher cost than bulk load. Change Data Capture (CDC): Tail the source database's transaction log (binlog, WAL) and stream only changed rows into a message queue (Kafka), then batch-load from the queue into the warehouse. Most production pipelines combine approaches: CDC for low-latency data feeds, nightly bulk loads for historical backfill, and micro-batching for middle-tier latency requirements. Data Freshness Trade-off Batch loading is fundamentally a freshness vs. throughput trade-off: Strategy Freshness Throughput Complexity Nightly batch Hours old Very high Low Hourly micro-batch ~1 hour old High Medium Streaming insert Seconds old Medium High Real-time CDC Near real-time Medium Very high For most business intelligence use cases — dashboards refreshed daily, weekly reports — an hourly or nightly batch is entirely adequate and far simpler to operate. Interactive Example: Bulk Insert and Transformation Interactive SQL · Staging table and transformation pipeline Key Takeaways Batch loading is orders of magnitude faster than row-by-row INSERT because it bypasses per-row logging, defers index building, and writes sorted pages directly. ETL transforms data outside the warehouse; ELT loads raw data first and transforms inside the warehouse using SQL — ELT is increasingly preferred with cloud warehouses. The COPY command (or equivalent bulk load API) is the right tool for loading large datasets; it is not just syntactic sugar over INSERT. Staging tables receive the raw bulk load, then a SQL transformation pipeline moves clean data into the final schema. Data freshness is always a trade-off: nightly batches are simple and fast to operate; real-time streaming is complex and expensive. Micro-batching and CDC offer middle-ground strategies when hourly-or-better latency matters. ← Previous Star schemas and fact tables Next → Materialized views

title: Materialized views
text: Queries that aggregate millions of rows into a dashboard metric are expensive — but they tend to run repeatedly against largely the same data. A materialized view turns this pattern inside-out: instead of recomputing the aggregation on every query, the result is precomputed and stored on disk, then served like a regular table. The computation moves from query time to refresh time. That trade-off — faster reads, potentially stale results — defines when materialized views shine and when they create problems. Regular Views vs Materialized Views A regular (logical) view is nothing more than a named query stored in the catalog. Every time you query the view, the database substitutes the view definition and runs the underlying query from scratch. -- Regular view: recomputed every time it is queried CREATE VIEW daily_revenue AS SELECT sale_date, SUM(sale_amount) AS revenue FROM fact_sales GROUP BY sale_date; -- This SELECT triggers the full aggregation every time: SELECT * FROM daily_revenue WHERE sale_date = '2024-06-25'; A materialized view stores the result of that query as actual rows on disk. The query above runs once during refresh, and subsequent reads hit the precomputed table. -- Materialized view: result stored on disk CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW daily_revenue_mv AS SELECT sale_date, SUM(sale_amount) AS revenue FROM fact_sales GROUP BY sale_date; -- Refresh (recompute and store): REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW daily_revenue_mv; -- This SELECT now reads from the stored rows — very fast: SELECT * FROM daily_revenue_mv WHERE sale_date = '2024-06-25'; Query Path Comparison Two query paths: the slow path scans the base fact table and aggregates at query time; the fast path reads directly from the precomputed materialized view. Refresh happens asynchronously from the base table. When to Use Materialized Views Materialized views are most valuable when: The underlying query is expensive (aggregating millions of rows, joining large tables) The query is run frequently (dashboard loaded by dozens of users per minute) The data does not need to be perfectly current (stale by minutes or hours is acceptable) The result set is significantly smaller than the base data (a daily summary is tiny vs. billions of transaction rows) They are less useful when: Data changes constantly and staleness of even a few seconds is unacceptable The query is run only occasionally (the refresh cost outweighs the savings) The result set is nearly as large as the base data (storage cost with no real benefit) Refresh Strategies How and when a materialized view is updated defines its behavior: Strategy How it works Freshness Cost Manual refresh Operator runs REFRESH explicitly Depends on schedule Low (full recompute on demand) Scheduled refresh Cron job or warehouse scheduler triggers refresh Predictable lag Medium On-commit refresh Refreshed automatically when base table commits Near real-time High — refresh on every write Incremental refresh Only rows changed since last refresh are reprocessed Near real-time Low per refresh, complex to implement Incremental Refresh Incremental (also called differential ) refresh is the most sophisticated and most valuable strategy. Instead of recomputing the entire result, the system tracks which base rows changed and merges only those deltas into the materialized view. new_mv_result = old_mv_result + aggregations of newly inserted rows - aggregations of deleted rows + updated aggregations for modified rows This works cleanly for additive aggregates (SUM, COUNT). It is harder for non-additive aggregates (MEDIAN, PERCENTILE) because you cannot easily combine partial results. Support for incremental refresh varies by database: PostgreSQL: No incremental refresh built-in; full refresh only with REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW Oracle: Supports incremental refresh via materialized view logs Snowflake: Dynamic tables with automatic incremental refresh BigQuery: Materialized views with incremental updates for supported query shapes Query Rewriting Advanced optimizers do not require you to explicitly query the materialized view — they automatically rewrite queries against the base table to use the materialized view instead, if the view's precomputed result can satisfy the query. -- User writes this query against the base table: SELECT sale_date, SUM(sale_amount) AS revenue FROM fact_sales GROUP BY sale_date; -- Optimizer rewrites it to: SELECT sale_date, revenue FROM daily_revenue_mv; -- (because the materialized view covers exactly this aggregation) This is called query rewriting or transparent rewriting . It means application code does not need to know that materialized views exist — the optimizer routes the query transparently. BigQuery, Oracle, and Snowflake support this; PostgreSQL does not (you must query the view by name). Materialized Views in Practice Dashboard Acceleration A BI dashboard that shows "revenue by region by week" aggregates facts constantly. Instead of scanning the fact table on every page load: CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW weekly_revenue_by_region AS SELECT d.year, d.week_number, s.region, SUM(f.sale_amount) AS revenue, COUNT(*) AS transactions FROM fact_sales f JOIN dim_date d ON f.date_key = d.date_key JOIN dim_store s ON f.store_key = s.store_key GROUP BY d.year, d.week_number, s.region; Refresh nightly. The dashboard reads millisecond-level queries instead of multi-second scans. Pre-aggregation Layers Data warehouses often layer multiple materialized views: fact_sales (base, billions of rows) └── mv_daily_product_sales (daily grain, millions of rows) └── mv_weekly_category (weekly grain, thousands of rows) └── mv_monthly_summary (monthly grain, hundreds of rows) Upper layers build on lower layers, each refresh being faster because the input is already smaller. Staleness and Correctness The fundamental risk of materialized views is stale data . A dashboard showing last night's numbers as if they were live can mislead decision-makers. Mitigations: Display the refresh timestamp clearly in every dashboard using a materialized view Set user expectations — label dashboards "Updated hourly" or "As of 06:00 UTC" Alert on refresh failures — a stale view that hasn't refreshed in 48 hours is a bug, not a feature Use on-commit refresh selectively only for small, critical aggregates where staleness is unacceptable Interactive Example: Creating and Querying a Materialized View Interactive SQL · Materialized view aggregation and query Key Takeaways A regular view is a stored query — recomputed every time it is queried with no performance benefit over writing the query inline. A materialized view stores the precomputed result on disk; reads are fast because they scan a small, pre-aggregated table instead of billions of base rows. Refresh strategies range from manual (lowest cost, maximum staleness) to on-commit (lowest staleness, highest write overhead) — incremental refresh balances both. Query rewriting allows optimizers to transparently route queries to materialized views even when the user queries the base table. Materialized views excel for expensive aggregations queried frequently — dashboard metrics, fixed KPI summaries, pre-aggregation layers. The central trade-off is speed vs. freshness : always surface the refresh timestamp to users so they understand when the data was last updated. Layered materialization (daily → weekly → monthly) amortizes refresh cost across multiple granularities. ← Previous Batch loading Next → Data lakes and lakehouses

title: Data lakes and lakehouses
text: The history of analytical data storage is a story of two opposing forces: the need to query data quickly and the need to store it cheaply. Data warehouses solved the speed problem by enforcing rigid schemas on expensive proprietary storage. Data lakes solved the cost problem by dumping everything raw into cheap object storage — and created a different set of headaches. The lakehouse is the current attempt to have both. The Data Warehouse: Fast but Rigid A traditional data warehouse (Redshift, Snowflake, BigQuery, Teradata) stores data in a highly curated, transformed state. Raw operational data is cleaned, typed, and loaded through an ETL pipeline before analysts ever see it. Property Data Warehouse Schema Enforced at write time (schema-on-write) Storage format Proprietary columnar (or managed Parquet) Storage cost High — often $200–$500/TB/month Query latency Fast — seconds to minutes Data types Structured only Flexibility Low — schema changes require migrations The warehouse excels for known, recurring queries: weekly revenue reports, monthly cohort dashboards, quarterly regulatory filings. But it struggles when: You want to store semi-structured or unstructured data (JSON logs, images, ML training data) Schema requirements change frequently Raw data volume is enormous and you only query a fraction of it You need to keep data for years at minimal cost The Data Lake: Cheap but Chaotic The data lake emerged around 2010–2014, driven by Hadoop and later cloud object storage (Amazon S3, Google Cloud Storage, Azure Blob). The idea: land everything raw, figure out structure later . s3://my-company-lake/ events/year=2024/month=01/day=15/part-00001.json.gz events/year=2024/month=01/day=15/part-00002.json.gz clickstream/2024-01-15T00:00:00.parquet ml-models/churn/v3/model.pkl raw-postgres-dumps/orders/2024-01-15.csv.gz Schema-on-read : the structure is imposed only when you run a query. You can store JSON blobs, CSV, Avro, Parquet — anything. A Spark or Hive job reads the files and applies a schema at query time. Why Data Lakes Became "Data Swamps" The flexibility that made lakes appealing also made them dangerous: No ACID transactions : two writers can corrupt the same partition simultaneously. A failed job leaves partial data. No schema enforcement : a pipeline change adds a new field, drops an old one, changes a type — downstream consumers silently break. Poor query performance : reading raw JSON or unoptimized Parquet across thousands of small files is slow. No statistics, no indexes. No time travel or versioning : once data is overwritten, the previous state is gone. Discovery nightmare : nobody knows what files are current, which are test data, which are backfills. Note: The term "data swamp" was coined to describe data lakes where governance broke down — nobody could find reliable, current data, and nobody trusted what they found. The Lakehouse: Merging Both Worlds The lakehouse architecture adds a metadata and transaction layer on top of cheap object storage, giving you warehouse-grade reliability without abandoning the lake's cost and flexibility benefits. Evolution from raw data lake and rigid data warehouse to the lakehouse architecture that combines the best of both. Open Table Formats: The Key Enabler The lakehouse idea works because of open table formats : metadata layers that sit on top of ordinary Parquet files in object storage and add transaction semantics. Delta Lake (Databricks) Delta Lake stores a _delta_log/ directory alongside Parquet data files. Each commit appends a JSON or Parquet checkpoint file listing which data files are part of the current table version. s3://my-bucket/orders/ _delta_log/ 00000000000000000000.json ← initial commit 00000000000000000001.json ← add rows 00000000000000000002.json ← delete rows 00000000000000000010.checkpoint.parquet part-00001-abc.parquet part-00002-def.parquet Apache Iceberg Iceberg uses a more sophisticated multi-level metadata tree: a catalog points to a metadata file , which points to manifest lists , which point to manifest files , which finally list individual data files. This structure enables: Partition evolution : change how data is partitioned without rewriting old files Hidden partitioning : users query by column value; Iceberg handles partition pruning transparently Row-level deletes : store delete vectors separately instead of rewriting entire files Apache Hudi Hudi (Hadoop Upserts Deletes and Incrementals) specializes in streaming upserts — efficiently applying CDC (change data capture) streams from operational databases into a lakehouse table. It maintains an index mapping record keys to file locations. What Time Travel Looks Like All three formats support time travel : querying a table as it existed at a previous point in time or transaction version. -- Delta Lake syntax SELECT * FROM orders VERSION AS OF 5; SELECT * FROM orders TIMESTAMP AS OF '2024-01-15 09:00:00'; -- Apache Iceberg syntax SELECT * FROM orders FOR VERSION AS OF 12345; SELECT * FROM orders FOR SYSTEM_TIME AS OF TIMESTAMP '2024-01-15 09:00:00'; This makes rollbacks, auditing, and reproducible ML training sets straightforward — capabilities that were impossible in a raw data lake. Schema Evolution Without Pain Traditional warehouses require ALTER TABLE migrations that lock tables and may fail on incompatible changes. Lakehouse formats handle common evolution patterns gracefully: Change type Handling Add nullable column Safe — old files return NULL for new column Rename column Tracked in metadata; old files still readable Change column type (widening) Safe — e.g., INT → BIGINT Change column type (narrowing) Blocked — e.g., BIGINT → INT would lose data Drop column Soft delete in metadata; old files unchanged Add partition New data uses new partition; old data unchanged The Storage Layer: Parquet All major lakehouse formats build on Apache Parquet , a columnar binary format. Key properties: Columnar layout : values for each column stored contiguously, enabling column pruning (only read the columns you need) Row groups : files split into row groups (~128 MB each), with per-column statistics (min, max, null count) stored in the footer Encoding : dictionary encoding, run-length encoding, and bit packing applied per column Compression : Snappy, ZSTD, or LZ4 applied per column chunk A query SELECT SUM(amount) FROM orders WHERE region = 'APAC' on a Parquet file can: Skip entire row groups where region statistics show no 'APAC' values (predicate pushdown) Read only the amount and region columns (column pruning) This can reduce I/O by 90%+ compared to reading raw CSV. Interactive SQL · Time travel with CTEs

title: Why analytics engines feel different
text: If you have used a transactional database — Postgres, MySQL, SQLite — and then sit down at a Snowflake console or a Spark shell, something feels off. Queries that should be "fast" take 20 seconds. Simple COUNT(*) commands trigger multi-second cluster activity. The mental model you built for OLTP does not transfer cleanly. This page explains why analytics engines are architected so differently, and what that means for how you use them. The Latency Contract Is Different The most fundamental difference is the expected response time . System type Acceptable query latency Why OLTP (Postgres, MySQL) < 10 ms for simple queries User is waiting at a UI; every 100 ms matters OLAP (Snowflake, BigQuery) Seconds to minutes Analyst runs a report; 30 seconds is fine Streaming (Flink, Kafka Streams) Milliseconds Per-event processing pipeline This difference in latency tolerance cascades through every design decision an analytics engine makes. When you're willing to wait 20 seconds, you can do things that would be unacceptable in an OLTP context: compile queries to native machine code, spin up dozens of worker processes, sort terabytes of data by shuffling it across a network. No Row-Level Locking Needed OLTP databases spend enormous effort on concurrency control : lock managers, MVCC version chains, deadlock detection. Analytics workloads are almost entirely read-only at query time . Analytical queries don't update individual rows — they aggregate millions of them. This means: No row-level locks No write-write conflicts to resolve Simpler isolation model — most analytics engines offer snapshot isolation at the query level, reading a consistent point-in-time view without holding any locks Writers (ETL jobs loading new data) operate separately, often with exclusive access to partitions they're writing The result is that analytics engines can devote their complexity budget to query execution rather than concurrency control. Massively Parallel Processing (MPP) The defining architectural feature of analytics engines is massively parallel processing . A single SQL query is automatically split across many nodes, each responsible for a subset of the data. MPP query fan-out: the coordinator distributes sub-queries to workers, each scans its own data partition independently, then partial results are merged at the coordinator. Shared-Nothing Architecture Each worker node in an MPP system has its own CPU, RAM, and local disk (or its own access to a slice of object storage). Nodes do not share memory or a storage bus. This design scales horizontally: double the nodes and you roughly double throughput for scan-heavy queries. Compare this to a single Postgres instance: all queries share one CPU pool, one buffer pool, one set of disk I/O channels. Shared-everything (OLTP) Shared-nothing (OLAP) ───────────────────────── ────────────────────────────── CPU / RAM Node1 Node2 Node3 │ CPU│RAM CPU│RAM CPU│RAM Shared disk Data1 Data2 Data3 Data Shuffles Not everything parallelizes perfectly. A JOIN between two large tables requires that matching rows land on the same worker node . The engine must shuffle rows across the network — redistributing data by join key before the join can execute. Shuffles are expensive: they saturate network bandwidth and introduce coordination overhead. Badly written queries that force large shuffles are a common cause of slow analytics jobs. Query Compilation vs. Interpretation Traditional databases interpret query plans at runtime: a tree of operator objects, each calling a next() method to pull one row at a time through the pipeline. This is flexible but slow — the CPU spends more time on virtual function dispatch overhead than on actual data work. Modern analytics engines take a different approach: Vectorized execution (DuckDB, ClickHouse, Velox): instead of processing one row at a time, process a batch of 1,024–8,192 rows per operator call. This: Amortizes function call overhead Enables SIMD (CPU vector instructions) to process multiple values in parallel Improves CPU cache utilization Full query compilation (Snowflake, HyPer, some Spark paths): generate native machine code specific to the query. The compiler emits tight loops without virtual dispatch. First-query latency is higher (compilation takes time), but execution is 2–10x faster for CPU-bound operations. Interpreted (row-at-a-time): Filter→Project→Agg each pulls 1 row Vectorized: Filter→Project→Agg each pulls 1024 rows Compiled: Fused tight loop in native machine code Result Caching Dashboards often run the same query repeatedly. Analytics engines implement multiple caching layers: Result cache : if the exact same SQL runs against unchanged data, return the cached result immediately — no execution at all. Snowflake and BigQuery both do this. Compiled plan cache : store the compiled query plan; skip re-optimization on repeated queries. Materialized views / pre-aggregated tables : manually or automatically pre-compute common aggregations. Note: Result caches are invalidated when underlying data changes. In a warehouse that loads data hourly, cache hit rates can exceed 90% for routine dashboard queries. Cost-Based Optimization: Higher Stakes In OLTP, query planning usually involves choosing the right index and join order for queries touching a few thousand rows. A wrong plan costs maybe 10x in extra work. In OLAP, query planning is existential . The difference between the right and wrong join order on billion-row tables can be the difference between a 2-minute query and a 3-hour query — or an out-of-memory failure. Analytics engines invest heavily in: Column statistics : track min, max, distinct count, histogram, and null fraction per column Table-level statistics : total row count, total byte size Partition statistics : which partitions contain which value ranges (enables partition pruning) Adaptive query execution : Spark and others re-optimize mid-query once actual row counts are known from earlier stages -- The optimizer must decide: which table to probe first? -- If orders has 10M rows and line_items has 500M rows, -- joining orders→products first (if products is tiny) saves a huge shuffle. SELECT p.category, SUM(li.amount) FROM line_items li JOIN orders o ON li.order_id = o.id JOIN products p ON li.product_id = p.id WHERE o.region = 'APAC' AND o.created_at >= '2024-01-01' GROUP BY p.category; Different Failure Modes Analytics queries fail differently from OLTP queries: Failure mode Cause Symptom Query timeout Query exceeded max runtime limit Error after 30 min; partial work discarded Memory spill Hash table or sort buffer exceeds RAM Query slows 10–100x as it writes to disk Skewed partitions One worker gets 80% of the data (e.g., NULL keys) 99th percentile worker becomes the bottleneck Shuffle OOM Redistribution step exceeds cluster memory Job fails; must be redesigned Quota exhaustion Slot or credit limits hit Query queued indefinitely or rejected Skewed partitions deserve special mention. If you join on a column where many rows share the same value (e.g., NULL , or a single viral user ID), one worker gets swamped while others idle. The fix is salting : add a random suffix to the join key, replicate the small side, and de-aggregate after. Concurrency Model Analytics engines are typically designed for low concurrency, high throughput per query — the inverse of OLTP systems. System Design target Postgres Thousands of concurrent short queries Snowflake Tens of concurrent long queries per virtual warehouse BigQuery Autoscales slots; concurrency managed by slot allocation DuckDB Single-process; one query at a time (excellent for local analysis) Running 500 concurrent analytical queries on a fixed cluster typically causes query queuing — queries wait for slot availability rather than running immediately. Dedicated compute clusters (virtual warehouses in Snowflake, reserved slots in BigQuery) are sized to match expected concurrency. Interactive SQL · GROUP BY aggregation (MPP fan-out simulation) Key Takeaways Analytics engines accept latencies of seconds to minutes — this unlocks architectural choices (compilation, MPP, large shuffles) that are impractical in OLTP. Shared-nothing MPP distributes data and compute across independent worker nodes, scaling scan throughput horizontally. Vectorized execution and query compilation replace row-at-a-time interpretation, leveraging SIMD and tight loops for CPU-bound work. Result caching means popular dashboard queries often return in milliseconds even from a "slow" warehouse. Cost-based optimization is critical at billion-row scale: a wrong join order can turn a 2-minute query into a multi-hour job. Common failure modes — skewed partitions, memory spill, shuffle OOM — have no equivalent in OLTP systems and require different debugging instincts. Analytics engines are optimized for low concurrency, high throughput — design your cluster size around expected concurrent query count, not raw CPU cores. ← Previous Data lakes and lakehouses Next → Good use cases

title: Good use cases
text: Knowing how an analytical database works is only half the picture. The other half is knowing when to reach for one — and when not to. Analytical databases and warehouses are powerful tools with a well-defined sweet spot: large volumes of historical data, read-heavy workloads, complex aggregations, and latency tolerance measured in seconds rather than milliseconds. This page maps the landscape of use cases and gives you a framework for making the decision. Business Intelligence and Dashboards The canonical use case. A BI tool (Tableau, Looker, Metabase, Power BI) issues pre-defined SQL queries that aggregate data across weeks or months of history. Typical patterns: Weekly sales trends : GROUP BY week, region, product_category over 52 weeks of order history Cohort analysis : group users by signup month, track their activity over subsequent months Funnel metrics : measure conversion rates at each step of a purchase or onboarding flow KPI dashboards : revenue, active users, churn rate, average order value — refreshed hourly or daily These queries are read-only, long-running, and repetitive . They run well in an analytical database because: Result caching makes repeated dashboard loads fast The queries are planned and optimized offline (not interactive) 5–30 second latency is acceptable; users aren't waiting at a transaction screen -- Typical BI query: weekly revenue trend by region SELECT DATE_TRUNC('week', order_date) AS week, region, SUM(revenue) AS total_revenue, COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id) AS unique_customers FROM orders WHERE order_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '1 year' GROUP BY 1, 2 ORDER BY 1, 2; Ad-Hoc Exploration Data scientists and analysts often need to explore data without a predefined question. They issue one-off queries: "how many users did X before doing Y?", "what's the distribution of session lengths?", "is there a correlation between feature usage and churn?" Ad-hoc workloads favor: Low barrier to query authorship : SQL or DataFrame APIs Fast iteration : query, see result, refine, repeat — 10–30 second turnarounds are fine Column-level access to raw events : not just pre-aggregated summaries Large data range : exploring months or years of raw event history Tools like DuckDB, BigQuery, and Athena are popular for ad-hoc work because they require no infrastructure — you point them at Parquet files and start querying. Financial Reporting End-of-period reporting has strict requirements: accuracy, auditability, and the ability to reproduce historical snapshots. Month/quarter/year-end aggregations : revenue recognition, expense summaries, balance sheet snapshots Regulatory reports : detailed breakdowns required by tax authorities or financial regulators Variance analysis : actual vs. budget, period-over-period comparison Multi-currency consolidation : convert subsidiary financials to reporting currency at period-end FX rates Lakehouses are well-suited here because time travel lets you reproduce the exact state of data at the close date — critical when an auditor asks "what did your books show on March 31 at 23:59?" Product Analytics Product teams track user behavior to understand how features are used and what drives retention. Feature adoption : what percentage of users have tried feature X within 30 days of signup? A/B test analysis : did users in the treatment group retain better after 14 days? Funnel drop-off : at which step of the onboarding flow do users abandon? User segmentation : which user attributes predict high lifetime value? These workloads generate very high event volumes (billions of rows per month for mid-sized products) but queries aggregate across the entire population. Analytical databases handle this well; OLTP databases do not. Machine Learning Feature Stores ML models consume features — derived attributes computed from raw data. Computing features at inference time (for each prediction request) is often too slow or too expensive. Instead, teams pre-compute features in batch using an analytical engine and serve them from a fast key-value store at inference time. Raw events (S3/GCS) │ ▼ Analytical query (Spark/BigQuery) ─ user_30d_purchase_count ─ user_avg_session_length_7d ─ user_days_since_last_login │ ▼ Feature store (Redis/DynamoDB) │ ▼ ML model inference (millisecond lookups) The analytical engine handles the heavy aggregation; the feature store handles the low-latency serving. This is the offline feature computation pattern used by most production ML systems. Log and Event Analytics Application logs, clickstreams, and IoT sensor data share a common profile: high write throughput, append-only, read-rarely-but-scan-much . Application log analysis : "how many 5xx errors occurred in the last hour, broken down by endpoint?" Clickstream analysis : "what pages did users visit before converting?" IoT sensor data : "what is the 99th percentile temperature reading per device per day?" Security event analysis : "show me all login attempts from unusual geolocations in the past week" ClickHouse, Apache Druid, and TimescaleDB are especially popular here because they optimize for time-series scans and high-cardinality GROUP BY queries on append-only data. When NOT to Use an Analytical Database This is as important as knowing when to use one. Workload Why analytics engines struggle Point lookups by primary key Columnar storage is slow for single-row reads; use Postgres or DynamoDB Frequent small writes Column stores batch-optimize writes; individual row inserts are inefficient Sub-millisecond latency No analytics engine achieves <1 ms; use a cache or OLTP DB Complex transactions (multi-table ACID) Most analytical engines lack row-level locking Heavy concurrent updates No concurrency control for writers; designed for bulk loads Small data (<1 GB) DuckDB or Postgres are faster; overhead of cluster setup is not justified Note: The boundary is blurring. Some systems like SingleStore (MemSQL) and YugabyteDB claim HTAP (Hybrid Transactional/Analytical Processing) — serving both workloads. But in practice, most organizations still separate OLTP and OLAP tiers. Decision Framework Workload characteristics matrix. Analytical databases belong in the lower-right quadrant: read-heavy workloads with complex, multi-table aggregations. Other quadrants are better served by OLTP, caches, or stream processors. Structured Decision Table Workload characteristic Recommended engine type Reads >> Writes; complex aggregations Analytical DB / Warehouse Mixed reads/writes; ACID required OLTP (Postgres, MySQL) Time-series, append-only, high cardinality ClickHouse, TimescaleDB, Druid Unstructured/semi-structured data Lakehouse (Iceberg, Delta) Sub-millisecond point lookups Key-value (DynamoDB, Redis) Full-text search Elasticsearch, OpenSearch Real-time streaming aggregations Flink, Kafka Streams, Druid Large-scale ML feature computation Spark, BigQuery Small data (<1 GB), local analysis DuckDB, SQLite Multi-model (graph + relational) ArangoDB, Amazon Neptune A Realistic BI Query: Cohort Retention A cohort retention query measures what percentage of users acquired in a given month are still active N months later. This is a staple of product analytics that requires analytical horsepower. Interactive SQL · Cohort retention funnel Sizing Guidance Rough rules of thumb for deciding which analytical tool fits: Data size Recommended approach < 1 GB DuckDB locally; no cluster needed 1 GB – 100 GB DuckDB, Postgres with columnar extension, or a small Snowflake/BQ slot 100 GB – 10 TB Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift, ClickHouse > 10 TB Spark on a lakehouse (Delta/Iceberg), BigQuery with reservation, ClickHouse cluster The biggest mistake teams make is reaching for a distributed system before they need it. DuckDB can query 50 GB of Parquet files on a laptop in under a second. Use the simplest tool that fits. Key Takeaways BI dashboards, ad-hoc exploration, financial reporting, product analytics, ML feature computation, and log analysis are all strong fits for analytical databases. Real-time transactional workloads, sub-millisecond latency requirements, and frequent small writes are poor fits — use an OLTP or streaming system instead. The core signal is the read/write ratio and query complexity . High read ratio + complex multi-table aggregations = analytical DB territory. Data size matters : DuckDB handles up to ~100 GB comfortably; only reach for distributed systems when you genuinely need them. Lakehouses add auditability (time travel) that is uniquely valuable for financial reporting and reproducible ML experiments. When in doubt, prototype with DuckDB against a sample of your data first — if it fits, you've saved yourself enormous operational complexity. ← Previous Why analytics engines feel different Next → Chapter 22 Summary: What We Learned

title: Chapter 22 Summary: What We Learned
text: Page Topic Takeaway in One Sentence 211 OLTP vs OLAP OLTP = many small, fast transactions (order entry). OLAP = few large, complex queries (business reporting). Different engines for each. 212 Columnar storage Values for each column are stored together — scanning a few columns on billions of rows reads only relevant data. 213 Compression and vectorized execution Columnar data compresses extremely well (run-length, dictionary, delta); the engine processes batches of values with SIMD instructions. 214 Star schemas and fact tables A fact table contains measurements (sales, clicks); dimension tables describe the context (time, product, customer). Querying is straightforward. 215 Batch loading Analytical databases are optimized for bulk ingestion (ETL/ELT pipelines), not real-time row-by-row inserts. 216 Materialized views Pre-computed query results that are updated incrementally or on refresh — turn hours-long queries into sub-second lookups. 217 Data lakes and lakehouses Data lakes store raw files (Parquet, Iceberg) in object storage; lakehouses add ACID transactions and SQL on top. 218 Why analytics engines feel different No indexes, no single-row updates, massive parallelism, different SQL dialects — they are built for scanning, not seeking. 219 Good use cases Business intelligence dashboards, periodic reports, machine learning feature engineering, log analysis, financial reconciliation. Reveal: How do these ideas connect to the next chapter? Chapter 22 closed Part V — you have now surveyed relational, key-value, document, column-family, graph, and analytical databases. You know what each one does best. Chapter 23 opens Part VI: Engineering Practice, starting with the question every team eventually faces: how do you make it fast? You will learn to diagnose bottlenecks, choose the right indexes, apply caching and denormalization, and separate OLTP from OLAP workloads. The database families you studied each have their own performance tuning playbook. ← Previous Good use cases Next → Latency, throughput, and concurrency

title: Latency, throughput, and concurrency
text: Performance conversations collapse quickly into three intertwined numbers: latency , throughput , and concurrency . Confuse them and you'll optimise the wrong thing — reducing latency when you need more throughput, or raising concurrency when the bottleneck is single-thread speed. Getting precise about each term is the foundation of every performance decision in this chapter. Latency: How Long One Request Takes Latency is the elapsed time between a client sending a request and receiving the final byte of the response. It is measured per-request and usually reported as a distribution, not a mean. The mean hides the tail. A query that averages 5 ms but has a 99th-percentile of 800 ms will feel broken to 1 in 100 users. Database engineers track: Percentile Common shorthand Why it matters p50 median Typical experience p95 — Most users' worst case p99 "nines" SLA boundary in most contracts p99.9 p999 Worst-case under load Latency Budget Every user-facing request has a budget — say 200 ms. That budget is split across network hops, application code, and database queries. A single slow query blows the whole budget. That's why even a "fast" 50 ms average query is unacceptable if it sits inside a budget of 100 ms. A 200 ms budget broken down by stage. The database and incidental N+1 queries consume more than half. Throughput: How Many Requests Per Second Throughput is the rate of completed work — requests per second, rows read per second, transactions per minute. It is a system-level property, not a per-request one. Latency and throughput are related but not the same: A system can have low latency and low throughput — each query is fast but the server handles only one at a time (no concurrency). A system can have high throughput and high latency — a batch ETL job processes millions of rows per second but each individual query takes seconds. The relationship is approximated by Little's Law : Throughput = Concurrency / Latency (requests/second = concurrent requests / seconds per request) If your database handles 50 concurrent queries and each takes 0.1 s, throughput is 500 req/s. To double throughput you can either halve latency or double concurrency — but concurrency has a ceiling set by your hardware. Concurrency: How Much Work Happens in Parallel Concurrency is the number of requests in flight simultaneously. The database manages concurrency through: Connection pooling — reusing a fixed set of connections rather than opening a new one per request. Thread / worker pools — each connection typically maps to an OS thread or async task. Lock management — concurrent writers must be serialised on the same row; concurrent readers can proceed in parallel under MVCC. The Concurrency Ceiling Every system has a point where adding more concurrency hurts throughput. Past that point, the overhead of context-switching, lock contention, and cache thrashing grows faster than the gain from parallelism. Profiling tools expose this as CPU saturation or lock-wait time climbing steeply. Interactive SQL · Latency Distribution Putting It Together Metric Unit Tuning lever Latency ms per request Indexes, query rewrites, caching Throughput requests / second Horizontal scaling, pooling, batching Concurrency simultaneous requests Pool size, async I/O, partitioning Key takeaway: Always measure latency as a distribution (p95/p99), not a mean. Use Little's Law to reason about throughput vs concurrency trade-offs. A fast-average, slow-tail query is a production incident waiting to happen. The next pages in this chapter translate these principles into concrete design decisions — choosing indexes, avoiding joins, caching, and batching — each of which shifts one or more of these three numbers in a targeted way. ← Previous Good use cases Next → Read-heavy vs write-heavy workloads

title: Read-heavy vs write-heavy workloads
text: Almost every performance decision in database engineering starts with a single question: is this workload read-heavy or write-heavy? The two cases have opposite bottlenecks, opposite tuning strategies, and — if you pick the wrong storage engine or replication topology — opposite failure modes. Answering the question early shapes every choice that follows. Defining the Terms A workload is read-heavy when SELECT queries dominate. The database spends most of its time retrieving pages from disk (or cache), evaluating predicates, and streaming results. E-commerce product pages, analytics dashboards, and content feeds are canonical examples. A workload is write-heavy when INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE operations dominate. The database spends most of its time maintaining indexes, writing WAL (write-ahead log) entries, and flushing dirty pages. Time-series telemetry, event ingestion, and financial ledgers are canonical examples. Most real systems are a mixture , but the dominant pattern dictates the architecture. Read-heavy and write-heavy workloads have opposite bottlenecks and opposite tuning levers. Why Indexes Hurt Writes Every index is a secondary copy of data that must be updated on every write. A table with five indexes on a write-heavy workload pays for six writes (the heap + five index updates) for every one logical row change. Under sustained insert throughput, this multiplier is the difference between a system that keeps up and one that falls behind. LSM-trees (used by Cassandra, RocksDB, ScyllaDB) flip this trade-off: writes go to an in-memory buffer and are merged and sorted on disk later. Read latency increases because the engine must merge multiple sorted runs, but write throughput can be an order of magnitude higher than a B-tree under the same hardware. B-trees (PostgreSQL, MySQL InnoDB) favour reads: the sorted structure means point lookups and range scans are a single traversal. Writes pay the cost of re-balancing pages in place. The Index Count Rule of Thumb Read-heavy table: index everything you query Write-heavy table: index only what you cannot afford to scan Replication Topology Choices Strategy Fits Drawback Primary + read replicas Read-heavy; distribute SELECTs Replication lag; writes still single-node Multi-primary Write-heavy, geographically distributed Conflict resolution complexity Sharding Write-heavy, high cardinality key Cross-shard queries become expensive Single primary, write-optimised storage Write-heavy, single region No horizontal write scaling Measuring the Ratio Before choosing an architecture, measure. Most databases expose counters: -- PostgreSQL: rows read vs rows inserted/updated/deleted SELECT relname, seq_tup_read AS rows_scanned, n_tup_ins AS inserts, n_tup_upd AS updates, n_tup_del AS deletes FROM pg_stat_user_tables ORDER BY (n_tup_ins + n_tup_upd + n_tup_del) DESC; SQLite does not expose cumulative read/write operation counters like PostgreSQL's pg_stat_user_tables . The sqlite_stat1 table (populated by ANALYZE ) stores query planner statistics about index selectivity — not cumulative row operation counts. For operation-level monitoring in SQLite, consider application-layer instrumentation or the SQLITE_DBSTATUS options in the C API. Use the widget below to simulate checking operation ratios on a small dataset. Interactive SQL · Estimating Read/Write Ratio Mixed Workloads: HTAP Some systems run HTAP (Hybrid Transactional/Analytical Processing) — heavy writes from an online application and heavy analytical reads from a reporting layer simultaneously. The common architectures are: Separate stores — writes go to an OLTP database; a nightly or streaming ETL moves data to a column-store (e.g., BigQuery, Redshift) for analytics. In-engine column stores — MySQL HeatWave, SQL Server's in-memory columnstore index, Oracle Dual Format — maintain a row store for writes and a column store for reads in the same engine. Change Data Capture — a CDC stream (Debezium, AWS DMS) replicates changes in near-real-time to an analytics store without a batch window. Key takeaways: Index count, storage engine choice, and replication topology all flip depending on whether your workload is read-heavy or write-heavy. Measure the actual ratio before choosing an architecture. The next page, "Access patterns first," shows how to capture that measurement systematically before writing a single DDL statement. ← Previous Latency, throughput, and concurrency Next → Access patterns first

title: Access patterns first
text: The most expensive database performance problems are not caused by missing indexes or bad SQL — they are caused by designing a schema before understanding how the application will actually query it. Access patterns first is the discipline of enumerating every query the application needs to make, before writing any DDL, and letting those patterns drive schema and index design. What an Access Pattern Is An access pattern is a precise description of one query the system will issue: Which table(s) are involved? What is the filter predicate? (equality, range, full-text, geospatial?) What columns are projected (SELECT'd)? What order is required? What volume and frequency? (10 req/s vs 10,000 req/s changes everything) What consistency is required? (Can it be slightly stale from a replica?) Write these down as a table before the first CREATE TABLE . This sounds like extra ceremony, but it costs 30 minutes to catalogue access patterns up front and weeks to re-schema a production table later. Access patterns drive schema, which drives index selection, which drives query plans. Profiling closes the loop. A Practical Access Pattern Catalogue Here is a worked example for an e-commerce order system: # Pattern Filter Projection Frequency Notes A1 Customer order history customer_id = order_id, total, status, created_at High (every page load) Needs index on customer_id A2 Order detail order_id = all columns High PK lookup, already fast A3 Pending orders by age status = 'pending' AND created_at < order_id, created_at Medium Composite index on (status, created_at) A4 Revenue by month aggregate over all rows SUM(total) Low (nightly) Table scan OK; or a materialized view A5 Search by product name full-text on line_items order_id, product Low-medium Full-text index or search service Pattern A4 is a full scan that runs nightly — it does not need an index. Indexing it would hurt write performance for no benefit. That is the conclusion you can only reach if you catalogued frequencies up front. Schema-First vs Pattern-First Schema-first (common mistake): CREATE TABLE orders (...) -- later, queries are slow -- add indexes reactively -- discover columns are missing or misnamed -- migration on a live table with millions of rows Pattern-first (correct approach): List all queries first Derive the tables and columns needed Choose indexes that serve the highest-frequency patterns CREATE TABLE orders (...) -- schema already fits the workload Denormalization Driven by Patterns Sometimes the pattern catalogue reveals that a join is required on every single request. That's the signal to denormalize : copy a column from the joined table into the primary table so the join disappears. This is a conscious, documented trade-off, not sloppiness — you're choosing read speed over write simplicity because the access pattern demands it. Interactive Example The widget below simulates an access pattern audit: checking which queries are run most often against an order table, then verifying that the right index supports them. Interactive SQL · Access Pattern Audit When Patterns Change Access patterns change as products grow. A reporting query that ran weekly at launch might become a real-time dashboard queried every second at scale. Build a lightweight process: Log slow queries (pg_stat_statements, MySQL slow query log, SQLite EXPLAIN). Compare actual patterns against the original catalogue monthly. Add, remove, or reshape indexes based on the delta. Key takeaway: Schema and index design are downstream of access patterns, not the other way around. Write down every query — filter, projection, order, and frequency — before the first CREATE TABLE . That document is worth more than any post-hoc tuning session. ← Previous Read-heavy vs write-heavy workloads Next → Picking the right indexes

title: Picking the right indexes
text: An index is a trade-off, not a free win. Every index you add speeds up the queries that use it and slows down every write to the table. Picking the right indexes means matching index type and column order to the specific query patterns your application needs — not indexing everything and hoping for the best. How Indexes Work (Briefly) A B-tree index stores column values in sorted order with pointers back to heap rows. Lookups are O(log n) — far better than a sequential scan for large tables. But the tree must be updated on every INSERT, UPDATE (if the indexed column changes), and DELETE. A table with ten B-tree indexes pays ten tree-update costs per write. An index scan is fast when it is selective — when the indexed condition filters most rows out. If a query returns 40 % of the table, the planner will often prefer a sequential scan over an index scan because random I/O to follow index pointers is more expensive than reading pages sequentially. Selectivity: The Most Overlooked Factor Selectivity = fraction of rows returned by the predicate. High selectivity = few rows returned = index is valuable. Column Distinct values Selectivity Index useful? order_id (PK) 10 million ~0.000001 Yes — point lookup status (3 values) 3 ~0.33 Usually no — low selectivity email ~10 million ~0.000001 Yes — login lookup country ~200 ~0.005 Maybe — depends on distribution Rule: Do not index low-cardinality columns in isolation. If status has only three values, indexing it alone is rarely worth the write overhead — the planner will do a sequential scan anyway. Combine it in a composite index where the leading column is selective. A B-tree index. Point lookups follow root → internal → leaf in O(log n). Range scans traverse leaf pages in order. Composite Indexes and Column Order A composite index on (a, b, c) can serve queries that filter on a , on (a, b) , or on (a, b, c) . It cannot efficiently serve a query that filters only on b or only on c — the left-most prefix rule. -- Composite index: (status, created_at) CREATE INDEX idx_orders_status_date ON orders(status, created_at); -- Uses the index (leading column present) SELECT * FROM orders WHERE status = 'pending' AND created_at > '2024-01-01'; -- Uses only the first column of the index SELECT * FROM orders WHERE status = 'shipped'; -- Does NOT efficiently use this index (skips leading column) SELECT * FROM orders WHERE created_at > '2024-01-01'; Put the equality column first, range column second. This lets the engine seek directly to the matching status value and then scan only the relevant date range within that partition of the index. Covering Indexes A covering index includes all columns the query needs — so the engine never touches the heap. This is the fastest possible index lookup because it eliminates the second I/O (the "heap fetch" or "bookmark lookup"). -- If this query runs millions of times per day: SELECT customer_id, total FROM orders WHERE status = 'pending'; -- A covering index makes it heap-free: CREATE INDEX idx_orders_pending_cover ON orders(status, customer_id, total); Partial Indexes A partial index indexes only rows matching a condition. Smaller index, faster lookups, lower write overhead: -- Only index unprocessed events — the hot minority CREATE INDEX idx_events_unprocessed ON events(created_at) WHERE processed = 0; Interactive Example Interactive SQL · Index Impact Indexes to Avoid Anti-pattern Problem Index on every column Writes pay N update costs; planner confusion Index on a boolean (true/false) Only 2 values — selectivity near 50 %, rarely useful Duplicate indexes Same columns in same order — pure overhead Unused indexes Write cost with no read benefit; check pg_stat_user_indexes Function-wrapped column in WHERE WHERE LOWER(email) = ? can't use an index on email without a function index Key takeaways: Match index type to query pattern. Put equality predicates before range predicates in composite indexes. Use covering indexes for hot queries. Use partial indexes to exclude cold rows. Audit unused indexes regularly — they are pure write overhead. ← Previous Access patterns first Next → Avoiding unnecessary joins

title: Avoiding unnecessary joins
text: Joins are one of the most powerful features of relational databases — and one of the most common sources of performance problems. The issue is rarely that joins are slow in principle; it is that unnecessary joins compound, hide behind ORMs, and make a query plan that should touch one table touch five. Understanding when to join, when to denormalize, and when to pre-compute transforms reactive firefighting into intentional design. Why Joins Cost What They Cost When two tables are joined, the database must match rows from each side. The planner chooses an algorithm: Algorithm Best when Cost Nested loop Small outer set + indexed inner O(n log m) with index Hash join Large unsorted tables O(n + m), high memory Merge join Both sides already sorted O(n + m), low memory The problem is not the algorithm — it is that each join multiplies the I/O and CPU. Three joins on 1 M-row tables can mean scanning tens of millions of logical rows even with good indexes. A nested-loop join: each outer row probes the inner table via an index. Fast when the outer set is small; expensive when N is large. The N+1 Query Problem The most common ORM-induced join disaster is the N+1 problem: loading a list of N records and then executing one query per record to fetch related data, because the developer forgot to eager-load the association. # N+1: loads 100 orders, then 100 separate customer queries orders = Order.all() # 1 query for order in orders: print(order.customer.name) # N queries # Fixed: a single join or eager load orders = Order.includes(:customer).all() # 1 query with join The N+1 problem looks trivial in development (small dataset, fast) and catastrophic in production (large dataset, slow + high connection pressure). When to Denormalize Normalization is a correctness tool. Denormalization is a performance tool. Use denormalization when: The join is on the critical path of a high-frequency query. The denormalized column is read far more than it is updated . The join crosses a network boundary (microservices, sharded databases). -- Normalized: must join orders + customers for every page load SELECT o.id, c.name, c.email FROM orders o JOIN customers c ON c.id = o.customer_id; -- Denormalized: customer_name copied into orders at insert time SELECT id, customer_name, customer_email FROM orders; -- Now a single-table lookup — no join at runtime Document the denormalization. Write the update trigger or application logic that keeps the copied column current. Denormalization that is not maintained becomes corrupt data. Avoiding Joins at Query Time: Materialized Views A materialized view pre-computes a join and stores the result. Reads hit the materialized result (fast). A refresh job or trigger updates it when source data changes. -- PostgreSQL: pre-compute the join nightly CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW order_summary AS SELECT o.id, o.total, c.name AS customer_name FROM orders o JOIN customers c ON c.id = o.customer_id; REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW order_summary; -- run on schedule SQLite does not have materialized views but you can simulate them with a regular table refreshed by application code. Interactive Example Interactive SQL · Join vs Denormalized Lookup When Joins Are Fine Do not over-apply this advice. Joins are appropriate when: The joined table is small (fits in the buffer pool / hash table). The join is infrequent (batch reports, admin queries). You need consistency — a denormalized copy can drift. The planner can use a good index on the join key. Key takeaways: Joins are powerful but compound. Audit your ORM queries for N+1 patterns. Denormalize hot join paths when the column is read far more than updated. Use materialized views to pre-compute expensive joins for reporting. Never denormalize without documenting how the copy is kept in sync. ← Previous Picking the right indexes Next → Caching and invalidation

title: Caching and invalidation
text: Caching is the most effective single lever for reducing database load. A well-placed cache turns a 10 ms database query into a sub-millisecond memory read, and it absorbs traffic spikes that would otherwise overwhelm the database. But caching introduces a second copy of data — and cache invalidation , keeping that copy accurate, is where most caching bugs live. The Cache Hierarchy Modern applications layer multiple caches between the user and the database. Each layer adds speed and adds a staleness risk: Every hop from browser to disk adds latency. Application-level caches (Redis) are the most common tuning point for database load. Cache Patterns Cache-Aside (Lazy Loading) The most common pattern. The application checks the cache first; on a miss, it reads from the database and populates the cache. def get_user(user_id): cached = redis.get(f"user:{user_id}") if cached: return deserialize(cached) # cache hit — no DB touch user = db.query("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ?", user_id) redis.setex(f"user:{user_id}", 300, serialize(user)) # TTL = 5 min return user Pros: Only caches what's actually requested. DB is the source of truth. Cons: Cold start is slow. First request after expiry (or after a deploy) always misses. Write-Through The application writes to the cache and the database at the same time. Cache is never stale — but writes are slower. def update_user(user_id, data): db.execute("UPDATE users SET name = ? WHERE id = ?", data['name'], user_id) redis.setex(f"user:{user_id}", 300, serialize(data)) # write cache too Write-Behind (Write-Back) The application writes to the cache first; an async job flushes to the database later. Very fast writes, but risks data loss on crash. The Hard Part: Invalidation Phil Karlton's famous observation: "There are only two hard things in computer science: cache invalidation and naming things." Cache invalidation fails in three ways: Failure mode Description Mitigation Stale reads Cache returns an old value after a write Short TTLs; event-driven invalidation Cache stampede TTL expires; many requests hit the DB simultaneously Probabilistic early expiry; locks; background refresh Phantom invalidation Invalidating a key that doesn't correspond to what changed Granular keys; structured key naming Event-Driven Invalidation Instead of relying on TTLs alone, publish an event when data changes and have consumers delete or update the relevant cache keys: [Write to orders table] → publish OrderUpdated event → consumer deletes "order:1234" from Redis → consumer deletes "customer:101:recent_orders" from Redis This keeps cache fresh without waiting for TTL expiry, but requires disciplined event publishing on every write path. Interactive Example Interactive SQL · Cache TTL Simulation What Not to Cache Type of data Why caching is risky Financial balances Stale balance can lead to double-spend Security tokens / permissions Stale permission = authorization bypass Counters with strict correctness Race conditions in cache increment ≠ DB increment Very small / fast queries Cache overhead exceeds query cost Cache Hit Rate Track your cache hit rate = hits / (hits + misses). A well-tuned cache should hit 90–99 % for stable data. If hit rate is below 70 %, the TTL is too short, keys are too granular, or the workload is too random for caching to help. Key takeaways: Cache-aside is the safest starting pattern. Use short TTLs as a safety net; use event-driven invalidation for correctness. Track hit rate — it tells you if the cache is actually working. Never cache financial or security-critical data that must be authoritative. ← Previous Avoiding unnecessary joins Next → Batch work vs online work

title: Batch work vs online work
text: Every database operation is either online (serving a user request right now, latency matters) or batch (processing a large volume of work in the background, throughput matters). Mixing the two in the same query path is one of the most reliable ways to degrade user-facing performance. Separating them is one of the most effective — and underused — performance patterns in database engineering. Defining the Boundary Online work is synchronous and user-facing. A user clicked a button; they are waiting for a response. Latency SLAs apply — typically under 200 ms, often under 50 ms. Batch work is asynchronous and infrastructure-facing. No user is blocked. The goal is to process the most data in the least time. Throughput matters; latency does not. The same database operation can be online or batch depending on context: Operation Online context Batch context SELECT SUM(total) Revenue widget on every page load → problem Nightly report job → fine UPDATE SET status = Mark one order shipped (user action) → fine Bulk-expire 500k sessions → problem INSERT One order checkout → fine ETL loading 10M rows → problem Why Batch Work Hurts Online Users When a heavy batch job runs alongside an OLTP workload, it competes for: Buffer pool / page cache — the batch evicts hot OLTP pages, causing cache misses. I/O bandwidth — sequential batch reads saturate disk, increasing latency for random OLTP reads. Lock contention — a batch UPDATE that locks millions of rows blocks concurrent writers. CPU — aggressive batch processing saturates query threads, queuing online queries. The result is a predictable pattern: user-facing latency spikes during ETL windows, nightly aggregation jobs, or bulk export operations. Online latency spikes during the batch window (02:00–04:00). Batch and OLTP workloads compete for buffer pool, I/O, and CPU. Separation Strategies 1. Time-Based Separation Schedule batch jobs during off-peak hours. Simple, effective for predictable low-traffic windows. Fails when traffic is global and there is no quiet window. 2. Read Replica Routing Run batch reads against a read replica, not the primary. This removes I/O pressure from the primary entirely. Works well for reporting and export jobs. -- Application config: route heavy reads to replica connection = db.connect(host='replica.db.internal') result = connection.execute("SELECT * FROM events WHERE ...") 3. Chunked Batch Processing A batch job that updates 10 million rows in one transaction holds locks and consumes resources for minutes. The same job processed in chunks of 1,000 rows releases locks between chunks, allowing online queries to proceed. -- Bad: one giant transaction UPDATE events SET processed = 1 WHERE processed = 0; -- locks millions of rows -- Better: chunked UPDATE events SET processed = 1 WHERE id IN ( SELECT id FROM events WHERE processed = 0 LIMIT 1000 ); -- repeat until done; sleep briefly between iterations 4. Outbox / Queue Pattern Instead of writing directly to the database in a batch loop, write to a queue (RabbitMQ, Kafka, SQS) and let workers consume at a controlled rate. The queue provides backpressure — if the database is under pressure, workers slow down automatically. 5. Dedicated Batch Store At scale, use a separate database optimised for batch workloads (columnar store, data warehouse) and replicate data from the OLTP store. Online queries never compete with batch queries because they run on different hardware.
