Interactive Example Interactive SQL · Chunked Batch Update Decision Guide Situation Approach Report that runs nightly, < 1h Schedule in off-peak window Report that must be near-real-time Materialized view or read replica Bulk update of millions of rows Chunk + sleep loop Event processing at high volume Queue + controlled worker pool Analytics over full dataset Separate column store / data warehouse Key takeaways: Never run batch work on the same resource path as online queries during peak traffic. Chunk large updates to release locks between batches. Route heavy reads to replicas. Use queues to decouple batch throughput from online availability. The boundary between online and batch is an architectural decision, not just a scheduling detail. ← Previous Caching and invalidation Next → Measuring before tuning

title: Measuring before tuning
text: The most common database performance mistake is not a bad index or a slow query — it is tuning without measuring . Engineers guess at the bottleneck based on intuition, add indexes, change settings, and rewrite queries, then measure after the fact to see if anything improved. This approach is slow, often counterproductive, and sometimes makes things worse. Measurement-first performance work is faster, more reliable, and produces changes you can actually explain. The Measurement-First Loop Observe → Measure → Hypothesize → Change ONE thing → Measure again → Compare Each step matters. Changing multiple things at once makes it impossible to attribute improvement or regression to a specific cause. A controlled, one-change-at-a-time approach is slower in the short term and dramatically faster when something goes wrong. Tools for Measurement EXPLAIN / EXPLAIN ANALYZE Every major database has a query plan inspection command. It shows how the planner intends to execute the query before running it, and what it actually did after. -- SQLite: see the query plan EXPLAIN SELECT o.id, c.name FROM orders o JOIN customers c ON c.id = o.customer_id WHERE o.status = 'pending'; Key things to look for: SCAN — sequential table scan (potentially slow on large tables) SEARCH using index — index was used (good) USING TEMP B-TREE — sorting required a sort buffer (may indicate missing index) Slow Query Logs Most databases can log queries that exceed a threshold. This gives you a ranked list of queries worth investigating: -- PostgreSQL: see the top 10 slowest queries SELECT query, calls, ROUND(total_exec_time::numeric, 2) AS total_ms, ROUND(mean_exec_time::numeric, 2) AS mean_ms, ROUND(stddev_exec_time::numeric, 2) AS stddev_ms FROM pg_stat_statements ORDER BY total_exec_time DESC LIMIT 10; Note: pg_stat_statements must be enabled as an extension. It is the single most useful built-in tool for production query analysis in PostgreSQL. Execution Statistics Table-level statistics tell you which tables are hot and what kind of operations dominate. The measurement-first loop: observe, measure, hypothesize, change one thing, measure again. Never skip directly from observation to change. Reading EXPLAIN Output A simplified guide to the most important plan nodes: Node type What it means When it's a problem Seq Scan Reading every row in the table Large table, low selectivity predicate Index Scan Following an index to heap rows Almost always fine Index Only Scan No heap fetch needed (covering index) Best possible Hash Join Building a hash table to join High memory use on large inputs Nested Loop Per-row inner probe Dangerous without inner index Sort Explicit sort step Missing index on ORDER BY column Baselines and Benchmarks Before any change, capture a baseline : -- Run the target query 5 times, record wall-clock time -- Document: table row counts, index list, configuration settings After the change, run the same measurement and compare. A 30 % improvement on a 10 ms query saves 3 ms — which may or may not matter. A 30 % improvement on a 500 ms query saves 150 ms, which is the difference between meeting and blowing your SLA. Interactive Example Interactive SQL · Reading Query Plans Common Measurement Mistakes Mistake Effect Measuring only once Query plan cache or OS page cache skews result Measuring in development with tiny data Indexes not used below a threshold; plans differ Measuring at off-peak hours Different data distribution, connection pool pressure Changing multiple things at once Cannot attribute improvement Ignoring p99 latency Mean hides the tail; SLA is about the tail When to Stop Tuning Performance tuning has diminishing returns. Stop when: The query meets its SLA with headroom. Further improvement requires architectural changes that are out of scope. The cost of the next change (risk, complexity, time) exceeds the benefit. Key takeaways: Always establish a baseline before making changes. Use EXPLAIN to understand plans, not guess at them. Change one thing at a time and measure the delta. Track p95/p99 latency, not means. Stop when you meet the SLA — perfection is the enemy of shipped. ← Previous Batch work vs online work Next → Performance tradeoffs

title: Performance tradeoffs
text: Every performance decision is a trade-off. There is no configuration, architecture, or tuning choice that makes everything faster simultaneously. Understanding the space of trade-offs — which axes exist, what moves on each axis, and what you sacrifice when you pull a lever — is what separates engineers who can reason about performance from those who can only cargo-cult solutions. The Fundamental Axes Database performance trade-offs cluster around a small set of opposing forces: Six axes of performance trade-off. Every tuning decision moves you toward one end of at least one axis. Trade-off Catalogue Normalization vs Denormalization Normalized Denormalized Write complexity Low — update one place High — update N copies Read speed Slower — join required Faster — single table Storage Smaller Larger Correctness risk Low High (copies can drift) When to denormalize: High-frequency reads, stable data (names, prices), or when a join crosses a network boundary (microservices, sharded DB). More Indexes vs Fewer Indexes More indexes make reads faster and writes slower. Every index is an additional write on every INSERT, UPDATE to that column, or DELETE. The break-even point depends on the read/write ratio: Break-even: index is worth adding when (reads_saved × read_time_saved) > (writes × write_time_added) For a table with 1,000 reads/s and 10 writes/s: a single index that saves 5 ms per read saves 5,000 ms/s total. If each write costs an additional 0.5 ms per index, adding the index costs 5 ms/s — a 1,000× win. At 1,000 writes/s, the same math produces a 10× win. Still worth it. At 10,000 writes/s and 1,000 reads/s, the index costs 5,000 ms/s to maintain and saves 5,000 ms/s — break even. Add more indexes only if read savings dominate. Synchronous vs Asynchronous Replication Synchronous Asynchronous Durability Full — replica confirmed before commit returns Risk of data loss on primary failure Write latency Higher — waits for replica acknowledgement Lower — write returns before replica Availability Lower — replica slowness blocks writes Higher — replica lag doesn't block PostgreSQL's synchronous_commit = on guarantees durability; synchronous_commit = off or local trades durability for lower write latency. MVCC vs Locking MVCC Strict locking Readers block writers? No — reads see a snapshot Yes — read locks block writes Writers block readers? No Yes Storage overhead Higher — old versions retained Lower Best for Mixed read/write OLTP Simple single-writer workloads PostgreSQL and MySQL InnoDB use MVCC. SQLite uses a simpler writer-lock model. Vertical vs Horizontal Scaling Vertical (bigger machine) Horizontal (more machines) Simplicity High — no code change Low — sharding/routing complexity Cost efficiency Diminishing returns at high end Linear with load Failure blast radius High — single point of failure Low — partial failure only Consistency Trivial Hard — distributed transactions Start vertical. Add horizontal complexity only when the vertical ceiling is hit or the cost is prohibitive. Interactive Example: Trade-off Decision Interactive SQL · Index Trade-off Analysis The Meta Trade-off: Complexity vs Performance Every performance optimization adds complexity. A cache means two systems to keep in sync. Denormalization means update logic in multiple places. Sharding means cross-shard query routing. Async replication means eventual consistency handling in the application. Complexity is a cost that compounds over time — it slows future development, increases bug surface, and makes incidents harder to diagnose. A 10 % performance gain that doubles operational complexity is usually the wrong trade. The rule: prefer the simplest solution that meets your SLA. Only cross the complexity threshold when profiling proves you cannot meet the SLA any other way. Key takeaways: Every performance lever moves at least one axis in an unfavorable direction. Document your trade-offs explicitly — future engineers need to understand why a choice was made, not just what was chosen. Complexity is a cost. Meet your SLA with the simplest system that can do it; add complexity only when measurement proves it is necessary. ← Previous Measuring before tuning Next → Chapter 23 Summary: What We Learned

title: Chapter 23 Summary: What We Learned
text: Page Topic Takeaway in One Sentence 221 Latency, throughput, and concurrency Latency = time for one operation; throughput = operations per second; concurrency = number of simultaneous operations. Optimize for your bottleneck. 222 Read-heavy vs write-heavy workloads Read-heavy: optimize indexes, add read replicas, use caching. Write-heavy: optimize WAL, partition data, batch writes. 223 Access patterns first Design your schema and indexes around how your application actually queries the data — not around how the data looks. 224 Picking the right indexes Create indexes on columns used in WHERE, JOIN, and ORDER BY clauses. Avoid redundant indexes. Measure, don't guess. 225 Avoiding unnecessary joins Denormalize when reads are critical and writes are rare; use covering indexes to avoid table lookups. 226 Caching and invalidation Cache frequently read, rarely written data; invalidate or update the cache on write. The two hardest problems: naming and invalidation. 227 Batch work vs online work Move heavy computation (reports, backfills, aggregations) to batch windows; keep online transactions fast and predictable. 228 Measuring before tuning Use query logs, slow-query analyzers, and monitoring dashboards; optimize the queries that consume the most total time, not just the slowest. 229 Performance tradeoffs Indexes speed reads but slow writes. Caching adds complexity. Partitioning helps writes but complicates reads. No free lunch. Reveal: How do these ideas connect to the next chapter? Chapter 23 taught you to make databases fast. But a fast database that loses your data is worse than a slow one. Chapter 24 covers reliability: backups, point-in-time recovery, high availability through failover, and safe schema migrations. You will learn that an untested backup is not a backup at all, that chaos engineering reveals real failure modes, and that operational runbooks matter as much as architectural choices. Speed wins sprints; reliability wins marathons. ← Previous Performance tradeoffs Next → Backups and restores

title: Backups and restores
text: Every database will eventually face hardware failure, software bugs, accidental deletes, or ransomware. Backups are the last line of defence — not a nice-to-have, but a survival mechanism. This page covers how database backups work physically, the different types in common use, and how a restore actually plays out under pressure. Why Backups Fail (and Why You Still Need Them) The uncomfortable truth: many backup schemes feel solid but fail at restore time. Common causes include: Backup jobs that silently error out, leaving files empty or corrupt Files stored on the same disk or RAID array as the database Backups that capture a logically inconsistent state (e.g., half-written transaction) No tested restore procedure — the first restore attempt happens during an outage A backup is only as good as a verified restore. Test restores regularly , not just during emergencies. Types of Backups Logical vs. Physical Logical Physical What is copied SQL statements or row-level dumps Raw data files (pages, WAL segments) Tool examples pg_dump , mysqldump pg_basebackup , file copy, snapshot Restore speed Slow — SQL must re-execute Fast — files are placed directly Portability High — schema can be modified Low — engine version must match Consistency Depends on snapshot isolation Native page-level consistency Full, Incremental, and Differential Full backup: [=============] captures everything Incremental: [F] [i1] [i2] [i3] each captures only changes since previous Differential: [F] [d1] [d2] each captures changes since last full Full — simplest to restore; expensive in storage and time to produce Incremental — smallest per-backup size; restore requires chaining: full + every increment in order Differential — restore is full + the single latest differential; middle ground in storage cost Most production setups combine approaches: a weekly full + daily differentials + continuous WAL archiving for point-in-time granularity. A weekly full backup combined with daily incrementals and continuous WAL archiving provides point-in-time restore granularity down to the second. How a Restore Works Physical Restore (PostgreSQL example) Stop the database server Replace the data directory with the base backup files Create recovery.conf (Postgres ≤ 11) or postgresql.conf + recovery.signal (Postgres ≥ 12) pointing to the WAL archive and target time Start the server — it replays WAL forward from the backup's end LSN until the target Verify row counts, application smoke tests Promote to read-write mode The total time = (copy base backup) + (replay WAL segments). For a 500 GB database replaying 6 hours of WAL, that can be 2–4 hours. Size the RTO (recovery time objective) accordingly. Logical Restore (pg_dump) # Dump pg_dump -Fc -d mydb -f mydb.dump # Restore pg_restore -d mydb_new -j 4 mydb.dump # -j 4 = 4 parallel workers Logical restores must re-execute every INSERT. On large databases this can take many hours. Use physical backups for large databases; reserve logical dumps for smaller databases or partial restores (single table, single schema). Interactive: Simulating a Backup Audit Table Production teams track every backup job in a log table. The query below lets you explore backup coverage and spot gaps. Interactive SQL · Backup Audit Log Key Design Decisions Storage Location (the 3-2-1 Rule) Keep 3 copies, on 2 different media types, with 1 offsite. A backup on the same SAN as the primary is worthless if the SAN fails. Object storage (S3, GCS, Azure Blob) has become the standard offsite target: cheap, durable (11-nines), globally accessible, and easy to version. Encryption Backups often contain regulated data (PII, payment records). Encrypt at rest (AES-256) and in transit (TLS). Manage keys separately from the backups — an encrypted backup and key stored together is no better than plaintext. Retention Policy Category Typical retention Daily incrementals 7–30 days Weekly fulls 3–12 months Monthly fulls 1–7 years (compliance) WAL archives Keep until next verified full covers the period Key Takeaways A backup you have never restored is an untested hypothesis — schedule restore drills Physical backups (file copy + WAL) are faster to restore; logical dumps are more portable Combine full + incremental + continuous log archiving for sub-minute RPO Apply the 3-2-1 rule: 3 copies, 2 media, 1 offsite Encrypt backups and store keys separately; audit every backup job with a status table ← Previous Performance tradeoffs Next → Point-in-time recovery

title: Point-in-time recovery
text: A developer runs DELETE FROM orders; without a WHERE clause at 14:32 on a Tuesday. With a weekly backup, you lose up to six days of data. With point-in-time recovery (PITR) , you restore to 14:31:59 and lose nothing but 60 seconds. PITR is the capability that transforms a coarse backup strategy into a precise surgical tool — and understanding how it works is essential for any database reliability practice. How PITR Works All major relational databases maintain a write-ahead log (WAL) — a sequential record of every change made to the database, written before the change hits the data files. Crash recovery already relies on this log. PITR extends that mechanism for restore purposes: Take a base backup (a consistent snapshot of the data files at a known LSN/position) Archive WAL segments continuously to durable storage as they are filled At restore time, copy the base backup to a fresh server, then replay WAL forward from the backup's end position to any target point (timestamp, transaction ID, or named restore point) The database reaches exactly the state it was in at the chosen moment — every committed transaction before that point is present, every transaction after is absent. PITR replay: the base backup is copied to a fresh server, then WAL segments are fed forward until the engine reaches the target timestamp and halts. Configuring PITR in PostgreSQL Step 1 — Enable WAL archiving # postgresql.conf wal_level = replica # or logical archive_mode = on archive_command = 'aws s3 cp %p s3://my-wal-archive/%f' %p is the WAL segment path; %f is the filename. The archive command must return exit code 0 only when the file is safely stored. Step 2 — Take a base backup pg_basebackup -h localhost -U replicator -Ft -z -Xs -P \ -D /mnt/backups/base_$(date +%Y%m%d) -Xs streams WAL during the backup so no segments are missed. -Ft -z produces a compressed tar. Step 3 — Restore to a point in time # 1. Place base backup in data directory tar -xzf base_20240624.tar.gz -C /var/lib/postgresql/data # 2. Create recovery configuration (Postgres ≥ 12) cat >> /var/lib/postgresql/data/postgresql.conf <<EOF restore_command = 'aws s3 cp s3://my-wal-archive/%f %p' recovery_target_time = '2024-06-24 14:31:59' recovery_target_action = 'promote' EOF touch /var/lib/postgresql/data/recovery.signal # 3. Start — engine replays WAL, stops at target, promotes to read-write pg_ctl start -D /var/lib/postgresql/data Named Restore Points Instead of a timestamp, you can use a named restore point created with SELECT pg_create_restore_point('before-big-migration'); . This is more reliable than matching a clock time and is common practice before large schema changes. RPO and RTO Implications Metric Meaning How PITR affects it RPO (Recovery Point Objective) Maximum data loss acceptable WAL archiving gives RPO down to the last archived segment (typically seconds) RTO (Recovery Time Objective) Maximum time to restore Depends on base backup size + WAL replay volume; can range from minutes to hours Practical note: WAL replay speed is roughly 1–3 GB/minute on modern hardware. A 500 GB base backup plus 60 GB of WAL (6 hours of heavy write load) may take 45–90 minutes to restore. Plan for this in your SLA. Try It: Simulating Restore Point Selection The widget below shows how you might query a WAL archive metadata table to identify the correct segments needed to restore to a given time. Interactive SQL · WAL Archive Segment Lookup Common Pitfalls Gaps in WAL archive — if one segment fails to archive, replay stops before the target time. Monitor archive success with alerts on pg_stat_archiver.failed_count . Clock skew — recovery target times are wall clock times on the standby server. Ensure NTP is synchronised. WAL level too low — wal_level = minimal does not produce enough WAL for PITR. Set to replica or logical . Forgotten recovery.signal — without this file (Postgres ≥ 12), the engine starts normally, ignoring restore_command and recovery_target_time . Key Takeaways PITR replays the WAL forward from a base backup to any chosen second — it is your best tool against accidental data loss Configure WAL archiving before you need it; you cannot archive retroactively Named restore points are more reliable than clock timestamps before risky operations WAL replay speed sets your RTO floor; size base backups and archive storage accordingly Monitor the archive process actively — a silent failure means the next restore will fall short ← Previous Backups and restores Next → High availability basics

title: High availability basics
text: A single database server is a single point of failure. When that server crashes — and it will, eventually — every application connected to it stops working. High availability (HA) is the set of architectural patterns that allow a database system to survive individual node failures without significant downtime. This page covers the foundational concepts: replication topologies, failover mechanics, and the trade-offs you accept when designing for HA. The Core Problem: Single Points of Failure Availability is measured in "nines": Availability Downtime per year Downtime per month 99% (two nines) 87.6 hours 7.3 hours 99.9% (three nines) 8.76 hours 43.8 minutes 99.99% (four nines) 52.6 minutes 4.4 minutes 99.999% (five nines) 5.26 minutes 26.3 seconds Moving from two to three nines requires planned maintenance windows and a simple restart procedure. Moving from three to four nines requires redundant hardware and automated failover. Five nines requires every layer of the stack to be redundant, including the database. Primary–Replica Replication The most common HA primitive is primary–replica (master–slave) replication : one node (the primary) accepts writes; one or more replicas receive a copy of every change and apply it locally. Changes flow from primary to replica via the same write-ahead log used for crash recovery (in PostgreSQL this is streaming replication; in MySQL it is the binary log). The replica is always slightly behind — this lag is the cost of asynchronous replication. A primary receives all writes and streams WAL to replicas. On failure, one replica is promoted and the virtual IP (VIP) is redirected — the application reconnects without code changes. Synchronous vs. Asynchronous Replication The biggest design decision in any HA setup is whether replication is synchronous or asynchronous . Asynchronous Synchronous Commit latency Low — primary does not wait Higher — must wait for replica acknowledgement Data loss on failover Possible — replica may lag Zero — replica has every committed transaction Replica failure impact None — primary continues Primary stalls if synchronous replica is unavailable Best for Read scale-out, reporting Financial systems, any zero-RPO requirement PostgreSQL's synchronous_commit = on (or remote_apply ) makes commits synchronous. MySQL Group Replication offers similar guarantees. The practical middle ground is semi-synchronous : the primary waits for at least one replica to acknowledge receipt before confirming the commit. Automated Failover Manual failover is too slow for four-nines availability. Automated failover requires three components: Health monitoring — continuously checks whether the primary is responding (TCP ping, query heartbeat) Consensus / fencing — ensures only one node promotes itself to primary at a time, preventing split-brain VIP / DNS update — redirects application connections to the new primary without code changes Common tools: Patroni (PostgreSQL) — uses etcd/Consul/ZooKeeper for distributed consensus; most production PostgreSQL HA uses this Orchestrator (MySQL) — topology-aware failover with visual dashboard AWS RDS Multi-AZ — fully managed; failover in ~60–120 seconds via DNS update Google Cloud Spanner — built-in multi-region HA, no operator intervention required The Split-Brain Problem Split-brain occurs when two nodes both believe they are the primary and accept writes simultaneously. Resolving the resulting divergence is extremely painful. Prevent it with STONITH (Shoot The Other Node In The Head) — the old primary's network is fenced before the new primary is promoted, guaranteeing the old primary cannot accept writes. Read Replicas for Scale-Out Beyond HA, replicas also serve read scale-out : route SELECT-heavy analytics and reporting queries to a replica, leaving the primary free for writes. This is the simplest form of horizontal read scaling and requires zero application changes beyond a read-only connection string. Warning: Because replicas lag, reads from a replica may not reflect the most recent write. Design applications to tolerate eventual consistency on the replica read path, or use a synchronous replica for reads that must be current. Interactive: Replica Lag Monitoring Interactive SQL · Replica Lag & Health Key Takeaways HA requires redundant nodes; a single server cannot meet four-nines availability Primary–replica replication with WAL streaming is the standard building block for PostgreSQL and MySQL HA Choose synchronous replication for zero RPO; accept the latency cost Automated failover requires health checks, distributed consensus (to prevent split-brain), and VIP/DNS redirection Read replicas provide both HA and read scale-out — but replica lag means reads may be slightly stale ← Previous Point-in-time recovery Next → Failure testing

title: Failure testing
text: High availability architecture means nothing if it has never been tested under real failure conditions. Failure testing — deliberately breaking things in production or in production-like environments — is the only reliable way to verify that your redundancy, monitoring, and runbooks actually work when you need them. This page covers the philosophy, techniques, and tooling behind systematic failure testing for databases. Why You Must Test Failures Every untested failover is a mystery. When a real failure strikes at 3 AM, you will face: Unknown failover time (is it 30 seconds or 30 minutes?) Unclear whether applications reconnect automatically Unverified replica promotion procedures Possibly corrupt backup files you have never actually restored Failure testing converts each of these unknowns into a known, documented, practised process. Teams that test regularly develop muscle memory — the operators have done the failover before and they know what normal looks like. Categories of Failure Database failures fall into several distinct categories, each requiring a different test: Category Examples What to test Node failure Server crash, OS panic, power loss Automatic failover, reconnect time, data integrity Network failure Partition between primary and replica Split-brain prevention, timeout behaviour Disk failure Full disk, I/O errors, corrupt page Error detection, backup restore, RAID rebuild Slow resource CPU/IO saturation, memory pressure Query degradation, connection pool exhaustion Logical failure Accidental DELETE, bad migration PITR restore accuracy, time to recover Process failure Database daemon crash Process supervision restart, crash recovery The failure-testing loop: inject a controlled fault, observe detection time, measure RTO/RPO, verify data integrity, then document findings and iterate. Chaos Engineering Chaos engineering , popularised by Netflix's Chaos Monkey, formalises failure injection as an engineering discipline. The core idea: Define "steady state" — a measurable baseline (error rate, latency p99, throughput) Hypothesise that the system will maintain steady state under a specific failure Inject the failure in a controlled way Measure whether steady state holds Fix any deviations; strengthen your hypothesis For databases specifically: Hypothesis: "If the primary crashes, Patroni will promote a replica within 30 seconds and applications will reconnect within 5 seconds after that." Experiment: kill -9 <postgres primary PID> Metrics: - Time from kill to replica promotion (monitor Patroni logs) - Time from promotion to application reconnect (application error rate) - Row count match between old primary and new primary Tooling Tool What it does Chaos Monkey Randomly terminates EC2 instances Chaos Mesh Kubernetes-native: injects network partitions, disk failures, pod kills Pumba Docker container chaos: kills, delays, packet loss tc / iptables Linux traffic control for manual network partition simulation Gremlin Commercial SaaS; broad failure catalog, scheduling For database testing specifically, you can use OS-level tools without any special framework: # Simulate primary crash systemctl stop postgresql # Simulate network partition (block replication port) iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 5432 -s 10.0.1.11 -j DROP # Simulate disk full fallocate -l 100G /var/lib/postgresql/fake_fill # Simulate slow disk (add 50ms latency to all I/O) tc qdisc add dev eth0 root netem delay 50ms Gameday and Disaster Recovery Drills A gameday is a scheduled event where the engineering team deliberately causes production (or staging) incidents and practises the response. Gamedays: Validate runbooks under realistic time pressure Reveal gaps in on-call knowledge Build confidence and reduce cognitive load during real incidents Surface hidden dependencies (e.g., the backup job that emails an address no one monitors) A DR drill specifically exercises the full restore path: shut down the production database, restore from backup, verify data, and measure RTO. Many teams run DR drills quarterly. Interactive: Failure Experiment Log Track each chaos experiment's outcome to build institutional knowledge. Interactive SQL · Failure Experiment Log What Good Looks Like After a mature failure-testing programme, a team should be able to answer these questions from documented data: What is our measured failover time (p50, p95)? Does our application reconnect automatically, or does it require a restart? What is our measured RTO from backup restore? Have we tested a network partition? Did split-brain occur? When did we last perform a full DR drill? Key principle: Anything you haven't tested in the last 90 days should be considered untested. Configurations drift, dependencies change, and teams turn over. Regular testing is the only antidote. Key Takeaways Failure testing is the only way to convert assumed HA into verified HA Chaos engineering provides a scientific framework: define steady state, inject fault, measure deviation OS tools ( kill , iptables , tc , fallocate ) are sufficient for most database failure injections Run gamedays to validate runbooks and DR drills to measure real RTO Log every experiment: detection time, failover time, data loss, and follow-up actions ← Previous High availability basics Next → Schema migrations in production

title: Schema migrations in production
text: Changing a database schema while the application is running is one of the most dangerous operations in software engineering. A naive ALTER TABLE that would complete instantly on a development laptop can lock millions of rows for minutes on a production table — taking down the application in the process. Schema migrations in production require a completely different mindset from development migrations: every change must be safe to run while traffic flows. Why Production Migrations Are Hard On a development database with 1,000 rows, ALTER TABLE orders ADD COLUMN discount NUMERIC; finishes in milliseconds. On a production table with 200 million rows, the same statement may: Acquire an exclusive table lock that blocks all reads and writes for the duration Cause every query touching that table to queue up, exhausting connection pool slots Time out after 30 seconds, leaving the schema unchanged but the application degraded The problem is compounded by high availability: with replicas, the DDL must also propagate and re-execute on each replica, which can lag behind and cause further issues. The Lock Hierarchy Understanding what each operation locks is the key to safe migrations. PostgreSQL's lock levels from weakest to strongest: Lock Blocks Common operations ACCESS SHARE Nothing except ACCESS EXCLUSIVE Normal SELECT ROW SHARE ACCESS EXCLUSIVE SELECT FOR UPDATE ROW EXCLUSIVE SHARE , SHARE ROW EXCLUSIVE , EXCLUSIVE , ACCESS EXCLUSIVE INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE , SHARE , and above VACUUM , CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY ACCESS EXCLUSIVE Everything ALTER TABLE , DROP TABLE , TRUNCATE Any ACCESS EXCLUSIVE operation will block all reads and writes. The goal of safe migrations is to avoid ACCESS EXCLUSIVE locks on hot tables , or to hold them for the absolute minimum time. The Expand-Contract Pattern The safest way to change a schema that is actively in use is the expand-contract (also called additive migration) pattern. Changes are split into three phases deployed independently: Phase 1 — Expand: Add the new column/table (nullable, no constraints) Phase 2 — Migrate: Backfill data; deploy code that writes both old and new Phase 3 — Contract: Drop the old column after all code uses the new one Example: Renaming a Column Renaming user_name to display_name in a live system: Phase 1 (Expand) -- Safe: adding a nullable column takes near-zero lock time ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN display_name TEXT; Application code update (dual-write) # Write to both columns; read from old until backfill complete user.display_name = user.user_name # copy on write Phase 2 (Backfill — in batches) -- Update in small batches to avoid long-running locks UPDATE users SET display_name = user_name WHERE id BETWEEN 1 AND 10000 AND display_name IS NULL; -- repeat for next batch... Phase 3 (Contract — after all code uses display_name) ALTER TABLE users DROP COLUMN user_name; Expand-contract splits a dangerous rename into three safe deployments, each independently rollback-able. Concurrent Index Creation Adding an index the normal way takes a full SHARE lock on the table, blocking writes for as long as the index build takes. For large tables this can be 10–60 minutes. -- Dangerous: blocks all writes while building the index CREATE INDEX idx_orders_user ON orders(user_id); -- Safe: builds index in the background without blocking writes CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_orders_user ON orders(user_id); CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY takes longer and uses more I/O, but only acquires SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE , which does not block normal reads or writes. It cannot be run inside a transaction block. Caveat: Concurrent index builds can fail partway through, leaving an INVALID index. Check pg_indexes or pg_index.indisvalid after creation. Drop and retry if the index is marked invalid. Online Schema Change Tools For the most disruptive changes (adding NOT NULL columns, changing column types), dedicated tools perform the migration transparently by creating a shadow table: Tool Database Technique pg_repack PostgreSQL Rebuilds table online, swaps with minimal lock pglogical PostgreSQL Logical replication-based schema changes gh-ost MySQL Ghost table + binary log streaming pt-online-schema-change MySQL Triggers on shadow table Online DDL MySQL 8+ / InnoDB Built-in: many operations are now lock-free Migration Tooling and Version Control Schema changes must be version-controlled like code. Common migration frameworks: Flyway: versioned SQL files (V001__add_discount.sql) Liquibase: XML or YAML changesets, supports rollback Alembic: Python (SQLAlchemy); auto-generates diffs sqitch: dependency-aware; works with any SQL dialect Every migration should have: A forward migration (apply) A rollback migration (undo) — or explicit documentation that the change is non-reversible A tested execution plan against production data volume Interactive: Checking for Dangerous Lock Holders Interactive SQL · Lock Wait Analysis Key Takeaways ALTER TABLE acquires ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock — it blocks everything; never run naively on a hot table Use the expand-contract pattern : add columns first (nullable), backfill in batches, then contract Use CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY to build indexes without blocking writes For extreme cases, use online schema tools (gh-ost, pg_repack) that shadow-copy and swap tables Version-control every migration; always have a rollback plan before running against production ← Previous Failure testing Next → Monitoring and alerts

title: Monitoring and alerts
text: You cannot fix what you cannot see. Database monitoring is the continuous measurement of database health, performance, and resource usage — and alerting is the automated escalation when those measurements breach defined thresholds. Together they close the gap between a problem forming and a human taking action. This page covers what to measure, how to structure alerts, and the common pitfalls that cause monitoring to fail when you need it most. The Four Golden Signals (Applied to Databases) Google's Site Reliability Engineering book defines four signals that, together, describe the health of almost any service. Applied to databases: Signal Database meaning Example metric Latency How long queries take p50/p95/p99 query duration Traffic How much work the database is doing Queries per second (QPS), transactions/sec Errors Failed queries, rejected connections Connection errors, deadlock rate, replication errors Saturation How close to limits the database is CPU%, memory%, connections used / max, disk I/O wait Monitoring fewer than these four leaves blind spots. Monitoring only one (e.g., just CPU) gives false confidence — a database can be dying from connection exhaustion while CPU is idle. What to Measure Query Performance -- PostgreSQL: long-running queries (adapt to your threshold) SELECT pid, now() - pg_stat_activity.query_start AS duration, state, left(query, 80) AS query_snippet FROM pg_stat_activity WHERE state != 'idle' AND query_start < now() - interval '30 seconds' ORDER BY duration DESC; Key query metrics to track: p99 query latency — 99th percentile; reveals tail latency invisible in averages Slow query rate — queries exceeding a threshold (e.g., 1 second) Cache hit ratio — shared_blks_hit / (shared_blks_hit + shared_blks_read) in PostgreSQL; below 95% indicates insufficient shared_buffers Connection Pool Connections used / max_connections: alert at 80% Connection wait time: alert if any connection waits > 500ms Idle connections: high idle count wastes server memory Exhausted connections are among the most common database outage triggers. Applications that open connections on every request without pooling regularly hit the limit under modest traffic. Replication Health Replica lag — bytes or seconds behind primary; alert if lag > 30 seconds (or whatever your RPO demands) Replication slot bloat — unused logical replication slots hold back WAL and can fill the disk Replica count — alert if fewer than expected replicas are connected Disk Disk usage % — alert at 75%, page at 90% WAL directory size — unchecked WAL archiving failures can fill the disk IOPS utilisation — sustained 100% IOPS saturates the disk queue A typical monitoring stack: postgres_exporter exposes metrics, Prometheus scrapes and evaluates alert rules, Grafana visualises dashboards, and Alertmanager routes notifications to on-call channels. Structuring Alerts The most common monitoring failure is alert fatigue : too many alerts that fire too often, training on-call engineers to ignore them. Good alerts are rare, actionable, and urgent. Alert Severity Levels Level Meaning Response Critical / Page Revenue-impacting or data-loss risk right now Wake someone up immediately Warning / Ticket Trend heading toward a problem Fix within business hours Info / Log Interesting event, no action needed Review in daily standup Only page for critical alerts. Paging for warnings trains on-call engineers to silence alerts without reading them. Alert Design Rules Alert on symptoms, not causes — "p99 latency > 2s" affects users; "CPU > 80%" may not. Prefer user-visible outcomes. Include a runbook link — every alert should link directly to the procedure for handling it Set a for duration — do not alert on a single data point; alert only if the condition persists for 5 minutes Deduplicate and group — if 10 replicas all lag at once, send one grouped alert, not 10 individual ones Example Prometheus Alert Rules groups: - name: database rules: - alert: DatabaseHighConnectionUsage expr: pg_stat_activity_count / pg_settings_max_connections > 0.8 for: 5m labels: severity: warning annotations: summary: "Connection pool near capacity ({{ $value | humanizePercentage }})" runbook: "https://wiki.internal/runbooks/db-connections" - alert: ReplicaLagHigh expr: pg_replication_lag_seconds > 30 for: 2m labels: severity: critical annotations: summary: "Replica {{ $labels.instance }} is {{ $value }}s behind primary" Interactive: Alert Threshold Simulation Interactive SQL · Alert Evaluation Key Takeaways Monitor the four golden signals: latency, traffic, errors, saturation — all four, not just CPU Page only for critical, user-visible problems; route warnings to tickets to prevent alert fatigue Every alert needs a runbook link, a for duration, and a clear description of the symptom Track replica lag actively — a drifting replica is often the first sign of a coming incident Use a proven stack (postgres_exporter → Prometheus → Grafana → Alertmanager) to get started quickly ← Previous Schema migrations in production Next → Capacity planning

title: Capacity planning
text: A database that runs out of disk space, connections, or memory does not degrade gracefully — it crashes. Capacity planning is the practice of measuring current resource consumption, modelling future growth, and acting before limits are reached. Done well, it is invisible engineering that prevents emergencies. Done poorly, it results in 2 AM pages and emergency infrastructure tickets. The Three Resource Families Database capacity problems cluster around three families of resources: Family Resources Typical failure mode Storage Disk space, IOPS, throughput Disk full → database halts all writes Memory Shared buffer pool, work_mem, OS page cache Buffer pool thrashing → cache hit rate collapses → 10–100× latency spike Compute CPU cores, connections, thread pool Connection exhaustion → new connections rejected → 503 errors Planning for all three is required; they interact (insufficient memory forces more IOPS, which saturates CPU for I/O wait). Measuring Current Usage Before projecting forward, establish accurate baselines. The key measurements: Storage -- PostgreSQL: table and index sizes SELECT relname AS table_name, pg_size_pretty(pg_total_relation_size(oid)) AS total_size, pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(oid)) AS table_size, pg_size_pretty(pg_indexes_size(oid)) AS index_size FROM pg_class WHERE relkind = 'r' ORDER BY pg_total_relation_size(oid) DESC LIMIT 10; Memory (Buffer Cache Hit Rate) -- Cache hit rate: target > 99% for OLTP workloads SELECT sum(heap_blks_hit) AS cache_hits, sum(heap_blks_read) AS disk_reads, ROUND(100.0 * sum(heap_blks_hit) / NULLIF(sum(heap_blks_hit) + sum(heap_blks_read), 0), 2) AS hit_rate_pct FROM pg_statio_user_tables; Connections -- Active connections vs. maximum SELECT count(*) AS active, (SELECT setting::int FROM pg_settings WHERE name = 'max_connections') AS max_conn, ROUND(100.0 * count(*) / (SELECT setting::int FROM pg_settings WHERE name = 'max_connections'), 1) AS pct_used FROM pg_stat_activity WHERE state != 'idle'; Growth Modelling Once you have baselines, project forward using historical growth rates. The simplest model is linear extrapolation from a time series of measurements: Current size: 800 GB Average growth/month: 40 GB (from past 6 months) Available disk: 2 TB (2048 GB) Headroom: 2048 - 800 = 1248 GB Months to 85% full: (2048 × 0.85 - 800) / 40 = 23.5 months For write-heavy workloads with exponential growth (e.g., event logs, telemetry), model with compound growth: size(t) = current × (1 + monthly_growth_rate)^t Always plan to 80%, not 100%. Running a disk past 80% degrades PostgreSQL performance (autovacuum needs free space to rewrite pages). Alert at 75%, page at 85%. Disk usage trend with linear projection. The 75% and 85% thresholds (dashed lines) indicate warning and critical alert points — order storage upgrades before the warning line. Connection Capacity The database's max_connections setting is a hard limit. Hitting it is immediately catastrophic — new connections fail with "too many connections" and the application returns errors to users. The correct approach is connection pooling (PgBouncer, ProxySQL): applications connect to the pooler, which maintains a smaller number of real database connections. A database can comfortably handle 200–400 real connections; behind a pooler, thousands of application threads share those connections. Application tier: 50 pods × 10 threads = 500 concurrent connections to pooler PgBouncer pool: 30 real connections to PostgreSQL PostgreSQL: max_connections = 100 (safe headroom) Table Bloat and Autovacuum In PostgreSQL, UPDATEs and DELETEs do not immediately reclaim space — they leave dead tuples that autovacuum must clean up. Tables that are vacuumed too infrequently grow bloated : a 10 GB table may physically occupy 40 GB on disk. -- Identify bloated tables (approximate) SELECT relname, n_dead_tup, n_live_tup, ROUND(100.0 * n_dead_tup / NULLIF(n_live_tup + n_dead_tup, 0), 1) AS dead_pct FROM pg_stat_user_tables WHERE n_dead_tup > 10000 ORDER BY dead_pct DESC; Tune autovacuum aggressively for hot tables; consider manual VACUUM ANALYZE after large bulk operations. Interactive: Storage Growth Projection Interactive SQL · Storage Runway Calculation Key Takeaways Capacity problems happen in three families: storage, memory, and compute — monitor all three Establish growth baselines from 3–6 months of historical data; linear extrapolation is usually sufficient Alert at 75% disk usage; order storage upgrades with enough lead time to avoid emergency provisioning Use connection pooling (PgBouncer) to keep real database connections well below max_connections Watch table bloat (dead tuple %) on high-churn tables and tune autovacuum accordingly ← Previous Monitoring and alerts Next → Operational runbooks

title: Operational runbooks
text: A database incident at 3 AM is the wrong time to figure out the recovery procedure. Operational runbooks are pre-written, step-by-step guides that on-call engineers follow when something breaks — eliminating guesswork, reducing cognitive load, and shortening time-to-resolution. A runbook is not a design document or a how-it-works explanation; it is a recipe written for a stressed engineer who may be half-awake and whose hands need to know what to type. What Makes a Good Runbook A runbook succeeds or fails based on specificity and trustworthiness. The test: could a competent engineer who has never seen this system before follow the runbook to resolution without additional research? Anatomy of a Runbook # Runbook: High Replication Lag Last updated: 2024-06-15 Owner: @db-team Severity: WARNING → CRITICAL ## Alert - Alert name: ReplicaLagHigh - Fires when: pg_replication_lag_seconds > 30 for 2+ minutes ## Impact - Reads from replicas may be stale by > 30 seconds - Risk: replica cannot be promoted quickly if primary fails ## Diagnosis (run in order, stop when you find the cause) 1. Check replica is still connected: SELECT client_addr, state, sent_lsn, write_lsn, flush_lsn, replay_lsn FROM pg_stat_replication; 2. Check replica's lag on the replica itself: SELECT now() - pg_last_xact_replay_timestamp() AS lag; 3. Check for long-running transactions on primary blocking vacuum / WAL: SELECT pid, now() - xact_start AS age, state, query FROM pg_stat_activity WHERE xact_start IS NOT NULL ORDER BY age DESC; 4. Check disk I/O on replica: iostat -x 5 3 ## Resolution - If replica disconnected: restart replication (pg_ctl reload on replica) - If long-running txn: pg_terminate_backend(pid) after confirming with team lead - If disk I/O saturated: page @infra-oncall to resize replica disk / instance - If lag > 5 minutes: switch read traffic away from this replica (update load balancer) ## Escalation - 0–10 min: on-call DB engineer - 10–30 min: escalate to DB team lead - 30 min+: escalate to VP Engineering The elements that matter: Alert name — links the runbook directly to the alert that triggered it Impact — tells the engineer why this matters before they start Ordered diagnosis steps — numbered, specific commands, stop-when-found structure Resolution options — covers the most common causes; links to escalation if none apply Escalation path — when to ask for help, and who to ask The Runbook Library Every recurring alert should have a corresponding runbook. Common database runbooks: Alert Runbook covers Disk usage > 85% Find largest tables/indexes, enable tablespace, escalate disk resize Connection pool exhausted Identify connection hogs, tune max_connections, restart pooler Replication lag Diagnose WAL lag, long transactions, disk saturation Deadlock spike Capture pg_locks snapshot, identify conflicting queries, notify developers Autovacuum not keeping up Tune autovacuum parameters, trigger manual VACUUM, identify bloat Backup job failed Check archive destination, re-run backup, verify WAL continuity Slow query (p99 > threshold) EXPLAIN ANALYZE suspect queries, check for missing indexes, check autovacuum Every alert links to a runbook; the engineer follows diagnosis steps to either resolve or escalate; findings are fed back to improve the runbook after each incident. Runbook Anti-Patterns Stale runbooks are dangerous — an outdated procedure can mislead an engineer into making the situation worse. Common problems: Commands reference old server names or deprecated flags Steps assume a software version that has since been upgraded Escalation contacts list people who have left the company The runbook was written once and never reviewed Fix: treat runbooks like code — they live in version control (Git), have owners, and are reviewed after every incident that used them. Too high-level runbooks give principles instead of commands: Bad: "Check the database health and restart if necessary" Good: "Run: SELECT pg_reload_conf(); — if that does not resolve, run: sudo systemctl restart postgresql@14-main" Missing expected state — a good runbook tells the engineer what normal looks like so they can confirm the fix worked: After restarting replication, verify: SELECT state, sent_lsn = replay_lsn AS caught_up FROM pg_stat_replication; -- Expected: state = 'streaming', caught_up = true within 60s Interactive: Runbook Coverage Audit Interactive SQL · Alert-to-Runbook Coverage Post-Incident Runbook Updates Every incident that used a runbook is an opportunity to improve it. After resolution, answer: Did the diagnosis steps lead to the actual cause, or did you have to improvise? Was any step unclear, incorrect, or missing? What command or observation actually identified the root cause — and should be added? Was the escalation path correct and fast enough? A runbook that gets updated after every incident becomes increasingly reliable. One that is never touched accumulates drift until it is worse than no runbook at all. Key Takeaways A runbook is a recipe, not an explanation — specific commands, expected output, stop-when-found structure Every recurring alert should link directly to a runbook Treat runbooks like code: version-controlled, owned, reviewed after every incident that uses them Include expected output ("expected: state = streaming") so engineers can verify the fix worked Audit runbook coverage regularly — high-firing alerts with no runbook are gaps that will bite you at 3 AM ← Previous Capacity planning Next → Common failure stories

title: Common failure stories
text: Real database outages rarely happen for novel reasons. They happen for the same ten reasons, over and over, at different companies with different stacks — because the underlying dynamics are architectural, not implementation-specific. Studying failure stories teaches pattern recognition: the ability to look at a set of symptoms and recognise the category of problem before the incident drags into its second hour. This page distills the most instructive recurring failure patterns. The Cascade of Slowness What happened: A poorly-indexed query on a hot table began taking 8 seconds instead of 80 milliseconds after a data volume milestone was crossed. Each slow query held a database connection for the full 8 seconds. The application had 20 database connections in its pool. At modest traffic, all 20 connections were occupied with slow queries, and new requests waited. Wait times accumulated, threads backed up, and within 3 minutes the service was returning 503 errors to 100% of users — for what was fundamentally a missing index. Why it cascades: Connection pools decouple request volume from database connections. Under normal conditions this is good. Under abnormal latency, it means a single slow query class can exhaust the pool because each connection is held for far longer than expected. Detection: Connection wait time metrics spike before error rates do. If you only alert on error rates, you lose the warning window. Prevention: Alert on p99 query latency, not just errors Set statement_timeout to bound worst-case query duration Use EXPLAIN ANALYZE on all queries that touch large tables before deploying The Replication Slot That Ate the Disk What happened: A team set up logical replication for a data pipeline. The downstream consumer was later decommissioned, but no one dropped the replication slot. PostgreSQL holds WAL segments for all active replication slots — so every WAL segment produced since the consumer died was retained. Over 6 weeks, the WAL directory grew to fill the disk. The database halted all writes with "no space left on device." -- Replication slots and their lag (bytes retained) SELECT slot_name, plugin, active, pg_size_pretty(pg_wal_lsn_diff(pg_current_wal_lsn(), restart_lsn)) AS retained_wal FROM pg_replication_slots ORDER BY pg_wal_lsn_diff(pg_current_wal_lsn(), restart_lsn) DESC; Prevention: Alert when any replication slot retains > 10 GB of WAL Document and automate slot cleanup when consumers are decommissioned Set max_slot_wal_keep_size (PostgreSQL 13+) to cap WAL retained per slot The Autovacuum Fallen Behind What happened: A high-throughput orders table received thousands of UPDATEs per second. Autovacuum was configured with default settings (tuned for small databases) and could not keep pace. Dead tuples accumulated; the table's pg_class.relpages count ballooned. Queries that had historically taken 50 ms began taking 4 seconds as the planner's row estimates became wildly wrong due to stale statistics. The fix — manually running VACUUM ANALYZE — completed in 40 minutes and immediately restored query performance. Why autovacuum falls behind: The defaults assume low-churn tables. A table with 10 million rows being updated at 5,000 rows/second needs autovacuum to run nearly continuously, not once every few hours. Tuning for hot tables: ALTER TABLE orders SET ( autovacuum_vacuum_scale_factor = 0.01, -- vacuum when 1% of rows are dead (default 20%) autovacuum_analyze_scale_factor = 0.005, -- analyze when 0.5% of rows change autovacuum_vacuum_cost_delay = 2 -- ms; lower = more aggressive (default 20ms) ); The Migration That Locked the Table What happened: A developer ran ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN last_login TIMESTAMP NOT NULL DEFAULT now(); in production. The statement acquired an ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock and held it for 14 minutes while rewriting every row in a 90M-row table. Every query touching users — login, profile update, session validation — queued behind that lock. The application effectively went down for 14 minutes. What should have happened: -- Step 1: Add nullable column (near-instant) ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN last_login TIMESTAMP; -- Step 2: Backfill in small batches (background job, no exclusive lock) UPDATE users SET last_login = now() WHERE id BETWEEN 1 AND 50000 AND last_login IS NULL; -- ... repeat for all batches ... -- Step 3: Add NOT NULL constraint (PostgreSQL 12+ can verify without full rewrite if null check already done) ALTER TABLE users ALTER COLUMN last_login SET NOT NULL; The 3 AM Pager With No Runbook What happened: A replica-lag alert fired. The on-call engineer, unfamiliar with the replication stack, spent 45 minutes reading documentation and experimenting with commands. Several of those commands had unintended effects. What turned out to be a straightforward disk I/O bottleneck became a 2-hour incident — not because the problem was hard, but because the response process was improvised. The same team subsequently wrote a replication-lag runbook covering five common causes and their specific resolutions. The next time the alert fired (4 months later), time-to-resolution was 8 minutes. Lesson: The hardest part of incident response is not technical — it is that stress, sleep deprivation, and time pressure severely impair diagnostic thinking. Runbooks remove the thinking burden and let the engineer execute. Each failure pattern has a recognisable shape, a known root cause, and a well-understood prevention — studying them builds the pattern recognition to catch them early. The Backup That Was Never Restored What happened: After a ransomware attack encrypted the primary database and all local backups, the team initiated a restore from S3. The backup files were present. But the restore had never been tested, and the procedure in the runbook referenced a deprecated pg_restore flag. The flag caused the tool to silently skip all data, producing an empty database. The team spent 6 hours debugging the restore process before successfully recovering data — 19 hours after the attack began. The lesson: A backup is a hypothesis. Only a tested restore confirms it. Run quarterly restore drills to a staging environment. Measure and record the actual restore time. The Query That Worked Yesterday What happened: A query that read from a reports table took 90 ms for 18 months. After a new month's data was loaded — growing the table from 8 million to 11 million rows — the same query took 45 seconds. The cause: the query planner had been using an index, but after the row count crossed a threshold, the planner estimated that a sequential scan would be cheaper (incorrectly, due to stale statistics). Running ANALYZE reports; restored the correct plan within seconds. Prevention: Run ANALYZE after large data loads (or let autovacuum do it — if it is configured aggressively enough) Monitor query latency trends over time, not just point-in-time Use pg_stat_statements to track query plans and flag dramatic latency regressions Interactive: Incident Pattern Log Interactive SQL · Incident Pattern Analysis Key Takeaways Most database outages are repeats of known patterns: slow queries cascading through connection pools, replication slot bloat, missed vacuum, lock-storm migrations, untested backups The recurring nature of these failures means each one is a learning opportunity for the entire industry Connection pool exhaustion is the most common user-visible symptom — but the root cause is almost always query latency, not the pool itself Backups are hypotheses until proven by a successful restore drill Pattern recognition — knowing "this looks like a replication slot problem" — is what separates experienced database operators from novices, and it is built entirely through studying past failures ← Previous Operational runbooks Next → Chapter 24 Summary: What We Learned

title: Chapter 24 Summary: What We Learned
text: Page Topic Takeaway in One Sentence 231 Backups and restores Regular backups (full + incremental) with tested restore procedures — untested backups are not backups at all. 232 Point-in-time recovery WAL archiving lets you restore to any moment, not just the last backup — critical for recovering from logical errors. 233 High availability basics Redundancy (replicas), automatic failover, and health checks keep the database available when individual components fail. 234 Failure testing Chaos engineering: deliberately kill nodes, networks, and disks to verify your recovery procedures work under real conditions. 235 Schema migrations in production Use expand-contract pattern: add columns, dual-write, migrate data, then remove old columns. Never rename; always add and deprecate. 236 Monitoring and alerts Track key metrics: query latency, error rates, replication lag, disk usage, connection count. Alert on trends, not thresholds. 237 Capacity planning Project growth in data volume, query rate, and connection count; plan upgrades before you hit limits. 238 Operational runbooks Documented step-by-step procedures for common incidents: failover, restore, scaling, and troubleshooting. 239 Common failure stories Running out of disk during a long weekend, replication lag causing data inconsistency, misconfigured connection pools — learn from others' pain. Reveal: How do these ideas connect to the next chapter? Chapter 24 covered keeping your database reliable — available, recoverable, and safely changeable. But reliability means nothing if anyone can access your data. Chapter 25 tackles security and access control: authentication, authorization, encryption at rest and in transit, audit logging, and SQL injection prevention. You will learn the principle of least privilege, how roles simplify permission management, and why parameterized queries are non-negotiable. A reliable database that leaks data is a liability. ← Previous Common failure stories Next → Authentication and authorization

title: Authentication and authorization
text: Every database interaction starts with two questions: who are you? and what are you allowed to do? Authentication answers the first; authorization answers the second. Getting both right is the foundation of database security — a misconfigured auth layer can expose every row to anyone on the network, while a misconfigured authorization model can let any authenticated user read or destroy data they should never touch. Authentication: Proving Identity Authentication is the mechanism by which a client proves it is who it claims to be. Databases support several mechanisms, and most production systems layer more than one. Password-Based Authentication The simplest mechanism: the client sends a username and password; the database verifies the password hash stored in its catalog. -- PostgreSQL: create a login role with a password CREATE ROLE app_user LOGIN PASSWORD 'hunter2'; -- MySQL equivalent CREATE USER 'app_user'@'%' IDENTIFIED BY 'hunter2'; Plain password auth is adequate when combined with TLS (so the password never travels in the clear) and a strong hashing algorithm. PostgreSQL uses SCRAM-SHA-256 by default since version 14; older deployments may still use the weaker MD5 method. Always check pg_hba.conf and set password_encryption = scram-sha-256 . Certificate-Based Authentication Databases that sit behind a load balancer or inside a VPC often use mutual TLS (mTLS) : the client presents an X.509 certificate; the database validates it against a trusted CA. No password is stored or transmitted. This is common in cloud-managed databases (AWS RDS, Google Cloud SQL) where the certificate authority is managed by the cloud provider. IAM / Token-Based Authentication Cloud databases increasingly delegate authentication entirely to an IAM system. The application exchanges a short-lived IAM token (valid for 15 minutes) for a database session. The token is signed by the IAM service and verified by the database plugin. # AWS RDS — generate an auth token (no permanent DB password) aws rds generate-db-auth-token \ --hostname mydb.cluster-xxx.us-east-1.rds.amazonaws.com \ --port 5432 --username app_user Benefits: no long-lived secrets in config files; automatic rotation; audit trail tied to IAM activity logs. Peer / OS Authentication On the same machine, PostgreSQL's peer method trusts that the OS username maps to a database role. Useful for local admin scripts; never use it over a network. Three common authentication paths. All converge on the same outcome: a verified session with an associated database role. Authorization: Controlling What Authenticated Users Can Do Once a user is authenticated, the database applies its access control model to decide what operations are permitted. SQL GRANT / REVOKE The standard SQL mechanism. Privileges are granted on objects (tables, views, sequences, functions) to roles. -- Grant read-only access to a reporting role GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA analytics TO reporter; -- Grant specific column access GRANT SELECT (id, email) ON users TO support_agent; -- Revoke dangerous privileges REVOKE DELETE ON orders FROM app_user; Principle of least privilege: Every role should have the minimum privileges required for its purpose. An application role that only reads product data should never hold DELETE or DROP TABLE rights — even if it never uses them. Row-Level Security (RLS) Column-level grants restrict which columns a role can see. Row-level security restricts which rows a role can access — an orthogonal and equally important dimension. -- Enable RLS on the orders table ALTER TABLE orders ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY; -- Policy: each user sees only their own orders CREATE POLICY user_orders ON orders USING (customer_id = current_setting('app.current_user_id')::int); With this policy in place, even a SELECT * FROM orders returns only the rows matching the policy expression. The filter is enforced by the database engine, not the application layer — so no amount of application-layer bugs can expose another user's data. Separation of Admin and Application Roles A common mistake is connecting the application to the database as a superuser ( postgres , root ) because it is convenient. This is catastrophic if the application is compromised. Role Typical privileges superuser / root Everything — DDL, DROP DATABASE, pg_read_file migration_user DDL on the target schema only; used only during deploys app_user DML (SELECT / INSERT / UPDATE / DELETE) on owned tables reporter SELECT on analytics schema only readonly SELECT on specific tables; used by dashboards The Authentication vs. Authorization Boundary It is tempting to blur the two concepts, but keeping them separate pays dividends: Authentication is stateless relative to data: it does not look at your rows. Authorization is context-sensitive: the same user may have different privileges on different schemas, tables, or even rows depending on tenant, time, or application state. Separating them also means you can swap authentication providers (move from password to IAM) without touching your GRANT structure, and you can audit each layer independently. Interactive SQL · Role Privileges Key Takeaways Authentication (who are you?) and authorization (what can you do?) are distinct layers — design and audit them separately. Prefer strong authentication: SCRAM-SHA-256 for passwords, mTLS or IAM tokens for production cloud deployments. Apply the principle of least privilege : every role gets only what it needs; revoke defaults aggressively. Use row-level security when multiple users or tenants share a table — enforcement in the database engine is safer than enforcement in application code. Never connect production applications as a superuser. ← Previous Common failure stories Next → Roles and permissions

title: Roles and permissions
text: Databases do not just store data — they guard it. Roles and permissions are the primary mechanism databases use to decide who can read, write, or administer which objects. A well-designed role hierarchy is invisible when everything works and invaluable when something goes wrong. This page walks through how role-based access control (RBAC) works in SQL databases, how to design a sensible role hierarchy, and how common mistakes create dangerous privilege escalation paths. Roles vs. Users Modern databases separate the concepts of a role (a named set of privileges) from a login (the credential used to connect). In PostgreSQL, a role with the LOGIN attribute is effectively a user; without it, the role is a group that can be granted to other roles. -- A group role (no login) — defines a privilege set CREATE ROLE readonly; GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA public TO readonly; -- A login role that inherits from the group CREATE ROLE alice LOGIN PASSWORD 'secret' IN ROLE readonly; Alice can now log in and read all tables in public . She inherits the readonly group's privileges automatically. When you later grant readonly permission to another table, all members get it immediately. The INHERIT Attribute By default, roles inherit the privileges of roles they are members of. You can suppress this with NOINHERIT and require explicit SET ROLE to activate sensitive privileges — useful for admin roles you don't want accidentally active: CREATE ROLE dba LOGIN NOINHERIT; GRANT superuser_ops TO dba; -- Must actively switch to activate superuser_ops: -- SET ROLE superuser_ops; Object-Level Privileges SQL defines a fixed set of privilege types per object class: Object Privilege types Table / view SELECT , INSERT , UPDATE , DELETE , TRUNCATE , REFERENCES , TRIGGER Column SELECT , INSERT , UPDATE Schema USAGE , CREATE Function EXECUTE Sequence USAGE , SELECT , UPDATE Database CONNECT , TEMPORARY , CREATE -- Fine-grained: read only two columns of a sensitive table GRANT SELECT (order_id, status) ON orders TO support_agent; -- Schema access: must grant USAGE before object-level grants matter GRANT USAGE ON SCHEMA finance TO analyst; GRANT SELECT ON finance.transactions TO analyst; -- Revoke a specific privilege REVOKE DELETE ON products FROM api_user; Common gotcha: Granting SELECT on a table in a schema the role cannot USAGE does nothing — the role can't even see the schema. Always grant schema USAGE first. A typical role hierarchy. Group roles (dba, app_writer, reporter) hold privileges; login roles inherit them. Superuser is never used for application connections. Designing a Safe Role Hierarchy Principle of Least Privilege Every role should have only the privileges it actually needs: Application service accounts — SELECT , INSERT , UPDATE , DELETE on owned tables. No DROP , no TRUNCATE , no DDL. Migration accounts — DDL grants ( CREATE , ALTER , DROP ) used only during deployments, then disconnected. Read-only analytics — SELECT on the analytics schema; no access to PII tables in other schemas. Admin roles — require explicit SET ROLE activation ( NOINHERIT ) so they're never accidentally on. Default Privileges PostgreSQL's ALTER DEFAULT PRIVILEGES automatically applies grants to future objects: -- Ensure future tables in 'public' schema are readable by reporter ALTER DEFAULT PRIVILEGES IN SCHEMA public GRANT SELECT ON TABLES TO reporter; Without this, every new table requires a manual GRANT — which is inevitably forgotten. Privilege Escalation Pitfalls SECURITY DEFINER Functions A function marked SECURITY DEFINER runs with the privileges of its creator, not the caller. This is powerful and dangerous: -- Dangerous: any caller gains the creator's privileges inside this function CREATE FUNCTION admin_delete(t TEXT) RETURNS void LANGUAGE plpgsql SECURITY DEFINER AS $ BEGIN EXECUTE 'DELETE FROM ' || t; END; $; If admin_delete is callable by a low-privilege role, that role can now delete from any table. Always validate inputs inside SECURITY DEFINER functions and grant EXECUTE to specific roles only. PUBLIC Schema and PUBLIC Role PostgreSQL's built-in PUBLIC role includes all users. Historically, CREATE on the public schema was granted to PUBLIC by default — meaning any user could create tables there. PostgreSQL 15 removed this default. If you're on an older version: REVOKE CREATE ON SCHEMA public FROM PUBLIC; Interactive Example Interactive SQL · Privilege Audit Key Takeaways Separate group roles (hold privileges) from login roles (hold credentials) — it makes auditing and onboarding dramatically simpler. Grant object privileges at the schema, table, or column level as needed; never give more than required. Use ALTER DEFAULT PRIVILEGES to ensure new objects inherit the right grants automatically. Treat SECURITY DEFINER functions as privileged code — audit them like sudo scripts. Revoke CREATE on the public schema from the PUBLIC role in older PostgreSQL versions. ← Previous Authentication and authorization Next → Encryption in transit and at rest

title: Encryption in transit and at rest
text: Data has two vulnerable moments: when it travels across a network and when it sits on a storage device. Encryption in transit protects data as it moves between clients and the database server; encryption at rest protects data that lives on disk. Both layers are essential, and neither substitutes for the other — a wire-tapper can't read an encrypted connection, and a stolen hard drive is useless if the data is encrypted at rest. Encryption in Transit: TLS Transport Layer Security (TLS) is the standard for encrypting database connections. Without it, anyone on the same network segment can intercept SQL queries, result sets, and authentication credentials in plain text. How TLS Works for Databases The client connects to the database server. The server presents an X.509 certificate signed by a trusted CA. They negotiate a cipher suite and establish a session key. All subsequent traffic — queries, results, error messages — is encrypted. Most modern databases default to requiring TLS or at least offering it as an option. In PostgreSQL, the pg_hba.conf hostssl record forces TLS: # pg_hba.conf — require TLS for all remote connections hostssl all all 0.0.0.0/0 scram-sha-256 Mutual TLS (mTLS) Standard TLS authenticates only the server. Mutual TLS also authenticates the client via a client certificate, so both sides prove their identity. This is common in database-as-a-service environments and microservice meshes. # PostgreSQL: require client certificate hostssl all app_user 0.0.0.0/0 cert Verifying Your Connection After enabling TLS, verify that connections actually use it: -- PostgreSQL: check connection encryption SELECT ssl, version, cipher, bits FROM pg_stat_ssl WHERE pid = pg_backend_pid(); Never disable certificate verification. Drivers often have a sslmode=require (encrypt but don't verify the certificate) option. Use sslmode=verify-full to also validate the server's hostname — otherwise you're vulnerable to man-in-the-middle attacks. PostgreSQL server-side config: TLS is configured via ssl_cert_file (server certificate), ssl_key_file (private key), and ssl_ca_file (CA certificate for verifying client certs) in postgresql.conf . On the client side, sslmode=verify-full requires the server certificate's Common Name (CN) to match the hostname used in the connection string; a mismatch will cause the connection to fail. The TLS handshake establishes a shared session key. All subsequent query and result traffic passes through the encrypted channel. Encryption at Rest Encryption at rest protects data stored on disk — data files, WAL/redo logs, temporary files, and backups. Without it, a stolen disk or misconfigured cloud storage bucket exposes all your data. Transparent Data Encryption (TDE) Most enterprise and cloud databases implement Transparent Data Encryption : the database engine encrypts data pages before writing them to disk and decrypts them on read. The application sees no difference; the encryption is handled below the SQL layer. Database TDE support PostgreSQL No native TDE; use pgcrypto for column-level encryption or file-system encryption (LUKS, ZFS native encryption) for at-rest protection MySQL / MariaDB InnoDB tablespace encryption (enabled per-table or globally) SQL Server Full TDE since 2008 Oracle Advanced Security TDE AWS RDS / Aurora AES-256 at rest via AWS KMS, enabled at instance creation -- MySQL: enable encryption for a table ALTER TABLE sensitive_data ENCRYPTION = 'Y'; -- MySQL: check encryption status SELECT table_name, create_options FROM information_schema.tables WHERE table_schema = 'mydb' AND create_options LIKE '%ENCRYPTION=Y%'; Encryption Granularity At-rest encryption can operate at different levels: Full-disk encryption (FDE) — the OS or hardware encrypts the entire volume. Protects against physical theft; the database process itself runs with unencrypted data in memory. File-system encryption — the file system layer (e.g., Linux LUKS, ZFS encryption) encrypts before writing to disk. Database-level TDE — the database engine encrypts pages. More portable; key management is explicit. Column-level / application-level encryption — the application encrypts specific fields before storing them. Strongest isolation; the database itself never sees plaintext. Used for PII, PAN data, credentials. Key Management Encryption is only as strong as its key management. Keys must be: Stored separately from the encrypted data (never on the same disk) Rotatable without downtime where possible Access-controlled so only authorized services can decrypt Cloud key management services (AWS KMS, Google Cloud KMS, HashiCorp Vault) provide hardware security module (HSM)-backed key storage with audit logs for every key access. # AWS: create a KMS key for database encryption aws kms create-key --description "RDS encryption key" \ --key-usage ENCRYPT_DECRYPT Backup encryption: Encrypting the live database is not enough if backups are stored in plaintext S3. Ensure backup encryption is enabled and that backup keys are independently managed. Column-Level Encryption For especially sensitive fields — social security numbers, payment card data, health records — column-level encryption adds a layer that even DBAs cannot bypass: -- PostgreSQL pgcrypto: encrypt before insert INSERT INTO patients (id, name, ssn_encrypted) VALUES (1, 'Alice', pgp_sym_encrypt('123-45-6789', 'secret_passphrase')); -- Decrypt only where authorized SELECT id, name, pgp_sym_decrypt(ssn_encrypted, 'secret_passphrase') AS ssn FROM patients WHERE id = 1; The downside: encrypted columns cannot be efficiently indexed or used in WHERE clauses (you can't range-scan ciphertext). This is an intentional trade-off — if you need the data, you decrypt it explicitly. Interactive SQL · Simulated Column Encryption Key Takeaways TLS in transit is non-negotiable. Use verify-full mode — not just require — so clients validate the server's certificate. TDE at rest protects against physical theft and storage misconfigurations; most cloud databases enable it by default. Keep encryption keys separate from encrypted data and rotate them on a schedule. Use a managed KMS rather than rolling your own. For the most sensitive data, use column-level encryption : the database stores only ciphertext, and only code with the decryption key can access the plaintext. Ensure backups are also encrypted — a plaintext backup negates all on-disk encryption. ← Previous Roles and permissions Next → Auditing and logging

title: Auditing and logging
text: When a security incident happens, the first question is always: what happened, who did it, and when? Auditing and logging are how databases answer that question. An audit trail is not just a compliance checkbox — it is the difference between a contained breach and one that goes undetected for months. This page covers what to log, how to configure it, and how to query audit data effectively. What to Log Not all database activity is equally worth capturing. Over-logging generates noise that buries real signals; under-logging leaves gaps attackers exploit.
