A useful audit strategy logs at three levels: Level What Why Connection events Login attempts, successful connections, disconnections Detect brute-force, credential stuffing, unexpected service accounts logging in DDL events CREATE , ALTER , DROP , TRUNCATE Schema changes are irreversible; catch accidental or malicious structural changes DML on sensitive tables SELECT , INSERT , UPDATE , DELETE on PII/financial tables Who read or changed sensitive data and when Privilege changes GRANT , REVOKE , role membership changes Privilege escalation is a common attack vector Failed queries / errors Permission-denied errors, syntax errors in bulk Probing and injection attempts often leave error traces PostgreSQL Audit Configuration PostgreSQL's built-in logging is configured in postgresql.conf : # Log all connections and disconnections log_connections = on log_disconnections = on # Log all DDL statements log_statement = 'ddl' # Log slow queries (useful for performance and unexpected bulk reads) log_min_duration_statement = 1000 # milliseconds # Log failed authentication log_failed_connections = on # (available in PG 15+) For finer-grained DML auditing, the community extension pgAudit (PostgreSQL Audit Extension) logs individual SELECT , INSERT , UPDATE , and DELETE statements with full context: -- Install the extension (requires superuser) CREATE EXTENSION pgaudit; -- Enable pgAudit for a specific role SET pgaudit.log = 'read,write'; SET pgaudit.log_relation = on; pgAudit emits structured log lines that include the statement class, object type, object name, and the full SQL text — far more useful than the raw PostgreSQL log. Audit Tables: Logging Data Changes For application-level auditing — tracking who changed what value in a business table — a trigger-based audit table is the most common pattern: -- Audit table: stores old and new values for every change CREATE TABLE audit_log ( log_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, table_name TEXT NOT NULL, operation TEXT NOT NULL, -- INSERT / UPDATE / DELETE changed_by TEXT NOT NULL, -- current_user changed_at TEXT NOT NULL, -- timestamp old_data TEXT, -- JSON of old row new_data TEXT -- JSON of new row ); -- Trigger function CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION audit_trigger_fn() RETURNS trigger LANGUAGE plpgsql AS $ BEGIN INSERT INTO audit_log(table_name, operation, changed_by, changed_at, old_data, new_data) VALUES ( TG_TABLE_NAME, TG_OP, current_user, to_char(now(), 'YYYY-MM-DD"T"HH24:MI:SS'), CASE WHEN TG_OP = 'DELETE' THEN row_to_json(OLD)::text ELSE NULL END, CASE WHEN TG_OP IN ('INSERT','UPDATE') THEN row_to_json(NEW)::text ELSE NULL END ); RETURN NULL; END; $; The trigger fires AFTER INSERT OR UPDATE OR DELETE on each monitored table and writes a row to audit_log . The audit log itself should be insert-only: revoke UPDATE and DELETE privileges from all application roles. A trigger fires after each DML on the business table, writes an immutable row to audit_log, and the audit log is consumed by reporting or SIEM tools. What Makes a Good Audit Log An audit log entry should contain enough context to reconstruct exactly what happened: Who — the database user ( current_user ), plus the application-level user if your app tracks that in a session variable When — timestamp with time zone (not just a date) What — the operation type and the affected table Old state / New state — for UPDATE and DELETE, the previous values; for INSERT, the new values Where from — client IP address (available in inet_client_addr() in PostgreSQL) Application-Level User Tracking The database only knows the database role. If five hundred users share one app_user role, the audit log shows app_user for all of them. Pass the application user ID through a session variable: -- Set at the start of each session SELECT set_config('app.user_id', '12345', false); -- Read in the trigger current_setting('app.user_id', true) Querying the Audit Log Interactive SQL · Audit Log Queries Protecting the Audit Log An audit log is only useful if it cannot be tampered with. Apply the following: Revoke modification rights — application roles should have INSERT only on audit_log , never UPDATE or DELETE . Ship logs off the database — stream logs to a separate system (Splunk, Datadog, S3) in real time. If an attacker compromises the database, they should not also be able to erase the trail. Immutable log stores — cloud storage with Object Lock (S3 Object Lock, GCS retention policies) makes log files legally immutable for a configured retention period. Rotate and archive — partition the audit log by month; archive old partitions to cold storage to keep the active table fast. Key Takeaways Log connections, DDL, privilege changes, and DML on sensitive tables at minimum; avoid logging everything (it creates noise and storage pressure). Use trigger-based audit tables to capture old and new values for changes — the database log alone does not record this. Always record who, what, when, and from where — including the application-layer user ID if multiple users share a database role. Protect the audit log itself : insert-only for application roles, shipped to an external immutable store. An audit log that exists only inside the database is a liability: a compromised database can be used to erase the trail. ← Previous Encryption in transit and at rest Next → Secrets management

title: Secrets management
text: Every application that talks to a database needs a secret: a password, a connection string, an API key for a cloud database proxy. The moment that secret is hard-coded in source code, pasted into a CI environment variable, or checked into a .env file in a repository, it becomes a liability that will eventually be breached. Secrets management is the discipline of storing, distributing, rotating, and auditing those secrets safely. The Problem with Hard-Coded Secrets Hard-coded database credentials appear in: Source files committed to version control ( .env , application.properties , config.yaml ) Docker images baked with environment variables CI/CD pipeline logs that echo environment variables during test runs Crash dumps and error messages that include connection strings Any one of these leak paths can expose credentials that allow an attacker to connect directly to the database with the same privileges as the application. Because leaked credentials are often not rotated, a single leak can be exploited months or years later. The fix is never to have secrets in those locations at all. Secrets Stores A secrets store is a purpose-built service that stores secrets encrypted, enforces access control, provides audit logs for every secret access, and supports automatic rotation. HashiCorp Vault Vault is the most widely deployed open-source secrets manager. Applications authenticate to Vault (using a cloud IAM role, Kubernetes service account, or AppRole) and receive a short-lived secret: # Write a database password to Vault vault kv put secret/myapp/db password="s3cr3t" # Read it back (application does this at startup) vault kv get -field=password secret/myapp/db Vault's database secrets engine goes further: it generates ephemeral, per-lease credentials directly in the database and revokes them automatically when the lease expires: # Configure Vault's database engine for PostgreSQL vault write database/config/mydb \ plugin_name=postgresql-database-plugin \ connection_url="postgresql://vault_admin:{{password}}@db:5432/myapp" \ allowed_roles="app-role" # Vault creates and returns a temporary credential vault read database/creds/app-role # Key Value # --- ----- # username v-app-role-abc123 # password A1B2-C3D4-E5F6 # lease_duration 1h The application gets credentials that expire in one hour. Even if the credential is leaked, the damage window is bounded. Cloud-Native Secret Managers Provider Service Key feature AWS Secrets Manager Automatic rotation for RDS; deep integration with IAM GCP Secret Manager Version-controlled secrets; CMEK encryption Azure Key Vault Stores secrets, keys, and certificates; HSM-backed AWS Secrets Manager can automatically rotate an RDS password on a schedule, updating both the Secrets Manager entry and the database password — zero manual intervention required. # Python: retrieve secret from AWS Secrets Manager at runtime import boto3, json client = boto3.client('secretsmanager', region_name='us-east-1') secret = json.loads(client.get_secret_value(SecretId='myapp/db')['SecretString']) conn_string = f"postgresql://{secret['username']}:{secret['password']}@{secret['host']}/myapp" The application authenticates to the secrets store using its IAM role (not a password). The store returns a short-lived database credential that expires automatically. Secret Rotation Rotation is the practice of regularly replacing secrets with new values. Why it matters: Limits the damage window of a leaked secret Satisfies compliance requirements (PCI-DSS, SOC 2, HIPAA all mandate rotation) Forces validation that the rotation process actually works Rotation Strategies Zero-downtime rotation for database passwords requires a two-phase approach: Phase 1: Create a new password and update both the database and the secrets store. The old password is still valid. Phase 2: After all running application instances have picked up the new secret, revoke the old password. AWS Secrets Manager automates this for RDS via a Lambda rotation function. For self-managed databases, Vault's database engine handles it through lease renewal. -- Manual rotation example: add new password, then revoke old ALTER ROLE app_user PASSWORD 'new_strong_password_v2'; -- ...after confirming apps use new password... -- (old password already overwritten above in PostgreSQL single-user model) Avoid long-lived shared passwords. If the same password has been used for two years and shared across teams, assume it is compromised. Rotate immediately and use Vault or a cloud secrets manager going forward. Environment Variables Done Right Environment variables are better than hard-coded secrets in source, but still have risks: they can appear in /proc , crash dumps, and docker inspect output. The correct pattern: # BAD: secret visible in shell history and process list export DATABASE_URL="postgresql://user:password@host/db" # BETTER: pull from secrets manager at startup, never store in env export DATABASE_URL=$(vault kv get -field=url secret/myapp/db) Better still: use a secrets manager SDK in the application and pull the secret programmatically at startup, never passing it through environment variables at all. Simulating Secret Access Patterns Interactive SQL · Secret Access Audit Key Takeaways Never hard-code secrets in source code, Docker images, or CI/CD environment variables — a single repository leak exposes all environments simultaneously. Use a dedicated secrets store — HashiCorp Vault or a cloud-native equivalent — that provides access control, audit logs, and automatic rotation. Prefer ephemeral, per-service credentials with short TTLs over long-lived shared passwords; the breach window shrinks from years to hours. Rotate regularly and test your rotation process before an incident forces you to use it under pressure. Audit every secret access: who read or rotated what, and when — this is the first place you look during a security investigation. ← Previous Auditing and logging Next → Multi-tenant isolation

title: Multi-tenant isolation
text: When a single database instance serves multiple customers — or multiple teams, environments, or organizational units — each group's data must be completely invisible to the others. This is multi-tenant isolation : ensuring that tenant A cannot read, modify, or even discover tenant B's data, even if both are authenticated to the same database. Getting this wrong does not just violate a policy; it violates the trust of every customer whose data leaks. The Three Isolation Models There is a fundamental trade-off in multi-tenant database design between isolation strength and resource efficiency . Three models sit at different points on that spectrum: Model Isolation mechanism Shared resources Typical use case Database per tenant Separate databases Server, OS, connection pool (partially) High-compliance SaaS, enterprise on-premise Schema per tenant Separate schemas in one database One database instance Mid-market SaaS Shared schema with tenant column Application + RLS policy Tables, indexes, storage High-scale SaaS with many tenants Database per Tenant Each tenant gets their own database. Maximum isolation: a bug or a compromised application cannot cross tenant boundaries at the database level. Drawbacks: resource overhead grows linearly with tenant count; schema migrations must be applied to every database; connection pooling is more complex. -- PostgreSQL: create a completely separate database per tenant CREATE DATABASE tenant_acme; CREATE DATABASE tenant_globex; -- Each database has its own roles, tables, and data Schema per Tenant All tenants share one database, but each gets their own schema. The schema name acts as a namespace: CREATE SCHEMA tenant_acme; CREATE SCHEMA tenant_globex; -- Same table structure, isolated data CREATE TABLE tenant_acme.orders (...); CREATE TABLE tenant_globex.orders (...); -- Set search_path per connection to restrict access SET search_path = tenant_acme; SELECT * FROM orders; -- sees only acme's orders Schema-per-tenant is popular because migrations can be applied programmatically across all schemas, and isolation is enforced at the schema permission level. Shared Schema with Row-Level Security The most resource-efficient model: one set of tables, every row tagged with a tenant_id column, and database-enforced row-level security policies that automatically filter every query. -- Shared orders table with tenant tag CREATE TABLE orders ( id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, tenant_id INTEGER NOT NULL, amount NUMERIC, status TEXT ); -- Enable RLS ALTER TABLE orders ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY; ALTER TABLE orders FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY; -- Policy: only see your own tenant's rows CREATE POLICY tenant_isolation ON orders USING (tenant_id = current_setting('app.tenant_id')::integer); -- Application sets this at session start: SELECT set_config('app.tenant_id', '42', false); After this policy is in place, SELECT * FROM orders always returns only the current tenant's rows — regardless of what the application sends. A developer bug that omits a WHERE tenant_id = ? clause is automatically corrected by the database. The three multi-tenancy models trade isolation for density. Shared schema with RLS is most efficient but requires careful policy design. Common Pitfalls Forgetting FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY By default, table owners bypass RLS. If the application connects as the table owner, policies are ignored. Always use FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY on tenant-shared tables, or connect as a non-owner role: ALTER TABLE orders FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY; Tenant ID Injection If the application sets app.tenant_id via SQL, a malicious or buggy application could set it to a different tenant's ID. Mitigate by: Setting the tenant ID immediately after connection, in the connection pool checkout hook Using a separate role per tenant in the schema-per-tenant model Treating tenant_id as a server-side session attribute, not a client-controlled variable Cross-Tenant Joins In a shared-schema model, a badly written query can still join across tenants if RLS is not applied uniformly. Enable RLS on every table that holds tenant-scoped data, not just the primary table. Backup Isolation Backups of a shared database contain all tenants' data. Consider whether your tenants' contracts require the ability to produce per-tenant exports or perform per-tenant restores. Interactive Example Interactive SQL · Tenant Isolation Simulation Key Takeaways Choose an isolation model based on tenant count, compliance requirements, and operational capacity: database per tenant for highest isolation, shared schema with RLS for highest density. Row-level security is the most efficient mechanism for shared-schema multi-tenancy, but requires FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY and RLS policies on every tenant-scoped table. Set the tenant_id session variable on the server side at connection time — never trust client-supplied values. Apply RLS uniformly: a missed table in a shared schema is a cross-tenant data leak waiting to happen. Plan for per-tenant backup and restore: even if data is physically co-located, tenants expect logical isolation at every level. ← Previous Secrets management Next → SQL injection and safe queries

title: SQL injection and safe queries
text: SQL injection is among the oldest and most consistently exploited vulnerabilities in software. It has persisted for decades not because it is subtle or hard to understand, but because the fix requires consistent discipline, and a single lapse anywhere in a large codebase is enough to expose the entire database. This page explains exactly how injection works at the query-parsing level, why parameterized queries prevent it, and the additional defenses that reduce impact even when injection occurs. How SQL Injection Works SQL injection exploits the fact that SQL query text and user-supplied data are mixed together in a single string. When the database receives the string, it cannot distinguish intended SQL syntax from injected syntax — it parses and executes everything. Consider a login check: # VULNERABLE: string concatenation username = request.form['username'] query = "SELECT id FROM users WHERE username = '" + username + "'" db.execute(query) A legitimate user submits alice . The query becomes: SELECT id FROM users WHERE username = 'alice' An attacker submits ' OR '1'='1 . The query becomes: SELECT id FROM users WHERE username = '' OR '1'='1' The OR '1'='1' is always true, so the query returns every row in the users table. Authentication is bypassed completely. A more destructive payload: '; DROP TABLE users; -- SELECT id FROM users WHERE username = ''; DROP TABLE users; --' The first statement returns nothing; the second drops the entire table; -- comments out the trailing quote. Parameterized Queries: The Only Real Fix The correct defense is to never construct queries by string concatenation. Instead, send the query template and the parameters separately — the database driver handles escaping at the protocol level before the SQL parser ever sees the input. # SAFE: parameterized query (Python / psycopg2) cursor.execute( "SELECT id FROM users WHERE username = %s", (username,) ) // SAFE: parameterized query (Node.js / pg) const result = await client.query( 'SELECT id FROM users WHERE username = $1', [username] ); // SAFE: PreparedStatement (Java / JDBC) PreparedStatement stmt = conn.prepareStatement( "SELECT id FROM users WHERE username = ?"); stmt.setString(1, username); With parameterized queries, the attacker's ' OR '1'='1 is treated as the literal string value to match against username , not as SQL syntax. It simply fails to find a matching row. ORMs are not automatically safe. Most ORM methods are parameterized by default, but all provide escape hatches for raw SQL. Any use of raw SQL in an ORM must be audited and parameterized. User.where("username = '#{params[:username]}'" in Rails is just as vulnerable as string concatenation in plain SQL. String concatenation merges attacker-controlled content into the SQL AST. Parameterized queries send template and data separately — the parser never sees user input as SQL syntax. Dynamic SQL: When You Must Construct Queries Sometimes query structure itself must be dynamic — sorting by a user-chosen column, filtering by a runtime-chosen operator. Parameterized queries handle values but not identifiers (column names, table names, operators). The safe approach for identifiers is allowlisting : # SAFE: allowlist of permitted column names ALLOWED_SORT_COLUMNS = {'id', 'created_at', 'amount', 'status'} sort_col = request.args.get('sort', 'id') if sort_col not in ALLOWED_SORT_COLUMNS: sort_col = 'id' query = f"SELECT * FROM orders ORDER BY {sort_col}" # sort_col is now guaranteed to be a safe identifier Never use user input directly as an identifier. There is no escaping mechanism that safely handles all edge cases across all databases. Defense in Depth Parameterized queries stop injection at the source. Additional layers reduce the blast radius if injection somehow occurs: Least privilege — the application database role has only SELECT , INSERT , UPDATE , DELETE ; no DROP , no EXECUTE on dangerous functions. Even a successful injection cannot drop tables or read arbitrary files. Web Application Firewall (WAF) — signature-based SQL injection detection at the HTTP layer. Not a substitute for parameterized queries, but catches opportunistic scanners. Error message suppression — never return raw database error messages to users. They reveal table names, column names, and query structure. Log errors server-side; return a generic error to the client. Stored procedures — centralizing data access in stored procedures limits the SQL surface area exposed to the application. If the application can only call CALL get_user_by_id(?) and not construct arbitrary queries, injection is structurally limited. Interactive Example Interactive SQL · Injection vs. Safe Query Key Takeaways SQL injection is structural : it works by merging user input into SQL syntax. String escaping is fragile; parameterized queries are the only reliable fix. Parameterize everything — values, filters, LIMIT values, pagination offsets. The only things you cannot parameterize are SQL identifiers (column and table names); those require an allowlist. ORMs are not magic : any raw SQL path in an ORM must be parameterized explicitly. Use least privilege so that a successful injection has limited impact — an attacker who can only run SELECT cannot drop your schema. Suppress database error messages in production; they provide attackers with the structural information needed to refine their payload. ← Previous Multi-tenant isolation Next → Privacy and data retention

title: Privacy and data retention
text: Databases routinely accumulate more personal data than they need to, for longer than they are permitted to keep it. Privacy is the practice of collecting and storing only the data that is necessary, protecting it appropriately, and giving individuals meaningful control over it. Data retention is the practice of deleting or anonymizing data when it is no longer needed or permitted. Both are increasingly non-optional: GDPR, CCPA, HIPAA, and similar laws impose strict obligations — and significant penalties — on organizations that handle personal data carelessly. Privacy Principles in Database Design Data Minimization Collect only the personal data that is strictly necessary for the stated purpose. Every field you store is a field that can be breached, subpoenaed, or disclosed by accident. Before adding a column: What business purpose does this serve? Is this the minimum data that satisfies that purpose? How long is this data needed? A date of birth might be needed to verify an age threshold. The full date is more than necessary — just a boolean is_over_18 or the year alone may suffice. Purpose Limitation Data collected for one purpose should not be repurposed for another without explicit consent. A common database smell: an events table collected for usage analytics that slowly becomes the source of truth for user profile data, fraud detection, and marketing — all without the user's knowledge. Data Accuracy Personal data that is incorrect can cause real harm (denial of services, financial errors, legal problems). Provide mechanisms for users to correct their data, and don't over-denormalize personal fields — a single authoritative location is easier to correct than copies scattered across ten tables. Implementing Right to Erasure ("Right to be Forgotten") GDPR Article 17 grants users the right to have their personal data deleted. In a relational database, this is harder than it sounds: The user's email address may be in users , email_campaigns , audit_logs , orders , support_tickets , and a dozen other tables. Foreign key constraints may prevent deletion of parent records while children exist. Backup tapes and data warehouse snapshots may contain the data even after it is deleted from the live database. A practical approach uses pseudonymization rather than deletion for records that are referenced by other data: -- Replace PII with anonymized values; preserve referential integrity UPDATE users SET email = 'deleted-' || id || '@anonymized.invalid', full_name = 'Deleted User', phone = NULL, date_of_birth = NULL, deleted_at = to_char(now(), 'YYYY-MM-DD"T"HH24:MI:SS') WHERE id = :user_id; The user's orders and financial records (needed for accounting and legal compliance) remain intact and linked by user_id , but the personal identifiers are gone. Tracking Erasure Requests Maintain a table that records each erasure request and its status — regulators can ask you to prove that you honored the request: CREATE TABLE erasure_requests ( id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, user_id INTEGER NOT NULL, requested_at TEXT NOT NULL, completed_at TEXT, status TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'pending' ); PII flows through a defined lifecycle. A retention policy determines when data transitions from active use to anonymization or deletion — both GDPR and CCPA require this to be explicit and enforceable. Data Retention Policies A retention policy defines: for each category of data, how long it is kept, and what happens to it afterward. Common retention periods: Data category Typical retention Regulation User PII (names, emails) Duration of account + 30 days post-deletion GDPR Financial transaction records 7 years Tax law (varies by country) Audit logs 1–7 years SOC 2, PCI-DSS Server / application logs 90 days Common practice; some regs require longer Health records 7–10 years (adults) HIPAA Marketing consent records 3 years after consent GDPR recital 171 Enforcing Retention with Scheduled Deletions -- Soft-deleted accounts older than 30 days: anonymize PII UPDATE users SET email = 'anon-' || id || '@deleted.invalid', full_name = 'Deleted', phone = NULL WHERE deleted_at IS NOT NULL AND deleted_at < to_char(now() - interval '30 days', 'YYYY-MM-DD"T"HH24:MI:SS'); -- Purge log rows older than retention period DELETE FROM app_logs WHERE created_at < to_char(now() - interval '90 days', 'YYYY-MM-DD"T"HH24:MI:SS'); Run these as scheduled jobs (cron, pg_cron, cloud scheduler). Make them idempotent so a failed run can be re-executed without harm. Data Masking for Non-Production Environments A significant percentage of data breaches involve development, staging, or analytics environments — which often receive production data copies without the same controls. Data masking replaces PII with realistic but fake values before populating non-production databases: Field Original Masked email alice@example.com anon_4921@test.invalid phone +1-555-234-5678 +1-555-000-0001 SSN 123-45-6789 XXX-XX-6789 credit card 4111-1111-1111-1111 4000-XXXX-XXXX-0000 Tools like faker libraries, Delphix, or AWS DMS data transformation rules automate masking during database refresh. Interactive Example Interactive SQL · Anonymization and Retention Key Takeaways Minimize collection : every personal data field is a liability — collect only what you need, store it only as long as you need it. Implement pseudonymization on deletion : nullify or replace PII when an account is deleted, but keep the record shell for referential integrity and legal records. Define and enforce retention policies per data category : automate the cleanup with scheduled jobs so it actually runs. Mask PII before copying to non-production environments — staging and dev databases are a common breach vector. Maintain an erasure request log to demonstrate compliance; regulators expect proof that you honored deletion requests. ← Previous SQL injection and safe queries Next → Secure database habits

title: Secure database habits
text: Security is not a feature you add at the end — it is a set of habits applied consistently across every decision: how you configure databases, how you write queries, how you handle credentials, and how you respond when something goes wrong. This page collects the most high-value, day-to-day practices that collectively move a database deployment from "probably fine" to genuinely defensible. Think of it as a checklist you run once per project and revisit on every new deployment. Principle of Least Privilege — Applied Everywhere The single most impactful habit is never granting more access than strictly needed. Apply it at every level: Database roles : one role per service, each with only the tables and operations it uses. The payment service does not need to read the analytics schema. OS users : the database process should run as a non-root user with minimal file system permissions. Network : the database port should not be reachable from the public internet. Place it in a private subnet; access via bastion host, VPN, or IAM-authenticated proxy only. Cloud IAM : if using a managed database service, restrict which IAM roles can list, describe, or modify the database instance. -- Revoke dangerous defaults REVOKE ALL ON DATABASE myapp FROM PUBLIC; REVOKE CREATE ON SCHEMA public FROM PUBLIC; -- Grant exactly what the app role needs GRANT CONNECT ON DATABASE myapp TO app_user; GRANT USAGE ON SCHEMA public TO app_user; GRANT SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA public TO app_user; Network Hardening Bind to the Right Interface By default, some databases bind to all interfaces ( 0.0.0.0 ). Bind to the private interface only: # PostgreSQL postgresql.conf listen_addresses = '10.0.1.5' # private IP only, not 0.0.0.0 # MySQL my.cnf bind-address = 10.0.1.5 Firewall Rules Allow inbound traffic on the database port only from the application server's IP range and your bastion/VPN. Deny everything else at the security group or firewall level — not at the database level, which is a second line of defense. Disable Unused Features Every enabled feature is attack surface. Disable what you don't use: -- PostgreSQL: disable COPY TO/FROM FILE (reads/writes OS files) -- Achieved by not granting pg_read_server_files / pg_write_server_files -- MySQL: disable local_infile (reads local files via LOAD DATA LOCAL INFILE) SET GLOBAL local_infile = 0; Configuration Hardening Setting Default Secure practice log_connections off on — log all auth attempts log_statement none ddl — capture all schema changes password_encryption md5 (old PG) scram-sha-256 ssl off (some installs) on — require TLS shared_preload_libraries empty Include pgaudit for audit logging client_min_messages notice error — don't leak schema info to clients Regularly review the configuration. Cloud provider defaults are not always security-optimal. Run a configuration review whenever you upgrade the database engine — new versions may change defaults or introduce new settings. Defense in depth: each layer addresses a different attack category. A breach of one layer does not automatically compromise the next. Patch Management Databases receive security patches regularly. Unpatched databases are among the most common breach vectors: Subscribe to security advisories for your database (PostgreSQL security announcements, MySQL security advisories, CVE feeds). Test patches in staging before applying to production — major version upgrades require careful compatibility testing; minor patches are usually safe to apply quickly. Automate minor updates where possible. Cloud managed databases (RDS, Cloud SQL) support automatic minor version upgrades during maintenance windows. Track your version across all environments — it is surprisingly common for staging and production to run different versions. Incident Response Readiness A breach is a question of when, not if. Be prepared: Know where your logs are — audit logs, connection logs, slow query logs — and confirm they are being shipped to an external system before you need them. Practice revocation : can you revoke a compromised service account credential in under five minutes? Test it in staging. Have a runbook for the "all credentials may be compromised" scenario: rotate every secret, invalidate every active session, alert on new connection attempts. Enable point-in-time recovery (PITR) : if data is deleted or corrupted, you need to be able to restore to a moment just before the incident. Interactive Security Checklist Simulation Interactive SQL · Security Configuration Audit The Security Habit Loop Security erodes without maintenance. Build these habits into your regular engineering cycle: Every deploy : verify that new tables have RLS if they hold tenant or user data; verify that new secrets go into the secrets manager, not the config file. Every quarter : review role memberships and revoke stale access; rotate long-lived credentials; check for unpatched versions. Every year : full access review; test your incident response runbook; verify backup restores actually work. After every incident : update threat models, close identified gaps, and add new security checks to your audit query. Key Takeaways Least privilege everywhere : network, OS, database roles, cloud IAM — each layer applies the same principle independently. Harden the configuration out of the box: disable unused features, bind to private interfaces, require TLS and strong password hashing. Patch promptly : subscribe to security advisories and apply patches quickly, especially for CVEs affecting your database engine. Prepare for incidents before they happen : know where your logs are, test credential revocation, and maintain point-in-time recovery. Security is a recurring habit loop , not a one-time setup — build quarterly reviews and per-deploy checks into your normal workflow. ← Previous Privacy and data retention Next → Chapter 25 Summary: What We Learned

title: Chapter 25 Summary: What We Learned
text: Page Topic Takeaway in One Sentence 241 Authentication and authorization Authentication verifies identity (who you are); authorization controls access (what you can do). Both are essential. 242 Roles and permissions Group permissions into roles (read-only, read-write, admin); grant roles to users — simpler to audit and manage. 243 Encryption in transit and at rest TLS/SSL for data in transit; transparent data encryption (TDE) or filesystem encryption for data at rest. 244 Auditing and logging Log who accessed what, when, and what changed — essential for compliance (GDPR, SOC 2) and forensic investigation. 245 Secrets management Never hardcode credentials; use a secrets manager (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager) and rotate credentials regularly. 246 Multi-tenant isolation Separate databases, separate schemas, or row-level security — each level trades isolation strength for operational simplicity. 247 SQL injection and safe queries Use parameterized queries or an ORM. Never concatenate user input into SQL strings. The number one database security vulnerability. 248 Privacy and data retention Minimize data collection; set retention policies; implement soft-delete and anonymization for GDPR/CCPA compliance. 249 Secure database habits Apply least-privilege access, update regularly, restrict network access, and review access logs. Security is a process, not a checkbox. Reveal: How do these ideas connect to the next chapter? Chapter 25 covered securing a database once you have one. But how do you decide which database to use in the first place? Chapter 26 pulls together everything you have learned — data models, query patterns, consistency needs, operational burden — into a decision framework for choosing a database. You will learn to evaluate databases against your workload, not against Hacker News hype; when to use managed services versus self-hosted; and how to avoid vendor lock-in without sacrificing productivity. ← Previous Secure database habits Next → Start from workload, not fashion

title: Start from workload, not fashion
text: Every year a new database system arrives with compelling benchmarks and enthusiastic blog posts. Teams that chase those signals — adopting the newest store because it's exciting, or because a conference talk made it sound inevitable — often find themselves fighting their tooling instead of building their product. The antidote is a simple discipline: start from the workload, not from the technology . What "Workload" Actually Means A workload is the specific combination of reads, writes, queries, and access patterns your application produces. It has measurable properties: Read/write ratio — mostly reads (a product catalog), mostly writes (an event log), or balanced (a transactional ledger)? Access pattern — exact-key lookups, range scans, full-text search, graph traversal, aggregate analytics? Dataset size — fits in RAM, fits on one disk, requires sharding across many machines? Latency requirements — milliseconds (user-facing), seconds (reporting), minutes (batch)? Consistency requirements — must every read see the latest write, or is eventual consistency acceptable? None of these properties can be answered by reading a database's marketing page. They come from understanding your own application. Workload properties should drive database selection — not conference buzz, not what another company uses. The Danger of Fashion-Driven Selection Fashion-driven selection happens in two directions: Chasing novelty. A graph database sounds exciting when you're building a social feature. But if your "graph" is a hundred users with an average of three connections each, a single friends table in PostgreSQL with a self-join handles it fine — forever. You've added an operational dependency and a new query language for no performance gain. Clinging to familiarity. The opposite error is equally common: reaching for the relational database you already know when the workload is genuinely a poor fit. Writing time-series sensor data to a normalized OLTP schema — with a new row per reading, a timestamp column, and a query that does GROUP BY hour — works, but a time-series database like TimescaleDB or InfluxDB will store, compress, and query that data an order of magnitude more efficiently. Rule of thumb: If you cannot describe your workload in concrete numbers — rows per second, query latency target, expected dataset size in 12 months — you are not ready to choose a database. Workload First in Practice A useful exercise before any database decision: Write down the five most-frequent queries or writes your application will issue. Not abstract descriptions — actual examples with realistic data shapes. Estimate the volume. How many times per second will each occur at peak load? Identify the bottleneck. Is it read latency? Write throughput? Storage size? Query expressiveness? Match to a category. Key-value if you always know the full key. Column-family if you're appending time-ordered events. Relational if you need ad-hoc joins and transactions. Document if your schema evolves frequently and values are deeply nested. Prototype under load. Benchmarks from the vendor's website use their ideal workload, not yours. A 30-minute proof-of-concept with your actual data shapes more often than not reveals surprises. Workload description checklist ────────────────────────────── [ ] Primary access pattern (point, range, graph, aggregate) [ ] Read:write ratio (e.g. 95:5 reads heavy) [ ] Peak writes per second [ ] Peak reads per second [ ] p99 latency target (ms) [ ] Dataset size today / in 12 months [ ] Consistency requirement (strong / eventual / session) [ ] Durability requirement (sync / async replication) [ ] Team's existing operational expertise The Right Default For most new products — where the schema is evolving, queries are unpredictable, and team size is small — a general-purpose relational database (PostgreSQL or similar) is the correct default. It supports ACID transactions, rich queries, joins, and a mature ecosystem of tools. You can add a specialized store later, precisely, when a specific workload proves that it's necessary. The pattern to avoid: designing your data layer around a database's interesting features rather than around what your application actually does. Interactive SQL · Workload Analysis Key Takeaways A workload has measurable properties: access pattern, volume, latency budget, consistency needs. Fashion-driven selection — chasing novelty or clinging to familiarity — leads to mismatched tooling. Describe your workload in concrete numbers before evaluating any database. Default to a general-purpose relational database; add specialized stores only when a specific workload justifies the operational cost. ← Previous Secure database habits Next → Questions to ask before choosing

title: Questions to ask before choosing
text: Picking a database without a structured evaluation is like buying a house by looking only at the photos. The listing is designed to show the best angles. The questions you ask — about the neighbourhood, the plumbing, the neighbours — are what surface the real trade-offs. The same discipline applies when evaluating databases: a short checklist of pointed questions cuts through vendor marketing and reveals whether a system actually fits your situation. The Seven Questions 1. What is the primary access pattern? This is the most important question. Access patterns map almost directly to database categories: Pattern Example Strong fit Exact key lookup GET session:user:42 Key-value store Range scan on ordered key All orders from Jan–Mar B-tree index, wide-column Flexible ad-hoc queries "All users in France who bought X" Relational Deeply nested documents User profiles with variable fields Document store Relationship traversal Friends of friends Graph database Time-bucketed aggregates Avg CPU per minute for 30 days Time-series database Large analytical scans Revenue by region, cohort analysis Columnar / data warehouse A single product often has multiple access patterns — which is a reason to consider multiple specialized stores, or a reason to pick one system that handles the dominant pattern well and tolerates the rest. 2. What are the read/write characteristics? Read-heavy (>90% reads): Optimise for read latency; caching, read replicas, and columnar layouts all help. Write-heavy: LSM-tree engines (RocksDB, Cassandra, ScyllaDB) are optimised for high-throughput sequential writes. B-tree engines achieve 10–50K writes/second on a single node, depending on SSD performance and index count. Mixed, transactional: Relational databases with row-level locking are designed for this balance. 3. What is the consistency requirement? Consistency is a spectrum. Stronger guarantees typically cost latency or availability; eventual consistency trades correctness risk for throughput. Transactions and bank balances demand strong consistency. Social media "like" counts can tolerate eventual consistency — being off by one for a few seconds is invisible to users. Knowing which camp your data falls into eliminates entire categories of databases. 4. How large is the dataset, and how fast is it growing? Fits in RAM (< ~100 GB): In-memory stores like Redis are viable and extremely fast. Fits on one machine (< ~10 TB): A well-tuned single-node relational or document database handles this comfortably. Requires horizontal sharding (> 10 TB, or > ~50K writes/s): You need a distributed database. This adds significant operational complexity and consistency trade-offs. Growth rate matters as much as current size. A dataset that doubles every six months will cross the sharding threshold in a predictable timeline; design for it early, or plan the migration path explicitly. 5. What are the latency and throughput targets? Be specific. "Fast" is not a target. These numbers drive hardware sizing and architecture: p50 read latency target: _____ ms p99 read latency target: _____ ms peak read throughput: _____ req/s peak write throughput: _____ req/s acceptable downtime/year: _____ minutes (maps to SLA %) 6. What is the team's operational expertise? A database that is theoretically optimal but that nobody on the team understands is a reliability risk. Every specialised database brings its own operational burden: backup strategy, monitoring, cluster management, failure modes, and upgrade path. PostgreSQL has two decades of documentation and community knowledge. A newer distributed database may be superior on paper and a nightmare to operate with a three-person team. Honest question: If this database wakes you up at 3 am, does your team know how to diagnose and recover from failure? 7. What are the schema evolution requirements? Relational databases require schema migrations — adding a column, renaming a field, changing a type all require a planned migration step. For a stable, well-understood domain this is fine; the schema enforces correctness. For a product where the data model changes every sprint, the overhead is real. Document databases that allow schemaless writes trade that migration overhead for the risk of inconsistent data shapes across documents over time. Assembling the Checklist Interactive SQL · Database Evaluation Scoring Key Takeaways Seven questions cover the essential evaluation surface: access pattern, read/write balance, consistency, dataset size, latency targets, team expertise, and schema evolution needs. Consistency and access pattern together eliminate most candidates quickly. Operational complexity is a real cost — the best database for a given workload is not necessarily the best database for a given team. Write the answers to these questions down before looking at any benchmark or product page. ← Previous Start from workload, not fashion Next → One database or many

title: One database or many
text: Many applications start with a single database that handles everything: user records, product catalog, orders, session state, analytics events. That's the right starting point — one system is dramatically simpler to operate, monitor, back up, and reason about. But as products grow, different parts of the system develop genuinely different needs, and the question of whether to introduce a second (or third) database becomes unavoidable. Getting this decision right — neither splitting too early nor holding on too long — is one of the most consequential architectural choices a team makes. The Case for One Database A single database is not a compromise. For most applications at most stages of their life, it is the correct answer: Transactions span naturally. Moving money between accounts, placing an order while decrementing inventory, updating a user profile and their associated records — all of these require atomicity across multiple entities. With one database, a single BEGIN / COMMIT block handles it. With multiple databases, you need distributed transactions or compensating logic, both of which are significantly harder to implement and test. No data synchronisation. Every time you write data to two stores, you introduce the risk of them diverging. Network partitions, failed writes, and clock skew all create windows where the stores disagree. One place to query. Debugging a production issue when data lives in one place is already non-trivial. When the user record is in PostgreSQL, the activity feed is in Cassandra, and the session is in Redis, a single user complaint requires correlating three different query interfaces. Simpler operations. One database means one backup strategy, one monitoring setup, one team who knows how to handle failure, one upgrade path. A single database handles everything simply; polyglot persistence optimises each workload at the cost of cross-store consistency and operational complexity. When One Database Is No Longer Enough The signal to introduce a second database is a specific, measurable bottleneck that the primary database cannot address without unreasonable cost. Common triggers: Bottleneck Typical specialisation Rationale Session lookup latency (>5 ms) Add Redis In-memory key-value at sub-millisecond latency Full-text search quality Add Elasticsearch Inverted index, relevance ranking Write throughput for events/metrics Add TimescaleDB or InfluxDB Optimised time-series compression and rollups Analytical query time (hours on OLTP) Add a columnar warehouse Scans optimised for aggregation, not row access Graph traversal depth Add Neo4j Native graph index, no recursive CTE hacks Notice the pattern: each trigger is a concrete problem in production, not a theoretical preference. Anti-pattern: Introducing a second database because you read that Netflix uses it, or because the engineering team finds the new technology interesting. Both are strong signals to resist. The Synchronisation Tax Every specialised store that shadows your primary database requires a synchronisation mechanism: Change Data Capture (CDC): Stream database change events (via tools like Debezium) into the secondary store. Eventually consistent — the secondary lags by seconds to minutes. Dual writes: The application writes to both stores in the same code path. Fast, but any write failure creates divergence. Requires careful error handling. Read-through / write-through cache: The application always reads through the cache; a cache miss fetches from the primary and populates the cache. Simpler, but only works for cache-shaped use cases. Each approach has failure modes. The key insight is that the synchronisation mechanism is itself a piece of infrastructure that must be monitored, tested for failure, and maintained. Practical Guidance Start with one. Even if you anticipate needing search or caching eventually, add them when you have the problem, not before. A premature polyglot architecture pays the operational complexity cost immediately and delays the performance benefit to some future date that may never arrive. Add the second store for a specific, isolated use case. The session cache, the search index, the analytics warehouse — each should have a clear boundary. The primary database remains the system of record; the specialised store is a derived view of that data optimised for one access pattern. Plan the failure mode. What happens if Redis goes down? Does your application fall back to the relational session table (slower but correct), or does it stop working entirely? The answer should be "graceful degradation to the primary." Interactive SQL · Simulating Dual-Store Session Fallback Key Takeaways One database is simpler to operate and enables cross-entity transactions; it is the right default. Add a second store only when a specific, measured bottleneck in production justifies the synchronisation complexity. Every specialised store requires a sync mechanism — CDC, dual writes, or cache-aside — each with its own failure modes. The primary database remains the system of record; specialised stores are derived, eventually-consistent views. ← Previous Questions to ask before choosing Next → Build vs buy

title: Build vs buy
text: Every database-adjacent decision eventually surfaces this question: should we build it ourselves or adopt an existing system? The question is rarely about databases themselves — you almost never build a storage engine from scratch — but it appears constantly at a higher level. Should you build a custom caching layer or adopt Redis? Should you build an analytics pipeline or use a managed data warehouse? Should you write a full-text search service or deploy Elasticsearch? The reasoning framework is the same regardless of the specific technology. What "Build" Actually Means "Build" rarely means writing a database engine from scratch. More commonly it means: Building a custom abstraction on top of a general-purpose store (e.g., a time-series schema on top of PostgreSQL) Writing bespoke pipeline code instead of adopting a CDC or ETL framework Implementing your own full-text search instead of integrating Elasticsearch or Typesense Building a custom in-memory cache instead of deploying Redis The real question is: are the off-the-shelf solutions a worse fit for your workload than the solution your team would build with the time and resources available? The Build Bias Is Almost Always Wrong Building has obvious appeal: perfect control, no vendor dependency, exactly the features you need. But the costs are consistently underestimated: Initial investment: The prototype is fast; production-ready is slow. A robust system needs failure handling, monitoring, backup, recovery, replication, performance tuning, and documentation. An experienced team building a reliable cache from scratch in front of a production database is committing months of engineering time. Maintenance is forever. The team that builds a system is implicitly committing to maintain it indefinitely. Bugs emerge. Schema requirements change. Load characteristics shift. Every hour spent keeping the bespoke system alive is an hour not spent on product features. Expertise asymmetry. The engineering team at Redis, Cassandra, or PostgreSQL has spent years solving the hard problems of your domain — durability, consistency, performance under load, graceful degradation. Unless your specific requirements are truly unusual, they have almost certainly already solved the problem you are about to attempt. Rule of thumb: If a battle-tested open-source system solves 80% of your need, adopt it. Spend engineering time integrating, configuring, and operating it — not reimplementing it. The build decision is driven by two axes: how unique your requirement is, and whether your team has capacity to build and maintain the system long-term. When Build Is the Right Answer Building makes sense when all three of the following are true: The requirement is genuinely unique. No existing system handles your workload's specific combination of constraints — access pattern, latency, consistency, or data model — without significant workarounds. The team has the expertise and capacity. Not just to build the initial version, but to maintain it through production incidents, scaling events, and personnel turnover. The strategic value is high. The system is a core competitive differentiator, not supporting infrastructure. A payment company's fraud-detection model is strategic; its session cache is not. A real example: Google built Bigtable and Spanner because their workloads (global web indexing, worldwide advertising auctions) were genuinely unprecedented at the time. Most companies building internal tools are not in that position. The Hybrid Path: Adopt and Extend Most real decisions are not binary. The practical options: Path When to use Adopt as-is Standard workload, off-the-shelf fits 90%+ Adopt and configure Fit is 80%; custom schemas, indexes, and query patterns close the gap Adopt and extend Open-source system; you contribute plugins or patches Adopt with an abstraction layer Isolate the vendor behind a thin interface; swap later if needed Build from scratch Unique requirements + team capacity + strategic value The abstraction layer deserves emphasis. Even when adopting a battle-tested system, wrapping the database client in a thin interface ( SessionStore , MetricsWriter ) makes the vendor choice reversible and makes testing easier. Open Source vs Commercial Both "buy" paths — open source and commercial — have different trade-off profiles: Open source (PostgreSQL, Redis, Cassandra): No license cost; operational and hosting costs remain You are responsible for upgrades, patches, and incident response Community support; quality varies by community size Full access to source code; bugs can be fixed upstream or forked Commercial / managed (AWS RDS, Snowflake, MongoDB Atlas): License or consumption cost; operational burden transferred Vendor responsible for patching, upgrades, backups Vendor support SLA; quality varies by tier Lock-in risk: proprietary features may be hard to migrate away from Interactive SQL · Build vs Buy Cost Model Key Takeaways Build almost never means writing a storage engine; it means reimplementing something an off-the-shelf system already provides. The true cost of building includes not just initial development but perpetual maintenance. Build only when requirements are genuinely unique, the team has capacity, and the system provides strategic differentiation. The hybrid path — adopt with a thin abstraction layer — gives flexibility without the full cost of building from scratch. ← Previous One database or many Next → Managed vs self-hosted

title: Managed vs self-hosted
text: Once you have decided which database to use, a second decision immediately follows: run it yourself, or let a cloud provider manage it for you? This choice shapes your operational burden, your cost structure, your hiring profile, and your ability to move quickly when something goes wrong. Neither answer is universally correct, but the trade-offs are well understood and the decision can be made with a short checklist. What "Managed" Actually Means A managed database service (Amazon RDS, Google Cloud SQL, MongoDB Atlas, PlanetScale, Supabase) takes ownership of a specific set of operational concerns: Provisioning and configuration — spinning up instances, setting memory and CPU, configuring storage. Automated backups — point-in-time recovery with defined retention windows. Patch management — the vendor applies database engine updates (with configurable maintenance windows). Failover — automated primary/replica promotion on hardware failure. Monitoring basics — CPU, memory, disk, connections; often integrated with the cloud provider's dashboard. What managed services typically do not handle: schema design, query optimisation, index strategy, application-level connection pooling, and cost control when queries are inefficient. Important: Managed does not mean maintenance-free. A slow query on a managed RDS instance is your problem to diagnose and fix, not Amazon's. What "Self-Hosted" Actually Means Self-hosted means you run the database process on infrastructure you control — whether on-premise servers, cloud VMs (EC2, GCE), or containers in Kubernetes. You are responsible for everything the managed service would otherwise provide: Installation, version management, and upgrades Backup scripts, restore testing, and offsite storage Replication setup and failover automation Monitoring, alerting, and on-call runbooks Security patches and OS hardening This is not inherently expensive or slow — large, experienced teams run self-hosted PostgreSQL clusters at enormous scale. But it requires expertise, process, and time. Managed services transfer infrastructure responsibilities to the vendor; application-layer concerns remain with the team regardless of hosting mode. The Cost Trade-Off The most common objection to managed services is cost. Cloud-managed databases carry a premium — typically 30–60% above the raw compute and storage cost of a self-hosted equivalent. For a small team this premium is almost always worth it. For a large team operating hundreds of database instances, the math sometimes shifts. The key comparison is total cost of ownership , not sticker price: Self-hosted true cost = infrastructure cost + (DBA/ops engineer hours × blended rate) + opportunity cost of engineer time not on product + incident response cost (downtime × revenue impact) + cost of backup infrastructure and testing A $500/month managed RDS instance versus a $250/month EC2 instance running PostgreSQL is not a $250/month saving if a DBA spends 10 hours/month on the self-hosted instance — that's $1,000–1,500 in engineering time at typical rates. When Self-Hosted Wins Self-hosting makes sense in specific situations: Regulatory or data-sovereignty requirements prevent data from leaving a specific physical location or cloud region. A managed service in the right region may still satisfy this, but on-premise self-hosting is sometimes the only compliant option. Massive scale where the volume discount from operating your own infrastructure outweighs the operational cost. Companies at Netflix or Uber scale operate their own database infrastructure for precisely this reason. Configuration control unavailable in managed offerings — specific OS tuning, custom extensions, non-standard replication topologies, or features not yet available in the managed version. Database version control — managed services sometimes lag behind the latest engine version. If your application requires a bleeding-edge feature, you may need to self-host. Managed Service Comparison Dimension Managed Self-hosted Operational burden Low High Configuration flexibility Limited Full Cost at small scale Higher unit price, lower total Lower unit price, higher total Cost at large scale Can become very expensive Often cheaper at volume Recovery from failure Fast (automated) Depends on runbooks Compliance flexibility Depends on vendor certifications Full control Upgrade friction Low (vendor managed) Requires testing and planning Interactive SQL · Managed vs Self-Hosted TCO Comparison Key Takeaways Managed services transfer infrastructure operations to the vendor; application-layer concerns (schema, query performance) always remain yours. The true cost of self-hosting includes engineering time, not just infrastructure; at small scale, managed is often cheaper in total. Self-hosting is justified by regulatory requirements, massive scale, or configuration needs that managed offerings cannot satisfy. Evaluate on total cost of ownership, not monthly infrastructure sticker price alone. ← Previous Build vs buy Next → Cost, team, and operations

title: Cost, team, and operations
text: The right database for a workload and the right database for a team are not always the same system. A distributed wide-column store might be the theoretically optimal choice for a write-heavy time-series workload — but if your team has three engineers, none of whom have ever operated Cassandra, you have traded a slow query for a slow disaster recovery. The human and organisational dimensions of database selection are as real as the technical ones, and they are more often underweighted. The Three Cost Buckets Every database deployment incurs costs in three categories that are easy to conflate but important to keep separate: 1. Infrastructure Cost The bill from your cloud provider or data centre: compute, storage, network egress, I/O operations. This is the most visible cost and the easiest to benchmark, but it is rarely the largest. 2. Engineering Cost The time your team spends designing schemas, writing migrations, tuning queries, building monitoring, responding to incidents, training new hires, and keeping the system upgraded. This is the least visible cost and almost always the largest at small to medium scale. 3. Operational Risk Cost The expected cost of failure events: downtime, data loss, degraded performance. This is a probabilistic cost — low probability, high impact — and it is routinely underestimated when choosing a less-familiar system. A team that has never operated a Cassandra cluster does not know what failure modes to monitor for, which makes recovery slower and more expensive. Infrastructure cost is the easiest to see; engineering cost is usually the largest; operational risk cost is probabilistic but can dominate in a bad incident. Team Size and Database Complexity There is a rough relationship between team size and the operational complexity a database can reasonably sustain: Team size Appropriate operational complexity 1–3 engineers Fully managed service; minimal ops; SQLite or single-node PostgreSQL 4–10 engineers Managed service with custom tuning; one dedicated ops person per database 10–30 engineers Self-hosted with dedicated DBA or platform team; well-documented runbooks 30+ engineers Custom tooling, internal database teams, specialised expertise per system These ranges are not rules — a three-person team with two experienced DBAs can operate a complex cluster effectively. The point is that complexity always has an operational cost that must be staffed, documented, and on-called. The On-Call Test A practical heuristic: imagine this database goes down at 3 am. Walk through the failure: Does your monitoring detect the failure automatically, or does a user report it? Does your on-call engineer know the database's failure modes and recovery procedures? Does your runbook cover this failure type? Can you restore from backup to a known-good state within your RTO (Recovery Time Objective)? If any of these answers is "no" or "I'm not sure," the database may be introducing operational risk your team isn't equipped to absorb. That is not a reason to avoid the database — it is a reason to invest in runbooks, training, and monitoring before relying on it for production traffic. Hiring and Knowledge Transfer Database expertise is part of your team's bus factor. If one engineer is the sole person who understands how to operate your Cassandra cluster, you have a single point of failure in your org chart. Sustainable database operations require: Documentation: runbooks for common failure modes, upgrade procedures, backup/restore verification steps Cross-training: more than one person can diagnose slow queries and manage failover Onboarding material: new engineers can reach baseline competence within weeks, not months Practical note: When interviewing engineers, "we run Cassandra" is a signal some candidates will see as exciting and others will see as a warning. A less common database shrinks your hiring pool. Cost Modelling Interactive SQL · Team Capacity vs Database Complexity Key Takeaways Database cost has three components: infrastructure (visible), engineering time (largest), and operational risk (probabilistic but impactful). Match operational complexity to team size; a cluster that requires a dedicated DBA is a liability for a three-person team. Apply the 3 am test: if your team cannot recover from failure confidently, the database is riskier than its benchmark numbers suggest. Document runbooks, cross-train the team, and factor hiring implications into the decision. ← Previous Managed vs self-hosted Next → Portability and lock-in

title: Portability and lock-in
text: Every database you adopt is a commitment, but not all commitments are equally binding. Some databases are built on open standards and can be swapped with modest effort. Others expose proprietary APIs, query languages, and data formats that make migration expensive enough to be effectively permanent. Understanding the spectrum of lock-in before you adopt a system gives you the ability to make that trade-off consciously rather than discovering it when you want to leave. What Lock-In Actually Looks Like Lock-in is not a binary property — it exists on a spectrum, and it operates through several distinct mechanisms: API and query language lock-in. A system that uses standard SQL (even with extensions) is easier to migrate from than one that uses a proprietary query language. Moving from PostgreSQL to MySQL is painful but tractable because both speak SQL. Moving from DynamoDB's API to a different key-value store requires rewriting every database call. Data format lock-in. If your data is stored in a proprietary binary format that requires the vendor's tools to read, export becomes an active project rather than a background concern. Databases that support standard export formats (CSV, Parquet, SQL dump, JSON) give you an escape hatch. Feature lock-in. Using a proprietary feature — DynamoDB Streams, Spanner's TrueTime-based external consistency, Snowflake's TIME_TRAVEL — adds value but creates migration cost. When the time comes to move, any feature without a direct equivalent in the target system requires application redesign. Operational lock-in. Your team's expertise, your monitoring setup, and your deployment automation are all built around the current system. Even if the data is portable, the operational knowledge is not. Lock-in is a spectrum. Standard SQL and open formats reduce migration cost; proprietary APIs and managed-only deployment increase it. The Cost of Migration The cost of migrating away from a database has two components: Data migration cost — exporting data from the source, transforming it to the target's format, validating completeness and correctness. For large datasets this can take weeks or months, with complex cutover logistics to avoid data loss during the transition. Application migration cost — rewriting every place in the application that talks to the database: connection setup, queries, transactions, error handling. The more proprietary the API, the larger this surface area. A rough heuristic: migrating between two databases that both speak standard SQL costs roughly one to three months of engineering effort for a medium-sized application. Migrating from a proprietary key-value API to anything else is more like rewriting the data access layer from scratch. Strategies to Reduce Lock-In Use standard SQL where possible. Even when your database supports proprietary extensions, prefer standard SQL for core queries. This does not mean avoiding useful extensions — PostgreSQL's JSONB or window functions are worth using — but it means being deliberate about which proprietary features you rely on critically. Wrap the database behind a thin abstraction layer. A repository or data-access layer that hides the database-specific client behind an interface means that changing the underlying database requires changing one layer, not every call site. This is more relevant for application code than for complex query logic. // Low lock-in: query language hidden behind interface interface UserRepository { findById(id: string): Promise<User> save(user: User): Promise<void> } // Higher lock-in: DynamoDB-specific code scattered across services const result = await dynamoClient.query({ TableName: 'users', KeyConditionExpression: 'pk = :pk', ExpressionAttributeValues: { ':pk': { S: userId } } }).promise() Plan data exports regularly. Test your ability to export the full dataset in a portable format (SQL dump, Parquet, JSON lines) on a schedule. This serves two purposes: it validates your backup, and it proves that the migration path is open. Separate the system-of-record from the specialised store. If your canonical data lives in a portable relational database and a specialised store (search, cache, analytics) holds derived copies, migrating the specialised store is much simpler — you can rebuild it from the primary. When Lock-In Is Acceptable Lock-in is not always bad. If the specialised capabilities of a system provide significant competitive or operational advantage, and the cost of migration is a known, acceptable risk, locking in may be the right choice. DynamoDB's managed infrastructure, multi-region active-active replication, and predictable pricing are genuinely valuable — many companies accept the API lock-in in exchange for that operational simplicity. The mistake is not accepting lock-in; the mistake is accepting it unknowingly, without having evaluated the migration cost. Interactive SQL · Migration Cost Estimation by Lock-In Level Key Takeaways Lock-in operates through four mechanisms: query language, data format, proprietary features, and operational knowledge. Migration costs have two components: data migration and application rewrite — both scale with lock-in level. Reduce lock-in by preferring standard SQL, wrapping databases in thin abstraction layers, and validating data exports regularly. Lock-in can be an acceptable trade-off if the capabilities justify it — the mistake is accepting it unknowingly. ← Previous Cost, team, and operations Next → When to switch systems

title: When to switch systems
text: No database lasts forever in the same role. Workloads grow, product requirements shift, team capabilities change, and a system that was the right choice at ten thousand users may be the wrong choice at ten million. Knowing when to switch — and, equally, when the pain is not yet severe enough to justify the disruption — is a skill that saves teams from both premature migrations and from staying far too long with a system that is actively harming them. The Cost of Switching Is Always Real Before exploring the signals that indicate a switch is warranted, it's worth stating clearly: database migrations are expensive . A migration that sounds straightforward at the planning stage routinely reveals data quality issues, undetected schema inconsistencies, query patterns that don't translate cleanly, and cutover complexity that requires weeks of parallel operation. Teams that treat migration as a background task or a quick weekend project consistently underestimate the investment. This is not an argument against switching — it's an argument for having a high bar. The pain of staying must be concretely worse than the pain of migrating. Signals That Warrant a Switch 1. Performance Has Hit a Structural Ceiling The most unambiguous signal: you have exhausted the reasonable tuning options (indexes, query optimisation, hardware scaling, read replicas) and the system still cannot meet your latency or throughput requirements. This is a structural ceiling — the database's architecture simply cannot serve your workload efficiently. Examples of structural ceilings: A relational database serving pure time-series writes at >50K events/second — the row-per-event model and B-tree index overhead make this painful regardless of hardware. A key-value store being asked to serve range queries and aggregates — the hash-based structure physically cannot do this efficiently. A single-node system where the dataset has grown beyond the memory and disk of any single machine. Caution: Distinguish structural ceilings from tuning problems. Many "the database is too slow" complaints are actually missing indexes, N+1 query patterns, or connection pool misconfiguration — problems that take days to fix, not months to migrate away from. 2. The Query Model Has Fundamentally Changed A product that started as a simple CRUD application may evolve to need full-text search, graph traversal, or analytical aggregations across billions of rows. If these new query types are fundamental to the product — not edge cases — and the current database cannot serve them well, a switch (or an addition) is warranted. The key question: is the new query type a core user-facing feature or an operational report? Core features justify architectural investment; operational reports often tolerate slower queries. 3. Operational Cost Has Become Unsustainable If maintaining the database is consuming a disproportionate share of engineering time — incident response, complex upgrades, scaling gymnastics, workarounds for fundamental limitations — that operational burden has a measurable opportunity cost. The team is not building product features; they are fighting infrastructure. This signal is often slow to appear. The cost accumulates gradually: one more workaround, one more migration script, one more three-day incident. The moment it becomes obvious often comes only when someone steps back and measures the cumulative hours. Before deciding to switch, exhaust tuning options; switch only when the pain is structural and measurable, not just frustrating. How to Execute a Migration Safely Once the decision to switch is made, the migration pattern that minimises risk follows a consistent sequence: Proof of concept with real data. Before committing, validate that the target database can serve your workload at realistic scale and query patterns. Use a representative subset of production data, not synthetic benchmarks. Build the new system in parallel. Run the new database alongside the old one. Write new data to both (dual write), with the old system as the authoritative source. Backfill historical data. Migrate historical data to the new system in batches, with validation at each step. Shadow reads. Route a percentage of read traffic to the new system and compare results. Surface discrepancies before they affect users. Cut over reads, then writes. Switch reads to the new system first; keep the old system warm as a fallback. Once confident, switch writes and retire the old system's primary role. Keep the old system available for rollback. Define a rollback point. The old system should remain operational and capable of serving traffic for a defined period (days to weeks) after cutover. When NOT to Switch The workload fits but performance is below expectations: Tune first. An unindexed column or a missing connection pool is not a reason to migrate. The team wants to use a newer, more interesting technology: Technical interest is legitimate but is not a workload signal. A single large query is slow: Fix the query or add an index. Do not redesign the data layer for one problematic query. A vendor published a benchmark that makes your database look bad: Vendor benchmarks use ideal conditions. Your workload is not ideal conditions. Interactive SQL · Migration Readiness Checklist Key Takeaways Database migrations are expensive; the pain of staying must be worse than the pain of migrating. Switch when performance has hit a structural ceiling (not a tuning problem), the query model has fundamentally changed, or operational cost is unsustainable. Execute migrations through a proof-of-concept, parallel operation, dual writes, shadow reads, and a defined rollback window. Resist the urge to switch for novelty, a single slow query, or a competitor's architecture. ← Previous Portability and lock-in Next → A practical decision framework

title: A practical decision framework
text: The previous pages in this chapter examined individual dimensions of the database selection problem: workload analysis, key questions, the build-vs-buy trade-off, managed vs self-hosted, team and cost considerations, lock-in, and when to switch. This final page assembles those dimensions into a single, actionable framework you can apply to a real decision — not a theoretical one, but the concrete choice in front of your team right now. The Framework in Four Steps Step 1: Write Down Your Workload Before evaluating any database, write a one-page description of your workload. It should answer: What are the five most-frequent reads and writes? What are the peak volumes (requests/second) for each? What is the p99 latency target for user-facing queries? What is the dataset size today and in 12 months? What consistency guarantee does the business require? What is the schema evolution pace (stable vs weekly changes)? If you cannot answer these questions, you are not ready to choose a database. The time spent gathering these numbers is the highest-leverage investment in the decision process. Step 2: Filter by Access Pattern Access pattern is the single most powerful filter. Map your dominant query type to the category that handles it natively: Filter by access pattern first; that alone eliminates most options. Then score the remaining candidates on operational and cost dimensions. Step 3: Score the Shortlist After step 2 you should have two or three candidate systems. Score each on these dimensions (1–5 scale, weighted by importance to your situation): Dimension Weight (example) Why it matters Access pattern fit 5 Structural — cannot be worked around Consistency guarantee 4 Business requirement — non-negotiable Write throughput 3 Only relevant if writes dominate Team operational expertise 4 Determines incident recovery quality Total cost of ownership 3 Infrastructure + engineering + risk Schema evolution ease 2 Product velocity impact Lock-in risk 2 Long-term strategic exposure Multiply each score by its weight, sum the columns, and compare. This is not a mechanical algorithm — it is a forcing function for making trade-offs explicit and visible to the whole team, not just the person who found the interesting new database.
